We also discuss why eye gaze isn\u2019t plug-and-play, why professional support ...\n\nRead](https://www.undivided.io/2026/02/11/undivided-voices-podcast-season-2-episode-4-how-eye-gaze-aac-really-works-and-what-families-should-know/)\n\n[![Child with a disability at school with peers](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/image.webp)\n\nFebruary 5, 2026\n\n### Big changes ahead? Let\u2019s talk transition IEPs for all ages\n\nTransitions and milestones have a way of sneaking up on us, even when we know they\u2019re coming. One day you\u2019re finally feeling like you\u2019re getting into the groove of your child\u2019s school routine, and the next you\u2019re being asked to consider what\u2019s next \u2013 a new grade level, a new school, new classmates, teachers, and ...\n\nRead](https://www.undivided.io/2026/02/05/big-changes-ahead-lets-talk-transition-ieps-for-all-ages/)\n\n[![Undivided Voices podcast logo](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/podcast-thumbnail-rectangle.png)\n\nFebruary 4, 2026\n\n### Undivided Voices Podcast Season 2 Episode 3: IEP Assessments 101 \u2013 What Parents Need to Know\n\nIn this episode of Undivided Voices, Dr. Sarah Pelangka, special education advocate and owner of KnowIEPs, breaks down one of the most important\u2014and often overwhelming\u2014parts of the special education process: assessments.\u00a0Dr. Pelangka explains how assessments work, how they\u2019re administered, how to read and understand results, and what families can do if they disagree with the ...\n\nRead](https://www.undivided.io/2026/02/04/undivided-voices-podcast-season-2-episode-3-iep-assessments-101-what-parents-need-to-know/)\n\n[![Student with a backpack using a wheelchair](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image.webp)\n\nJanuary 29, 2026\n\n### Watch out for these IEP red flags\n\nMost parents don\u2019t walk into IEP meetings looking for red flags. \ud83d\udea9 We\u2019re just trying to get through the day and make sure our kids are supported by an IEP that does what it\u2019s supposed to. But sometimes the biggest problems show up quietly: a goal that\u2019s too vague, progress that isn\u2019t really progress, or ...\n\nRead](https://www.undivided.io/2026/01/29/watch-out-for-these-iep-red-flags/)\n\n[![Undivided Voices podcast logo](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/podcast-thumbnail-rectangle.png)\n\nJanuary 28, 2026\n\n### Undivided Voices Podcast Season 2, Episode 2: Behind the Scenes With a Special Education Administrator\n\nEver wonder what really happens behind closed doors in special education? \ud83d\udc40 IEP meetings are just the tip of the iceberg\u2014and there\u2019s a lot families don\u2019t always hear. If you\u2019re weighing options, asking questions, or wondering whether your child\u2019s current placement is the right fit, we\u2019re here to help you make sense of it all.\n\n![Related free resources](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/header-relatedresources.png)\n\n**Explore the least restrictive environment (LRE)**\n\nPlacement is tied to the idea of LRE, meaning our kids should be included with peers in general education as much as possible while still getting the supports they need \u2014 though agreeing on how that works isn\u2019t always simple. Placement discussions are meant to start with inclusive options, like GenEd with accommodations, modifications, and 1:1 aides, or a mix of GenEd and SpEd classes, before moving toward separate classrooms, separate schools, home education options, and finally, the most restrictive options. But how is all this decided and what\u2019s the best fit for your child? [Explore LRE and how placement is decided](https://undivided.io/resources/124)\u2192\n\n**If public school doesn\u2019t seem like the right fit\u2026**\n\nSeparate schools:\n\n* Private school can be a promising option for some families, but it comes with important differences to understand. Private schools operate independently and aren\u2019t always required to provide special education services or accept students with disabilities. [Learn about private school\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/1963)\n* A non-public school (NPS) is a separate school for students with various disabilities who need more support than a general education setting can provide. But is it right for your child? [Read about NPS here\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/1964)\n\n[![Questions to ask when touring a private school](https://join.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/private-school-infographic.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/private-school-101-1963)\n\n[![NPS factors for parents to consider non-public school](https://join.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/nps-infographic.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/non-public-school-nps-101-1964)\n\nHome education options:\n\n* Independent study offers students a way to learn outside the traditional classroom. Explore how it works within special education in our newest article. [Independent study in California\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/3692)\n* Homeschooling is more than cozy learning at home, especially when your child has an IEP and needs services. We break down homeschooling options, from charter schools to curriculum choices and more. [Homeschooling in California\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/4)\n* Home Hospital (HH) can help when your child needs to step away from school temporarily due to illness or mental health needs. But who qualifies for the program, how does it work, and what are the steps to request it? [Learn about home hospital here\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/285)\n\n[![Homeschool options in California](https://join.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/homeschool-infographic.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/california-homeschooling-101-4)\n\nMost restrictive environments:\n\n* Residential placement is the most restrictive option in which the student lives at a residential facility where they access their schooling. Our new article breaks down residential placement and what parents need to know. [Read about residential placement here\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/3690)\n\n[![When would an IEP team consider residential placement?](https://join.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/Residential-placement-infographic.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/residential-placement-101-3690)\n\n**Disagreeing about placement?**\n\nWhat can you do when your child\u2019s placement no longer feels like the right fit \u2014 whether they need more support, less support, or a change in setting? [What to do if your child\u2019s placement isn\u2019t working\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/2782)\n\nIf they say, \u201cYour child is not at grade level. They can\u2019t keep up in a gen ed classroom,\u201d you say\u2026? [8 ways to respond to your IEP team when you disagree about placement \u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/2813)\n\n![Undivided events you won't want to miss](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/header-events.png)\n\n**Office hours \u2192 Wednesday, February 25, 12:00 p.m. PT**\n\nIf you\u2019re a California resident, join us for office hours with Lisa Concoff Kronbeck, Undivided\u2019s public benefits specialist, to ask your questions about Regional Center, IHSS, SSI, Medi-Cal, and other public benefits programs. If you\u2019re not a member, [sign up here to participate](https://undivided.io/app/join).\n\n**Undivided Conversations \u2192 Saturday, February 27, 10:00 a.m. PT**\n\nResidential Placement 101: In this brand new conversation, Non-Attorney Education Advocate Hadassah Lynn Foster, Education Consultant Kelly Rain Collin, Senior Attorney Diana Maltz, and Special Education Attorney Tania Whiteleather explain what residential placement is, when it may be appropriate, and how it works within the special education system. Catch the conversation streaming on [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/undividedapp) and [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/@undividedapp).\n\n![Connect with Undivided](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/header-socials.png)\n\n![https://stratus.campaign-image.com/images/794625000067134004_zc_v1_1762203007152_unnamed_1.png](https://ci3.googleusercontent.com/meips/ADKq_Nb8klAgQaGm_o_dd-M-SPEczIUmg_jpq1kVcvwnn3w27zjc-M18BnIRW8DbwoT6bUmvi2qhRlFyaFVKGmSVA-tl6Vj3H2weh2qYkWSSDbuzbwjQNpKYSk7hxU4hNZqLJ-hZNH1CWUipd6uPrMT1fQqQxmyS=s0-d-e1-ft#https://stratus.campaign-image.com/images/794625000067134004_zc_v1_1762203007152_unnamed_1.png)\u00a0Can the school change your child\u2019s location without changing their placement? [Learn the difference here on our YouTube channel\u2192](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W4Jf9FbMcF8)\n\n![https://stratus.campaign-image.com/images/794625000067134004_zc_v1_1762202847896_unnamed_2.png](https://ci3.googleusercontent.com/meips/ADKq_Na4zFr2mrb8cx4dTAkhmXVenhAX9k6X0euU5NwAd6KjrBnilk-2p99qSweR1bh8ly_n1GbbZrlfs0cdhAZ2HTRy2qQIQyyqg0QarF_mNCY16YCUOqDS511flKr9fEq1VOX1AKJ_TcYJCo-m39-tBLl8XJDp=s0-d-e1-ft#https://stratus.campaign-image.com/images/794625000067134004_zc_v1_1762202847896_unnamed_2.png)\u00a0What does special education law say about placement? [Save this Instagram post for a quick explanation\u2192](https://www.instagram.com/p/DUo4AfIF8HH)\n\n![https://stratus.campaign-image.com/images/794625000067134004_zc_v1_1762202921651_unnamed_3.png](https://ci3.googleusercontent.com/meips/ADKq_NbRytlHGxGM-FUPJEAGn7pTFogTTFyIeTDy5e_DV2uzNUFV0W7c_0-bMcPbQS9E6uCa_qkOjJNgtN-LFaXB8kJR8TpAKIv5ODZPViLqCXh2qA7KHRgegZI4WzhVAXosKm3AXlLpxWekfBmM88mQGP8S2rjy=s0-d-e1-ft#https://stratus.campaign-image.com/images/794625000067134004_zc_v1_1762202921651_unnamed_3.png)\u00a0This week in our parents-only Facebook group: GPS tracker recommendations, a public benefits survey, helpful webinars, and more! Let\u2019s talk transition IEPs for all ages\n\n![Child with a disability at school with peers](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/image.webp)\n\nTransitions and milestones have a way of sneaking up on us, even when we know they\u2019re coming. One day you\u2019re finally feeling like you\u2019re getting into the groove of your child\u2019s school routine, and the next you\u2019re being asked to consider what\u2019s next \u2013 a new grade level, a new school, new classmates, teachers, and even life after high school. For families of kids with disabilities, these moments can come with even more questions, more planning, and a transition IEP that suddenly feels\u2026 like a lot.\n\nFrom preschool drop-offs to high school goodbyes, we\u2019re here to help you tackle transition IEPs \u2014 and make the whole transition easier for your child (and for you, too).\n\n![Related free resources](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/header-relatedresources.png)\n\nCongratulations \u2014 your little one is getting ready for preschool! What kind of classroom will your child thrive in? What should you ask potential schools? And how do you even begin the IEP process? [Learn how to choose a preschool and prepare for your child\u2019s first IEP\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/256)\n\n[![How to find an inclusive preschool near you](https://join.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/inclusive-preschool-updated.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/choosing-a-preschool-and-preparing-for-your-childs-first-iep-256)\n\nPreschool to elementary school is a big leap. It\u2019s normal to have questions. How will they handle a longer day or a more academic classroom? How can their IEP help set the tone for a positive elementary school experience? [Tips to make the transition to kindergarten with an IEP easier\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/216)\n\n[![Tips for IEP meeting from preschool to elementary](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/transition-preschool-to-elementary.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/making-the-transition-to-kindergarten-with-an-iep-216)\n\nMiddle school is a whole different world. Bigger campuses, multiple teachers, lockers, changing for gym class, and a cafeteria that can feel like the Wild West! [What supports can help your child thrive? Make the transition middle school with an IEP\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/199)\n\n[![IEP meeting transition to middle school tips](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/transition-elementary-to-middle.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/making-the-transition-to-middle-school-with-an-iep-199)\n\nFinally, high school. How will your child manage the expectation of increased independence? What supports still matter, and which ones need to evolve? And how can their IEP help lay the groundwork for a meaningful future? [Read about the transition to high school with an IEP\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/235) and [explore the individual transition plan (ITP)\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/476)\n\n[![What to discuss in IEP meeting transition to high school](https://join.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/transition-middle-to-high-school-updated-2.png)](https://undivided.io/app/resources/making-the-transition-to-high-school-with-an-iep-235)\n\nTransferring schools and districts with an IEP? It can feel stressful, whether you\u2019re moving by choice, looking for a better fit, or relocating for work or military service. But with the right information, it\u2019s possible to protect your kiddo\u2019s services and make the transition smoother. [Tips and resources for transferring school districts with an IEP\u2192](https://undivided.io/resources/1887)\n\n**Downloadable \u2192 Printable school tour checklists**\n\nDuring an educational placement transition, it\u2019s important to take a tour of your child\u2019s prospective school(s) so that you can get a sense of the teachers, the classroom, and how well students with disabilities are (or are not) included. Undivided members can download our printable school tour checklists for [preschool](https://undivided.io/resources/2503) and [K-12.](https://undivided.io/resources/2501)\n\n![Read our newest articles](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/header-articles.png)\n\n**Disability-friendly sleepaway and family camps in California**\n\nIt\u2019s time to start thinking about summer break already?! Yep \u2014 registration for 2026 summer camps is open for many organizations, and spots are starting to fill up! The Undivided Research Team has put together a list of disability-friendly overnight and sleepaway camps across California, some of which are open to the whole family! From adaptive sports to arts and crafts, these camps facilitate all kinds of activities for campers of varying ages and support needs. Registration for many of them is open from now through spring, and some offer scholarships and/or accept Regional Center funding, so [check out our roundup of sleepaway camps in California for summer 2026](https://undivided.io/resources/2682).\n\n![Undivided events you won't want to miss](https://www.undivided.io/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/header-events.png)\n\n**Event recap \u2192 Meet your new IEP Assistant!**\n\nUndivided\u2019s brand-new IEP Assistant is here! A first-of-its-kind tool designed to help parents review key information in their child\u2019s IEP and prepare for more productive conversations with the school. How does it work, and how can you try it for yourself? [Watch the replay](https://undivided.io/resources/3633) of our walkthrough and Q&A with CEO Seth Besse, and [read the answers to common parent questions about IEP Assistant here](https://undivided.io/resources/3609).\n\n**Office hours \u2192 Wednesday, February 11, 12:00 p.m. PT**\n\nIf you have questions about your child\u2019s accommodations, services, goals, or anything else in their IEP, join us for office hours with non-attorney education advocate Lisa Carey. During office hours, you can ask your question in our members-only Zoom session and get answers right away. If you\u2019re not a member, [sign up here to participate](https://undivided.io/app/join).\n\n**Undivided Conversations \u2192 Saturday, February 7, 10:00 a.m. PT**\n\nWhen your child is moving from preschool to kindergarten, or to middle or high school, your school will hold a transition IEP meeting to set your child up for a successful move. Join Dr. Pelangka as she takes us through your transition IEP and how to introduce your child to their new team and school. Links](/resources/helpful-links)\n\n# Special Education Basics Toolkit\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\n# Special Education Basics Toolkit\n\nShare:\n\n[![Facebook icon](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/images/FB_Share_v2_0.png)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=&t= \"Share on Facebook\")\n\n[![Twitter icon](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/images/Twitter_Share_v2_0.png)](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet? \"Tweet\")\n\n[![LinkedIn icon](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/images/LinkedIn_Share_v2_0.png)](http://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=&title=&summary=&source= \"Share on LinkedIn\")\n\n[![Email icon](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/images/Email_Icon_v2_0.png)](mailto:?subject=&body=:%20 \"Email\")\n\n![Image of two children with a disability in a classroom. A teacher is show them how to do a project](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/images/Special_Education_Basics_Toolkit_header.png)\n\n**Disclaimer:** These materials are based on the law at the time we write them. We try to update our materials; however, laws are regularly changing. If you want to make sure the law has not changed, contact us or another legal source.\n\n![](/system/files/styles/original_small/private/images/Special_Education_Basics_Toolkit_header.png?itok=3TMzuZhw)\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nNov 1, 2021\n\n#7170.01\n\n\n\n## [Common District Statements to Parents/Guardians and Possible Parent/Guardian Responses to Common District Statements](/publications/common-district-statements-to-parents-guardians-and-possible-parent-guardian-responses)\n\nSchool districts make common statements that may deny or delay your child\u2019s right to special education and related services. This resource has common statements that school districts make to parents/guardians. If your child\u2019s school district says one of these common statements to you, we have provided a brief summary of the law and a possible response that you can say to the school district.\n\n[Read more about Common District Statements to Parents/Guardians and Possible Parent/Guardian Responses to Common District Statements](/publications/common-district-statements-to-parents-guardians-and-possible-parent-guardian-responses \"Common District Statements to Parents/Guardians and Possible Parent/Guardian Responses to Common District Statements\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nJun 1, 2020\n\n#5130.01\n\n\n\n## [17 Special Education Advocacy Tips](/publications/17-special-education-advocacy-tips)\n\nThis document will provide you with tips before, during, and after an IEP meeting to help you advocate for your child.\n\n[Read more about 17 Special Education Advocacy Tips](/publications/17-special-education-advocacy-tips \"17 Special Education Advocacy Tips\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n\n\n## [Know Your Rights Wallet Card](/publications/know-your-rights-wallet-card)\n\nYou have the right to access services you need with reasonable accommodations during the COVID-19 pandemic. The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), the Unruh Civil Rights Act and the Disabled Persons Act prohibit disability discrimination by California businesses and government agencies. Present this wallet card to help you explain your rights and request the accommodations you need in places like the grocery store, agency offices and other businesses.\n\n[Read more about Know Your Rights Wallet Card](/publications/know-your-rights-wallet-card \"Know Your Rights Wallet Card\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n#7172.01\n\n\n\n## [Special Education Timelines](/publications/special-education-timelines)\n\nSpecial Education has a lot of timelines and it can be hard to keep track! Use this document to look up when you can expect meetings, responses, assessments, and more!\n\n[Read more about Special Education Timelines](/publications/special-education-timelines \"Special Education Timelines\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n#8081.01\n\n\n\n## [Special Education Terms to Know](/publications/special-education-terms-to-know)\n\nReading Special Education-related records or documents can be hard! Sometimes it can feel like trying to understand a new language. Below is a list of some of the acronyms and terms. We hope it will help you better understand your child\u2019s records.\n\n[Read more about Special Education Terms to Know](/publications/special-education-terms-to-know \"Special Education Terms to Know\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nSep 1, 2021\n\n#8082.01\n\n\n\n## [Understanding Psycho-Educational Evaluations](/publications/understanding-psycho-educational-evaluations)\n\nPsycho-educational evaluations are an important type of special education assessment. Sometimes, these evaluations can be hard to understand. Below is information to help you read these assessments, including: (1) Components of a Psycho-Educational Evaluation, (2) Types of Tests Commonly Used, and (3) Common Terms Defined.\n\n[Read more about Understanding Psycho-Educational Evaluations](/publications/understanding-psycho-educational-evaluations \"Understanding Psycho-Educational Evaluations\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nFeb 1, 2021\n\n#5476.01\n\n\n\n## [How To Obtain An Independent Educational Evaluation At Public Expense](/publications/how-to-obtain-an-independent-educational-evaluation-at-public-expense)\n\nThis publication provides information on how to obtain an independent educational evaluation at public expense from a school district. Another way that you can obtain an independent educational evaluation is to pay for the evaluation on your own.\n\n[Read more about How To Obtain An Independent Educational Evaluation At Public Expense](/publications/how-to-obtain-an-independent-educational-evaluation-at-public-expense \"How To Obtain An Independent Educational Evaluation At Public Expense\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n#8083.01\n\n\n\n## [Educationally Related Mental Health Services (ERMHS)](/publications/educationally-related-mental-health-services-ermhs)\n\nEducationally Related Mental Health Services (ERMHS) are mental health services for students who qualify for special education. ERMHS support student\u2019s social-emotional needs. They can also help to improve the student\u2019s academics, behavior, and overall well-being. ERMHS are individually tailored to the student\u2019s needs and to their Individual Education Program (\u201cIEP\u201d) goals. ERMHS may include counseling (individual, group) and/or social work services (collaboration and case management).\n\n[Read more about Educationally Related Mental Health Services (ERMHS)](/publications/educationally-related-mental-health-services-ermhs \"Educationally Related Mental Health Services (ERMHS)\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n#8084.01\n\n\n\n## [What Is Individualized Education Program (IEP) Progress?](/publications/what-is-individualized-education-program-iep-progress)\n\nThis publication provides necessary information to educate yourself\u00a0on IEP progress and all of its inner workings.\n\n[Read more about What Is Individualized Education Program (IEP) Progress?](/publications/what-is-individualized-education-program-iep-progress \"What Is Individualized Education Program (IEP) Progress?\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n\n\n## [Template Letter for a Request for Assessment](/publications/template-letter-for-a-request-for-assessment)\n\nUpdate this template to ask your school district to assess your student for special education. This template is written for\u00a0initial\u00a0requests for special education assessments. But, you can edit the form as-needed.\n\n[Template Letter (rtf)](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/file-attachments/2021.07.28%20Template%20request%20for%20assessment.rtf \"2021.07.28 Template request for assessment.rtf\")\n\n[Read more about Template Letter for a Request for Assessment](/publications/template-letter-for-a-request-for-assessment \"Template Letter for a Request for Assessment\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n\n\n## [Template Letter for Requesting Educational Records](/publications/template-letter-for-requesting-educational-records)\n\nUpdate this template to ask your school district to send you copies of your student\u2019s educational records.\n\n[Template Letter (rtf)](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/file-attachments/2021.07.28%20Template%20request%20ed%20records.rtf \"2021.07.28 Template request ed records.rtf\")\n\n[Read more about Template Letter for Requesting Educational Records](/publications/template-letter-for-requesting-educational-records \"Template Letter for Requesting Educational Records\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n\n\n## [Template Letter for Revising an Existing IEP plus Recording an IEP](/publications/template-letter-for-revising-an-existing-iep-plus-recording-an-iep)\n\nUpdate this template letter to ask your school district to hold an IEP meeting for your student. If you would like to audio record the meeting, be sure to use the optional language.\n\n[Template Letter (rtf)](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/file-attachments/2021.07.20%20Sample%20Letter%20-%20revise%20existing%20IEP%20%2B%20recording%20-%20FINAL.rtf \"2021.07.20 Sample Letter - revise existing IEP + recording - FINAL.rtf\")\n\n[Read more about Template Letter for Revising an Existing IEP plus Recording an IEP](/publications/template-letter-for-revising-an-existing-iep-plus-recording-an-iep \"Template Letter for Revising an Existing IEP plus Recording an IEP\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nSep 1, 2021\n\n\n\n## [Worksheet to Prepare for an Upcoming IEP Meeting](/publications/worksheet-to-prepare-for-an-upcoming-iep-meeting)\n\nIt can be overwhelming to prepare for an IEP meeting. Use this worksheet prepare for an upcoming IEP meeting.\n\n[IEP Worksheet (pdf)](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/file-attachments/IEP%20Prep%20Toolkit%20Item%20-%20Final.pdf \"IEP Prep Toolkit Item - Final.pdf\")\n\n[Read more about Worksheet to Prepare for an Upcoming IEP Meeting](/publications/worksheet-to-prepare-for-an-upcoming-iep-meeting \"Worksheet to Prepare for an Upcoming IEP Meeting\")\n\n![DRC Logo](/sites/default/files/default_images/default_banner.png)\n\nPublications\n\nAug 1, 2021\n\n\n\n## [Worksheet Tracking Log for IEP/504 Plan Services](/publications/worksheet-tracking-log-for-iep-504-plan-services)\n\nYour child has a right to receive the services outlined in their IEP. Use this tracking log to keep track of the services that your child receives. You can use this as evidence should you need to ask your school district to make up for services they did not provide your student. (This is especially important as school districts offer students learning loss recovery services and compensatory education for services not provided during distance learning!)\n\n[Worksheet Tracking Log (pdf)](https://www.disabilityrightsca.org/system/files/file-attachments/Tracking%20Log%20for%20Services.pdf \"Tracking Log for Services.pdf\")\n\n[Read more about Worksheet Tracking Log for IEP/504 Plan Services](/publications/worksheet-tracking-log-for-iep-504-plan-services \"Worksheet Tracking Log for IEP/504 Plan Services\")\n\n[![Disability Rights California](/system/files/images/DRC-Logo-Footer.png)](/ \"Homepage\") School](/images/guide-badge-english.svg)](https://www.ednavigator.org/guide)\n\nFeatured Guide\n\n# Special Education\n\nIf your child is struggling in school, there\u2019s a good chance you are also feeling overwhelmed by the process of getting them support. We get it: Getting our kids what they need to thrive in school is one of our biggest goals as parents. Here are some resources to help you help them.\n\n![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/guide-graphics/featured-guide-special-education-hero.svg)\n\n## If you think your kid is struggling in school but you\u2019re not sure why, start here:\n\n[![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Boy_Special_Needs_Painting_web.jpg)\n\n### What Parents Need to Know About Special Education\n\nAll kids learn differently\u2014and some children need additional support or specialized services to help them thrive.\n\nCommunicating with School\n\n/\n\nSpecial Education\n\n/\n\nAges\n5\n-\n18](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/what-parents-need-to-know-about-special-education)\n\n## Dig Deeper\n\n### Explore All of Our Resources for Special 5 Things Pediatricians Should Know about Special Education](https://www.ednavigator.org/blog/5-things-pediatricians-should-know-about-special-education)\n\n## Contact\n\n* School](/images/guide-badge-english.svg)](https://www.ednavigator.org/guide)\n\nCommunicating with School / Special Education\n/\nAges 5-18\n\n# What Parents Need to Know About Special Education\n\nAll kids learn differently\u2014and some children need additional support or specialized services to help them thrive.\n\n![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Boy_Special_Needs_Painting_web.jpg)\n\nIn the United States, all children have the legal right to a \u201cfree and appropriate public education.\u201d This is true regardless of your immigration status, or what language you speak at home, or any characteristic of your child\u2014including whether or not they have a disability.\n\n![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/video-posters/_850x478_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/EDN_IntroScreen_SpecialEducation_English_2024-05-10-150650_ahtg.jpg)\n\n## If you think your kid is struggling in school but you\u2019re not sure why or what to do about it, here\u2019s what you need to know:\n\n* **Children without diagnosed disabilities can still have access to support in school.**Many states use what\u2019s called a \u201cmulti-tiered system of support\u201d (MTSS) or \u201cresponse to intervention\u201d (RTI) to provide targeted support in school to students who need it. These *aren\u2019t* special education programs. Instead, they\u2019re ways of identifying students who are struggling in their learning and providing specific support strategies to give them what they need. Like the name suggests, MTSS provides multiple tiers of support for children across both academic and behavioral needs. RTI is an approach to providing support through targeted teaching strategies called \u201cinterventions.\u201d You might hear from your child\u2019s teacher about these types of support. You can also raise your concerns with your child\u2019s teacher and ask what types of tiered or targeted support is available.\n\n* **As a parent or guardian, you have the right to request an evaluation for your child.** If your child is having trouble in school and the support they\u2019re receiving in their classroom isn\u2019t sufficient, you can ask for an evaluation to figure out what might be going on. (You don\u2019t have to wait until the teacher or pediatrician suggests an evaluation, although in some cases they might.) This evaluation will be free of cost to you, or you can choose to pay for a private one if you prefer.\n\n* **All children with disabilities that present challenges to thriving in school have the right to support that meets their needs.** What those services look like\u2014and how you access them for your child\u2014will depend on your child\u2019s specific needs. Children with diagnoses that meet specific criteria might receive an Individual Education Plan (IEP) to support their needs. Other children might receive support through\u00a0a 504 plan, which provides accommodations to allow all children equitable access to\u00a0education. Although they are similar, an IEP and a 504 have some distinctions\u2014and which one your child is eligible for will depend on their particular needs. (Here\u2019s a chart from Understood that [breaks down the differences between IEPs and 504 plans](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/the-difference-between-ieps-and-504-plans?_sp=9b6118b8-f8d7-4c4f-985b-d1e2e50bed29.1657053419598).)\n\n* **If your child is struggling in school, there\u2019s a good chance you are also feeling overwhelmed by the process of getting them support.** We get it, really: Getting our kids what they need to thrive in school is one of our biggest goals as parents.\n\n## Here are some resources to help you figure out what\u2019s next:\n\n* **[What Parents Need to Know About IEPs](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/what-parents-need-to-know-about-ieps):** Here\u2019s a rundown of why a child might need an IEP to thrive in school, what goes into making one, and what your rights are as you seek support for your child.\n\n* **[5 Questions to Ask About Your Child\u2019s IEP](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/5-questions-to-ask-about-your-childs-iep):** As you start the process of getting your child support in school, knowing what to ask can be overwhelming. Here are 5 questions to start with.\n\n### For detailed information on everything related to students with learning differences, we love Understood.org.\n\nAs you get started seeking support for your child, here are a few essential resources from their archives:\n\n* [**What is response to intervention (RTI)?**](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/understanding-response-to-intervention)\n\n* [**What is MTSS (Multi-tiered System of Support)?**](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/mtss-what-you-need-to-know)\n\n* [**What is an IEP?**](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/what-is-an-iep?_sp=9b6118b8-f8d7-4c4f-985b-d1e2e50bed29.1655822438915)\n\n* [**What are your rights in the IEP process?**](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/what-are-your-rights-in-the-iep-process)\n\n* [**What you need to know about evaluations and how to request one**](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/understanding-evaluations?_sp=9b6118b8-f8d7-4c4f-985b-d1e2e50bed29.1655828873169)\n\n* [**A roadmap to your IEP journey**](https://assets.ctfassets.net/p0qf7j048i0q/4znp9r96cgmoHiEvYzVJMP/87a9fd6baf40c20b207e7e36be8824cd/IEP_Roadmap_Understood.pdf)\n\n[Recommended Resources](# \"Toggle Recommended Resources Dropdown \")\n\n[![The Understood.org logo](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/logos/understood.svg)\n\n### Understood\n\nOur first stop for anything related to learning differences. This is a great place to look for initial guidance if you have questions about your child\u2019s social-emotional development or their learning needs.](https://www.understood.org)\n\n![Sign Up for Email](/images/cta-email-signup.svg)\n\n## Don\u2019t miss a beat.\n\nSign up for our email list to get timely resources and early access to new content.\n\nFirst Name\u00a0\\*\n\nLast 5 Things Pediatricians Should Know about Special Education](https://www.ednavigator.org/blog/5-things-pediatricians-should-know-about-special-education)\n\n## Contact\n\n* [[email\u00a0protected]](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection#6e070008012e0b0a000f1807090f1a011c40011c09)\n* [888.MyEdNav](tel:+18886933628)\n* [1033 South Blvd, Oak Park, IL 60302, United States](https://maps.app.goo.gl/kFHNFoXefQXMFuHt8)\n\n[Keep up with EdNavigator](https://www.ednavigator.org/newsletter)\n\n[![The Busy Family\u2019s Guide to School](/images/guide-badge-english.svg)](https://www.ednavigator.org/guide)\n\nCommunicating with School / English Language Learners / Special Education\n/\nAges 2-18\n\n# What Parents Need to Know About IEPs\n\nSome amount of struggle in school is expected. As a parent, how do you know when it\u2019s time for your child to receive additional support?\n\n![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Mom_Kid_Down_Syndrome_Web.jpg)\n\nMany students struggle in school from time to time. They might have a hard time with some aspect of academic work; they might be struggling socially; they might be bored. Some amount of struggle is expected and can be productive for kids to work through, and not every challenge requires intervention. But as a parent, how do you know when it\u2019s time for your child to receive additional support? Or what if it\u2019s very clear that your child has needs that aren\u2019t being met in school, but you\u2019re not sure how to change that?\n\nThe Individuals with Disabilities Act (IDEA) is a federal law that protects the rights of students with disabilities to have access to a \u201cfree and appropriate public education.\u201d Under IDEA, students who are \u201cadversely affected\u201d by a certain subset of disabilities are eligible for Individualized Education Plans (IEPs), which define a program of instruction that allows them to fully access their education. An IEP is not about putting a \u201cproblem\u201d label on your child or stigmatizing them; it\u2019s about establishing a personalized set of supports and resources that meet their individual needs, so they can thrive in school. But getting an IEP, understanding what it means, and making sure it\u2019s actually helping your child can be an overwhelming process.\n\n## Here\u2019s what all parents need to know about IEPs, whether or not your child has one.\n\n* **An IEP articulates a specific program of instruction and supports to ensure that a student can thrive in school.** The plan will include language about your child\u2019s strengths and areas of challenge, their annual goals, and the supports and teacher actions that will help them achieve those goals. This might be things like small group academic support in class; visual aids, graphic organizers, or other tools and resources; additional time for specific tasks; support from a school counselor or psychologist; and much more.\n\n* **The process of getting an IEP begins with an evaluation of the student.** As a parent, you can request that your child be evaluated for special education services. Public schools must offer these evaluations for free, but you can also opt for a private evaluation if you prefer. Alternatively, your school might suggest that your child be evaluated, but they must obtain your permission before moving forward. [This chart](https://assets.ctfassets.net/p0qf7j048i0q/4znp9r96cgmoHiEvYzVJMP/87a9fd6baf40c20b207e7e36be8824cd/IEP_Roadmap_Understood.pdf) from Understood outlines the steps involved in obtaining an IEP.\n\n* **Eligibility for an IEP is defined by federal law.** Under IDEA, students are eligible for IEPs if they are \u201cadversely affected\u201d by a disability that falls into [13 particular categories](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/conditions-covered-under-idea). These categories include specific learning disabilities (like dyslexia, dyscalculia, or auditory processing disorder), deafness, visual impairment, traumatic brain injury, autism spectrum disorder, speech and language difficulties, and more. Students who struggle in school for reasons outside of these 13 categories are not eligible for an IEP, but they might still be eligible for a 504 plan, which covers any other exceptionality that interferes with a student\u2019s ability to learn in a general education classroom. (Many things could meet this criteria: for example, attention issues, \u201cgifted or talented\u201d status, or a severe food allergy. Check out this helpful guide to understand the [differences between IEPs and 504 plans](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/the-difference-between-ieps-and-504-plans).) And while the categories defined by IDEA represent a baseline for IEP eligibility, states do have some latitude to expand upon what is included or how it is categorized, and you will be walked through those criteria during an eligibility determination meeting.\n\n* **An IEP is written by a team\u2014including parents.** Your child\u2019s IEP team will include you as well as several others: most likely their classroom teacher, a special education teacher, an expert related to their disability (like a speech and language therapist or a reading interventionist), a representative from the school district, and a trained interpreter if needed. Together, you will discuss your child\u2019s needs and how to meet them, prior to a teacher or district special education staff member putting these plans in writing. As your child gets older, you may wish to include them in the IEP meetings as well. When they\u2019re over 16, they can be an official member of their IEP team.\n\n* **IEPs are legal documents, as well as roadmaps for your child\u2019s school experience.** Once written and signed, the IEP is a legally binding document. But it shouldn\u2019t just be a contract that gets tossed in a drawer. The IEP should include enough detail to articulate how your child\u2019s supports will change their daily life at school and improve their experience. If the IEP doesn\u2019t tell you those things, it\u2019s important to ask your child\u2019s teachers.\n\n## What can you do if you think your child needs an IEP?\n\n\u201cAs the parent, you are an equal member of your child\u2019s IEP team. The IEP should be developed in collaboration with you\u2014not presented to you.\u201d\n\n1\n\n### Know your rights.\n\n**As the parent, you are an equal member of your child\u2019s IEP team.** The IEP should be developed in collaboration with you\u2014not presented to you. If English is not your primary language, you have the right to have a trained interpreter present at all meetings. (Your child or an untrained member of school staff does not count, even if they are fully bilingual.) And you should not feel pressured to agree to an IEP that seems insufficient to you. If the IEP doesn\u2019t have clear goals and make good sense to you given your child\u2019s needs, keep pushing the team. You can also bring a support person to IEP meetings if you\u2019d like, such as your Navigator or another parent advocate, or even a friend who can listen and take notes. Once the IEP is written, your child\u2019s school is required to follow through on the supports they\u2019ve committed to. Finally, if you have concerns that your child isn\u2019t receiving those supports, or that the support they\u2019re receiving is not sufficient, you have the right to raise it with your child\u2019s IEP team or request a formal team reconvene. (Read more about [your rights in the IEP process](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/what-are-your-rights-in-the-iep-process).)\n\n2\n\n### Know your point person.\n\n**The process of navigating special education services can be overwhelming.** Try to identify a point person at your child\u2019s school: This might be a special education teacher or director, a school psychologist, or a therapist or interventionist who supports your child. Make sure you know who to turn to when you have questions, and how to reach them most efficiently.\n\n3\n\n### Be persistent.\n\n**Your child\u2019s school might not want to complete an evaluation, since it may require more work from them.** They may suggest that the existing supports\u2014without an evaluation and eventual IEP eligibility determination\u2014are sufficient, or they may keep telling you to \u201cwait and see.\u201d But too much waiting and seeing can cost your child valuable learning time. If you feel that their needs aren\u2019t being met, keep asking. It\u2019s your right and your child\u2019s right to have an evaluation completed and receive specialized support if they are eligible.\n\n4\n\n### Ask questions.\n\n**You probably have a lot of them, and you are entitled to answers provided in understandable terms.** It can be daunting to ask questions in a room full of education professionals, but don\u2019t feel afraid to speak up. Sometimes evaluation results may be provided in educational or psychological jargon\u2014ask the questions you need answered so you feel entirely comfortable with what\u2019s in your child\u2019s IEP. If you\u2019re feeling overwhelmed, [these five questions](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/5-questions-to-ask-about-your-childs-iep) are a good place to start.\n\n[Recommended Resources](# \"Toggle Recommended Resources Dropdown \")\n\n[![The Understood.org logo](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/logos/understood.svg)\n\n### Understood\n\nOur first stop for anything related to learning differences. This is a great place to look for initial guidance if you have questions about your child\u2019s social-emotional development or their learning needs.](https://www.understood.org)\n\n![Sign Up for Email](/images/cta-email-signup.svg)\n\n## Don\u2019t miss a beat.\n\nSign up for our email list to get timely resources and early access to new content.\n\nFirst Name\u00a0\\*\n\nLast Name\u00a0\\*\n\nEmail\u00a0\\*\n\nPreferred Language\u00a0\\*\n\nEnglishSpanish\n\nZIP Code\n\nTags\n\nFG Registered User\n\nSign Up for Email\n\n## Related Resources\n\n[![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Boy_Special_Needs_Painting_web.jpg)\n\n### What Parents Need to Know About Special Education\n\nCommunicating with School\n\n/\n\nSpecial Education\n\n/\n\nAges\n5\n-\n18](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/what-parents-need-to-know-about-special-education)\n[![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Teen_Struggling_Reader.jpg)\n\n### What If My Big Kid Struggles With Reading?\n\nReading\n\n/\n\nSpecial Education\n\n/\n\nAges\n11\n-\n18](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/what-if-my-big-kid-struggles-with-reading)\n[![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/OrangeLockers_Blog.jpg)\n\n### What Parents Need to Know When School Isn\u2019t Being Responsive\n\nCommunicating with School\n\n/\n\nAges\n2\n-\n18](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/what-parents-need-to-know-when-school-isnt-being-responsive)\n[![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Boy_Mask_Library.jpg)\n\n### What Parents Need to Know About Pandemic Learning Recovery\n\nMath - 5 Things Pediatricians Should Know about Special Education](https://www.ednavigator.org/blog/5-things-pediatricians-should-know-about-special-education)\n\n## Contact\n\n* [[email\u00a0protected]](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection#a2cbccc4cde2c7c6ccc3d4cbc5c3d6cdd08ccdd0c5)\n* [888.MyEdNav](tel:+18886933628)\n* [1033 South Blvd, Oak Park, IL 60302, United States](https://maps.app.goo.gl/kFHNFoXefQXMFuHt8)\n\n[Keep up with EdNavigator](https://www.ednavigator.org/newsletter)\n\n[![The Busy Family\u2019s Guide to School](/images/guide-badge-english.svg)](https://www.ednavigator.org/guide)\n\nSpecial Education\n/\nAges 5-18\n\n# How to File a Special Education Legal Complaint\n\nTo file a complaint with your state, here\u2019s what you\u2019ll need to know.\n\n![](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/resource-images/_850x566_crop_center-center_82_line_ns/Courthouse_Web_2025-07-30-183756_klex.jpg)\n\nIf you believe your child is not receiving the special education services they are legally entitled to, you can file a legal complaint with the state.\n\nBefore you file a legal complaint with your state\u2019s education agency, consider these questions:\n\n### Is your concern about something that happened within the last year?\n\nIf the answer is **no**, it is too late to file a legal complaint due to time limits on the process.\n\nIf the answer is **yes**, your concern may be eligible for a special education legal complaint.\n\n### Is your concern related to a violation of the Individuals with Disabilities in Education Act (IDEA)?\n\nIf the answer is **no**, your concern is not considered a legal special education violation.\n\nIf the answer is **yes**, your concern may be eligible for a special education legal complaint.\n\n**If you aren\u2019t sure**, it can be helpful to consult a lawyer or legal support services organization.\n\n* [Legal support in Massachusetts](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/how-to-get-legal-support-for-special-education-in-massachusetts)\n* [Legal support in California](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/how-to-get-legal-support-for-special-education-in-california)\n* [Legal support in D.C./Maryland](https://www.ednavigator.org/resources/how-to-get-legal-support-for-special-education-in-dc-and-maryland)\n\n### **What should you include in your complaint?**\n\nAccording to the law, your school district is required to provide you with a model complaint form and a copy of your rights in the process. This is called the Procedural Safeguards Notice. You can also use the sample complaint [here](https://www.understood.org/en/articles/download-sample-letters-for-dispute-resolution) at Understood.org as an example.\n\nAll complaints should include the following information in writing:\n\n* Your child\u2019s name, address, and school name\n* An explanation of the problem and how the school violated IDEA\n* Facts supporting your explanation\n* A proposed solution\n* Your signature and contact information\n\n**Include specific examples of how this problem affects your child in school**. For example, if your child has not received the support services listed in their IEP, explain how they\u2019ve struggled in school as a result.\n\n**You should also include any documentation related to your child\u2019s diagnosis.** This includes the IEP, medical records, and evaluation reports.\n\n**Also include evidence of your correspondence with school about this issue**. For example, include copies of emails you have sent to your child\u2019s teacher, principal, and other school or district staff.\n\n### **How do you file a complaint?**\n\n**Learn about your state\u2019s process**. Use the Advocacy Institute\u2019s [database](https://www.advocacyinstitute.org/iscrc/links.shtml) to find your state\u2019s specific forms and instructions for submitting your complaint.\n\n**Make a copy for your district**. In addition to filing the complaint with your state\u2019s education agency, send a copy to your school district.\n\n[Recommended Resources](# \"Toggle Recommended Resources Dropdown \")\n\n[![The Understood.org logo](https://www.ednavigator.org/assets/logos/understood.svg)\n\n### Understood\n\nOur first stop for anything related to learning differences.         "text": "     1\t---\n     2\tname: xlsx\n     3\tdescription: \"Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path \u2014 even casually (like \\\"the xlsx in my downloads\\\") \u2014 and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.\"\n     4\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     5\t---\n     6\t\n     7\t# Requirements for Outputs\n     8\t\n     9\t## All Excel files\n    10\t\n    11\t### Professional Font\n    12\t- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user\n    13\t\n    14\t### Zero Formula Errors\n    15\t- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)\n    16\t\n    17\t### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)\n    18\t- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files\n    19\t- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns\n    20\t- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines\n    21\t\n    22\t## Financial models\n    23\t\n    24\t### Color Coding Standards\n    25\tUnless otherwise stated by the user or existing template\n    26\t\n    27\t#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions\n    28\t- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios\n    29\t- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations\n    30\t- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook\n    31\t- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files\n    32\t- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated\n    33\t\n    34\t### Number Formatting Standards\n    35\t\n    36\t#### Required Format Rules\n    37\t- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., \"2024\" not \"2,024\")\n    38\t- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers (\"Revenue ($mm)\")\n    39\t- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros \"-\", including percentages (e.g., \"$#,##0;($#,##0);-\")\n    40\t- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)\n    41\t- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)\n    42\t- **Negative numbers**: Use parentheses (123) not minus -123\n    43\t\n    44\t### Formula Construction Rules\n    45\t\n    46\t#### Assumptions Placement\n    47\t- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells\n    48\t- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas\n    49\t- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05\n    50\t\n    51\t#### Formula Error Prevention\n    52\t- Verify all cell references are correct\n    53\t- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges\n    54\t- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods\n    55\t- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)\n    56\t- Verify no unintended circular references\n    57\t\n    58\t#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes\n    59\t- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: \"Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]\"\n    60\t- Examples:\n    61\t  - \"Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    62\t  - \"Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    63\t  - \"Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity\"\n    64\t  - \"Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen\"\n    65\t\n    66\t# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis\n    67\t\n    68\t## Overview\n    69\t\n    70\tA user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.\n    71\t\n    72\t## Important Requirements\n    73\t\n    74\t**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)\n    75\t\n    76\t## Reading and analyzing data\n    77\t\n    78\t### Data analysis with pandas\n    79\tFor data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:\n    80\t\n    81\t```python\n    82\timport pandas as pd\n    83\t\n    84\t# Read Excel\n    85\tdf = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet\n    86\tall_sheets = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name=None)  # All sheets as dict\n    87\t\n    88\t# Analyze\n    89\tdf.head()      # Preview data\n    90\tdf.info()      # Column info\n    91\tdf.describe()  # Statistics\n    92\t\n    93\t# Write Excel\n    94\tdf.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)\n    95\t```\n    96\t\n    97\t## Excel File Workflows\n    98\t\n    99\t## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values\n   100\t\n   101\t**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.\n   102\t\n   103\t### \u274c WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values\n   104\t```python\n   105\t# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result\n   106\ttotal = df['Sales'].sum()\n   107\tsheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000\n   108\t\n   109\t# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python\n   110\tgrowth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']\n   111\tsheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15\n   112\t\n   113\t# Bad: Python calculation for average\n   114\tavg = sum(values) / len(values)\n   115\tsheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5\n   116\t```\n   117\t\n   118\t### \u2705 CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas\n   119\t```python\n   120\t# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum\n   121\tsheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'\n   122\t\n   123\t# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula\n   124\tsheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'\n   125\t\n   126\t# Good: Average using Excel function\n   127\tsheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'\n   128\t```\n   129\t\n   130\tThis applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.\n   131\t\n   132\t## Common Workflow\n   133\t1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting\n   134\t2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file\n   135\t3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting\n   136\t4. **Save**: Write to file\n   137\t5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script\n   138\t   ```bash\n   139\t   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx\n   140\t   ```\n   141\t6. **Verify and fix any errors**: \n   142\t   - The script returns JSON with error details\n   143\t   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations\n   144\t   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again\n   145\t   - Common errors to fix:\n   146\t     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references\n   147\t     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero\n   148\t     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula\n   149\t     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name\n   150\t\n   151\t### Creating new Excel files\n   152\t\n   153\t```python\n   154\t# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting\n   155\tfrom openpyxl import Workbook\n   156\tfrom openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment\n   157\t\n   158\twb = Workbook()\n   159\tsheet = wb.active\n   160\t\n   161\t# Add data\n   162\tsheet['A1'] = 'Hello'\n   163\tsheet['B1'] = 'World'\n   164\tsheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])\n   165\t\n   166\t# Add formula\n   167\tsheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'\n   168\t\n   169\t# Formatting\n   170\tsheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')\n   171\tsheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')\n   172\tsheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')\n   173\t\n   174\t# Column width\n   175\tsheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20\n   176\t\n   177\twb.save('output.xlsx')\n   178\t```\n   179\t\n   180\t### Editing existing Excel files\n   181\t\n   182\t```python\n   183\t# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting\n   184\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   185\t\n   186\t# Load existing file\n   187\twb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')\n   188\tsheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet\n   189\t\n   190\t# Working with multiple sheets\n   191\tfor sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:\n   192\t    sheet = wb[sheet_name]\n   193\t    print(f\"Sheet: {sheet_name}\")\n   194\t\n   195\t# Modify cells\n   196\tsheet['A1'] = 'New Value'\n   197\tsheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2\n   198\tsheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3\n   199\t\n   200\t# Add new sheet\n   201\tnew_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')\n   202\tnew_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'\n   203\t\n   204\twb.save('modified.xlsx')\n   205\t```\n   206\t\n   207\t## Recalculating formulas\n   208\t\n   209\tExcel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:\n   210\t\n   211\t```bash\n   212\tpython scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tExample:\n   216\t```bash\n   217\tpython scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30\n   218\t```\n   219\t\n   220\tThe script:\n   221\t- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run\n   222\t- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets\n   223\t- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)\n   224\t- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts\n   225\t- Works on both Linux and macOS\n   226\t\n   227\t## Formula Verification Checklist\n   228\t\n   229\tQuick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:\n   230\t\n   231\t### Essential Verification\n   232\t- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model\n   233\t- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)\n   234\t- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)\n   235\t\n   236\t### Common Pitfalls\n   237\t- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`\n   238\t- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ \n   239\t- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first\n   240\t- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)\n   241\t- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)\n   242\t- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets\n   243\t\n   244\t### Formula Testing Strategy\n   245\t- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly\n   246\t- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist\n   247\t- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values\n   248\t\n   249\t### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output\n   250\tThe script returns JSON with error details:\n   251\t```json\n   252\t{\n   253\t  \"status\": \"success\",           // or \"errors_found\"\n   254\t  \"total_errors\": 0,              // Total error count\n   255\t  \"total_formulas\": 42,           // Number of formulas in file\n   256\t  \"error_summary\": {              // Only present if errors found\n   257\t    \"#REF!\": {\n   258\t      \"count\": 2,\n   259\t      \"locations\": [\"Sheet1!B5\", \"Sheet1!C10\"]\n   260\t    }\n   261\t  }\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t## Best Practices\n   266\t\n   267\t### Library Selection\n   268\t- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export\n   269\t- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features\n   270\t\n   271\t### Working with openpyxl\n   272\t- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)\n   273\t- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`\n   274\t- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost\n   275\t- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing\n   276\t- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values\n   277\t\n   278\t### Working with pandas\n   279\t- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`\n   280\t- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`\n   281\t- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`\n   282\t\n   283\t## Code Style Guidelines\n   284\t**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:\n   285\t- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments\n   286\t- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations\n   287\t- Avoid unnecessary print statements\n   288\t\n   289\t**For Excel files themselves**:\n   290\t- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions\n   291\t- Document data sources for hardcoded values\n   292\t- Include notes for key calculations and model sections",

 return cell\n\ndef altfill(row):\n    return PatternFill(\"solid\", fgColor=LIGHT_GRAY) if row % 2 == 0 else PatternFill(\"solid\", fgColor=WHITE)\n\ndef apply_fill(ws, row, ncols, fill):\n    for c in range(1, ncols+1):\n        ws.cell(row=row, column=c).fill = fill\n\n# ============================================================\n# SHEET 1: MASTER ENTITY MATRIX (Expanded)\n# ============================================================\nws1 = wb.active\nws1.title = \"Master Entity Matrix\"\n\nheaders1 = [\n    \"Category\", \"Entity / Agency / Process\", \"What They Do / What It Is\",\n    \"Qualifying Diagnoses / Eligibility\", \"Age Range\",\n    \"Income / Financial Requirements\", \"Key Paperwork / Documents\",\n    \"Application / Request Process\", \"Timeline to Decision\",\n    \"Follow-Up Type & Frequency\", \"Appeal / Dispute Process\",\n    \"Cross-Entity Interactions\", \"Equity Barriers & Supports\",\n    \"Source(s)\"\n]\nncols1 = len(headers1)\n\nws1.cell(row=1, column=1, value=\"WAYPOINT ENTITY NAVIGATION MATRIX v2.0 \u2014 Expanded with Undivided, DRC & EdNavigator Research\")\nws1.cell(row=1, column=1).font = Font(name=\"Arial\", size=14, bold=True, color=NAVY)\nws1.merge_cells(start_row=1, start_column=1, end_row=1, end_column=ncols1)\n\nws1.cell(row=2, column=1, value=\"California-Specific + Federal ncols1)\n\nentities = [\n    # --- ORIGINAL ENTITIES (expanded) ---\n    [\"State/CA\", \"Regional Center (RC)\", \"21 RCs across CA under DDS. Coordinates services for people with developmental disabilities under the Lanterman Act. Provides service coordination, funds therapies, respite, supported living, day programs, early intervention (Early Start).\",\n     \"5 qualifying conditions: Intellectual Disability, Cerebral Palsy, Epilepsy, Autism, conditions requiring similar services. Diagnosed before age 18.\",\n     \"Birth\u2013lifetime\", \"No income requirement for eligibility. Some services have family cost participation (FCP) based on income.\",\n     \"Intake application, medical/psych evaluations, IFSP (0-3) or IPP (3+)\",\n     \"Self-refer or physician referral \u2192 Intake \u2192 Assessment \u2192 IFSP/IPP meeting\",\n     \"120 days from referral to eligibility determination; 45 days for Early Start\",\n     \"Annual IPP review; quarterly service coordinator contact; ongoing\",\n     \"Fair Hearing through Office of Administrative Hearings (OAH). File within 30 days of notice of action. Mediation available. DRC is the protection & advocacy org.\",\n     \"Links to: IHSS, Medi-Cal, CCS, IEP/school district, DOR (transition), SSI, CalABLE, SNT\",\n     \"White children receive ~2x more RC spending than Latino children. 17 of 21 RCs showed disparities despite $66M state equity effort. Language access failures. Waypoint: auto-generates IPP goal suggestions, tracks service hours, flags missed services.\",\n     \"Undivided (RC resources), DRC (RULA manual)\"],\n\n    [\"State/CA\", \"IHSS (In-Home Supportive Services)\", \"County-administered program providing in-home care to help recipients remain safely at home. Services include domestic/related, personal care, paramedical, meal prep, accompaniment to medical appointments.\",\n     \"Medi-Cal eligible + functional limitations requiring assistance. No specific diagnosis required but must demonstrate need.\",\n     \"All ages\", \"Must be Medi-Cal eligible. Income limits apply for Medi-Cal qualification.\",\n     \"SOC 295 application, physician verification (SOC 873), functional assessment\",\n     \"Apply at county social services \u2192 Assessment by social worker \u2192 Authorization of hours\",\n     \"30-45 days from application to assessment\",\n     \"Annual reassessment; can request increase anytime if needs change\",\n     \"State hearing through CDSS. Request within 90 days of notice of action.\",\n     \"Links to: Medi-Cal (prerequisite), RC (may fund similar services), SSI\",\n     \"60%+ of CA counties lack sufficient IHSS caregivers. Rural deserts. Low wages drive shortage. Waypoint: tracks authorized vs. used hours, sends reassessment reminders.\",\n     \"Undivided (IHSS resources), DRC\"],\n\n    [\"Federal\", \"SSI (Supplemental Security Income)\", \"Federal cash benefit for low-income individuals who are aged, blind, or disabled. For children under 18: functional limitations in activities, learning, social functioning.\",\n     \"Disability that results in marked and severe functional limitations (child). Expected to last 12+ months or result in death.\",\n     \"All ages (child & adult criteria differ)\", \"Strict income/resource limits. $2,000 individual resource limit ($3,000 couple). In CA, SSI includes State Supplementary Payment.\",\n     \"SSA application, medical records, school records, functional reports\",\n     \"Apply at local SSA office or online \u2192 Medical review \u2192 Determination\",\n     \"3-6 months initial; can take longer. CDRs every 1-7 years.\",\n     \"CDR (Continuing Disability Review) every 1-7 years depending on severity\",\n     \"Reconsideration \u2192 ALJ hearing \u2192 Appeals Council \u2192 Federal court. 60 days to appeal at each level.\",\n     \"Links to: Medi-Cal (automatic in CA), IHSS, CalABLE, SNT, RC\",\n     \"$2K resource trap prevents asset building. Waypoint: calculates SSI impact of income changes, tracks CDR dates, connects to CalABLE.\",\n     \"DRC, EdNavigator\"],\n\n    [\"Federal\", \"IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act)\", \"Federal law guaranteeing FAPE (Free Appropriate Public Education) for all children with disabilities. Requires schools to identify, evaluate, and provide services through IEPs. Governs special education from birth (Part C: Early Intervention) through age 21 (Part B).\",\n     \"13 disability categories: Autism, Deaf-blindness, Deafness, Emotional disturbance, Hearing impairment, Intellectual disability, Multiple disabilities, Orthopedic impairment, Other health impairment, Specific learning disability, Speech/language impairment, TBI, Visual impairment.\",\n     \"Birth\u201321 (Part C: 0-3; Part B: 3-21)\", \"None \u2014 free regardless of income or immigration status.\",\n     \"Evaluation request, assessment plan, IEP document, procedural safeguards notice\",\n     \"Parent or school referral \u2192 Evaluation \u2192 Eligibility \u2192 IEP development \u2192 Placement\",\n     \"15 days to respond to assessment request; 60 days to complete assessment; 30 days to hold IEP after assessment (CA timelines)\",\n     \"Annual IEP review; triennial re-evaluation; progress reports per IEP schedule\",\n     \"IEP meeting request \u2192 Mediation \u2192 State complaint (CDE) \u2192 Due process hearing (OAH) \u2192 Federal court. Complaints must be filed within 1 year (CA) or 2 years (federal).\",\n     \"Links to: RC (Early Start\u2192IEP transition at 3), ERMHS, Section 504, OCR, school district, SELPA\",\n     \"Hispanic/Latino students 40% less likely to be identified with autism. Black children misdiagnosed with ID instead of autism. 10K+ teacher vacancies in rural/low-income areas. Waypoint: tracks evaluation timelines, flags overdue assessments, generates IEP prep checklists.\",\n     \"EdNavigator, DRC (SpEd Basics Toolkit), Undivided\"],\n\n    [\"Education\", \"IEP (Individualized Education Program)\", \"Legal document specifying a student's present levels, annual goals, services, accommodations, modifications, and placement. Developed by team including parents, GenEd teacher, SpEd teacher, district rep, and evaluator. IEP is both a document and a process.\",\n     \"Must qualify under one of 13 IDEA disability categories AND disability must adversely affect educational performance.\",\n     \"3\u201322 (or until diploma)\", \"None \u2014 FAPE guarantee.\",\n     \"Assessment reports, present levels, annual goals, service minutes, BIP if applicable, transition plan (ITP) at 16+\",\n     \"Evaluation \u2192 Eligibility determination meeting \u2192 IEP development meeting \u2192 Implementation\",\n     \"IEP meeting within 30 days of eligibility; annual review; triennial re-evaluation\",\n     \"Annual IEP review required. Progress reports on goal frequency (quarterly typical). Parent can request IEP meeting at any time.\",\n     \"Request new IEP meeting \u2192 File state complaint with CDE \u2192 Request due process \u2192 Mediation \u2192 OAH hearing. DRC publishes template letters for all disputes.\",\n     \"Links to: IDEA, Section 504, RC, ERMHS, school district, SELPA, NPS/residential placement\",\n     \"Parents not treated as equal team members. Jargon barriers. Schools may delay evaluations. Undivided offers IEP Assistant tool. Broader eligibility than IEP. Covers any disability \u2014 physical or mental \u2014 that limits learning.\",\n     \"Any physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities. Does NOT require one of 13 IDEA categories.\",\n     \"K\u201312+\", \"None.\",\n     \"Medical documentation, 504 plan document, accommodation list\",\n     \"Parent or school request \u2192 Evaluation \u2192 504 meeting \u2192 Plan development\",\n     \"No specific federal timeline; varies by district. Generally faster than IEP.\",\n     \"Annual review recommended; can request changes anytime\",\n     \"OCR complaint (federal) or school grievance process. No due process hearing right under Section 504 alone (can pair with ADA complaint).\",\n     \"Links to: IEP (may transition to/from), school district, OCR\",\n     \"Often offered instead of IEP to avoid service obligations. Fewer procedural protections than IEP. Includes psycho-educational, speech/language, OT, behavioral, and other assessments. District must assess in ALL areas of suspected disability.\",\n     \"Any student suspected of having a disability. Parent can request evaluation at any time \u2014 do not need to wait for teacher or school referral.\",\n     \"Birth\u201322\", \"Free when requested through school district.\",\n     \"Written request for assessment (DRC provides template letter), signed assessment plan, assessment reports\",\n     \"Parent submits written request \u2192 District has 15 days to respond with assessment plan \u2192 Parent signs \u2192 District has 60 days to complete assessments \u2192 IEP meeting within 30 days\",\n     \"15 days to respond; 60 days to complete; 30 days to IEP meeting (CA specific)\",\n     \"Triennial re-evaluation required. Parent can request additional assessments anytime if new concerns arise.\",\n     \"If parent disagrees with district assessment, can request IEE (Independent Educational Evaluation) at public expense. Parent has right to IEE at public expense if they disagree with district's evaluation. District must either fund the IEE or file due process to defend their own evaluation.\",\n     \"Parent disagrees with any district assessment. No need to explain why.\",\n     \"Birth\u201322\", \"Free at public expense (district pays). Parent can also pay privately at any time.\",\n     \"Written request for IEE at public expense, district's IEE criteria list\",\n     \"Parent submits written disagreement with district assessment \u2192 District must respond without unnecessary delay \u2192 Either funds IEE or files due process\",\n     \"District must respond 'without unnecessary delay' \u2014 no specific day count in federal law. CA: generally interpreted as 15-30 days.\",\n     \"One IEE per district assessment. New assessment = new IEE right.\",\n     \"If district refuses IEE, they must file due process to prove their assessment was appropriate.\",\n     \"Links to: IEP assessments, IEP, OAH, school district\",\n     \"Many parents don't know they have this right. Must follow Least Restrictive Environment (LRE) principle \u2014 start with GenEd inclusion and move toward more restrictive only when necessary. Continuum: GenEd \u2192 GenEd w/ supports \u2192 Resource room \u2192 SDC \u2192 NPS \u2192 Residential \u2192 Home/Hospital.\",\n     \"Students with IEPs. Tier 1: universal instruction. Tier 2: small-group targeted intervention. Tier 3: intensive individual intervention. NOT special education \u2014 but may lead to evaluation referral.\",\n     \"All students. Tier 2/3 interventions for students showing academic or behavioral struggles. No disability diagnosis required.\",\n     \"K\u201312\", \"None.\", \"Progress monitoring data, intervention plans\",\n     \"Teacher or parent identifies concern \u2192 Tier 1 supports \u2192 If insufficient, Tier 2 \u2192 If still insufficient, Tier 3 \u2192 May lead to SpEd referral\",\n     \"Varies by district. First-party (self-settled) or third-party. Payback to Medi-Cal required for first-party SNTs.\",\n     \"Person with disability receiving or likely to receive means-tested benefits (SSI, Medi-Cal, IHSS).\",\n     \"Any age\", \"No income requirement. \"DBEAFE\"\nLIGHT_PURPLE = \"EDE9FE\"\nLIGHT_ORANGE = \"FFEDD5\"\nLIGHT_GRAY = \"F1F5F9\"\nMED_GRAY = \"E2E8F0\"\nDARK = \"334155\"\n\nhf = Font(name=\"Arial\", size=10, bold=True, color=WHITE)\nhfill = PatternFill(\"solid\", fgColor=NAVY)\nbf = Font(name=\"Arial\", size=9)\nbfb = Font(name=\"Arial\", size=9, bold=True)\nwrap = Alignment(horizontal=\"left\", vertical=\"top\", wrap_text=True)\ncwrap = Alignment(horizontal=\"center\", vertical=\"top\", wrap_text=True)\ntb = Border(\n    left=Side(style=\"thin\", color=\"CCCCCC\"), right=Side(style=\"thin\", color=\"CCCCCC\"),\n
