        "text": "Giacomo Vivanti, Ph.D., dives into the complexities of establishing evidence-based autism interventions. He describes the ever-changing state of intervention literature, noting that autistic individuals still experience health inequalities. The speaker discusses four aspects of the interplay between research and practice and details studies demonstrating each assertion. Vivanti calls for universal outcome metrics based on well-being and considers how behavioral therapies can impact core autism symptoms (i.e., communication). The presenter asserts the usefulness of public health frameworks in assessing disparities between research and practice. He summarizes the presentation before the Q&amp;A.\n\nGiacomo Vivanti, Ph.D., dives into the complexities of establishing evidence-based autism interventions. He describes the ever-changing state of intervention literature, noting that autistic individuals still experience health inequalities. The speaker discusses four aspects of the interplay between research and practice and details studies demonstrating each assertion. Vivanti calls for universal outcome metrics based on well-being and considers how behavioral therapies can impact core autism symptoms (i.e., communication). The presenter asserts the usefulness of public health frameworks in assessing disparities between research and practice. He summarizes the presentation before the Q&A.\n\nVivanti explains that while the quality of early interventions has increased substantially, our knowledge is still rapidly evolving (4:05). Despite this, autistic individuals continue to experience health inequalities that lead to preventable adverse outcomes associated with mental and physical health, self-realization and self-determination, community participation, and educational opportunities (4:55). The presenter cites a study that found the chance of early onset dementia (Alzheimer\u2019s) in autistic individuals is double that of the non-autistic population. He posits that this is due to the continued lack of cognitive, social, and educational support for autistic adults (6:45). Vivanti specifies four complexities of establishing evidence-based interventions: The speaker recalls that his mother was blamed for his brother\u2019s autism diagnosis in the 1990s, even though \u201ccold parenting\u201d had been disproven in the 1960s (9:00).\n\nTherefore, he continues, the first main issue in establishing evidence-based interventions is the gap between research and practice (11:40). The speaker explains how intervention procedures in community settings are often different from the procedures used in clinical trials (11:40). Vivanti details a study that assessed the association between outcomes of the Early Start Denver Model (ESDM) and intervention fidelity (adherence) to clinical implementation. Researchers found a lot of implementation variation not only across practitioners but also across patients of each practitioner (13:30). Children with the best outcomes were treated by therapists implementing interventions at higher fidelity (15:35). The presenter considers feasibility and philosophical commitments as reasons for such variations in community implementation (16:23). He underscores that procedures should be flexible to the individuals but rigorous as well.\n\nVivanit explains that practitioners rely on information from different agencies and reviews that analyze the literature. However, each agency/review adheres to its own guidelines and criteria for what qualifies as evidenced-based (e.g., randomized trial vs. single subject design) (20:02). Different agencies therefore come to different conclusions about evidence-based interventions. Such confusion around facts makes clinicians and caregivers hesitant to adopt new interventions, despite documented effectiveness (23:05). The speaker outlines a survey study of 901 board-certified behavior analysts\u2019 knowledge of naturalistic developmental behavioral interventions (NDBI). Although significant evidence supports NDBI efficacy, few participants believed these practices were effective or appropriate (24:15). Review agencies generally classify interventions according to conceptual categories like ABA, NDBI, CBT, etc.",

      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Navigating Research | Organization for Autism Research",
        "url": "https://researchautism.org/families/navigating-research/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "researchautism.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=researchautism.org",
          "site_name": "Organization for Autism Research"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "The National Autism Center\u2019s National Standards Project (NSP) conducted a similar review of interventions for autistic children and adults. The NSP identified 14 \u201cEstablished Treatments,\u201d its rating for interventions with the strongest evidence. The NSP also developed a manual for school staff and parents to assist in the treatment decision process. The Internet is filled with testimonials about \u201ccures\u201d or \u201cmiracle treatments\u201d for autistic individuals. These strategies can assist families, autistic people, and practitioners in sorting truth from fiction: Review the source of the claim. Look first to see if the described results have been published in a peer-reviewed journal. This is the highest standard for quality and ensures that experts in the field have reviewed the methods and results to ensure rigor and accuracy. If the results are from an unpublished study or dissertation, website, pamphlet, or interview, however, there are no safeguards to ensure that the study was well-conduct\n\nThe autism field is filled with articles, websites, books, and testimonials about the potential benefits of various treatments or intervention strategies. From diets to dolphin therapy to discrete trial training, the sheer volume of options along with their purported evidence can be overwhelming. These resources and tips can help families and practitioners navigate those claims and make informed choices about intervention options. Several independent research groups have recently conducted rigorous and thorough reviews of the autism literature and have identified a number of evidence-based, effective practices for autistic children and young adults: The National Professional Development Center on Autism Spectrum Disorder (NPDC) recently released a report that identifies 27 evidence-based practices. Online modules provide step-by-step instructions on how to understand and implement each of the evidence-based practices.\n\nThe National Autism Center\u2019s National Standards Project (NSP) conducted a similar review of interventions for autistic children and adults. The NSP identified 14 \u201cEstablished Treatments,\u201d its rating for interventions with the strongest evidence. The NSP also developed a manual for school staff and parents to assist in the treatment decision process. The Internet is filled with testimonials about \u201ccures\u201d or \u201cmiracle treatments\u201d for autistic individuals. These strategies can assist families, autistic people, and practitioners in sorting truth from fiction: Review the source of the claim. Look first to see if the described results have been published in a peer-reviewed journal. This is the highest standard for quality and ensures that experts in the field have reviewed the methods and results to ensure rigor and accuracy. If the results are from an unpublished study or dissertation, website, pamphlet, or interview, however, there are no safeguards to ensure that the study was well-conduct\n\nResources and information accessed via the Internet can be very difficult to evaluate. Here are things to look for in a website that imply higher reliability and evidence-based information: Links to other known, credible sites (eg. autism organizations, research institutions, and professional research articles) Is hosted by government agencies or nonprofit organizations with demonstrated expertise ... Online databases retrieve information using a \u201ckeyword\u201d system, i.e. they identify all articles that contain the keyword. They are very easy to use. The disadvantage is that queries often produce more information than the average person needs or can possibly review. Some online databases are for members only and only accessible to the general public through university libraries. Here are two databases that are accessible to the general public: PubMed (Medical Publications) \u2014 maintained by the National Library of Medicine at the NIH.\n\nHaving a child diagnosed with autism can be a life-changing event for parents. They often turn to early intervention as the key to their child\u2019s development. Their immediate dilemma is determining which interventions to consider. Some are soundly based on research; others, sometimes making claims akin to \u201cmiracle cures,\u201d are not. One way or the other, all roads lead to research in some form. Learn how to identify high-quality research that addresses your immediate needs, and about important things to know when participating in research. ... Reading and interpreting research can be a difficult task, especially if you don\u2019t have a background in science. The nature of science and scientific examination is built on defining conditions and standards, using specific measures, applying consistent procedures, and observing objectively. In other words, it is predicated on order.",

        "text": "All of this literature was put through a standardized review process, and a list of established, emerging and unestablished treatments for individuals with autism was created. The Phase 1 report was released in 2009. In 2015 the National Standards Project released Phase 2, which included research that was conducted between 2007 and 2012, essentially providing an update on treatments from when Phase 1 was completed. Findings from the Phase 1 report were updated, and the \u201cemerging\u201d treatments were reviewed to determine if they now met criteria for \u201cestablished\u201d treatments, or should fall into the \u201cunestablished\u201d treatments. Additionally, interventions for individuals age 22 and older were also reviewed and categorized as established, emerging, or unestablished, which was never done previously. Interventions that are described as \u201cestablished\u201d have been shown through numerous research studies over many years to be an effective treatment for individuals with autism.\n\nThe National Professional Development Center on Autism Spectrum Disorder, also reviewed scientific literature and research, publishing a report based on data from 1997-2007 in their initial report. This was updated in their second review to include research from 1990-2011, with plans for a third report including data through 2017 to be released in the coming year. These reports only provide information on evidence-based treatment for individuals age 0-21. ... The National Standards Project, conducted by the National Autism Center at May Institute, is a comprehensive review of the scientific evidence for autism treatments. The goal of this project is to reduce uncertainty around autism treatment, and provide guidelines for how to make choices about interventions. The first phase of the National Standards Project looked at educational and behavioral intervention literature for the treatment of autism between 1957 and the fall of 2007.\n\nAll of this literature was put through a standardized review process, and a list of established, emerging and unestablished treatments for individuals with autism was created. The Phase 1 report was released in 2009. In 2015 the National Standards Project released Phase 2, which included research that was conducted between 2007 and 2012, essentially providing an update on treatments from when Phase 1 was completed. Findings from the Phase 1 report were updated, and the \u201cemerging\u201d treatments were reviewed to determine if they now met criteria for \u201cestablished\u201d treatments, or should fall into the \u201cunestablished\u201d treatments. Additionally, interventions for individuals age 22 and older were also reviewed and categorized as established, emerging, or unestablished, which was never done previously. Interventions that are described as \u201cestablished\u201d have been shown through numerous research studies over many years to be an effective treatment for individuals with autism.\n\nInterventions that are described as \u201cunestablished\u201d have little or no evidence in the scientific literature to determine if they are effective treatments for individuals with autism. Individuals should not assume that these interventions are effective in any way, and because there is little to no scientific evidence about these treatments there is no way to rule out the possibility that they are ineffective or even harmful. There are 14 Established Interventions that have been thoroughly researched and have sufficient evidence to confidently state they are effective: ... There are 18 treatments that have been reviewed and classified as Emerging Interventions, meaning there is need for further research before being considered established interventions: Augmentative and Alternative Communication Devices ... There are 13 treatments that have been reviewed and classified as Unestablished Interventions.\n\nThere is one treatment that has been reviewed and classified as an Emerging Intervention, meaning there is need for further research before being considered established interventions: Vocational Training Package \u00b7 There are 4 treatments that have been reviewed and classified as Unestablished Interventions. These treatments have little to no evidence to allow conclusions to be drawn about their effectiveness, and there is no way to rule out the possibility that these interventions are harmful or ineffective: ... The National Professional Development Center for Autism Spectrum Disorder, is a comprehensive review of literature and scientific research on treatments for autism. The initial report focused on research dating from 1997-2007, a ten-year time span. In this initial report, they found 24 focused intervention practices that met criteria for being evidence-based.",

The National Clearinghouse on Autism Evidence and Practice (NCAEP) recently released their new report identifying EBPs. The new report reviews literature published from 1990-2017 and identifies 28 EBPs and 10 manualized interventions that also met criteria. The list of EBPs is here. The NCAEP and AFIRM teams are working to update our user-friendly materials and AFIRM modules to reflect these changes. Until then, please select an EBP identified in our 2014 review to access the AFIRM module about the practice. Each module provides an overview and general description, step-by-step instructions of implementation, an implementation checklist, and the evidence-base which includes the list of references that demonstrate the practice meets\n\nAn intervention was considered to be an evidence-based practice if it met the specific criteria outlined below. randomized or quasi-experimental design studies. Two high quality experimental or quasi-experimental group design studies conducted by at least two different researchers or research groups, OR \u00b7 single-subject design studies. Five high quality single subject design studies conducted by three different investigators or research groups and having a total of at least 20 participants across studies, OR \u00b7 combination of evidence. One high quality randomized or quasi-experimental group design study and at least three high quality single subject design studies conducted by at least three different investigators or research groups (across the group and single subject design studies).         "text": "Many traditional systematic review processes, such as the Cochrane Collaborative (https://www.cochrane.org/) or Project AIM (Sandbank et al. 2020), have only included studies that employed a randomized experimental group design (i.e., randomized control trial or RCT) and have excluded single case experimental design (SCD) studies. By excluding SCD studies, such reviews omit a vital experimental research methodology recognized as a valid scientific approach (What Works Clearinghouse 2020). It is important to note that SCDs are often excluded in more traditional systematic reviews and meta-analyses, in part because such designs are thought to not be sufficiently scientific or rigorous. For example, on the previously noted Autism Research website (http://www.researchautism.net/autism-interventions/other-aspects-autism-interventions/process-for-evaluating-studies/our-ratings-system), the National Autism Society describes SCD studies as \u201cGrade C\u201d methodology (i.e., contrasted with Grade A a\n\nFocused intervention practices and comprehensive program models (i.e., previously identified as comprehensive treatment models and modified because of the potentially ableist language implied) are two broad classes of interventions that appear in the research literature (Smith 2013). Focused intervention practices are designed to address a single skill or goal of a student with autism (Odom et al. 2010a, b). These practices are operationally defined and address specific learner outcomes. Teachers, clinicians, or other practitioners select and use the practices (e.g., prompting, reinforcement, time delay) in interventions or instruction that addresses a learners individual learning goal. Focused intervention practices could be considered the building blocks of educational programs for children and youth with autism. In contrast, comprehensive program models consist of a set of practices designed to achieve a broad learning or developmental impact on the core deficits of autism (Odom et \n\nProtocols went through two iterations of pilot testing within the research group and then were reviewed by two national leaders in research methodology and intervention research, with expertise in SCD and group design, respectively for finalization. Only minor updates were made in the current review (e.g., include SMART design as a design option). To assist in quality appraisals, external reviewers were recruited through professional organizations (e.g., Association for Behavior Analysis International, Council for Exceptional Children), professional contacts, social media, the NCAEP website, and solicitation to reviewers from the previous review. The criteria for qualifying for the subsequent training was that the individual had to have a graduate degree or be a current Ph.D. student, had to have finished coursework in experimental group design and/or single case design research, and had to have had coursework related to and/or experience working with individuals with autism.\n\nMany traditional systematic review processes, such as the Cochrane Collaborative (https://www.cochrane.org/) or Project AIM (Sandbank et al. 2020), have only included studies that employed a randomized experimental group design (i.e., randomized control trial or RCT) and have excluded single case experimental design (SCD) studies. By excluding SCD studies, such reviews omit a vital experimental research methodology recognized as a valid scientific approach (What Works Clearinghouse 2020). It is important to note that SCDs are often excluded in more traditional systematic reviews and meta-analyses, in part because such designs are thought to not be sufficiently scientific or rigorous. For example, on the previously noted Autism Research website (http://www.researchautism.net/autism-interventions/other-aspects-autism-interventions/process-for-evaluating-studies/our-ratings-system), the National Autism Society describes SCD studies as \u201cGrade C\u201d methodology (i.e., contrasted with Grade A a\n\nMany systematic reviews of interventions for autistic children and youth now appear in the research literature. Such reviews are useful in their focus on individual practices like functional communication training (Gregori et al. 2020), intervention for autistic children/youth of a certain age (Sandbank et al. 2020), or interventions occurring in certain locations such as schools (Martinez et al. 2016), and allow for more in depth review of contextual factors impacting the intervention or outcomes. They do not, however, provide a comprehensive critical summary of evidence across practices, ages, and outcomes. To date, only the National Professional Development Center on ASD (NPDC) and National Standards Project (NSP) have conducted comprehensive reviews of focused intervention practices for children and youth with autism and included both group and SCD studies.",

        "title": "What We Need to Know About AAC Interventions | Organization for Autism Research",
        "url": "https://researchautism.org/oaracle-newsletter/what-we-need-to-know-about-aac-interventions/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "researchautism.org",

        "text": "Evidence-based practice is the major paradigm to implement an AAC intervention. AAC intervention is a comprehensive process that is not limited to one-on-one sessions with a single therapist.\n\nAlthough such approaches may be appealing to families due to exaggerated claims about amazing and unprecedented benefits, there is consensus that they are pseudoscientific, unsafe, and unethical. Leading organizations such as the American Speech-Language-Hearing Association, the American Association on Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities, and the International Society for Augmentative and Alternative Communication have published position papers cautioning practitioners about the use of such methods. AAC interventions for non-speaking autistic children and adults should be grounded in scientific evidence for their effectiveness. It is important to know that many AAC interventions such as the Picture Exchange Communication System or the use of speech-generating devices combined with proper intervention protocols have a solid research base and meet the standards for an evidence-based practice as outlined in reports by the National Autism Project and the National Clearinghouse on A\n\nOne of the most significant characteristics of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a \u201cdelay in, or total lack of, the development of spoken language.\u201d The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) report that approximately 40% of autistic children are non-speaking and do not develop sufficient natural speech or writing to meet their daily communication needs. Individuals who are non-speaking qualify for augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) interventions to supplement (\u201caugmentative\u201d) or replace (\u201calternative\u201d) natural speech. AAC interventions include unaided strategies, such as gestures, manual signing, and fingerspelling, and aided strategies, such as speech-generating devices, tablet devices (e.g., iPad) with AAC-specific applications, communication boards, and the Picture Exchange Communication System (PECS). The essential goal of AAC interventions is to enable communication and language through any combination of modalities (e.g., remaining natural speech, synthet\n\nBoth have shown strong potential in facilitating language growth during AAC interventions. Language modeling approaches include techniques such as aided language stimulation or aided language modeling. These are based on the notion that the learner can observe and mimic an interventionist using correct symbol utterances and combinations and reproduce these through an AAC modality. Matrix training is an innovative approach for teaching the combination of multiple symbols into full sentences; it also enhances generalization of newly learned language skills. Successful intervention results have been reported for both approaches, yet further replications are necessary to refine intervention protocols and elevate their empirical foundation to the level of an evidence-based practice. Another challenge is to create meaningful literacy experiences and interventions for non-speaking AAC users on the autism spectrum.\n\nIt is not true that there is a single best hardware device or software application for AAC intervention in autism. Individuals on the autism spectrum present with varying needs and learning profiles which necessitates individualized AAC solutions. Whenever possible, consider solutions that are backed by proper research evidence and have been validated by solid intervention research. Evidence-based practice is the major paradigm to implement an AAC intervention. AAC intervention is a comprehensive process that is not limited to one-on-one sessions with a single therapist. The process should involve family members and a variety of communication partners to create communicative interactions throughout the entire day. AAC has a facilitative effect on natural speech production and does not hinder the non-speaking individual from acquiring spoken language.",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://researchautism.org/oaracle-newsletter/what-we-need-to-know-about-aac-interventions/",

        "text": "     1\t---\n     2\tname: xlsx\n     3\tdescription: \"Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path \u2014 even casually (like \\\"the xlsx in my downloads\\\") \u2014 and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.\"\n     4\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     5\t---\n     6\t\n     7\t# Requirements for Outputs\n     8\t\n     9\t## All Excel files\n    10\t\n    11\t### Professional Font\n    12\t- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user\n    13\t\n    14\t### Zero Formula Errors\n    15\t- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)\n    16\t\n    17\t### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)\n    18\t- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files\n    19\t- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns\n    20\t- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines\n    21\t\n    22\t## Financial models\n    23\t\n    24\t### Color Coding Standards\n    25\tUnless otherwise stated by the user or existing template\n    26\t\n    27\t#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions\n    28\t- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios\n    29\t- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations\n    30\t- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook\n    31\t- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files\n    32\t- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated\n    33\t\n    34\t### Number Formatting Standards\n    35\t\n    36\t#### Required Format Rules\n    37\t- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., \"2024\" not \"2,024\")\n    38\t- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers (\"Revenue ($mm)\")\n    39\t- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros \"-\", including percentages (e.g., \"$#,##0;($#,##0);-\")\n    40\t- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)\n    41\t- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)\n    42\t- **Negative numbers**: Use parentheses (123) not minus -123\n    43\t\n    44\t### Formula Construction Rules\n    45\t\n    46\t#### Assumptions Placement\n    47\t- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells\n    48\t- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas\n    49\t- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05\n    50\t\n    51\t#### Formula Error Prevention\n    52\t- Verify all cell references are correct\n    53\t- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges\n    54\t- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods\n    55\t- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)\n    56\t- Verify no unintended circular references\n    57\t\n    58\t#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes\n    59\t- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: \"Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]\"\n    60\t- Examples:\n    61\t  - \"Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    62\t  - \"Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    63\t  - \"Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity\"\n    64\t  - \"Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen\"\n    65\t\n    66\t# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis\n    67\t\n    68\t## Overview\n    69\t\n    70\tA user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.\n    71\t\n    72\t## Important Requirements\n    73\t\n    74\t**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)\n    75\t\n    76\t## Reading and analyzing data\n    77\t\n    78\t### Data analysis with pandas\n    79\tFor data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:\n    80\t\n    81\t```python\n    82\timport pandas as pd\n    83\t\n    84\t# Read Excel\n    85\tdf = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet\n    86\tall_sheets = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name=None)  # All sheets as dict\n    87\t\n    88\t# Analyze\n    89\tdf.head()      # Preview data\n    90\tdf.info()      # Column info\n    91\tdf.describe()  # Statistics\n    92\t\n    93\t# Write Excel\n    94\tdf.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)\n    95\t```\n    96\t\n    97\t## Excel File Workflows\n    98\t\n    99\t## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values\n   100\t\n   101\t**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.\n   102\t\n   103\t### \u274c WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values\n   104\t```python\n   105\t# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result\n   106\ttotal = df['Sales'].sum()\n   107\tsheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000\n   108\t\n   109\t# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python\n   110\tgrowth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']\n   111\tsheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15\n   112\t\n   113\t# Bad: Python calculation for average\n   114\tavg = sum(values) / len(values)\n   115\tsheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5\n   116\t```\n   117\t\n   118\t### \u2705 CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas\n   119\t```python\n   120\t# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum\n   121\tsheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'\n   122\t\n   123\t# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula\n   124\tsheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'\n   125\t\n   126\t# Good: Average using Excel function\n   127\tsheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'\n   128\t```\n   129\t\n   130\tThis applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.\n   131\t\n   132\t## Common Workflow\n   133\t1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting\n   134\t2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file\n   135\t3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting\n   136\t4. **Save**: Write to file\n   137\t5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script\n   138\t   ```bash\n   139\t   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx\n   140\t   ```\n   141\t6. **Verify and fix any errors**: \n   142\t   - The script returns JSON with error details\n   143\t   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations\n   144\t   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again\n   145\t   - Common errors to fix:\n   146\t     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references\n   147\t     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero\n   148\t     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula\n   149\t     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name\n   150\t\n   151\t### Creating new Excel files\n   152\t\n   153\t```python\n   154\t# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting\n   155\tfrom openpyxl import Workbook\n   156\tfrom openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment\n   157\t\n   158\twb = Workbook()\n   159\tsheet = wb.active\n   160\t\n   161\t# Add data\n   162\tsheet['A1'] = 'Hello'\n   163\tsheet['B1'] = 'World'\n   164\tsheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])\n   165\t\n   166\t# Add formula\n   167\tsheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'\n   168\t\n   169\t# Formatting\n   170\tsheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')\n   171\tsheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')\n   172\tsheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')\n   173\t\n   174\t# Column width\n   175\tsheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20\n   176\t\n   177\twb.save('output.xlsx')\n   178\t```\n   179\t\n   180\t### Editing existing Excel files\n   181\t\n   182\t```python\n   183\t# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting\n   184\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   185\t\n   186\t# Load existing file\n   187\twb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')\n   188\tsheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet\n   189\t\n   190\t# Working with multiple sheets\n   191\tfor sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:\n   192\t    sheet = wb[sheet_name]\n   193\t    print(f\"Sheet: {sheet_name}\")\n   194\t\n   195\t# Modify cells\n   196\tsheet['A1'] = 'New Value'\n   197\tsheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2\n   198\tsheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3\n   199\t\n   200\t# Add new sheet\n   201\tnew_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')\n   202\tnew_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'\n   203\t\n   204\twb.save('modified.xlsx')\n   205\t```\n   206\t\n   207\t## Recalculating formulas\n   208\t\n   209\tExcel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:\n   210\t\n   211\t```bash\n   212\tpython scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tExample:\n   216\t```bash\n   217\tpython scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30\n   218\t```\n   219\t\n   220\tThe script:\n   221\t- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run\n   222\t- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets\n   223\t- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)\n   224\t- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts\n   225\t- Works on both Linux and macOS\n   226\t\n   227\t## Formula Verification Checklist\n   228\t\n   229\tQuick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:\n   230\t\n   231\t### Essential Verification\n   232\t- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model\n   233\t- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)\n   234\t- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)\n   235\t\n   236\t### Common Pitfalls\n   237\t- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`\n   238\t- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ \n   239\t- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first\n   240\t- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)\n   241\t- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)\n   242\t- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets\n   243\t\n   244\t### Formula Testing Strategy\n   245\t- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly\n   246\t- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist\n   247\t- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values\n   248\t\n   249\t### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output\n   250\tThe script returns JSON with error details:\n   251\t```json\n   252\t{\n   253\t  \"status\": \"success\",           // or \"errors_found\"\n   254\t  \"total_errors\": 0,              // Total error count\n   255\t  \"total_formulas\": 42,           // Number of formulas in file\n   256\t  \"error_summary\": {              // Only present if errors found\n   257\t    \"#REF!\": {\n   258\t      \"count\": 2,\n   259\t      \"locations\": [\"Sheet1!B5\", \"Sheet1!C10\"]\n   260\t    }\n   261\t  }\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t## Best Practices\n   266\t\n   267\t### Library Selection\n   268\t- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export\n   269\t- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features\n   270\t\n   271\t### Working with openpyxl\n   272\t- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)\n   273\t- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`\n   274\t- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost\n   275\t- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing\n   276\t- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values\n   277\t\n   278\t### Working with pandas\n   279\t- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`\n   280\t- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`\n   281\t- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`\n   282\t\n   283\t## Code Style Guidelines\n   284\t**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:\n   285\t- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments\n   286\t- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations\n   287\t- Avoid unnecessary print statements\n   288\t\n   289\t**For Excel files themselves**:\n   290\t- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions\n   291\t- Document data sources for hardcoded values\n   292\t- Include notes for key calculations and model sections",

Cross-disability, cross-age, inclusive of all conditions including behavioral challenges, prematurity, acquired disabilities.\\\",\\n     \\\"All families of individuals with disabilities and/or special health care needs \\u2014 birth through lifespan. Every state has at least one PTI \\u2014 Waypoint should auto-connect every user to theirs.\\\",\\n    \\\"CPIR AS CONTENT MODEL: CPIR's 1,100+ resource library organized by topic is the gold standard for family-facing disability information. Waypoint's content architecture should mirror this structure.\\\",\\n    \\\"PEER SUPPORT GAP: P2P USA provides emotional support Waypoint cannot replace with AI. Partnership or content licensing opportunity.\\\",\\n    \\\"EPSDT UNDERUTILIZATION: Family Voices data confirms EPSDT (Medi-Cal must cover ANY medically necessary service for children under 21) is the most underutilized entitlement. Waypoint must surface this.\\\",\\n    \\\"9.5M CHILDREN: COPAA documents 9.5M children with disabilities (7M+ IEPs + 1.1M+ 504 plans). Combined with 20% of children having special health care needs (Family Voices/NSCH), Waypoint's addressable market is enormous.\\\",\\n    \\\"EVIDENCE-BASED PRACTICES: OAR/NCAEP identify 28 evidence-based practices for autism. Center](https://childmind.org/resources/)\n\n\n\n[Autism](https://childmind.org/topics/autism/)\n\n# Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA) in Kids\n\nWhen children resist anything that feels like a demand\n\nWriter:\n[Caroline Miller](https://childmind.org/bio/caroline-miller/)\n\nClinical Expert:\n[Cynthia Martin, PsyD](https://childmind.org/bio/cynthia-martin-psyd/)\n\n[en Espa\u00f1ol](https://childmind.org/es/articulo/evitacion-patologica-de-la-demanda-en-ninos/)\n\n## Key Takeaways\n\n* Pathological demand avoidance (PDA) is extreme resistance to things perceived as a demand, most commonly seen in autistic children.\n* Kids with PDA often avoid routine tasks through excuses, distractions, or meltdowns, even when the tasks are easy for them to complete.\n* Kids with PDA respond better if you find what motivates them rather than confronting their resistance.\n\n* [What is pathological demand avoidance (PDA)?](#what-is-pathological-demand-avoidance-pda)\n* [What does PDA behavior look like?](#what-does-pda-behavior-look-like)\n* [What\u2019s behind pathological demand avoidance?](#what%e2%80%99s-behind-pathological-demand-avoidance)\n* [Executive functions and PDA](#executive-functions-and-pda)\n* [Can a child who\u2019s not autistic have PDA?](#can-a-child-who%e2%80%99s-not-autistic-have-pda)\n* [PDA autism](#pda-autism)\n* [How to work with kids with PDA behaviors](#how-to-work-with-kids-with-pda-behaviors)\n* [Focus on adaptive skills](#focus-on-adaptive-skills)\n* [Building flexibility](#building-flexibility)\n\n[Back to Top](#top_of_page)\n\nAll kids avoid doing things they\u2019re asked to do from time to time. But some go to extremes to avoid or resist anything they perceive as a demand. Avoidance can take many forms, including making excuses, creating a distraction, intense focus on something else, withdrawing, escaping, or having a meltdown or panic attack. That pattern of behavior is called pathological demand avoidance.\n\n## **What is pathological demand avoidance (PDA)?**\n\nPathological demand avoidance (PDA) is a pattern of behavior in which kids go to extremes to ignore or avoid anything they perceive as a demand. It is seen most often in people diagnosed with autism.\n\nBecause of that,\u00a0parents sometimes call it \u201cPDA autism.\u201d Identifying PDA can be a helpful way to understand some kinds of difficult behavior, but it is not a subtype of autism or a separate diagnosis.\n\nPeople with PDA tend to have an adverse reaction to being told how to behave or what to do, even when it\u2019s something that\u2019s an ordinary part of their daily life \u2014 and even when it would benefit them. It\u2019s called pathological when the avoidance is extreme and interferes with functioning at home or at school.\n\nIn kids who fit the PDA profile, resistance is sometimes mistaken for willful defiance, says [Cynthia Martin, PsyD](https://childmind.org/bio/cynthia-martin-psyd/), a clinical psychologist who specializes in autism. But it\u2019s better understood as a result of anxiety and inflexibility. As she explains, \u201cAny external demand that is coming from somebody else, or that the person *perceives* is coming from another person \u2014 that generates a lot of internal discomfort, which leads to the avoidance.\u201d\n\n## **What does PDA behavior look like?**\n\nTasks like putting on shoes, getting dressed, sitting down at a table, drawing, or saying the names of common items or pictures in a book may all be things a young child has the skills to do. But a child with PDA might only do these things when they\u2019re personally motivated to do them. Experts call this \u201cself-directed behavior.\u201d The parent can ask, show, and direct all they want, but their child will not do it when another person asks them to. This can be a frustrating experience for parents \u2014 especially when their child is being evaluated, because the child may appear unable to do things parents know they can do.\n\nSimilarly, older children and adolescents may appear to have difficulty completing basic school assignments, even though parents know them to be capable of advanced academics.\n\n[![Get Personalized Mental Health Care for Your Child](https://childmind.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Care-CTA-400x400.jpg)\n\n## Personalized Mental Health Care for Your Child\n\nLearn More](/care/)\n\n## **What\u2019s behind pathological demand avoidance?**\n\nMany children with autism spectrum disorder are inherently inflexible in their behavior and have rigid patterns of thinking, Dr. Martin adds. They are less tuned into social communication than other kids, so when a demand or expectation is placed on them, it can feel like it came out of nowhere.\n\nWhile neurotypical kids do many routine things because they\u2019re expected to or because that\u2019s what everyone else is doing, autistic kids might not feel the same motivation to fit in. And rigidity may make them uncomfortable going with the flow.\n\nFor example, she says, take the situation in a classroom. \u201cFor many kids, it\u2019s clear that the teacher is the one in charge. They understand that they need to do what the person in charge is telling them to do, and most everybody else is doing that as well.\u201d  \n  \nThat\u2019s social learning, she explains, where kids are just naturally tuning in to things other people are tuning in to and learning things without having to be directly taught. \u201cBut we know with autism there is a disconnect in that type of social learning. So, kids with autism will be less likely to go along to get along.\n\nIn school, this can lead to perplexing behaviors.\u00a0\u201cEven a child with a very, very high IQ, who is in a mainstream setting, as they should be, and has no trouble doing math, may be thinking, \u2018Everybody\u2019s doing this math worksheet. I don\u2019t want to do that, so I\u2019m going to draw sharks instead, because I like sharks.\u2019 The teacher\u2019s going to come by and say, \u2018You need to do your math,\u2019 and the child might respond by saying no or getting upset, which could escalate to the child flipping their desk over.\u201d\n\n## **Executive functions and PDA**\n\nPoor executive functioning can be another trigger for PDA, since it makes it hard for kids to grasp the schedule or structure in a social environment. That means they don\u2019t anticipate expectations that are not of their choosing.\n\n\u201cSo for the child who\u2019s drawing sharks rather than doing a math worksheet,\u201d Dr. Martin explains, \u201che might feel that the teacher asking him to do his math comes out of nowhere. To shift from what he wants to do to something that somebody else wants him to do, out of the blue, is very challenging.\u201d\n\n## **Can a child who\u2019s not autistic have PDA?**\n\nDr. Martin sees demand avoidance behaviors in many children with an autism diagnosis, at all levels \u2014 \u201ckids with an IQ of 140 as well as kids with an IQ of 50.\u201d But she thinks PDA traits also show up in kids who aren\u2019t on the spectrum. And she reports that families are increasingly asking whether their child might have PDA and *not* autism.\n\nDr. Martin sees PDA behaviors as analogous to sensory processing challenges. Although sensory challenges are very common in autistic kids and are now considered a symptom of autism spectrum disorder, a child can have sensory issues without autism. This includes kids with ADHD and kids with no diagnosed developmental disorder.\n\nLike sensory processing, she says,\u00a0\u201cPDA behaviors are traits that will be in our broader population, too,\u201d she adds. \u201cAnd just like with any kind of trait, you\u2019re going to have variability in the population, with some people having more of those traits than others.\u201d\n\nBut PDA is not a recognized disorder. \u201cWhile we are seeing some claims that PDA is a neurological condition,\u201d Dr. Martin says, \u201cwe don\u2019t have any\u00a0empirical\u00a0evidence of this. Rather than all kids with demand avoidance\u00a0behaviors sharing a single disability, we see a lot of variety in what is behind the PDA behavior in individual kids, and what we need to do to work with them.\u201d\n\n## PDA autism\n\nParents are also asking whether their children have what they call \u201cPDA autism,\u201d she reports. While there isn\u2019t a PDA subtype of autism, she notes, the value in identifying PDA behavior as a \u201cprofile\u201d is to respond to the fact that these kids may need a different type of support than other children on the spectrum.\n\nSome of them may have co-occurring disorders, such as ADHD, anxiety, or a mood disorder, which complicate their behavior. In other cases, since autism itself is highly heterogeneous, there may be a lot of complexity in the way their autism presents. Providers and teachers may be approaching them in a way that is misaligned with the child\u2019s needs, leading to conflict and lack of progress.\n\n\u201cTo approach these kids effectively we need to better understand why they are avoiding something, identify if there are co-occurring disorders that have not been diagnosed, and then respond to their individual needs,\u201d Dr. Martin explains.\n\n## **How to work with kids with PDA behaviors**\n\nSome strategies for working with kids on the autism spectrum don\u2019t work effectively with children who have PDA behaviors. These children don\u2019t respond as positively to direct communication about what is expected of them, which can trigger anxiety and avoidance. Instead, a collaborative approach with negotiation can make them feel more in control and less anxious.\n\nThe key is to find out what the child is motivated by, says Dr. Martin. Then you can spark that motivation and build on it.  \n  \nIt\u2019s easy to assume that a small, routine task shouldn\u2019t be that big of a deal. \u201cBut from a neuro-biological standpoint, that may be asking the child to climb a mountain,\u201d says Dr. Martin. \u201cWe want to get them to climb the mountain, but we need to create steps in the scaffold for them to get up there.\u201d  \n  \nThe goal is to avoid butting heads with the child, which tends to escalate the resistance without building the skills the child needs. \u201cWe have to find what the child is motivated by and be creative and flexible in our implementation,\u201d she explains. \u201cSuppose the child is interested in\u00a0sharks or cataloging music playlists. In that case, we\u2019re going to build sharks or cataloging into the intervention, to increase the child\u2019s own motivation to complete the thing that we want them to do.\u201d\n\n## **Focus on adaptive skills**\n\nSome autism advocates feel that calling a child\u2019s avoidance of demands on them \u201cpathological\u201d could violate their autonomy. But, Dr. Martin notes, a big focus in working with kids with PDA behaviors is on building what are called \u201cadaptive skills\u201d \u2014 skills that will improve their quality of life and ability to live independently.\n\n\u201cWe\u2019ve known for decades that individuals with autism have a big discrepancy between their IQ and adaptive skills,\u201d explains Dr. Martin. \u201cThese kids aren\u2019t performing ordinary daily tasks, even though they have shown that they know how to do them. This leads to being dependent on parents and other caretakers despite their capacity to do things themselves.\u201d Often parents perform tasks for kids on the spectrum long after other kids are doing things independently, she adds, thinking they\u2019ll grow out of their dependence. But they may not, if they have been successful avoiding demands for a long time.\n\nEstablishing clear consequences when kids don\u2019t comply with demands is important. But, she notes that changing what leads up to the avoidance \u2014 harnessing motivation for the child to comply and encouraging flexibility \u2014 is more effective as a tool to increase compliance in kids who fit the PDA profile. \u201cWe also may need to taper our expectations for what \u2018compliance\u2019 will look like because a neurodiverse child with autism may do things differently, so we cannot confuse those differences as being non-compliant.\u201d\n\n## **Building flexibility**\n\nPrograms like [*Unstuck and on Target*](https://www.unstuckontarget.com/) work on building flexibility in behavior and decreasing some of the intense emotional responses kids have to demands placed on them. \u201cTo build flexibility, we have to teach the child that there are benefits to being flexible \u2014 they will get what they want more often and have more time for the things they want,\u201d Dr. Martin explains.\n\nThis can involve learning ways to come up with a plan B when plan A does not work, how to compromise with others, and how to identify \u201cchoice\u201d vs. \u201cno choice\u201d types of situations. When the child can have a lot of options within \u201cchoice\u201d situations, they will likely be more likely to compromise and work with an adult to develop alternative plans in \u201cno choice\u201d situations.  \n  \nDr. Martin reports that kids with pathological demand avoidance make more progress and maintain it over time when they see that their ideas are considered. \u201cWhen we are only taking a heavy behavioral approach, where it\u2019s very reward-and-consequence driven, we often see treatment effects that are high initially, but then taper off over time.\u201d\n\n## Frequently Asked Questions\n\nWhat is pathological demand avoidance, or PDA?\n\nPathological demand avoidance (PDA) is a pattern of behavior in which kids go to extremes to ignore or avoid anything they perceive as a demand. It is most often seen in people with autism.\n\nWhat is the meaning of PDA?\n\nPDA or pathological demand avoidance is a pattern of behavior characterized by extreme resistance to ordinary demands.\n\nWhat is PDA autism?\n\nPDA is often seen in children diagnosed with with autism. Because of that, parents sometimes call it \u201cPDA autism.\u201d Identifying PDA can be a helpful way to understand some kinds of difficult behavior, but it is not a subtype of autism or a separate diagnosis.\n\nLast reviewed or updated on January 14, 2026.\n\nLearn more about our [Family Resource Center](https://childmind.org/resources/) and our [editorial mission](https://childmind.org/about-us/editorial-mission/).\n\n### References\n\nThe Child Mind Institute publishes articles based on extensive research and interviews with experts, including child and adolescent psychiatrists, clinical psychologists, clinical neuropsychologists, pediatricians, and learning specialists. Other sources include peer-reviewed studies, government agencies, medical associations, and the latest Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM-5). [Staff & Consultants](https://pdanorthamerica.org/team/)\n    - [Board Of Directors](https://pdanorthamerica.org/board-of-directors/)\n  + [Contact](https://pdanorthamerica.org/contact/)\n* [Donate](https://www.zeffy.com/en-US/donation-form/5ef6a4ac-e921-43aa-b7b5-d7f4307f550e)\n\n# PDA North America\n\n## Support & Resources for Pathological Demand Avoidance / Pervasive Drive for Autonomy (PDA) Created for Families, Professionals and PDA Individuals\n\n## Understanding & Supporting PDA\n\n### PDA is an acronym that stands for **Pathological Demand Avoidance**, or as many prefer, **Pervasive Drive for Autonomy**. PDA was identified in the UK in the 1980s by Elizabeth Newson and has recently received more attention in the US and Canada since PDA North America was established in March 2020. **Many (including us) view it as a profile of autism, with some recent studies linking it to ADHD as well as other neurodevelopmental conditions.** We are hopeful for more high quality, peer-reviewed, unbiased research to better understand PDA.\n\n### **PDA individuals** **avoid everyday demands using \u2018social strategies\u2019 as part of the avoidance, have a strong need for autonomy and control, require unique supports and are socially oriented. These highly stress-prone individuals are often not recognized as autistic, are deeply misunderstood and in need of support.**\n\n### **At PDA North America,** **we view PDA as a nervous system difference.**\n\n### Many families, educators, and therapists are unfamiliar with PDA, and it is often misunderstood or misdiagnosed. **Since PDA is not recognized as a distinct diagnosis in the DSM-5 or the ICD-11, many professionals lack awareness or a clear understanding of it.** Our organization aims to provide deep insights into PDA and offer practical strategies for supporting and accommodating individuals who experience it.\n\n### At PDA North America, **we are a non-profit, 501(c)3**, that offers a number of resources, trainings and supports to the PDA community & professionals that support them, while focusing on spreading awareness.\n\n[What is PDA?](https://pdanorthamerica.org/what-is-pda)\n\n[Download our Free 10-Page PDA Guide](https://learn.pdanorthamerica.org/products/digital_downloads/ten-page-PDA-guide)\n\n[Check out the PDA Learning Lab](https://learn.pdanorthamerica.org/)\n\n### We are still in the infancy of this mission as there is so much work to be done. We hope that you will find information and belonging. We are a small but mighty, non-profit community and we have only just started.\n\n#### **Welcome to the PDA movement. It's an exciting place to be.**\n\n[**Donate to PDANA**](https://donate.stripe.com/4gwdRLgxF9cRfaofYY)\n\n# RESOURCES\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Learning-LAb-NEW-1.png)](https://learn.pdanorthamerica.org/)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Conference-NEW.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/conference/)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Community-List.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/community-list/)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/PDA-Affirming.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/pda-affirming-providers/)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Monthly-Events.png)](https://www.zeffy.com/en-US/organizations/pda-north-america)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Support-Groups.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/support-groups/)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/3.png)](https://www.youtube.com/@PDANorthAmerica)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/PDA-Insights.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/insights/)\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/support-shop.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/support-shop/)\n\n# OUR MISSION & VALUES\n\n**Our mission is to provide advocacy, education, and resources to autistic PDA individuals, others who identify as PDA and their families along with the professionals who support them.**\n\n**Our Vision is bold.**\u00a0 We want to spread awareness of PDA to parents, individuals, educators and therapists throughout the US and Canada. We want to create resources for families to have a place to turn for support and guidance, we want professionals to be able to receive training and information in order to effectively work with. PDAers, and we want PDAers to know that they are understood and supported. In short, to build a strong and vibrant community for this underserved population.\n\n**Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion**\n\nThe PDA North America community and resources are open to all who identify as PDA, as well as those who wish to learn about PDA and support PDAers, regardless of age, background, abilities, ethnicity, gender, sexual orientation, nationality, socio-economic situation, religion. We value the richness diverse experiences bring to our community and wish to create a welcoming space for all our employees, contractors, and those who seek out our services.\n\nWe recognize that we are still learning and growing in this area, and are committed to education for our staff and board to examine our own practices and culture with the goals of working to eliminate bias and have our work, resources, and program reflect diverse experiences within the PDA community.\n\nOur work centers the lived experiences of PDAers. Appreciating that access to evaluation, formal diagnosis, and support services is not universally accessible to all who need them due to systemic barriers disproportionately impacting people belonging to marginalized communities, we welcome all who identify as PDA in our spaces, including those who have self-identified or may be exploring their relationship to PDA outside of a formal diagnosis.\n\n**With your support and donations,** we will continue to grow and offer more support services and programming for the PDA community and professionals that support them. Since our inception, no one has ever been turned away from any of our virtual offerings due to inability to pay. If you\u2019d like to get involved in the PDA Movement in North America, don\u2019t hesitate to reach out!\n\n*PDA North America is a non-profit organization with 501(c)(3) status. All donations are tax-deductible.*\n\n[Donate to PDANA](https://www.zeffy.com/en-US/donation-form/5ef6a4ac-e921-43aa-b7b5-d7f4307f550e)\n\n![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/logo_light@4x.png)\n\n400 Lake Cook\n\nSuite 112\n\nDeerfield IL 60015\n\n[![](https://pdanorthamerica.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/newsletter-1024x512.png)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/newsletter/)\n\n* [2026 PDA Conference](https://pdanorthamerica.org/conference/)\n* [What is PDA?](https://pdanorthamerica.org/what-is-pda/)\n* [PDA Research](https://pdanorthamerica.org/research/)\n* [PDANA Trainings](#)\n  + [New to PDA Training](https://learn.pdanorthamerica.org/products/courses/Just-learned-about-pda-now-what)\n  + [PDA Training for Therapists](https://pdanorthamerica.org/pda-training-for-therapists/)\n  + [PDA & Education Training](https://pdanorthamerica.org/pda-education-training/)\n  + [Level 1 Trainings (Social work CEs Avail)](https://pdanorthamerica.org/pda-trainings/)\n  + [Special Guest Trainings](#)\n    -         "text": "Share what works and doesn\u2019t work for your child. Explain what success looks like for your child (e.g., feeling regulated, safe, and happy at school). Explain that traditional structured approaches may lead to emotional outbursts or shutdowns. Note how sensory sensitivities (e.g., noise, transitions, crowded spaces) affect regulation. Advocate for flexibility &amp; accommodations. List effective supports that do work. If your child has had a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA), add the FBA information and other relevant information. Even if educators are not aware of PDA or are skeptical of it, you can still talk to them about your child\u2019s specific needs and interventions and supports that work best for them and request that these be part of the IEP. Again, the label PDA is not as important as finding what works for your child and getting on the same page about that with your IEP team.\n\nParent and caregiver training: educating parents and caregivers about PDA and effective strategies can enhance their ability to support the individual. Training can focus on understanding PDA, implementing intervention strategies, and managing challenging behaviors. PDA is not a recognized qualifying category for an IEP, but if your child is eligible under another category, you can talk to your IEP team about accommodations and supports. Knowing that your child has PDA can provide important insights for their IEP. Parents can request that PDA or demand avoidance be included in the IEP along with specific information and strategies that will work for the child. Non-Attorney Education Advocate Lisa Carey suggests that you have information about what works for your child around placing demands in the IEP under accommodations. You can get the description of what PDA is and add it to the IEP.\n\nShare what works and doesn\u2019t work for your child. Explain what success looks like for your child (e.g., feeling regulated, safe, and happy at school). Explain that traditional structured approaches may lead to emotional outbursts or shutdowns. Note how sensory sensitivities (e.g., noise, transitions, crowded spaces) affect regulation. Advocate for flexibility & accommodations. List effective supports that do work. If your child has had a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA), add the FBA information and other relevant information. Even if educators are not aware of PDA or are skeptical of it, you can still talk to them about your child\u2019s specific needs and interventions and supports that work best for them and request that these be part of the IEP. Again, the label PDA is not as important as finding what works for your child and getting on the same page about that with your IEP team.\n\nWhen it comes to IEP goals for PDA, check out this handy resource from PDA North America with some sample IEP goals. When crafting goals that are related to PDA, they tell parents to make sure your child\u2019s IEP goals are neurodiversity-affirming by including supports that align with their needs. Executive functioning, sensory, and motor supports can help create goals that truly support their learning. It\u2019s also important for school staff to respect all forms of communication \u2014 whether that\u2019s AAC, speech, vocalizations, sign language, or gestures. And for PDA learners, using declarative language in their goals can make a big difference. If you believe your child requires a 1:1 aide at school \u2014 such as a behavior aide \u2014 you have the right to request one during your IEP meeting. If it is determined that a 1:1 aide is required for your child to access FAPE, the district is required to figure out how to provide that service to you at no cost.\n\nDebates aside, descriptions of PDA resonate strongly with many parents and clinicians, so PDA has attracted a lot of attention in recent years. As a result, more children are being identified as having PDA, more parents are requesting that their children be assessed for PDA, and more clinicians and advocates are calling for further research and clinical recognition. In the US, most clinicians are not familiar with PDA (it is more well-known and researched in the UK), but a PDA advocacy community has been growing. Parents, adults who identify as having PDA, and other advocates have led the way in creating robust PDA communities, and organizations such as PDA North America provide extensive resources and support for parents of kids with PDA, who are sometimes called \u201cPDAers.\u201d \u00b7 Some parents have a \u201clightbulb moment\u201d when they first hear about PDA.",

        "title": "Navigating Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA): A Guide for Parents to Support Their Children's Return to School - School Avoidance Alliance",
        "url": "https://schoolavoidance.org/navigating-pathological-demand-avoidance-pda-a-guide-for-parents-to-support-their-childrens-return-to-school/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "schoolavoidance.org",

        "text": "Visual schedules can help children with PDA understand and anticipate the daily routine at school. Work with your child to create a visual schedule that outlines the activities and expectations for each day, including schoolwork, breaks, and preferred activities. Display the schedule in a prominent place at home and review it regularly with your child to reinforce familiarity and predictability. ... Establish open and collaborative communication with your child\u2019s teachers, support staff, and school administrators. Share information about your child\u2019s diagnosis, strengths, challenges, and individualized support needs. Collaborate on developing strategies and accommodations to support your child\u2019s success in the classroom. ... Sensory sensitivities are common in children with PDA and can significantly impact their ability to participate in school activities.\n\nWork collaboratively with school staff to develop an Individualized Education Program (IEP) or a 504 plan outlining accommodations, modifications, and goals to support your child\u2019s academic and social-emotional development. Supporting a child with PDA in returning to school necessitates a multifaceted approach that encompasses various aspects of their development and well-being. Firstly, parents and caregivers must cultivate patience and understanding as they navigate the challenges associated with PDA. This involves recognizing and validating their child\u2019s unique needs, preferences, and struggles, while also providing unwavering support and encouragement. Additionally, fostering proactive collaboration between parents, caregivers, and school personnel is essential. This collaboration ensures that everyone involved in the child\u2019s education is informed, engaged, and working towards the common goal of supporting the child\u2019s success. By implementing strategies to prepare for the transitio\n\nVisual schedules can help children with PDA understand and anticipate the daily routine at school. Work with your child to create a visual schedule that outlines the activities and expectations for each day, including schoolwork, breaks, and preferred activities. Display the schedule in a prominent place at home and review it regularly with your child to reinforce familiarity and predictability. ... Establish open and collaborative communication with your child\u2019s teachers, support staff, and school administrators. Share information about your child\u2019s diagnosis, strengths, challenges, and individualized support needs. Collaborate on developing strategies and accommodations to support your child\u2019s success in the classroom. ... Sensory sensitivities are common in children with PDA and can significantly impact their ability to participate in school activities.\n\nCollaborating with school staff to develop individualized support plans, including accommodations and modifications, ensures that the child\u2019s needs are met in the classroom environment. Implementing positive behavior support strategies, such as using reinforcement techniques and offering choices and flexibility, helps promote desired behaviors and reduce resistance to demands. Furthermore, fostering emotional regulation and resilience through teaching coping skills and promoting self-expression empowers children with PDA to navigate the inevitable challenges of the school environment with confidence and adaptability. Finally, accessing professional support when needed, such as consulting with mental health professionals or attending support groups, provides additional resources and guidance for parents and caregivers. With advocacy, support, and a nurturing home-school partnership, children with PDA can thrive academically, socially, and emotionally, unlocking their full potential and \n\nAs a parent, supporting your child\u2019s return to school can be challenging, especially if your child experiences Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA). PDA is a complex profile within the autism spectrum characterized by an extreme avoidance of everyday demands and expectations. Children with PDA may struggle with transitioning back to school due to the overwhelming demands and sensory stimuli present in the educational environment. In this guide, we\u2019ll explore strategies and tips to help parents support their children with PDA in returning to school successfully. ... Before diving into strategies, it\u2019s essential to understand PDA and its impact on children. PDA is characterized by an intense need to be in control and a pervasive avoidance of demands, leading to difficulties with authority figures, unpredictable behaviors, and extreme anxiety. Children with PDA often struggle with social interactions, emotional regulation, and sensory sensitivities, making the school environment particular",

But masking takes a toll. These kids may melt down at home or after school because they\u2019ve been holding it together all day. Others might be so overwhelmed by demand avoidance that they don\u2019t mask much at all. This leads to a phenomenon called after school restraint collapse, aka after school meltdowns. The overlap can make it really hard for parents and educators to spot what\u2019s going on. A kid might look \u201cfine\u201d at school and fall apart at home. Or, their masking might be so strong that their true level of stress and anxiety gets totally missed. The short answer is: PDA and masking aren\u2019t directly assessed by standard school-based IEP evaluations, but that doesn\u2019t mean they can\u2019t be addressed. ... This may include therapy with a PDA-informed psychologist, mindfulness practices, nervous system supports like neurofeedback, and accommodations in work or home life to reduce demand-based stress. If you\u2019ve been blamed, judged, or told to \u201cjust parent better,\u201d let me say this: You\u2019re not doing anything wrong. PDA isn\u2019t about discipline or behavior charts\u2014it\u2019s a nervous system issue. That means the path to healing starts with regulation, not correction. Your child isn\u2019t giving you a hard time\u2026 they\u2019re having a hard time. Marcus, age 7, melted down over bedtime routines. Even the mention of brushing his teeth or turning off the TV would spark a screaming match or a complete shutdown.         "text": "Strategies for supporting PDA children include low-demand, collaborative parenting; reducing or rephrasing demands; and offering autonomy. Take this self-test to see if your child may be showing signs of PDA and share the results with a licensed ...\n\nPathological demand avoidance (PDA) symptoms in children include resistance to everyday demands and a strong desire for autonomy.\n\nPDA is thought to arise from intense anxiety, intolerance of uncertainty, and a strong need for autonomy; PDA individuals, stress-prone and with sensitive nervous systems, view demands as inherently threatening to their sense of safety and control. Strategies for supporting PDA children include low-demand, collaborative parenting; reducing or rephrasing demands; and offering autonomy. Take this self-test to see if your child may be showing signs of PDA and share the results with a licensed medical professional. Find a PDA-affirming provider through PDA North America, a non-profit organization. ... Can\u2019t see the self-test questions above? Click here to open this test in a new window. The questions in this self-test were informed by resources from PDA North America and PDA Society (UK), ADDitude articles on PDA (see next steps below), and published research on PDA.\n\nPathological demand avoidance (PDA), also known as pervasive or persistent drive for autonomy, is a behavioral profile characterized by intense and extreme resistance to, and avoidance of, everyday demands. In the context of PDA, what counts as a demand extends beyond direct instructions like \u201cclean your room.\u201d The common, trivial requirements and expectations of daily living \u2014 from getting dressed for school and responding to a greeting, to following the body\u2019s internal cues to eat and sleep \u2014 are often perceived as demands. Casual suggestions can also be interpreted as demands. For children with PDA, even engaging in activities they enjoy can become difficult if it feels like it was someone else\u2019s idea or even a self-imposed demand. Children with PDA might go to extreme or unusual lengths to avoid complying with a demand. When pressured to comply, they\u2019ll often melt down or react explosively.\n\n3 Nawaz, S., Speer, S.
