        "text": "Because of that, parents sometimes call it \u201cPDA autism.\u201d Identifying PDA can be a helpful way to understand some kinds of difficult behavior, but it is not a subtype of autism or a separate diagnosis. People with PDA tend to have an adverse reaction to being told how to behave or what to do, even when it\u2019s something that\u2019s an ordinary part of their daily life \u2014 and even when it would benefit them. It\u2019s called pathological when the avoidance is extreme and interferes with functioning at home or at school. In kids who fit the PDA profile, resistance is sometimes mistaken for willful defiance, says Cynthia Martin, PsyD, a clinical psychologist who specializes in autism. But it\u2019s better understood as a result of anxiety and inflexibility. As she explains, \u201cAny external demand that is coming from somebody else, or that the person perceives is coming from another person \u2014 that generates a lot of internal discomfort, which leads to the avoidance.\u201d \u00b7\n\nBecause of that, parents sometimes call it \u201cPDA autism.\u201d Identifying PDA can be a helpful way to understand some kinds of difficult behavior, but it is not a subtype of autism or a separate diagnosis. People with PDA tend to have an adverse reaction to being told how to behave or what to do, even when it\u2019s something that\u2019s an ordinary part of their daily life \u2014 and even when it would benefit them. It\u2019s called pathological when the avoidance is extreme and interferes with functioning at home or at school. In kids who fit the PDA profile, resistance is sometimes mistaken for willful defiance, says Cynthia Martin, PsyD, a clinical psychologist who specializes in autism. But it\u2019s better understood as a result of anxiety and inflexibility. As she explains, \u201cAny external demand that is coming from somebody else, or that the person perceives is coming from another person \u2014 that generates a lot of internal discomfort, which leads to the avoidance.\u201d \u00b7\n\nTasks like putting on shoes, getting dressed, sitting down at a table, drawing, or saying the names of common items or pictures in a book may all be things a young child has the skills to do. But a child with PDA might only do these things when they\u2019re personally motivated to do them. Experts call this \u201cself-directed behavior.\u201d The parent can ask, show, and direct all they want, but their child will not do it when another person asks them to. This can be a frustrating experience for parents \u2014 especially when their child is being evaluated, because the child may appear unable to do things parents know they can do. Similarly, older children and adolescents may appear to have difficulty completing basic school assignments, even though parents know them to be capable of advanced academics. ... Many children with autism spectrum disorder are inherently inflexible in their behavior and have rigid patterns of thinking, Dr.\n\nParents are also asking whether their children have what they call \u201cPDA autism,\u201d she reports. While there isn\u2019t a PDA subtype of autism, she notes, the value in identifying PDA behavior as a \u201cprofile\u201d is to respond to the fact that these kids may need a different type of support than other children on the spectrum. Some of them may have co-occurring disorders, such as ADHD, anxiety, or a mood disorder, which complicate their behavior. In other cases, since autism itself is highly heterogeneous, there may be a lot of complexity in the way their autism presents. Providers and teachers may be approaching them in a way that is misaligned with the child\u2019s needs, leading to conflict and lack of progress. \u201cTo approach these kids effectively we need to better understand why they are avoiding something, identify if there are co-occurring disorders that have not been diagnosed, and then respond to their individual needs,\u201d Dr.\n\nPDA is often seen in children diagnosed with with autism. Because of that, parents sometimes call it \u201cPDA autism.\u201d Identifying PDA can be a helpful way to understand some kinds of difficult behavior, but it is not a subtype of autism or a separate diagnosis.         "text": "Objectives: Pathological demand avoidance (PDA) is characterized by an extreme difficulty in tolerating everyday demands, with a range of \u2018socially manipulative\u2019 strategies used to avoid demands. Children also display poor social identity, impulsivity, and obsessive behavior. While research and knowledge about PDA is slowly growing, families continue to encounter considerable difficulties in ensuring their children\u2019s needs are understood and met by health and education services. This study explored what parents of children with extreme demand avoidance valued about professionals\u2019 input. Methods: Parents of 42 children who scored above the cut-off on a standardized measure with validated sensitivity and specificity for PDA responded to questionnaire items asking them to identify professionals\u2019 involvement and helpfulness. They were also asked what had characterized the most helpful professional involvement.\n\nObjectives: Pathological demand avoidance (PDA) is characterized by an extreme difficulty in tolerating everyday demands, with a range of \u2018socially manipulative\u2019 strategies used to avoid demands. Children also display poor social identity, impulsivity, and obsessive behavior. While research and knowledge about PDA is slowly growing, families continue to encounter considerable difficulties in ensuring their children\u2019s needs are understood and met by health and education services. This study explored what parents of children with extreme demand avoidance valued about professionals\u2019 input. Methods: Parents of 42 children who scored above the cut-off on a standardized measure with validated sensitivity and specificity for PDA responded to questionnaire items asking them to identify professionals\u2019 involvement and helpfulness. They were also asked what had characterized the most helpful professional involvement.\n\nConclusions: Parents felt positive about practitioners who had listened to their experiences, made efforts to understand the child, and provided or arranged for help. Parents found involvement most helpful when it resulted in comprehensive assessment, appropriate intervention, practical advice and management strategies, and a focus on the well-being of all family members. The overall ratings of helpfulness are encouraging, and the specific feedback about what is most helpful could be of value in shaping services. Keywords: autism spectrum disorder (ASD), pathological demand avoidance (PDA), extreme demand avoidance, service user satisfaction, service development \u00b7 Pathological demand avoidance (PDA) is attracting growing attention and recognition in the UK. First identified by English psychologist Elizabeth Newson in the 1980s and described in the research literature by Newson and her colleagues in 2003, PDA is named for the core difficulty with tolerating everyday pressures and demand\n\nIn practice, information about PDA is not widely available and, even when parents do find out about PDA, the diagnosis does not form part of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders 5 (American Psychiatric Association 2013) or the International Classification of Diseases 10 (World Health Organization 1992), the two classification systems used by diagnosticians. Although a standardized clinical measure for identifying PDA is under development (O\u2019Nions et al. 2016), the willingness of clinicians to use the term \u2018PDA\u2019 varies according to local knowledge and practices (O\u2019Nions et al. 2014). There are also objections among professionals about the term \u2018pathological,\u2019 which was intended to describe the extent and impact of children\u2019s demand avoidance, but instead, some feel, \u2018pathologizes\u2019 the child. Where parents can obtain a diagnosis, this is the beginning rather than the end of the journey for them as PDA is rarely recognized within systems that assess and provide for ed\n\nProfessionals\u2019 skills and qualities were identified as important by the parents in our sample. Many of these skills, such as listening, being sympathetic and caring, being available, and providing a continuous relationship, were generic across the professional groups. The emphasis on these skills may reflect the particular emotional strain on this group of parents, who run the risk of encountering professionals who consider the symptoms of PDA to represent attachment difficulties caused by parenting and who judge parents who raise the possibility of PDA as being keen to \u2018pathologise\u2019 their children or as over-anxious self-appointed \u2018internet paediatricians.\u2019 It was interesting that parents did not require professionals to have prior knowledge and understanding of PDA; although they valued this, there was an equal emphasis on professionals being open to learning about PDA.",

(General guidance mirrors this framing across reputable sources.) Schools can be overwhelming\u2014constant demands, transitions, and sensory load. A low-arousal, collaborative plan makes learning possible. Parents in UK studies report high support needs for PDA-profile students (Gore Langton & Frederickson, 2015). Practice guidance stresses tailoring supports to a constellation of traits, not a one-size plan. Low-demand entry to tasks; offer choice and novelty \u00b7 Predictable routines with opt-in breaks and sensory regulation \u00b7 Assessment flexibility (orals, visuals, chunked work) Collaborative scripts (\u201cHow could we make this easier?\u201d) No healing without calm. Our sequence\u2014Regulate \u2192 Connect \u2192 Correct\u2122\u2014puts nervous system balance ahead of behavior change.\n\nPDA is debated and isn\u2019t a formal diagnosis in the DSM right now. Some organizations describe it as a demand-avoidant profile seen in some autistic people. Many PDAers use social strategies such as negotiation, distraction, or role-play to avoid demands. They often respond better to autonomy-supportive approaches than to traditional behavior plans. PDA can look a lot like oppositional defiant disorder (constant arguing or refusals) or ADHD (struggles with focus and follow-through). But here\u2019s the key difference: with PDA, the behaviors are usually driven by anxiety and a strong need for control, not defiance or laziness. Research shows that kids with PDA traits often have their own unique \u201cdemand avoidance\u201d profile and challenges with social communication (O\u2019Nions et al., 2017). In other words, the behavior isn\u2019t random\u2014it\u2019s their nervous system saying, \u201cI feel unsafe.\u201d \u00b7\n\nIf Pathological Demand Avoidance is part of your family\u2019s story, remember: you have tools, and your child has strengths. No. PDA behaviors are anxiety-driven and tied to autonomy needs, which are different from willful defiance. PDAers may escalate with traditional behavior plans; low-demand, collaborative strategies and co-regulation usually work better. Choice-rich, time-buffered, collaborative language (\u201cWould you like to start in 5 or 10 minutes?\u201d) reduces threat and boosts follow-through.\n\nPathological Demand Avoidance (PDA): An anxiety-driven profile marked by extreme avoidance of everyday demands and a strong need for autonomy. Low-demand approach: Language and routines that reduce threat and increase choice. Co-regulation: Your calm body and voice help your child\u2019s nervous system calm down.         "text": "Dr. Taylor Day discusses Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA), focusing on supporting children with PDA in both home and educational settings. Learn about the drive for autonomy, the impact of the fight-or-flight response, and effective strategies to respect boundaries while honoring children&#x27;s autonomy. Dr. Tay also shares insights from her expertise in neuroaffirming care and early intervention.v\n\nDr. Taylor Day and I recently discussed Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA) on the podcast. We dove into the unique drive for autonomy in children with PDA, the impact of the fight-or-flight response, and practical strategies for supporting these children both at home and in educational settings. Dr. Tay emphasizes the importance of a balanced approach to parenting and teaching\u2014one that honors a child\u2019s autonomy while still maintaining essential boundaries. Dr. Taylor Day is a licensed psychologist who specializes in neuro affirming care for autistic children and their families, with a focus on early diagnosis and intervention. She holds a Ph.D. in clinical psychology and is the CEO and founder of Dr. Tay Concierge Clinical Care. Her practice integrates The Whole Family Approach, a unique process she created using evidence-informed principles after recognizing a gap in autism care.\n\nDr. Taylor Day discusses Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA), focusing on supporting children with PDA in both home and educational settings. Learn about the drive for autonomy, the impact of the fight-or-flight response, and effective strategies to respect boundaries while honoring children's autonomy. Dr. Tay also shares insights from her expertise in neuroaffirming care and early intervention.v\n\nTara: This is why IEPs are important\u2014they help personalize support when issues arise. Too often, parents of high-masking kids are dismissed by schools, especially when their children hide stress at school but show it at home.\n\nDr. Tay: The challenge is meeting individuals where they are and providing the right accommodations. This lack of understanding causes PDA to be dismissed as something that needs fixing instead of something to better understand.         "text": "Table 8 outlines the qualitative methods used in 50% of studies, all of whom, except O&#x27;Nions (2013), also used quantitative methods. The most common method was semi-structured interview (64%) with parents (91%), with the most common topic being their child\u2019s current presentation and school experience (91%). Some interviews were at least partly based on widely used diagnostic interviews such as the Autism Diagnostic Interview- Revised (ADI-R), and the DISCO-II or on Newson\u2019s PDA criteria or on the EDA-Q. Similarly, observations were at least partly based on the ADOS-II, a play-based autism observation assessment. In exploring tolerance of uncertainty through a behavioral presentation, Stuart et al. (2020) employed an Antecedent, Behavior Consequence (ABC) structure during a telephone interview. 18% of studies gained a developmental history, where the focus for Graham-White (2015) in his case file review was on creating communication profiles at different stages.\n\nTable 8 outlines the qualitative methods used in 50% of studies, all of whom, except O'Nions (2013), also used quantitative methods. The most common method was semi-structured interview (64%) with parents (91%), with the most common topic being their child\u2019s current presentation and school experience (91%). Some interviews were at least partly based on widely used diagnostic interviews such as the Autism Diagnostic Interview- Revised (ADI-R), and the DISCO-II or on Newson\u2019s PDA criteria or on the EDA-Q. Similarly, observations were at least partly based on the ADOS-II, a play-based autism observation assessment. In exploring tolerance of uncertainty through a behavioral presentation, Stuart et al. (2020) employed an Antecedent, Behavior Consequence (ABC) structure during a telephone interview. 18% of studies gained a developmental history, where the focus for Graham-White (2015) in his case file review was on creating communication profiles at different stages.\n\nHowever, studies such as McFadzen (2020) have shown that the EDA-Q, a questionnaire for assessing PDA traits, can identify significant numbers of non-autistic CYP. This discrepancy suggests that the definition and inclusion criteria for PDA can significantly impact research outcomes. If O\u2019Nions et al. (2021) had adopted a more inclusive approach to defining PDA and included diverse demographic groups such as those with ADHD, they might have produced a different version of the EDA-Q, potentially leading to a different outcome than the EDA-8. Therefore, it is essential for future research to consider the broader context and potential overlaps between PDA and other categories, as well as the implications of different measurement approaches. Most quantitative studies relied on parent report which some argue increases risk of bias as parents may have elected to take part owing to interest in PDA (Green et al., 2018b).\n\nHowever, the different thresholds values in use to indicate PDA across studies based on the DISCO, (Gillberg et al., 2015; O\u2019Nions et al., 2016; O'Nions et al., 2018a) further highlights the difficulty with defining the PDA construct. Perhaps reflecting a more established field of study, many different measures are in use in assessment of characteristics of autism. Descriptive statistics were used in all quantitative studies, often accompanied by simple inferential analysis. Many studies used psychometric scales but reporting of validity scores was inconsistent. The lack of complex statistical analyses may be explained by the relatively smaller samples in use in studies, the largest study had 326. Predominantly, qualitative methods have been used alongside quantitative methods in the study of PDA. In using qualitative approaches, most papers did not address researcher positionality. Like the quantitative methods, to date the source of information has heavily relied on parents who, in s\n\nAligned with Newson\u2019s observations that children exhibit challenging features even within autism services, Gore Langton and Frederickson (2016) assert that success in schools is contingent upon staff flexibility rather than access to more specialized provisions, such as autism classes. Contrary to the structured and repetitive approaches rooted in the principles of Lovaas (1987) and Kunce and Mesibov (1998), which have historically dominated education for autistic individuals (Moore, 2020), Newson advocates for a different approach. Recent research (e.g., Gore Langton and Frederickson, 2018; Ozsivadjian, 2020) and practitioner insights (e.g., Fidler and Christie, 2019) support Newson\u2019s approach, which places anxiety as a central driver (O'Nions and Eaton, 2020). For parents whose children do not thrive under traditional autism interventions, Pathological Demand Avoidance (PDA) offers a significant recognition factor and a roadmap for more tailored support (Gore Langton and Frederickson",

(4) Collaborate rather than direct. (5) Regulate yourself first (co-regulation). (6) Accept that compliance may look different. Research brief for hearing officers on PDA evidence base.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 5\n    [\"5\\nTherapy &\\nIntervention\\n(PDA-Informed)\", \"5.1\", \"Find PDA-Affirming Therapists & Providers\",\n     \"Locate therapists who understand PDA profile and use PDA-informed approaches. PDA North America maintains a PDA-Affirming Provider Directory. Key provider types: (1) Psychologist \u2014 CBT adapted for PDA/anxiety, NOT standard behavioral therapy. (2) OT \u2014 sensory integration, interoception, nervous system regulation. (3) Speech \u2014 functional communication, AAC if needed, NOT compliance-based drills. (4) Family therapist \u2014 whole-family approach, parent coaching. (5) SPACE treatment therapist \u2014 evidence-based parent training for childhood anxiety. This is NOT truancy \u2014 it is anxiety-driven inability to tolerate the school environment. Approaches: (1) Gradual reintroduction with dramatically reduced demands. (2) Modified schedule (part-day, specific classes only). (3) Homebound instruction (home/hospital) during crisis. (4) Independent study options. (5) Virtual/online school. (6) Alternative school with lower demands. Independent study requires specific agreement. District resists modified schedule.\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL: School avoidance documentation template (disability-related, not truancy). SARB defense packet. Home/hospital instruction request generator. Modified schedule IEP amendment template. Virtual school option finder. Letter to district framing school avoidance as FAPE failure, not attendance problem.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"6.2\", \"Manage PDA Crisis Episodes (Meltdowns/Shutdowns)\",\n     \"PDA meltdowns and shutdowns are NOT behavioral \u2014 they are nervous system dysregulation. During crisis: (1) Remove all demands immediately. (2) Ensure safety. (3) Do NOT try to talk, reason, or redirect during crisis. (4) Offer calm, quiet presence (co-regulation). (5) Allow time and space for nervous system to reset. (6) Do NOT punish or consequence the crisis. After crisis: (1) Reconnect gently. (2) Identify trigger (was it a demand? Transition? Sensory? Unexpected change?). (3) Problem-solve collaboratively for next time. (4) Reduce overall demand load going forward.\",\n     \"Parents\\nSchool staff\\nTherapist\", \"None\",\n     \"Crisis plan (in IEP/504). Safe space identified at school and home.\",\n     \"Immediate response needed during crisis. Prevention is ongoing.\",\n     \"School staff physically restrain child during meltdown (common, traumatic for PDA children). School calls police for 'violent behavior.' Seclusion room used. Child suspended or expelled for crisis behavior. Parent called to pick up child repeatedly \u2014 job loss risk. Crisis misidentified as manipulative behavior rather than dysregulation.\",\n     \"PDA crisis response protocol for school staff (one-page). Restraint/seclusion documentation and complaint tools. Emergency IEP meeting request template when crisis interventions are harmful. De-escalation guide specific to PDA. Post-crisis analysis tool (identify trigger \u2192 reduce demand).\"],\n\n    # PHASE 7\n    [\"7\\nLong-Term\\nPlanning &\\nTransition\", \"7.1\", \"Ongoing IEP/504 Review \u2014 PDA-Specific Monitoring\",\n     \"Annual IEP review must assess whether PDA-informed accommodations are working. (5) Is school using low-demand approach consistently? (6) Are goals focused on regulation/self-advocacy, not compliance? Request that IEP progress reports include multi-setting data (school + home).\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"Parent\\nPrivate therapist\",\n     \"Progress reports. Parent home data. Parent must advocate for PDA-informed services: low-demand therapeutic approaches, OT for sensory regulation, speech therapy focused on functional communication not compliance, parent coaching in low-demand strategies, respite care. Key principles: (1) Reduce demands to child's current capacity. (2) Use declarative language instead of imperative ('I notice it's getting cold' vs 'Put on your jacket'). (3) Offer genuine choices. (4) Collaborate rather than direct. (5) Regulate yourself first (co-regulation). (6) Accept that compliance may look different. (7) Build flexibility gradually \\u2014 'Regulate \\u2192 Connect \\u2192 Correct.' Therapeutic approaches: CBT adapted for PDA (anxiety-focused), OT (sensory regulation), play therapy, family therapy. Programs: 'Unstuck and on Target' for building flexibility. Parent exhaustion and burnout from constant negotiation. Other family members (grandparents, co-parent) don't understand low-demand approach \\u2014 see it as 'giving in.' School uses opposite approach from home, creating inconsistency. Cost of PDA-informed private therapy.\\\",\\n     \\\"Low-demand language guide with examples ('Instead of X, try Y'). (5) Allow time and space for nervous system to reset. (6) Do NOT punish or consequence the crisis. After crisis: (1) Reconnect gently. (2) Identify trigger (was it a demand? Transition? Sensory? Unexpected change?). (3) Problem-solve collaboratively for next time. The journey map captures this throughout:\n\n**Phase 1 \u2014 Recognition & 'Lightbulb Moment' (2 steps)**\n- Parent recognizes extreme demand avoidance pattern (not just \"won't\" but CAN'T comply)\n- Parent discovers PDA framework through PDANA, social media, or parent communities\n- Key insight: traditional interventions (sticker charts, time-outs, consequences) make things WORSE\n\n**Phase 2 \u2014 Seeking Diagnosis Through ASD Pathway (3 steps)**\n- Must pursue autism evaluation since PDA has no standalone diagnosis\n- Standard ADOS-2 may MISS PDA children (they're socially oriented, make eye contact, mask)\n- Navigation of diagnostic language: \"ASD with PDA profile\" or \"ASD with extreme demand avoidance traits\"\n- \"What Can Go Wrong\" column flags: misdiagnosis as ODD, evaluators unfamiliar with PDA, masking during assessment\n\n**Phase 3 \u2014 PDA-Informed Service Planning (2 steps)**\n- Critical warning: traditional ABA (40 hrs/week compliance training) typically ESCALATES PDA behaviors\n- Low-demand parenting framework: declarative language, genuine choices, co-regulation, \"Regulate \u2192 Connect \u2192 Correct\"\n- SPACE treatment method, \"Unstuck and on Target\" flexibility program\n\n**Phase 4 \u2014 School Navigation (5 steps)**\n- IEP strategy: describe BEHAVIORS, don't use \"PDA\" label \u2014 \"anxiety-driven avoidance\" works better\n- PDA-specific IEP accommodations (10 specific items, all documented with rationale)\n- After-school restraint collapse documentation\n- 504 alternative if IEP denied (masking children often don't qualify)\n- Dispute resolution when school uses counterproductive behavioral approaches\n\n**Phase 5 \u2014 PDA-Informed Therapy (2 steps)**\n- PDANA PDA-Affirming Provider Directory integration\n- Insurance authorization strategies: frame PDA therapy as anxiety treatment (covered separately from ABA)\n- Provider desert reality: very few U.S. therapists understand PDA\n\n**Phase 6 \u2014 School Avoidance & Crisis Management (2 steps)**\n- School avoidance is disability-related, NOT truancy \u2014 SARB defense documentation\n- PDA crisis protocol: meltdowns are nervous system dysregulation, not behavioral \u2014 remove demands, don't restrain\n\n**Phase 7 \u2014 Long-Term Planning & Transition (3 steps)**\n- PDA-specific IEP monitoring (masking alerts, multi-setting wellness tracking)\n- Transition planning emphasizing self-advocacy and autonomy-driven career paths\n- Adult PDA: workplace accommodations, SSI, burnout prevention\n\n**9 PDA-specific sources added** (PDANA, PDA Society UK, Child Mind Institute, Undivided PDA 101, SPACE Treatment, Reframing Autism, PDA Parents, A Day in Our Shoes, ADDitude)\n\nThe critical Waypoint insight from PDA research: **the IEP advocacy strategy for PDA is completely different from standard autism.** You don't fight for more ABA hours \u2014 you fight AGAINST traditional behavioral approaches and FOR low-demand, anxiety-informed accommodations.",

ADHD is a lifelong neurodevelopmental condition that affects executive function, self-regulation, and emotional processing. Research shows that ADHD-related challenges can shorten life expectancy by up to 13 years*, yet outcomes improve dramatically when individuals have access to accurate diagnosis, compassionate education, effective treatment, skill-building, and supportive communities. Through peer-led groups, educational meetups, and community learning events, CHADD of California helps people connect, learn, and grow together.\n\nWe serve everyone touched by ADHD \u2014 children, teens, and adults with ADHD; their parents, caregivers, and partners; as well as the professionals and educators who support them.\n\nTogether, we are creating a connected, informed, and empowered ADHD community \u2014 one that celebrates strengths, supports struggles, and helps every individual with ADHD live a fuller, more balanced life.",

Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a common behavioral condition affecting 11 percent of school-age children. Symptoms continue into adulthood in more than three-quarters of cases.         "title": "Prevalence and Characteristics of School Services for High School Students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder - PMC",
        "url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4261048/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

        "text": "% of students with ADHD and a current IEP or 504 plan (n=107) identified by school staff as having different disabilities. Students may have multiple categories of identification, including \u201cADHD\u201d, which is not a federal disability category. LD = Learning Disability; ED/BED = Emotionally Disturbed/Behaviorally and Emotionally Disturbed; Other includes autism (n = 2), visually impaired (n = 1), developmental delay (n = 4), mild mental retardation (n = 2), multiple disabilities (n = 4) and \u201cother\u201d (n = 8). OHI = Other Health Impaired, Speech/Lang = Speech or Language Impaired. Three subjects with IEPs/504 plans did not have this data available. Students may have more than one disability identified. As depicted in Figure 2, the majority of students with ADHD who had an IEP or 504 plan had goals targeting overall academic performance, although more than a third also had primary goals to prepare for post-secondary education, improve specific academic skills, and improve general behavior.\n\nThis study examines the prevalence and characteristics of services reported by school staff for 543 high school students participating in the 8 year follow-up of the multi-site Multimodal Treatment study of ADHD (MTA). Overall, 51.6% of students with a history of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) were receiving services through an Individualized Educational Plan (IEP) or a 504 plan, a rate higher than expected for this age group. Less than 5% of these had 504 plans; 35.5% attended special education classes. Very few services (except tutoring) were provided outside of an IEP or 504 plan. Almost all students with services received some type of academic intervention, whereas only half received any behavioral support or learning strategy. Less than one-fourth of interventions appear to be evidence-based. Students receiving services showed greater academic and behavioral needs than those not receiving services.\n\n% of students with ADHD and a current IEP or 504 plan (n=107) identified by school staff as having different disabilities. Students may have multiple categories of identification, including \u201cADHD\u201d, which is not a federal disability category. LD = Learning Disability; ED/BED = Emotionally Disturbed/Behaviorally and Emotionally Disturbed; Other includes autism (n = 2), visually impaired (n = 1), developmental delay (n = 4), mild mental retardation (n = 2), multiple disabilities (n = 4) and \u201cother\u201d (n = 8). OHI = Other Health Impaired, Speech/Lang = Speech or Language Impaired. Three subjects with IEPs/504 plans did not have this data available. Students may have more than one disability identified. As depicted in Figure 2, the majority of students with ADHD who had an IEP or 504 plan had goals targeting overall academic performance, although more than a third also had primary goals to prepare for post-secondary education, improve specific academic skills, and improve general behavior.\n\nAlthough federal law clarified in 1999 that ADHD can be included under the \u201cOther Health Impairment\u201d (OHI) category when it creates educational impairment, it appears that most students with ADHD are actually served in other disability categories such as \u201cSpecific Learning Disability\u201d (SLD) and \u201cEmotional Disturbance\u201d (ED) [US Department of Education, 2008] . Students with ADHD may also be eligible for accommodations through Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 (Section 504) depending on the nature of their impairment at school; however, reliable rates of 504 plans for ADHD have not been documented. This appears to be an important omission in that 504 plans can be provided to students who do not qualify for special education (which has more restrictive qualifications), and may be considered appropriate for providing classroom accommodations for older students.\n\nKeywords: ADHD, high school, School Services, IEP, special education, 504 plan, MTA \u00b7 Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) has a significant negative impact on children\u2019s educational functioning and academic performance. Students with ADHD typically experience difficulties completing work and perform at levels below their ability in the classroom. They score 10-30 points lower than students without ADHD on various achievement tests, despite having generally average intelligence (see Barkley, Fischer, Smallish & Fletcher, 2006 for a review). In addition, prior work across a range of ages estimates that approximately 30% of children with ADHD repeat a grade, as many as 56% receive tutoring, and 46% may be suspended (Barkley et al., 2006; DuPaul & Stoner, 2003). High school students with ADHD appear to experience even greater academic impairment, with lower grade point averages, placement in lower level classes (e.g., remedial vs.",

        "text": "Because IDEA is very specific, children who have been diagnosed with ADHD only are often denied services under this law. If your child has another challenge, such as a learning disability, this law might apply.\n\nWhich is better for a child with ADHD: an IEP or a 504 Plan? Here, understand the differences in accommodations and special services, learn how to secure the most effective school help for your child, and understand how the process works from the first assessment to the final paperwork.\n\nADHD falls into the classification of Other Health Impaired (OHI). Because IDEA is very specific, children who have been diagnosed with ADHD only are often denied services under this law. If your child has another challenge, such as a learning disability, this law might apply. If your child\u2019s ADHD is so severe that it causes major impairment, he might qualify under IDEA. If so, a document called an Individualized Education Program (IEP) is designed to outline services, accommodations, special education, and goals for your child. The IEP document is usually about 10 to 12 pages long. It must contain goals and transitional services that might be needed after high school. Under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, the needs of children with physical and mental impairments are required to be met as adequately as those of children without disabilities.",

        "title": "Predictors of Receipt of School Services in a National Sample of",

        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

        "text": "Under Section 504, educational accommodations (e.g., preferential seating, extra time on tests) in the general \u00b7 education classroom are warranted when a student has a disability or could be considered to \u00b7 have a disability that limits one or more life activities (e.g., learning).\n\nadolescents with ADHD continue to show deficits in reading, math, and spelling compared ... Section 504 (Schnoes, Reid, Wagner, & Marder, 2006). An individualized education \u00b7 program (IEP) is mandated by federal law when a student meets criteria for an educational \u00b7 disability (e.g., LD, other health impairment) and the disability limits educational \u00b7 functioning to the extent that special education services are necessary. Under Section 504, educational accommodations (e.g., preferential seating, extra time on tests) in the general \u00b7 education classroom are warranted when a student has a disability or could be considered to \u00b7 have a disability that limits one or more life activities (e.g., learning).         "text": "Davila et al.\u2019s (1991) policy memorandum posited that the Other Health Impairment (OHI) category was \u201cnot exhaustive\u201d and could include \u201cchronic and acute impairments that result in limited alertness\u201d and therefore ADHD could be included within this classification. The inclusion of ADHD within the OHI category was especially confusing given that all other OHI examples were wholly medical conditions that would interfere with education (e.g., diabetes). It further noted that if a student with ADHD had a comorbid condition, it was possible that the student could be classified as having a Specific Learning Disability or an Emotional Disturbance. The policy document also noted that if a student was not eligible for special education, it was possible that the student would be eligible for accommodations under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 (29 U.S.C.\n\nJ Sch Psychol. Author manuscript; available in PMC: 2025 Jun 1. Published in final edited form as: J Sch Psychol. 2024 Apr 22;104:101309. doi: 10.1016/j.jsp.2024.101309 ... Address correspondence to: Gregory A. Fabiano, Ph.D. Florida International University , Department of Psychology, Center for Children and Families , 4600 Main Street, Suite 101 , Amherst NY 14226 , Gfabiano@fiu.edu \u00b7 Issue date 2024 Jun. ... The long-term academic outcomes for many students with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) are strikingly poor. It has been decades since students with ADHD were specifically recognized as eligible for special education through the Other Health Impaired category under the Education for all Handicapped Children Act of 1975, and similarly, eligible for academic accommodations through Section 504 of the 1973 Rehabilitation Act. It is time to acknowledge that these school-policies have been insufficient for supporting the academic, social, and behavioral outcomes for stu\n\nThere are multiple ways responsibility for supporting children with ADHD are delegated to those outside of the school system. Take, for example, the consideration of formal school supports through a Section 504 accommodation plan or through an IEP based on special education eligibility within the OHI category. In both cases, the precipitating reason for identification of the student to receive these supports is a diagnosis of ADHD, something that is not universally assigned by a school psychologist using current evidence-based diagnostic procedures (e.g., Handler & DuPaul, 2005). Although an ADHD diagnosis is not required to receive special education supports or accommodations through a Section 504 accommodation plan (U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education and Rehabilitative Services, 2016), many districts have implicitly provided formal supports only with the presence of an outside provider\u2019s diagnosis of ADHD (e.g., Briesch et al., 2023).\n\nMany of the approaches to formal support of children and adolescents with ADHD in educational settings may promulgate power differentials within schools. It is challenging for a lay person unaccustomed to terms such as IEP, OHI, or Section 504 plan to negotiate within jargon-filled conversations (Rossetti et al., 2021). Lost within timelines for initial evaluations or diagnostic requirements are discussions about practical ways to support the student\u2019s success. Furthermore, although they are invited to meetings and can participate in intervention planning, parents may be under-utilized or have their concerns left unaddressed in the IEP development process (Kurth et al., 2019, 2020). Moreover, schools may struggle to mitigate this lack of engagement as there is not a collection of evidence-based strategies to guide parents on how to best support and advocate for their child during these meetings (Goldman & Burke, 2017).\n\nDavila et al.\u2019s (1991) policy memorandum posited that the Other Health Impairment (OHI) category was \u201cnot exhaustive\u201d and could include \u201cchronic and acute impairments that result in limited alertness\u201d and therefore ADHD could be included within this classification. The inclusion of ADHD within the OHI category was especially confusing given that all other OHI examples were wholly medical conditions that would interfere with education (e.g., diabetes). It further noted that if a student with ADHD had a comorbid condition, it was possible that the student could be classified as having a Specific Learning Disability or an Emotional Disturbance. The policy document also noted that if a student was not eligible for special education, it was possible that the student would be eligible for accommodations under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 (29 U.S.C.",

        "text": "Educational support (62.3%) was nearly twice as prevalent as classroom behavior management (32.0%). More than 3 times as many students with ADHD had an individualized education program (IEP; 42.9%) as a Section 504 plan (13.6%). At least one in five students with ADHD do not receive school ...\n\nThe majority (69.3%) of students with ADHD currently receive one or more school services. Educational support (62.3%) was nearly twice as prevalent as classroom behavior management (32.0%). More than 3 times as many students with ADHD had an individualized education program (IEP; 42.9%) as a Section 504 plan (13.6%). At least one in five students with ADHD do not receive school services despite experiencing significant academic and social impairment, a gap that is particularly evident for adolescents and youth from non\u2013English-speaking and/or lower income families. Keywords: ADHD, academic functioning, social functioning, school services \u00b7 Youth with ADHD display clinically significant levels of inattentive and/or hyperactive-impulsive behaviors relative to peers of the same gender and age, and must exhibit impairment in functioning to receive an ADHD diagnosis (American Psychiatric Association [APA], 2013).\n\nGiven the scope and severity of functional impairment experienced by youth with ADHD in academic and social domains, school-based intervention and services often are necessary (Pfiffner & DuPaul, 2015). Randomized controlled trials and other experimental studies have shown school-based interventions (e.g., contingency management, daily report card) improve classroom behavior and academic performance with moderate to large effect sizes (DuPaul et al., 2012; Fabiano et al., 2009). Students with ADHD may also qualify for individualized instruction and related special education services (Bussing, Zima, Perwien, Belin, & Widawski, 1998) as well as educational accommodations and support through Section 504 (Schnoes, Reid, Wagner, & Marder, 2006). An individualized education program (IEP) is mandated by federal law when a student meets criteria for an educational disability (e.g., LD, other health impairment) and the disability limits educational functioning to the extent that special educati\n\nFew studies have examined the extent to which students with ADHD receive various school-based support and intervention services. Early studies indicated that only about 25% of children with ADHD received school-based services for ADHD and related impairments (Leslie & Wolraich, 2007). However, Merikangas and colleagues (2011) found that 63% of adolescents with ADHD ever received school-based mental health services. Murray et al. (2014) examined the prevalence of special education services and Section 504 plan receipt in 543 high school students with ADHD who were participants in the Multimodal Treatment study of ADHD (MTA). Just over half (51.6%) of students received special education services through an IEP while less than 5% of students had Section 504 plans. IEP and 504 plans primarily provided academic intervention, with only half of students with these plans receiving any behavioral intervention or learning strategy support.\n\nTo describe the receipt of school services, parents were asked whether their child had ever received the following treatments or interventions for ADHD or other difficulties with their child\u2019s emotions, concentration, or behavior: (a) school-based educational support, intervention, or accommodation, such as tutoring, extra help from a teacher, preferential seating, extra time to complete work, or being enrolled in special education (hereafter referred to as \u201cschool support\u201d), or (b) classroom management, such as reward systems, behavioral modification, or a daily report card (hereafter referred to as \u201cclassroom management\u201d). If the parent reported that the child had ever received any of these, they were asked whether the child was currently receiving that intervention. Parents were also asked whether their child currently had an IEP or 504 plan.",

        "text": "To qualify for a 504 Plan, ADHD must substantially limit one or more major life activities, such as learning, concentrating, or thinking. This requires documented proof. Many school districts require parents to provide a diagnosis of ADHD from ...\n\nTo qualify for a 504 Plan, ADHD must substantially limit one or more major life activities, such as learning, concentrating, or thinking. This requires documented proof. Many school districts require parents to provide a diagnosis of ADHD from a qualified medical professional. This is because such a diagnosis serves as documented proof that ADHD substantially limits one or more major life activities, as outlined in the DSM-5 diagnostic criteria. In addition to the medical diagnosis, evidence of ADHD\u2019s educational impact is often gathered from teacher communications, including emails, notes, and reports. In cases where ADHD significantly impacts a child\u2019s ability to access or benefit from education, an Individualized Education Program (IEP) may be developed to provide tailored support. Under IDEA, ADHD is specifically included in the disability category of \u201cOther Health Impairment\u201d (OHI).\n\nLet me say that again for people in the back\u2026.Achieving high grades doesn\u2019t disqualify a student from a 504 Plan or IEP! Under federal law, students with ADHD are eligible for support through Section 504 and IDEA, regardless of their academic performance. In a letter dated March 8, 2007, Dr. Alexa Posny, then Director of the Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP), emphasizes that: \u201cEducational performance\u2019 as used in the IDEA and its implementing regulations is not limited to academic performance. Schools must consider not only academic performance but also how a child\u2019s emotional, health, or other conditions may adversely affect their non-academic performance in social, behavioral, and other domains. \u201c \u00b7 Achieving high grades or performing at grade level does not negate the substantial impact ADHD can have on other major life activities, such as concentrating, organizing, emotional regulation or completing tasks.\n\nWe can help by including \u201cteacher training on ADHD\u201d on our students IEP\u2019s and 504 plans. This will help to ensure their educators are adequately informed about the challenges of ADHD and how to best support the student in the classroom through appropriate strategies and accommodations. How often do we see this? Things are going great and then, the teacher is out. This happens, as believe it or not teachers are humans and have lives, families and get sick (despite how superhuman they appear). The problem we see is that when a substitute teacher is in the classroom, students with ADHD often struggle. Unnecessarily. A substitute\u2019s lack of awareness can not excuse a lapse in support. Under both Section 504 and IDEA, schools are required to provide consistent accommodations and services to students with disabilities, regardless of who is teaching the class.\n\nA student\u2019s right to accommodations doesn\u2019t take a day off. Schools must create systems to ensure that substitutes are informed about and can implement 504 Plan or IEP accommodations. Consistency is critical, and failing to provide it violates FAPE. ... Reasonable accommodations are not limited to generic options from a drop-down list! Reasonable accommodations are not limited to academic needs, they can address barriers to participation across all domains of educational performance, outlined in the image below. Accommodations change the context, they enable the learner to access their education and mitigate the impact of ADHD. They are powerful management tools and so much more than \u201d extra time\u201d and \u201d preferential seating\u201d. Learn more here. Finally, the age old conversation about which is best for students with ADHD, a 504 Plan or an IEP .",

Many students with ADHD will qualify under the category of Other Health Impairment, or OHI. It is important for parents to understand that not every child with a diagnosis of ADHD will qualify for an IEP. In order for this to happen, evaluation must show that the ADHD is impeding the child\u2019s progress in the school curriculum. A 504 plan is another option for parents to consider. It differs in many ways from an IEP. First, 504 plans are covered by Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, which is a piece of civil rights legislation. These plans serve students who have a diagnosis or impairment (which can include ADHD) that restricts one or more life activities. 504 plans allow these students to receive accommodations that make it easier to access their school\u2019s general education curriculum.\n\n\u201cDisability \u201d is a narrowly and specifically defined term under IDEA, which recognizes 13 different disability categories. Specifically, we examined the problem areas identified in the statement of students\u2019 present level of academic achievement and functional performance (PLAAFP) and targeted in the students\u2019 measurable annual goals and objectives (MAGOs). In addition, we compared services to lists of recommended services provided by the U.S. Department of Education (ED) and reviews of evidence-based practices. Participants were 97 middle school students with ADHD, 61.9% with an IEP and 38.1% with a 504 Plan.         "url": "https://www.qbtech.com/blog/clinicians-guide-to-adhd-in-2025/",

        "text": "Approximately 50% of these diagnoses were made in adulthood. Additionally, a 2024 study reports that 7.1 million US children (around 11.4%) have received an ADHD diagnosis. In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) ...\n\nTaipale et al., (2024) found that taking ADHD medication helped to significantly lower a patient\u2019s risk of suicidal behavior and hospitalization (for both psychiatric and non-psychiatric reasons). ... New research into treatment effects is positive for clinicians and patients and shows the tangible benefits treatment can offer. It also evidences the need for ongoing research in the area, coupled with continuous education, and adding to published guidelines, to ensure that prescribing clinicians are aware of new developments. Despite these encouraging findings, clinicians and patients continue to face challenges from ongoing medication shortages. CDC data from 2024 shows that 71.5% of US adults taking stimulant medication had difficulty filling their prescriptions in the previous year due to availability issues. Further research identifying suitable medication alternatives and further action to minimize the impacts of shortages is crucial.\n\nRecent data from the National Center for Health Statistics indicates that 15.5 million US adults had received an ADHD diagnosis by late 2023. Approximately 50% of these diagnoses were made in adulthood. Additionally, a 2024 study reports that 7.1 million US children (around 11.4%) have received an ADHD diagnosis. In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) estimates that around 5% of children and 3-4% of UK adults have ADHD. ADHD was once thought of as a childhood condition, yet its prevalence and new diagnoses amongst adults demonstrates the lifelong nature of the condition. As more people become aware of symptoms, the identification of adults with ADHD is likely to continue. Our understanding of the different presentations of ADHD is continuing to develop. We know that women and girls are less likely to be diagnosed with ADHD than men and boys.\n\nLater this year, the American Professional Society of ADHD and Related Disorders (APSARD) will publish guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of ADHD in adults \u2013 a much-needed resource for clinicians. In the UK, 2024 saw the publication of a significant new Diagnostic Guideline by the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE). The guidance provides clarity on ADHD assessment best practices by recommending the use of an objective test (QbTest) as part of the diagnostic process in children and young people aged 6-17. Sweden\u2019s National Board of Health and Welfare also published significant new guidelines on ADHD and autism in 2024. Clinicians have a difficult role in assessing patients for ADHD and these guidelines provide critical advice. Such guidance also supports a more consistent approach to ADHD evaluation, which can help multi-site clinics in establishing standardized models of care.\n\nThere is an established body of clinical research into the use of objective measures in ADHD evaluation and diagnosis. However, in 2024, two new studies highlighted the further usefulness of objective testing technologies for clinicians. Sanyal et al., highlighted the potential use of objective tests like QbCheck, to aid treatment decisions, monitor the impact of ADHD medications on symptoms, and optimize treatments accordingly., monitor the impact of ADHD medications on symptoms, and optimize treatments accordingly. Grossman and Berger suggest that the inclusion of a computerized test during assessment can increase patient trust in clinician decisions and also increase their intent to follow treatment recommendations. Public awareness of ADHD is greater now than ever before. This new era of awareness has presented opportunities and challenges. Social media has become a giant platform for discussion around ADHD.",

        "text": "During 2020\u20132022, the prevalence of ever diagnosed attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was 11.3% in children ages 5\u201317 years, with boys (14.5%) having a higher prevalence than girls (8.0%). Children ages 5\u201311 years were less likely than children ages 12\u201317 years to have ADHD.\n\nDuring 2020\u20132022, the prevalence of ever diagnosed attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was 11.3% in children ages 5\u201317 years, with boys (14.5%) having a higher prevalence than girls (8.0%). Children ages 5\u201311 years were less likely than children ages 12\u201317 years to have ADHD. White non-Hispanic children ages 5\u201317 years were more likely to have ADHD (13.4%) than Black non-Hispanic (10.8%) and Hispanic (8.9%) children. The prevalence of ADHD decreased as the level of family income increased. Children with public (14.4%) or private (9.7%) health insurance were more likely to have ADHD than children without insurance (6.3%). ... Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is one of the most common mental disorders of childhood (1). It is characterized by a pattern of inattention, impulsivity, or hyperactivity that leads to functional impairment experienced in multiple settings (2).\n\nBoys (14.5%) were more likely than girls (8.0%) to have ADHD, and this pattern was seen for children ages 5\u201311 years and 12\u201317 years. Among both boys and girls, the prevalence of ADHD was lower in children ages 5\u201311 years (8.6%) than in those ages 12\u201317 years (14.3%). Figure 1. Prevalence of children ages 5\u201317 years ever diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, by age group and sex: United States, 2020\u20132022 \u00b7 1Significantly different from girls (p < 0.05). 2Significantly different from children ages 12\u201317 years (p < 0.05). NOTES: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder is based on a response to the question, \u201cHas a doctor or other health professional ever told you that [sample child] had Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder or ADHD or Attention-Deficit Disorder or ADD?\u201d Estimates are based on household interviews of a sample of the civilian noninstitutionalized population.\n\nData table for Figure 1. Prevalence of children ages 5\u201317 years ever diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, by age group and sex: United States, 2020\u20132022 \u00b7 NOTES: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder is based on a response to the question, \u201cHas a doctor or other health professional ever told you that [sample child] had Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder or ADHD or Attention-Deficit Disorder or ADD?\u201d Estimates are based on household interviews of a sample of the civilian noninstitutionalized population. SOURCE: National Center for Health Statistics, National Health Interview Survey, 2020\u20132022. Among children ages 5\u201317 years, White non-Hispanic (subsequently, White) children were more likely to have ever been diagnosed with ADHD (13.4%) than Black non-Hispanic (subsequently, Black) (10.8%) and Hispanic (8.9%) children (Figure 2, Table 2).\n\nAmong children ages 5\u201311 years, White children were more likely than Hispanic children to have ADHD; however, observed differences among Black children and either White or Hispanic children were not significant. Among children ages 12\u201317 years, the prevalence of ADHD was higher among White children (17.0%) than for Black (13.0%) and Hispanic (11.7%) children. The prevalence of ADHD was higher in children ages 12\u201317 years than in children ages 5\u201311 years for all race and Hispanic-origin groups. Figure 2.         "text": "Among children with current ADHD, ... demographic and clinical subgroups (n=45,169). Approximately 1 in 9 U.S. children have ever received an ADHD diagnosis (11.4%, 7.1 million children) and 10.5% (6.5 million) had current ADHD....\n\nIssue date 2024 May-Jun. ... To provide updated national prevalence estimates of diagnosed attention-deficit/ hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), ADHD severity, co-occurring disorders, and receipt of ADHD medication and behavioral treatment among U.S. children and adolescents by demographic and clinical subgroups using data from the 2022 National Survey of Children\u2019s Health (NSCH). This study used 2022 NSCH data to estimate the prevalence of ever diagnosed and current ADHD among U.S. children aged 3\u201317 years. Among children with current ADHD, ADHD severity, presence of current co-occurring disorders, and receipt of medication and behavioral treatment were estimated. Weighted estimates were calculated overall and for demographic and clinical subgroups (n=45,169). Approximately 1 in 9 U.S. children have ever received an ADHD diagnosis (11.4%, 7.1 million children) and 10.5% (6.5 million) had current ADHD.\n\nJ Clin Child Adolesc Psychol. Author manuscript; available in PMC: 2025 May 22. Published in final edited form as: J Clin Child Adolesc Psychol. 2024 May 22;53(3):343\u2013360. doi: 10.1080/15374416.2024.2335625 ... 1National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Atlanta, GA \u00b7 Find articles by Melissa L Danielson 1, Angelika H Claussen \u00b7 1National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Atlanta, GA \u00b7 Find articles by Angelika H Claussen 1, Rebecca H Bitsko \u00b7 1National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Atlanta, GA \u00b7 Find articles by Rebecca H Bitsko 1, Samuel M Katz \u00b7 1National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Atlanta, GA \u00b7\n\nHowever, the prevalence ratio of ever diagnosed ADHD among Hispanic children was closer to 1 in 2022 (0.78, 95% CI: 0.66\u20130.92) than in 2016 (0.65, 95% CI: 0.54\u20130.79) (Danielson, Bitsko, et al., 2018), suggesting a possible narrowing of gaps between Hispanic and non-Hispanic children. Published data on estimates of ADHD among Asian children and for other racial and ethnic groups are limited and do not allow for similar exploration of changes over time (Bitsko et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2021). Otherwise, patterns of prevalence across sociodemographic groups were generally consistent with previous data (Bitsko et al., 2022; Danielson, Bitsko, et al., 2018; Pastor et al., 2015; Perou et al., 2013; Zablotsky & Black, 2020; Zablotsky et al., 2019).         "text": "     1\t---\n     2\tname: xlsx\n     3\tdescription: \"Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path \u2014 even casually (like \\\"the xlsx in my downloads\\\") \u2014 and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.\"\n     4\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     5\t---\n     6\t\n     7\t# Requirements for Outputs\n     8\t\n     9\t## All Excel files\n    10\t\n    11\t### Professional Font\n    12\t- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user\n    13\t\n    14\t### Zero Formula Errors\n    15\t- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)\n    16\t\n    17\t### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)\n    18\t- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files\n    19\t- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns\n    20\t- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines\n    21\t\n    22\t## Financial models\n    23\t\n    24\t### Color Coding Standards\n    25\tUnless otherwise stated by the user or existing template\n    26\t\n    27\t#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions\n    28\t- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios\n    29\t- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations\n    30\t- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook\n    31\t- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files\n    32\t- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated\n    33\t\n    34\t### Number Formatting Standards\n    35\t\n    36\t#### Required Format Rules\n    37\t- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., \"2024\" not \"2,024\")\n    38\t- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers (\"Revenue ($mm)\")\n    39\t- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros \"-\", including percentages (e.g., \"$#,##0;($#,##0);-\")\n    40\t- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)\n    41\t- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)\n    42\t- **Negative numbers**: Use parentheses (123) not minus -123\n    43\t\n    44\t### Formula Construction Rules\n    45\t\n    46\t#### Assumptions Placement\n    47\t- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells\n    48\t- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas\n    49\t- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05\n    50\t\n    51\t#### Formula Error Prevention\n    52\t- Verify all cell references are correct\n    53\t- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges\n    54\t- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods\n    55\t- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)\n    56\t- Verify no unintended circular references\n    57\t\n    58\t#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes\n    59\t- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: \"Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]\"\n    60\t- Examples:\n    61\t  - \"Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    62\t  - \"Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    63\t  - \"Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity\"\n    64\t  - \"Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen\"\n    65\t\n    66\t# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis\n    67\t\n    68\t## Overview\n    69\t\n    70\tA user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.\n    71\t\n    72\t## Important Requirements\n    73\t\n    74\t**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)\n    75\t\n    76\t## Reading and analyzing data\n    77\t\n    78\t### Data analysis with pandas\n    79\tFor data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:\n    80\t\n    81\t```python\n    82\timport pandas as pd\n    83\t\n    84\t# Read Excel\n    85\tdf = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet\n    86\tall_sheets = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name=None)  # All sheets as dict\n    87\t\n    88\t# Analyze\n    89\tdf.head()      # Preview data\n    90\tdf.info()      # Column info\n    91\tdf.describe()  # Statistics\n    92\t\n    93\t# Write Excel\n    94\tdf.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)\n    95\t```\n    96\t\n    97\t## Excel File Workflows\n    98\t\n    99\t## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values\n   100\t\n   101\t**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.\n   102\t\n   103\t### \u274c WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values\n   104\t```python\n   105\t# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result\n   106\ttotal = df['Sales'].sum()\n   107\tsheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000\n   108\t\n   109\t# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python\n   110\tgrowth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']\n   111\tsheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15\n   112\t\n   113\t# Bad: Python calculation for average\n   114\tavg = sum(values) / len(values)\n   115\tsheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5\n   116\t```\n   117\t\n   118\t### \u2705 CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas\n   119\t```python\n   120\t# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum\n   121\tsheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'\n   122\t\n   123\t# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula\n   124\tsheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'\n   125\t\n   126\t# Good: Average using Excel function\n   127\tsheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'\n   128\t```\n   129\t\n   130\tThis applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.\n   131\t\n   132\t## Common Workflow\n   133\t1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting\n   134\t2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file\n   135\t3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting\n   136\t4. **Save**: Write to file\n   137\t5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script\n   138\t   ```bash\n   139\t   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx\n   140\t   ```\n   141\t6. **Verify and fix any errors**: \n   142\t   - The script returns JSON with error details\n   143\t   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations\n   144\t   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again\n   145\t   - Common errors to fix:\n   146\t     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references\n   147\t     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero\n   148\t     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula\n   149\t     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name\n   150\t\n   151\t### Creating new Excel files\n   152\t\n   153\t```python\n   154\t# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting\n   155\tfrom openpyxl import Workbook\n   156\tfrom openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment\n   157\t\n   158\twb = Workbook()\n   159\tsheet = wb.active\n   160\t\n   161\t# Add data\n   162\tsheet['A1'] = 'Hello'\n   163\tsheet['B1'] = 'World'\n   164\tsheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])\n   165\t\n   166\t# Add formula\n   167\tsheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'\n   168\t\n   169\t# Formatting\n   170\tsheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')\n   171\tsheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')\n   172\tsheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')\n   173\t\n   174\t# Column width\n   175\tsheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20\n   176\t\n   177\twb.save('output.xlsx')\n   178\t```\n   179\t\n   180\t### Editing existing Excel files\n   181\t\n   182\t```python\n   183\t# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting\n   184\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   185\t\n   186\t# Load existing file\n   187\twb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')\n   188\tsheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet\n   189\t\n   190\t# Working with multiple sheets\n   191\tfor sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:\n   192\t    sheet = wb[sheet_name]\n   193\t    print(f\"Sheet: {sheet_name}\")\n   194\t\n   195\t# Modify cells\n   196\tsheet['A1'] = 'New Value'\n   197\tsheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2\n   198\tsheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3\n   199\t\n   200\t# Add new sheet\n   201\tnew_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')\n   202\tnew_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'\n   203\t\n   204\twb.save('modified.xlsx')\n   205\t```\n   206\t\n   207\t## Recalculating formulas\n   208\t\n   209\tExcel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:\n   210\t\n   211\t```bash\n   212\tpython scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tExample:\n   216\t```bash\n   217\tpython scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30\n   218\t```\n   219\t\n   220\tThe script:\n   221\t- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run\n   222\t- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets\n   223\t- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)\n   224\t- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts\n   225\t- Works on both Linux and macOS\n   226\t\n   227\t## Formula Verification Checklist\n   228\t\n   229\tQuick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:\n   230\t\n   231\t### Essential Verification\n   232\t- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model\n   233\t- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)\n   234\t- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)\n   235\t\n   236\t### Common Pitfalls\n   237\t- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`\n   238\t- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ \n   239\t- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first\n   240\t- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)\n   241\t- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)\n   242\t- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets\n   243\t\n   244\t### Formula Testing Strategy\n   245\t- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly\n   246\t- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist\n   247\t- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values\n   248\t\n   249\t### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output\n   250\tThe script returns JSON with error details:\n   251\t```json\n   252\t{\n   253\t  \"status\": \"success\",           // or \"errors_found\"\n   254\t  \"total_errors\": 0,              // Total error count\n   255\t  \"total_formulas\": 42,           // Number of formulas in file\n   256\t  \"error_summary\": {              // Only present if errors found\n   257\t    \"#REF!\": {\n   258\t      \"count\": 2,\n   259\t      \"locations\": [\"Sheet1!B5\", \"Sheet1!C10\"]\n   260\t    }\n   261\t  }\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t## Best Practices\n   266\t\n   267\t### Library Selection\n   268\t- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export\n   269\t- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features\n   270\t\n   271\t### Working with openpyxl\n   272\t- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)\n   273\t- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`\n   274\t- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost\n   275\t- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing\n   276\t- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values\n   277\t\n   278\t### Working with pandas\n   279\t- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`\n   280\t- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`\n   281\t- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`\n   282\t\n   283\t## Code Style Guidelines\n   284\t**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:\n   285\t- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments\n   286\t- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations\n   287\t- Avoid unnecessary print statements\n   288\t\n   289\t**For Excel files themselves**:\n   290\t- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions\n   291\t- Document data sources for hardcoded values\n 'Start with one thing' implementation guide.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"6.2\", \"Executive Function Skill Building\",\n     \"Executive function deficits are the CORE challenge of ADHD. Key EF skills to build: (1) Working memory \u2014 external memory aids (written lists, phone reminders, visual cues). (2) Time management \u2014 timers, visual time tools, 'time estimation' practice. (3) Organization \u2014 systems that are simple enough to maintain (the '2-minute rule'). (4) Planning/prioritizing \u2014 break projects into steps, use planners/apps. (5) Task initiation \u2014 'just do the first step,' body doubling, accountability partners. (6) Emotional regulation \u2014 identify triggers, use coping strategies, self-advocacy for breaks. (7) Flexible thinking \u2014 practice plan B thinking. (8) Self-monitoring \u2014 learn to recognize when attention has drifted. Programs: Unstuck and on Target, Understood.org EF guides, CHADD EF resources.\",\n     \"Parents\\nTherapist\\nSchool\", \"OT\\nExecutive function coach\",\n     \"None formal. EF assessment results from school evaluation (BRIEF-2) helpful.\",\n     \"Ongoing \u2014 EF skills develop through mid-20s in ADHD (delayed maturation).\",\n     \"EF skills taught in isolated context \u2014 don't generalize to daily life. School and home not coordinating EF approach. Child resistant to 'more strategies.' Parent doing EF work FOR child instead of coaching. Developmental expectations don't account for ADHD EF delay (30% maturity gap).\",\n     \"EF skill builder organized by age and specific deficit. Understood.org EF tool integration. Home-school EF coordination template. Age-appropriate independence progression guide (accounting for ADHD 30% maturity gap). EF app and tool recommendations by age.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 7: CO-OCCURRING CONDITIONS\n    [\"7\\nCo-occurring\\nConditions &\\nComplex ADHD\", \"7.1\", \"Screen & Address Anxiety (Co-occurs ~40%)\",\n     \"Anxiety co-occurs with ADHD in approximately 40% of children. Can be: (1) Primary anxiety + ADHD (both independent conditions). (2) Anxiety CAUSED by ADHD experiences (academic failure, social rejection, constant criticism). (3) ADHD symptoms that LOOK like anxiety (restlessness, difficulty concentrating, avoidance). Treatment: anxiety may need to be treated first or alongside ADHD. SSRIs for anxiety can be combined with stimulants for ADHD. CBT for anxiety is first-line behavioral treatment. School accommodations should address BOTH conditions.\",\n     \"Pediatrician / Psychiatrist /\\nPsychologist\", \"Therapist\\nSchool\",\n     \"Anxiety screening (GAD-7, SCARED). Anxiety causes school avoidance that is attributed to ADHD avoidance. CBT for anxiety conflicts with ADHD behavioral strategies. Child on multiple medications \u2014 complex interactions.\",\n     \"ADHD + anxiety co-occurrence screening tool. Medication interaction checker for combined treatment. School accommodation merger (ADHD + anxiety accommodations). CBT + ADHD behavioral strategy integration guide.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"7.2\", \"Screen & Address Learning Disabilities (Co-occurs ~30-50%)\",\n     \"Learning disabilities co-occur with ADHD in 30-50% of children. Most common: dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, processing disorders. CRITICAL: ADHD medication improves attention but does NOT remediate LD \u2014 both must be addressed separately. Child may have IEP for LD and accommodate for ADHD, or IEP covering both. Evaluation should test academic skills AT CURRENT GRADE LEVEL. Watch for: child whose reading/math/writing doesn't improve with ADHD treatment alone, significant discrepancy between ability and achievement, specific academic areas of persistent difficulty.\",\n     \"School Psychologist /\\nNeuropsychologist\", \"School\\nPediatrician\",\n     \"Comprehensive psychoeducational evaluation.
