Private: 2-8 weeks.\",\n     \"\u2605 LD MISSED: ADHD medication helps attention \u2014 everyone assumes academics will improve. They don't. Underlying LD never identified. Child labeled 'not trying hard enough' despite medication. LD evaluation not requested because 'we already know it's ADHD.' School attributes academic difficulties to ADHD alone. Gifted + ADHD + LD (triple exceptional) \u2014 all three mask each other.\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL: 'ADHD medication helping but grades still struggling?' \u2014 automatic LD screening prompt. ADHD + LD co-occurrence guide for parents. Academic progress tracker post-medication to identify persistent deficits. LD evaluation request template. SLD Journey Map cross-reference.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"7.3\", \"Screen & Address Behavioral/Conduct Problems (Co-occurs ~50%)\",\n     \"Behavioral or conduct problems co-occur with ADHD in approximately 50% of children. Includes: ODD (Oppositional Defiant Disorder) \u2014 approximately 40% of ADHD children. Conduct Disorder \u2014 approximately 15-20% of ADHD children (more common in adolescence). ADHD + ODD/CD significantly increases risk for: school discipline, substance use, justice system involvement. Key: distinguish ADHD impulsivity from intentional defiance. ADHD impulsivity = acting without thinking. ODD defiance = deliberate opposition. Treatment: behavioral parent training effective for both. Some medications help both ADHD and aggression (guanfacine, risperidone for severe cases).\",\n     \"Psychiatrist /\\nPsychologist\", \"School\\nBehavioral therapist\",\n     \"Behavioral assessment. Child disciplined/suspended for ADHD-driven behaviors. Zero tolerance policies disproportionately affect ADHD children. School-to-prison pipeline risk for ADHD + conduct issues. Medication alone insufficient for combined ADHD + ODD.\",\n     \"ADHD impulsivity vs ODD defiance differentiation guide. Discipline record tracker with manifestation determination guidance. Behavioral parent training program finder. School discipline rights guide for ADHD families. FBA request template specifically addressing ADHD + behavioral concerns.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"7.4\", \"Address Emotional Dysregulation\",\n     \"Emotional dysregulation is increasingly recognized as a CORE feature of ADHD, not just a co-occurring condition. Includes: intense emotional reactions disproportionate to situation, difficulty calming down once upset, low frustration tolerance, rejection sensitivity ('Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria' \u2014 not a formal diagnosis but widely discussed), rapid mood shifts, emotional impulsivity (saying hurtful things in the moment). Treatment: emotional regulation skills training, CBT, medication (some children respond to guanfacine or atomoxetine for emotional regulation), dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) skills for adolescents.\",\n     \"Therapist\\nPsychiatrist\", \"School\\nParent\",\n     \"Emotional regulation assessment. (4) Annual medication 'holiday' consideration (controversial \u2014 AAP does not recommend routine drug holidays). (5) Transition from pediatric to adult prescriber. Medication stops working (tolerance or dose needs adjustment) \u2014 parent assumes ADHD is untreatable. Adolescent medication non-compliance. Provider shortage for stimulant prescribers. AAP Clinical Practice Guideline (2019) recommends: evaluation for ADHD in children 4-18 with academic/behavioral problems and symptoms of inattention, hyperactivity, or impulsivity. Pediatrician should use validated rating scales (Vanderbilt, Conners, SNAP-IV) completed by BOTH parent AND teacher. Pediatrician reviews developmental/medical history, considers other conditions (anxiety, sleep disorders, thyroid, vision/hearing).\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / PCP\\\", \\\"School (teacher rating scales)\\\",\\n     \\\"Parent rating scale (Vanderbilt Parent). Teacher rating scale (Vanderbilt Teacher). Developmental history. School records/report cards.\\\",\\n     \\\"Can be initiated at any well-child visit. Evaluation may take 1-3 visits depending on complexity.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician dismisses concerns ('just a boy thing,' 'they'll grow out of it'). Only parent rating collected, no teacher input. Brief office visit insufficient for thorough evaluation. Pediatrician jumps to medication without full evaluation. Co-occurring conditions (anxiety, learning disabilities) not screened. Parent pressured to medicate or pressured NOT to medicate by conflicting family opinions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Vanderbilt rating scale auto-score and interpretation tool. Multi-informant comparison (parent vs teacher ratings). AAP evaluation checklist for pediatrician visits. Co-occurring conditions screening prompts. Visit preparation guide: what to bring, what to ask.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 2: EVALUATION & DIAGNOSIS\\n    [\\\"2\\\\nEvaluation\\\\n& Diagnosis\\\", \\\"2.1\\\", \\\"Comprehensive ADHD Evaluation\\\",\\n     \\\"Full evaluation per AAP guidelines should include: (1) DSM-5 criteria assessment \\u2014 6+ symptoms of inattention and/or 6+ hyperactivity-impulsivity symptoms, present before age 12, in 2+ settings, for 6+ months. (2) Validated rating scales from multiple informants (parent, teacher, child if older). (3) Developmental and medical history. (4) Assessment of functional impairment. (5) Screening for co-occurring conditions (78% of children with ADHD have at least one: anxiety ~40%, behavior/conduct problems ~50%, learning disabilities ~30-50%, depression, autism, Tourette syndrome). (6) Rule out other causes. Can be conducted by: pediatrician, child psychiatrist, psychologist, neuropsychologist, developmental pediatrician.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician OR\\\\nSpecialist\\\", \\\"School (teacher data)\\\\nPsychologist (if comprehensive eval)\\\",\\n     \\\"Completed rating scales (parent + teacher). Medical records. School records. Developmental history questionnaire. Insurance pre-auth if specialist referral.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician: 1-3 visits. Specialist wait: 3-12 months for dev ped, 1-4 months for psychologist. Private neuropsych: 2-6 weeks but $$.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 EVALUATION GAPS: Diagnosis based solely on brief office visit without teacher input. 3 DSM-5 presentations (Predominantly Inattentive, Predominantly Hyperactive-Impulsive, Combined) not differentiated \\u2014 affects treatment. Co-occurring conditions missed (especially anxiety, which mimics and co-occurs with ADHD in ~40% of cases). Learning disabilities not screened \\u2014 child gets ADHD diagnosis but underlying LD unaddressed. IQ testing not done \\u2014 2e children missed. Evaluation focuses on deficits rather than also documenting strengths.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: ADHD evaluation completeness checker \\u2014 were rating scales collected from 2+ informants? Were co-occurring conditions screened? DSM-5 presentation type identifier. Insurance coverage checker for evaluation types. Specialist waitlist tracker. Evaluation preparation kit for parents.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"2.2\\\", \\\"Receive ADHD Diagnosis & Understand Presentation\\\",\\n     \\\"DSM-5 recognizes three ADHD presentations: (1) Predominantly Inattentive (formerly ADD) \\u2014 difficulty sustaining attention, organizing, following through, often overlooked in girls. (2) Predominantly Hyperactive-Impulsive \\u2014 fidgeting, excessive talking, difficulty waiting, acting without thinking. (3) Combined Presentation \\u2014 meets criteria for both. ADHD severity: Mild, Moderate, Severe. 2022 NSCH data: among 6.5M children with current ADHD, 36% mild, 52% moderate, 12% severe. Key: ADHD is a RECOGNIZED DSM-5 diagnosis (314.00/314.01) with established diagnostic codes \\u2014 unlike PDA, this is widely accepted by clinicians, insurance, and schools.\\\",\\n     \\\"Diagnosing Provider\\\", \\\"None\\\",\\n     \\\"Completed evaluation. Diagnostic report with DSM-5 code, presentation type, severity, and co-occurring conditions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Diagnosis typically within 1-3 visits after initial evaluation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Diagnosis documentation insufficient for school/insurance needs. Presentation type not specified. Co-occurring conditions not documented. Parent receives diagnosis without adequate explanation of what ADHD is and isn't. Grief, guilt, relief \\u2014 complex emotional response not addressed. Misinformation from social media about ADHD.\\\",\\n     \\\"Diagnosis documentation organizer \\u2014 ensures report includes all elements needed for IEP/504 and insurance. ADHD presentation type explainer for parents. 'What ADHD is and isn't' evidence-based resource. Emotional support resources at diagnosis. Link to CHADD, ADDitude, CDC materials.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 3: TREATMENT PLANNING\\n    [\\\"3\\\\nTreatment\\\\nPlanning\\\", \\\"3.1\\\", \\\"Develop Multimodal Treatment Plan\\\",\\n     \\\"AAP recommends multimodal approach: (1) For ages 4-5: behavioral therapy FIRST, medication only if therapy insufficient. (2) For ages 6-11: BOTH medication AND behavioral therapy (gold standard). (3) For ages 12+: medication with consent of adolescent, behavioral therapy recommended. Treatment components: ADHD medication, behavioral parent training, school accommodations/interventions, organizational skills training, social skills support, executive function coaching. Evidence base: MTA study (largest ADHD treatment study) showed combination of medication + behavioral therapy produces best outcomes.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / Psychiatrist\\\", \\\"Therapist\\\\nSchool\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD diagnosis documentation. Discussion of treatment options. Informed consent for medication if applicable.\\\",\\n     \\\"Treatment plan should be developed within weeks of diagnosis.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 TREATMENT TRAPS: Medication prescribed without behavioral component. Behavioral therapy recommended without medication for moderate-severe ADHD in school-age children (undertreated). Only medication OR only therapy when both are needed. Family opposed to medication based on stigma/misinformation \\u2014 child undertreated. Medication prescribed without monitoring plan. No school accommodations discussed alongside clinical treatment.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Multimodal treatment planner \\u2014 based on age and severity, recommends AAP-aligned combination. Treatment decision guide: behavioral therapy, medication, both \\u2014 with evidence for each. Medication vs behavioral therapy comparison by age group. Treatment monitoring schedule generator. CHADD parent training course link.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"3.2\\\", \\\"Navigate ADHD Medication (If Applicable)\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD medications: STIMULANTS (first-line, 70-80% response rate): methylphenidate (Ritalin, Concerta, Focalin) and amphetamine (Adderall, Vyvanse, Dexedrine). Short-acting (4 hrs), intermediate (6-8 hrs), long-acting (10-14 hrs). NON-STIMULANTS (second-line): atomoxetine (Strattera), guanfacine (Intuniv), clonidine (Kapvay), viloxazine (Qelbree). Process: start low, titrate slowly, monitor for efficacy and side effects. Common side effects: appetite suppression, sleep difficulties, mood changes. Medication shortage has been ongoing issue (FDA Adderall shortage 2022-2024).\\\",\\n     \\\"Prescribing Provider\\\\n(Pediatrician/Psychiatrist)\\\", \\\"Pharmacy\\\\nInsurance\\\",\\n     \\\"Prescription. Prior authorization if required. Growth chart tracking. Side effect monitoring checklist.\\\",\\n     \\\"Titration: 4-8 weeks to find optimal dose. Follow-up: monthly initially, then every 3-6 months.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 MEDICATION CHALLENGES: Medication shortage \\u2014 71.5% of adults on stimulants had difficulty filling prescriptions (2024 CDC data). Prior authorization delays for specific medications. Generic vs brand-name differences. Side effects cause parents to discontinue without physician guidance. Medication doesn't cover full school day (wearing off). Diversion/misuse concerns at school. Medication stigma. Growth suppression concerns require monitoring.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medication tracking log (dose, time, efficacy, side effects). Insurance formulary checker \\u2014 is preferred medication covered? Prior auth template for specific ADHD medications. Side effect vs therapeutic effect monitoring tool. Medication shortage alert system. Growth chart tracker. Medication administration plan for school.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"3.3\\\", \\\"Behavioral Parent Training & Family Strategies\\\",\\n     \\\"Evidence-based parent training programs: CHADD Parent-to-Parent (free, peer-led), Triple P (Positive Parenting Program), Parent-Child Interaction Therapy (PCIT, ages 2-7), Incredible Years. Key strategies: (1) Positive reinforcement systems (specific praise, token economies). (2) Clear, consistent expectations with visual supports. (3) Environmental modifications (reduce distractions, organize spaces). (4) Routine/structure with flexibility. (5) Break tasks into smaller steps. (6) Movement breaks. (7) Emotional coaching for dysregulation. (8) Collaborative problem-solving (Ross Greene's 'The Explosive Child' approach). Russell Barkley's 'Taking Charge of ADHD' widely recommended.\\\",\\n     \\\"Therapist / CHADD\\\\nParent Training\\\", \\\"Pediatrician\\\\nSchool\\\",\\n     \\\"Referral to behavioral therapist or parent training program. Insurance authorization.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parent training: typically 8-16 sessions. Ongoing at home.\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance doesn't cover parent training programs. CHADD chapters not available in all areas. Waitlists for behavioral therapists. Parent burnout from implementing strategies consistently. Co-parent/family disagreement on approach. Strategies that work for typical ADHD may not work for ADHD + co-occurring conditions.\\\",\\n     \\\"CHADD chapter finder by ZIP. Parent training program locator. CHADD free online course registration link. Evidence-based strategy library organized by challenge type. Home environment modification checklist. Daily routine builder with ADHD-friendly structure. Recommended book list with reading guides.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 4: SCHOOL SUPPORTS\\n    [\\\"4\\\\nSchool\\\\nSupports\\\\n(IEP/504)\\\", \\\"4.1\\\", \\\"Request School District Evaluation\\\",\\n     \\\"Parent sends written request for special education evaluation. ADHD qualifies under IEP category 'Other Health Impairment' (OHI) \\u2014 specifically, ADHD causes 'limited alertness with respect to the educational environment' or 'heightened alertness to environmental stimuli' that results in limited alertness to educational tasks. IMPORTANT: ADHD diagnosis alone does NOT guarantee IEP eligibility \\u2014 must demonstrate that ADHD adversely affects educational performance. Educational performance includes academics AND non-academic domains (social, behavioral, emotional regulation, organizational). OSEP has clarified 'educational performance' is NOT limited to grades.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician (documentation)\\\",\\n     \\\"Written assessment request letter. ADHD diagnosis documentation. Medical records. Report cards, teacher observations, behavioral data.\\\",\\n     \\\"District: 15 days to respond with assessment plan. 60 days from consent to completed assessment.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 SCHOOL TRAP: District says child's grades are too good for IEP ('A/B student doesn't need services'). OSEP has clarified that grades alone don't determine eligibility \\u2014 but many districts still use this gatekeeping. District claims ADHD is 'medical, not educational.' District refuses OHI eligibility because child doesn't have physical health impairment (wrong \\u2014 ADHD is specifically listed under OHI). Assessment only tests academics, misses behavioral/social/organizational impact. School psychologist doesn't assess executive functioning.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Assessment request letter generator for ADHD \\u2014 cites OHI eligibility and OSEP guidance on 'educational performance.' ADHD-specific evaluation request checklist (must include: executive function, behavioral observation, teacher input, functional impact). OSEP letter references for 'grades don't determine eligibility.' Pre-filled parent input form highlighting ADHD-specific challenges.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.2\\\", \\\"School District Evaluation \\u2014 ADHD-Focused\\\",\\n     \\\"Assessment plan should include: psychoeducational testing (to identify co-occurring LD), behavioral/social-emotional assessment (BASC-3, Conners teacher rating), executive function evaluation (BRIEF-2), classroom observation during structured AND unstructured activities, parent/teacher interviews, review of existing ADHD diagnosis and medical records. REQUEST SPECIFICALLY: (1) Executive function assessment \\u2014 critical for ADHD. (2) Behavioral observation during HIGH-DEMAND tasks. (3) Academic fluency testing (not just accuracy \\u2014 ADHD impacts speed/efficiency). (4) Social-emotional assessment across settings.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\\n(School Psychologist)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nTeacher\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"Signed assessment plan. All ADHD documentation. Rating scales. Outside evaluation reports.\\\",\\n     \\\"60 calendar days from consent.\\\",\\n     \\\"Executive function not assessed (BRIEF-2 not administered). Only academic testing done \\u2014 behavioral and organizational impact missed. Observation done during preferred activity (looks fine) not during high-demand task. Teacher report minimizes issues ('manageable with redirection'). Child with inattentive type appears compliant in structured observation. Assessment doesn't connect ADHD symptoms to functional impairment.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD-specific assessment plan review tool \\u2014 flags missing components. Executive function assessment advocacy guide. Template request for BRIEF-2 inclusion. Observation request specifying high-demand settings. Teacher questionnaire that captures ADHD-specific classroom challenges.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.3\\\", \\\"IEP Development \\u2014 ADHD-Specific Accommodations & Goals\\\",\\n     \\\"If eligible under OHI, IEP team develops plan. KEY ADHD ACCOMMODATIONS: (1) Preferential seating (reduced distractions). (2) Extended time on tests/assignments. (3) Breaks for movement/sensory regulation. (4) Chunked assignments with check-ins. (5) Written/visual instructions (not just verbal). (6) Organizational system support (binder checks, assignment tracking). (7) Reduced homework load. (8) Testing in separate quiet setting. (9) Daily report card/home-school communication. (10) Executive function coaching. (11) Medication administration plan. (12) Teacher training on ADHD. (13) Transition support between activities. IEP GOALS should target: executive functioning, self-regulation, organizational skills, self-advocacy, social skills \\u2014 NOT just 'paying attention' or 'staying in seat.'\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician\\\\nTherapist\\\",\\n     \\\"All assessment reports. ADHD accommodations list. Parent concerns letter. Medical documentation including medication schedule.\\\",\\n     \\\"30 days from completed assessment to IEP meeting.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IEP PITFALLS: Generic accommodations that don't address specific child's ADHD presentation. 'Extra time' and 'preferential seating' offered as only accommodations (insufficient). Behavior plan focuses on compliance rather than teaching self-regulation. Goals about 'staying on task' without addressing WHY (executive function deficit). Medication expected to be sole intervention \\u2014 school reduces support. After-medication 'rebound' period not addressed. Substitute teachers don't implement accommodations. IEP goals not updated to address executive function as child ages.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 ADHD IEP accommodation menu organized by presentation type (inattentive, hyperactive, combined). Sample IEP goals for executive function, self-regulation, organization, self-advocacy. 'Red flag' checker for insufficient IEP plans. Medication administration plan template. Daily report card templates. Teacher training request template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.4\\\", \\\"504 Plan Alternative (If IEP Denied or Preferred)\\\",\\n     \\\"Many children with ADHD receive 504 Plans rather than IEPs. 504 Plan may be appropriate when: ADHD impacts functioning but child doesn't need specialized instruction (just accommodations). Section 504 broader eligibility: disability that 'substantially limits one or more major life activities' (learning, concentrating, thinking, reading, communicating). 504 accommodations can include: extended time, preferential seating, movement breaks, reduced homework, organizational support, testing modifications, behavioral expectations adjustments. NOTE: 504 Plans do NOT include specialized instruction or related services \\u2014 if child needs those, IEP is more appropriate.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\\n(504 Coordinator)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD diagnosis documentation. 504 referral. Medical documentation. Teacher input on functional impact.\\\",\\n     \\\"No specific federal timeline \\u2014 'reasonable' time.\\\",\\n     \\\"504 Plan offered as lesser alternative when IEP is warranted. 504 Plan is generic (only extra time, preferential seating). No enforcement mechanism as strong as IEP. 504 Plan not updated annually unless parent requests. Staff inconsistently implement 504. Substitute teachers unaware of 504 accommodations.\\\",\\n     \\\"504 vs IEP decision guide for ADHD. 504 accommodation menu organized by ADHD presentation. 504 monitoring checklist. Annual 504 review reminder. Rights comparison: 504 vs IEP.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.5\\\", \\\"IEP/504 Dispute Resolution\\\",\\n     \\\"If district denies eligibility or provides inadequate supports: (1) Request another IEP/504 meeting. (2) Provide additional documentation. (3) Request Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense. (4) File state complaint with CDE (California). (5) Request mediation. (6) Due process complaint. Key ADHD arguments: cite OSEP guidance that 'educational performance' includes non-academic areas. Cite specific functional impairment data. Provide private evaluation documenting executive function deficits. Document failed RTI/classroom interventions.\\\",\\n     \\\"School District / CDE / OAH\\\", \\\"DRC\\\\nSpecial Ed Attorney\\\\nAdvocate\\\",\\n     \\\"Written disagreement. Prior Written Notice. Attorney fees prohibitive. Parent burned out from adversarial process.\\\",\\n     \\\"Dispute resolution decision tree for ADHD. Template disagreement letters citing OSEP guidance. IEE request template. DRC referral. COPAA attorney directory. OSEP letter database on ADHD eligibility.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 5: INSURANCE & THERAPY\\n    [\\\"5\\\\nInsurance &\\\\nTherapy\\\", \\\"5.1\\\", \\\"Insurance Authorization for ADHD Treatment\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD treatment coverage: Medication \\u2014 generally covered under prescription benefit (stimulants may require prior auth, step therapy). Behavioral therapy \\u2014 covered under mental health parity (MHPAEA). Psychological testing/evaluation \\u2014 coverage varies widely. Parent training \\u2014 often NOT covered. Key: Mental Health Parity Act requires equivalent coverage for mental health conditions including ADHD. Medi-Cal covers ADHD evaluation, medication, and behavioral therapy. Private insurance: check formulary for preferred medications, prior auth requirements.\\\",\\n     \\\"Private Insurance / Medi-Cal\\\", \\\"Pediatrician\\\\nTherapist\\\\nPharmacy\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD diagnosis. Prescription. Prior authorization forms. Treatment plan from provider.\\\",\\n     \\\"Prior auth: 5-15 business days. Formulary check: immediate.\\\",\\n     \\\"Preferred medication not on formulary \\u2014 step therapy required (try cheaper option first). Prior auth delays for stimulant medications. Behavioral therapy sessions limited. Neuropsychological testing not covered or limited. Medication shortage compounds insurance issues. Out-of-network provider costs for specialists. Telehealth restrictions for controlled substance prescriptions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance formulary checker for ADHD medications. Prior auth template for stimulant medications. Step therapy appeal template. Mental health parity rights guide. Medi-Cal ADHD benefit explainer. Out-of-network exception request for ADHD specialists.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"5.2\\\", \\\"Access Behavioral & Organizational Therapy\\\",\\n     \\\"Evidence-based therapies for ADHD: (1) Behavioral Parent Training (ages 4-12): most supported by evidence. (2) CBT for ADHD (ages 12+): addresses organizational skills, time management, emotional regulation. (3) Organizational Skills Training (OST): evidence-based for academic organization. (4) Social Skills Groups: address peer relationship difficulties common in ADHD. (5) Executive Function Coaching: not therapy per se, but growing field for building skills. (6) Occupational Therapy: for sensory, motor, and organizational needs. CHADD offers free/low-cost parent education programs including online courses.\\\",\\n     \\\"Behavioral Therapist\\\\nCHADD\\\", \\\"Insurance\\\\nSchool\\\\nOT\\\",\\n     \\\"Referral from provider. Insurance authorization. Treatment goals aligned with functional needs.\\\",\\n     \\\"Therapy: weekly for 8-20 sessions typically. Parent training: 8-16 sessions. Ongoing as needed.\\\",\\n     \\\"Long waitlists for ADHD-specialized therapists. Insurance limits sessions. Generic therapy not ADHD-specific. OT often denied for ADHD by insurance. Parent training programs not locally available. Executive function coaching not insurance-covered.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD therapist finder by ZIP and specialty. CHADD chapter and program locator. Insurance appeal template for OT. Online parent training options (CHADD, Triple P). Executive function coaching directory. Therapy type recommendation engine by age and challenge area.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 6: HOME & DAILY LIFE\\n    [\\\"6\\\\nHome &\\\\nDaily Life\\\\nManagement\\\", \\\"6.1\\\", \\\"Build ADHD-Friendly Home Environment\\\",\\n     \\\"Environmental modifications: (1) Dedicated homework station \\u2014 minimal distractions, organized supplies. (2) Visual schedules and routines (morning, after-school, bedtime). (3) Timer-based task systems (Pomodoro for older kids). (4) Movement opportunities built into day. (5) Consistent routines with advance warnings for transitions. (6) Organization systems (labeled bins, color-coded folders, landing pad for belongings). (7) Technology management (content filters, app timers, phone-free homework zones). (8) Sleep hygiene \\u2014 ADHD children often have sleep difficulties (medication timing, racing thoughts). (9) Nutrition support \\u2014 regular meals/snacks (especially important with stimulant appetite suppression). (10) 'Body doubling' for focus support.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents\\\", \\\"Therapist\\\\nOT\\\",\\n     \\\"None formal. Home organization supplies. Visual schedule materials.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 implement gradually, not all at once.\\\",\\n     \\\"Overwhelm from trying to change everything at once. System works initially then compliance fades. Technology battles. Sleep difficulties persist despite hygiene changes. Stimulant appetite suppression causes nutritional concerns. Sibling resentment about 'different rules.' Parent burnout from constant structure-building.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD home environment audit checklist. Room-by-room organization guide. Visual schedule templates (morning, homework, bedtime). Technology management plan by age. Sleep hygiene checklist for ADHD. Meal planning around medication schedule. 'Start with one thing' implementation guide.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"6.2\\\", \\\"Executive Function Skill Building\\\",\\n     \\\"Executive function deficits are the CORE challenge of ADHD. Key EF skills to build: (1) Working memory \\u2014 external memory aids (written lists, phone reminders, visual cues). (2) Time management \\u2014 timers, visual time tools, 'time estimation' practice. (3) Organization \\u2014 systems that are simple enough to maintain (the '2-minute rule'). (4) Planning/prioritizing \\u2014 break projects into steps, use planners/apps. (5) Task initiation \\u2014 'just do the first step,' body doubling, accountability partners. (6) Emotional regulation \\u2014 identify triggers, use coping strategies, self-advocacy for breaks. (7) Flexible thinking \\u2014 practice plan B thinking. (8) Self-monitoring \\u2014 learn to recognize when attention has drifted. Programs: Unstuck and on Target, Understood.org EF guides, CHADD EF resources.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents\\\\nTherapist\\\\nSchool\\\", \\\"OT\\\\nExecutive function coach\\\",\\n     \\\"None formal. EF assessment results from school evaluation (BRIEF-2) helpful.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 EF skills develop through mid-20s in ADHD (delayed maturation).\\\",\\n     \\\"EF skills taught in isolated context \\u2014 don't generalize to daily life. School and home not coordinating EF approach. Child resistant to 'more strategies.' Parent doing EF work FOR child instead of coaching. Developmental expectations don't account for ADHD EF delay (30% maturity gap).\\\",\\n     \\\"EF skill builder organized by age and specific deficit. Understood.org EF tool integration. Home-school EF coordination template. Age-appropriate independence progression guide (accounting for ADHD 30% maturity gap). EF app and tool recommendations by age.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 7: CO-OCCURRING CONDITIONS\\n    [\\\"7\\\\nCo-occurring\\\\nConditions &\\\\nComplex ADHD\\\", \\\"7.1\\\", \\\"Screen & Address Anxiety (Co-occurs ~40%)\\\",\\n     \\\"Anxiety co-occurs with ADHD in approximately 40% of children. Can be: (1) Primary anxiety + ADHD (both independent conditions). (2) Anxiety CAUSED by ADHD experiences (academic failure, social rejection, constant criticism). (3) ADHD symptoms that LOOK like anxiety (restlessness, difficulty concentrating, avoidance). Treatment: anxiety may need to be treated first or alongside ADHD. SSRIs for anxiety can be combined with stimulants for ADHD. CBT for anxiety is first-line behavioral treatment. School accommodations should address BOTH conditions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / Psychiatrist /\\\\nPsychologist\\\", \\\"Therapist\\\\nSchool\\\",\\n     \\\"Anxiety screening (GAD-7, SCARED). Differentiation assessment. Medication review if both conditions require pharmacological treatment.\\\",\\n     \\\"Screening at initial ADHD evaluation and ongoing. Treatment concurrent.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Anxiety masked by ADHD diagnosis \\u2014 provider treats only ADHD. Stimulant medication worsens anxiety in some children. Anxiety causes school avoidance that is attributed to ADHD avoidance. CBT for anxiety conflicts with ADHD behavioral strategies. Child on multiple medications \\u2014 complex interactions.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD + anxiety co-occurrence screening tool. Medication interaction checker for combined treatment. School accommodation merger (ADHD + anxiety accommodations). CBT + ADHD behavioral strategy integration guide.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.2\\\", \\\"Screen & Address Learning Disabilities (Co-occurs ~30-50%)\\\",\\n     \\\"Learning disabilities co-occur with ADHD in 30-50% of children. Most common: dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, processing disorders. CRITICAL: ADHD medication improves attention but does NOT remediate LD \\u2014 both must be addressed separately. Child may have IEP for LD and accommodate for ADHD, or IEP covering both. Evaluation should test academic skills AT CURRENT GRADE LEVEL. Watch for: child whose reading/math/writing doesn't improve with ADHD treatment alone, significant discrepancy between ability and achievement, specific academic areas of persistent difficulty.\\\",\\n     \\\"School Psychologist /\\\\nNeuropsychologist\\\", \\\"School\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"Comprehensive psychoeducational evaluation. Achievement testing. Cognitive testing. Cross-reference with ADHD evaluation.\\\",\\n     \\\"School: within IEP timeline. Private: 2-8 weeks.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 LD MISSED: ADHD medication helps attention \\u2014 everyone assumes academics will improve. They don't. Underlying LD never identified. Child labeled 'not trying hard enough' despite medication. LD evaluation not requested because 'we already know it's ADHD.' School attributes academic difficulties to ADHD alone. Gifted + ADHD + LD (triple exceptional) \\u2014 all three mask each other.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: 'ADHD medication helping but grades still struggling?' \\u2014 automatic LD screening prompt. ADHD + LD co-occurrence guide for parents. Academic progress tracker post-medication to identify persistent deficits. LD evaluation request template. SLD Journey Map cross-reference.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.3\\\", \\\"Screen & Address Behavioral/Conduct Problems (Co-occurs ~50%)\\\",\\n     \\\"Behavioral or conduct problems co-occur with ADHD in approximately 50% of children. Includes: ODD (Oppositional Defiant Disorder) \\u2014 approximately 40% of ADHD children. Conduct Disorder \\u2014 approximately 15-20% of ADHD children (more common in adolescence). ADHD + ODD/CD significantly increases risk for: school discipline, substance use, justice system involvement. Key: distinguish ADHD impulsivity from intentional defiance. ADHD impulsivity = acting without thinking. ODD defiance = deliberate opposition. Treatment: behavioral parent training effective for both. Some medications help both ADHD and aggression (guanfacine, risperidone for severe cases).\\\",\\n     \\\"Psychiatrist /\\\\nPsychologist\\\", \\\"School\\\\nBehavioral therapist\\\",\\n     \\\"Behavioral assessment. FBA if school-based. Conduct disorder screening if severe/persistent.\\\",\\n     \\\"Screening at ADHD evaluation and ongoing.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD impulsivity mislabeled as ODD/defiance. Child disciplined/suspended for ADHD-driven behaviors. Zero tolerance policies disproportionately affect ADHD children. School-to-prison pipeline risk for ADHD + conduct issues. Medication alone insufficient for combined ADHD + ODD.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD impulsivity vs ODD defiance differentiation guide. Discipline record tracker with manifestation determination guidance. Behavioral parent training program finder. School discipline rights guide for ADHD families. FBA request template specifically addressing ADHD + behavioral concerns.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.4\\\", \\\"Address Emotional Dysregulation\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional dysregulation is increasingly recognized as a CORE feature of ADHD, not just a co-occurring condition. Includes: intense emotional reactions disproportionate to situation, difficulty calming down once upset, low frustration tolerance, rejection sensitivity ('Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria' \\u2014 not a formal diagnosis but widely discussed), rapid mood shifts, emotional impulsivity (saying hurtful things in the moment). Treatment: emotional regulation skills training, CBT, medication (some children respond to guanfacine or atomoxetine for emotional regulation), dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) skills for adolescents.\\\",\\n     \\\"Therapist\\\\nPsychiatrist\\\", \\\"School\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional regulation assessment. Mood tracking. Evaluation for co-occurring mood disorders.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 skill building is long-term.\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional dysregulation misdiagnosed as bipolar disorder or mood disorder. Medication changes made for 'mood instability' when issue is ADHD emotional dysregulation. RSD causes social withdrawal and avoidance. Emotional outbursts at school lead to suspension/restraint. Parent doesn't recognize emotional component as part of ADHD.\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional dysregulation education resource for parents (it's ADHD, not just 'bad behavior'). Mood tracking tool. Coping skills library organized by age. RSD education and management strategies. School crisis plan template for emotional dysregulation. DBT skills app recommendations for teens.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 8: LONG-TERM & TRANSITION\\n    [\\\"8\\\\nLong-Term\\\\nPlanning &\\\\nTransition\\\", \\\"8.1\\\", \\\"Ongoing Medication Management & Monitoring\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD medication requires ongoing management: (1) Growth monitoring every 3-6 months (height, weight, BMI). (2) Medication efficacy review \\u2014 is current dose/medication still working? (3) Dosage adjustments as child grows. (4) Annual medication 'holiday' consideration (controversial \\u2014 AAP does not recommend routine drug holidays). (5) Transition from pediatric to adult prescriber. (6) Medication diversion prevention for stimulants. (7) Manage ongoing shortage issues. (8) Re-evaluation of medication need as child matures. 30.1% of children with current ADHD receive neither medication nor behavioral treatment \\u2014 significant treatment gap.\\\",\\n     \\\"Prescribing Provider\\\", \\\"Pharmacy\\\\nInsurance\\\\nSchool nurse\\\",\\n     \\\"Growth charts. Medication stops working (tolerance or dose needs adjustment) \\u2014 parent assumes ADHD is untreatable. Adolescent medication non-compliance. Provider shortage for stimulant prescribers. Pharmacy switches generic manufacturers \\u2014 different response.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medication monitoring dashboard (dose, growth, efficacy, side effects). Renewal reminder system. Rating scale repeat prompts. Growth trajectory tracker. Medication appointment preparation guide.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.2\\\", \\\"Annual IEP/504 Review \\u2014 ADHD-Specific Monitoring\\\",\\n     \\\"Annual IEP/504 review should assess: (1) Are accommodations still appropriate for current ADHD presentation? (2) Are IEP goals addressing executive function development? (3) Has medication change affected school needs? (4) Are co-occurring conditions (anxiety, LD) adequately addressed? (5) Is organizational support scaling appropriately with increasing academic demands? (6) Does child need more/fewer supports based on current functioning? Key: academic demands INCREASE each year while ADHD supports should EVOLVE, not just continue unchanged.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician\\\\nTherapist\\\",\\n     \\\"Progress reports. Medication changes at home not communicated to school. Success with supports used to justify removing them. Transition to middle school/high school with inadequate accommodation transfer.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD IEP review checklist by grade band (elementary vs middle vs high). Accommodation evolution guide as academic demands increase. School transition preparation toolkit (elementary\\u2192middle, middle\\u2192high). Medication-school coordination template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.3\\\", \\\"Transition Planning (Age 14+)\\\",\\n     \\\"By age 14-16, IEP must include transition goals. For ADHD: (1) Self-advocacy \\u2014 understanding ADHD and communicating needs. (2) Self-management \\u2014 medication management, using organizational tools independently. (3) College preparation \\u2014 SAT/ACT accommodations (apply 6+ months early), college disability services registration, 504/IEP documentation transfer. (4) Career exploration \\u2014 careers matching ADHD strengths (entrepreneurship, creative fields, emergency services, sales). (5) Driving \\u2014 ADHD increases accident risk 2-4x; medication timing for driving important. (6) DOR services if vocational support needed. (7) Financial literacy \\u2014 ADHD impulsivity affects spending.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"DOR\\\\nCollege disability office\\\\nDMV (driving)\\\",\\n     \\\"Transition assessments. College disability services documentation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Begin by age 14 (recommended), required by 16.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 TRANSITION GAPS: Self-advocacy skills never taught \\u2014 child always had adults advocating. Medication management transitions from parent to teen \\u2014 gap in adherence. ADHD impulsivity + new freedom = higher risk period (substance use, risky driving, academic failure). Financial literacy resources adapted for ADHD.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.4\\\", \\\"Adult ADHD \\u2014 Ongoing Support\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD persists into adulthood in 60-75% of cases. Adult needs: (1) Transition to adult prescriber \\u2014 many pediatric patients lose continuity. (2) Workplace accommodations under ADA. (3) Ongoing medication management. (4) Relationship skills \\u2014 ADHD affects partnerships. (5) Financial management strategies. (6) Executive function support systems. (7) Mental health monitoring \\u2014 ADHD adults at higher risk for depression, anxiety, substance use. (2) Medication is CENTRAL to treatment (70-80% response rate for stimulants) \\u2014 unlike autism where medication treats co-occurring but not core symptoms. (3) School eligibility is under OHI (Other Health Impairment), not Autism category. (4) 504 Plans are MORE COMMON than IEPs for ADHD (many ADHD children get 504 only). (7) Girls/inattentive type systematically under-identified. (8) Medication shortage is an ongoing practical barrier.\\\",\\n    \\\"\\\",\\n    \\\"KEY DIFFERENCES FROM PDA JOURNEY: (1) Traditional behavioral approaches (reward systems, structure) generally WORK for ADHD \\u2014 they BACKFIRE for PDA. (4) ADHD is universally recognized by U.S. clinicians \\u2014 PDA is not recognized by most.     "type": "text",
    "text": "**V7 \u2014 12 sheets, 63 entities, 48 sources, with the complete ADHD Journey Map.**\n\n**New Sheet: ADHD Journey Map \u2014 8 Phases, 26 Steps**\n\nHere's how ADHD differs from the Autism and PDA journeys:\n\n**Phase 1 \u2014 Recognition & Concern (2 steps)**\n- Parent/teacher notices attention, behavior, or learning patterns\n- Well-child visit with validated rating scales (Vanderbilt, Conners) \u2014 AAP requires multi-informant data (parent + teacher)\n- Girls/inattentive type systematically under-identified\n\n**Phase 2 \u2014 Evaluation & Diagnosis (2 steps)**\n- Full DSM-5 evaluation: **ADHD is a recognized diagnosis** (unlike PDA) \u2014 codes 314.00/314.01\n- Three presentations: Inattentive, Hyperactive-Impulsive, Combined\n- **1 in 9 U.S. children** (7.1M, 11.4%) have ever been diagnosed\n- 78% have at least one co-occurring condition\n\n**Phase 3 \u2014 Treatment Planning (3 steps)**\n- **Gold standard = medication + behavioral therapy** (this is the biggest difference from autism/PDA)\n- Stimulants: 70-80% response rate (methylphenidate, amphetamine families)\n- Behavioral parent training: CHADD Parent-to-Parent, Triple P, PCIT\n- MTA study evidence base for multimodal approach\n\n**Phase 4 \u2014 School Supports (5 steps)**\n- IEP eligibility under **OHI (Other Health Impairment)** \u2014 ADHD specifically listed\n- **504 Plans more common than IEPs for ADHD** (different from autism where IEP is standard)\n- OSEP has clarified: educational performance \u2260 just grades\n- Key accommodations: executive function support, organizational systems, daily report cards, movement breaks\n\n**Phase 5 \u2014 Insurance & Therapy (2 steps)**\n- Medication coverage with ongoing shortage issues (71.5% had difficulty filling prescriptions)\n- Behavioral therapy + organizational skills training + social skills groups\n\n**Phase 6 \u2014 Home & Daily Life (2 steps)**\n- ADHD-friendly home environment modifications\n- Executive function skill building \u2014 the **core deficit** of ADHD\n\n**Phase 7 \u2014 Co-occurring Conditions (4 steps)** \u2014 UNIQUE to ADHD journey\n- Anxiety co-occurrence (~40%)\n- Learning disabilities (~30-50%) \u2014 medication helps attention but does NOT remediate LD\n- Behavioral/conduct problems (~50%) \u2014 ODD, conduct disorder\n- Emotional dysregulation \u2014 increasingly recognized as core ADHD feature, not just co-occurring\n\n**Phase 8 \u2014 Long-Term Planning & Transition (4 steps)**\n- Ongoing medication management and monitoring\n- Annual IEP/504 evolution (demands increase each year)\n- Transition planning with SAT/ACT accommodations, college disability services, driving safety\n- Adult ADHD: 60-75% persistence, 15.5M U.S. adults diagnosed\n\n**3 ADHD entities added to Master Matrix:** CHADD, ADDitude Magazine, AACAP\n\n**10 ADHD sources added** (CHADD, ADDitude, CDC data, Danielson 2024 prevalence study, NCHS Data Brief, AACAP, AAP Guidelines, OSEP guidance, Understood.org ADHD, ADD Resource Center)\n\n**Key cross-journey comparisons built into the summary rows** showing how ADHD navigation differs from Autism, PDA, and SLD \u2014 critical for Waypoint's decision engine to route families to the right journey map.",

        "text": "Approximately 50% of these diagnoses were made in adulthood. Additionally, a 2024 study reports that 7.1 million US children (around 11.4%) have received an ADHD diagnosis. In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) ...\n\nTaipale et al., (2024) found that taking ADHD medication helped to significantly lower a patient\u2019s risk of suicidal behavior and hospitalization (for both psychiatric and non-psychiatric reasons). ... New research into treatment effects is positive for clinicians and patients and shows the tangible benefits treatment can offer. It also evidences the need for ongoing research in the area, coupled with continuous education, and adding to published guidelines, to ensure that prescribing clinicians are aware of new developments. Despite these encouraging findings, clinicians and patients continue to face challenges from ongoing medication shortages. CDC data from 2024 shows that 71.5% of US adults taking stimulant medication had difficulty filling their prescriptions in the previous year due to availability issues. Further research identifying suitable medication alternatives and further action to minimize the impacts of shortages is crucial.\n\nRecent data from the National Center for Health Statistics indicates that 15.5 million US adults had received an ADHD diagnosis by late 2023. Approximately 50% of these diagnoses were made in adulthood. Additionally, a 2024 study reports that 7.1 million US children (around 11.4%) have received an ADHD diagnosis. In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) estimates that around 5% of children and 3-4% of UK adults have ADHD. ADHD was once thought of as a childhood condition, yet its prevalence and new diagnoses amongst adults demonstrates the lifelong nature of the condition. As more people become aware of symptoms, the identification of adults with ADHD is likely to continue. Our understanding of the different presentations of ADHD is continuing to develop. We know that women and girls are less likely to be diagnosed with ADHD than men and boys.\n\nLater this year, the American Professional Society of ADHD and Related Disorders (APSARD) will publish guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of ADHD in adults \u2013 a much-needed resource for clinicians. In the UK, 2024 saw the publication of a significant new Diagnostic Guideline by the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE). The guidance provides clarity on ADHD assessment best practices by recommending the use of an objective test (QbTest) as part of the diagnostic process in children and young people aged 6-17. Sweden\u2019s National Board of Health and Welfare also published significant new guidelines on ADHD and autism in 2024. Clinicians have a difficult role in assessing patients for ADHD and these guidelines provide critical advice. Such guidance also supports a more consistent approach to ADHD evaluation, which can help multi-site clinics in establishing standardized models of care.\n\nThere is an established body of clinical research into the use of objective measures in ADHD evaluation and diagnosis. However, in 2024, two new studies highlighted the further usefulness of objective testing technologies for clinicians. Sanyal et al., highlighted the potential use of objective tests like QbCheck, to aid treatment decisions, monitor the impact of ADHD medications on symptoms, and optimize treatments accordingly., monitor the impact of ADHD medications on symptoms, and optimize treatments accordingly. Grossman and Berger suggest that the inclusion of a computerized test during assessment can increase patient trust in clinician decisions and also increase their intent to follow treatment recommendations. Public awareness of ADHD is greater now than ever before. This new era of awareness has presented opportunities and challenges. Social media has become a giant platform for discussion around ADHD.",

        "text": "During 2020\u20132022, the prevalence of ever diagnosed attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was 11.3% in children ages 5\u201317 years, with boys (14.5%) having a higher prevalence than girls (8.0%). Children ages 5\u201311 years were less likely than children ages 12\u201317 years to have ADHD.\n\nDuring 2020\u20132022, the prevalence of ever diagnosed attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was 11.3% in children ages 5\u201317 years, with boys (14.5%) having a higher prevalence than girls (8.0%). Children ages 5\u201311 years were less likely than children ages 12\u201317 years to have ADHD. White non-Hispanic children ages 5\u201317 years were more likely to have ADHD (13.4%) than Black non-Hispanic (10.8%) and Hispanic (8.9%) children. The prevalence of ADHD decreased as the level of family income increased. Children with public (14.4%) or private (9.7%) health insurance were more likely to have ADHD than children without insurance (6.3%). ... Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is one of the most common mental disorders of childhood (1). It is characterized by a pattern of inattention, impulsivity, or hyperactivity that leads to functional impairment experienced in multiple settings (2).\n\nBoys (14.5%) were more likely than girls (8.0%) to have ADHD, and this pattern was seen for children ages 5\u201311 years and 12\u201317 years. Among both boys and girls, the prevalence of ADHD was lower in children ages 5\u201311 years (8.6%) than in those ages 12\u201317 years (14.3%). Figure 1. Prevalence of children ages 5\u201317 years ever diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, by age group and sex: United States, 2020\u20132022 \u00b7 1Significantly different from girls (p < 0.05). 2Significantly different from children ages 12\u201317 years (p < 0.05). Prevalence of children ages 5\u201317 years ever diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, by age group and sex: United States, 2020\u20132022 \u00b7 NOTES: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder is based on a response to the question, \u201cHas a doctor or other health professional ever told you that [sample child] had Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder or ADHD or Attention-Deficit Disorder or ADD?\u201d Estimates are based on household interviews of a sample of the civilian noninstitutionalized population. SOURCE: National Center for Health Statistics, National Health Interview Survey, 2020\u20132022. Among children ages 5\u201317 years, White non-Hispanic (subsequently, White) children were more likely to have ever been diagnosed with ADHD (13.4%) than Black non-Hispanic (subsequently, Black) (10.8%) and Hispanic (8.9%) children (Figure 2, Table 2).\n\nAmong children ages 5\u201311 years, White children were more likely than Hispanic children to have ADHD; however, observed differences among Black children and either White or Hispanic children were not significant. Among children ages 12\u201317 years, the prevalence of ADHD was higher among White children (17.0%) than for Black (13.0%) and Hispanic (11.7%) children. The prevalence of ADHD was higher in children ages 12\u201317 years than in children ages 5\u201311 years for all race and Hispanic-origin groups. Figure 2. Approximately 1 in 9 U.S. children have ever received an ADHD diagnosis (11.4%, 7.1 million children) and 10.5% (6.5 million) had current ADHD....\n\nIssue date 2024 May-Jun. ... To provide updated national prevalence estimates of diagnosed attention-deficit/ hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), ADHD severity, co-occurring disorders, and receipt of ADHD medication and behavioral treatment among U.S. children and adolescents by demographic and clinical subgroups using data from the 2022 National Survey of Children\u2019s Health (NSCH). This study used 2022 NSCH data to estimate the prevalence of ever diagnosed and current ADHD among U.S. children aged 3\u201317 years. Among children with current ADHD, ADHD severity, presence of current co-occurring disorders, and receipt of medication and behavioral treatment were estimated. Weighted estimates were calculated overall and for demographic and clinical subgroups (n=45,169). Approximately 1 in 9 U.S. children have ever received an ADHD diagnosis (11.4%, 7.1 million children) and 10.5% (6.5 million) had current ADHD.\n\nJ Clin Child Adolesc Psychol. Published data on estimates of ADHD among Asian children and for other racial and ethnic groups are limited and do not allow for similar exploration of changes over time (Bitsko et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2021). Otherwise, patterns of prevalence across sociodemographic groups were generally consistent with previous data (Bitsko et al., 2022; Danielson, Bitsko, et al., 2018; Pastor et al., 2015; Perou et al., 2013; Zablotsky & Black, 2020; Zablotsky et al., 2019). This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path \u2014 even casually (like \\\"the xlsx in my downloads\\\") \u2014 and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.\"\n     4\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     5\t---\n     6\t\n     7\t# Requirements for Outputs\n     8\t\n     9\t## All Excel files\n    10\t\n    11\t### Professional Font\n    12\t- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user\n    13\t\n    14\t### Zero Formula Errors\n    15\t- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)\n    16\t\n    17\t### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)\n    18\t- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files\n    19\t- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns\n    20\t- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines\n    21\t\n    22\t## Financial models\n    23\t\n    24\t### Color Coding Standards\n    25\tUnless otherwise stated by the user or existing template\n    26\t\n    27\t#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions\n    28\t- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios\n    29\t- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations\n    30\t- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook\n    31\t- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files\n    32\t- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated\n    33\t\n    34\t### Number Formatting Standards\n    35\t\n    36\t#### Required Format Rules\n    37\t- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., \"2024\" not \"2,024\")\n    38\t- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers (\"Revenue ($mm)\")\n    39\t- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros \"-\", including percentages (e.g., \"$#,##0;($#,##0);-\")\n    40\t- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)\n    41\t- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)\n    42\t- **Negative numbers**: Use parentheses (123) not minus -123\n    43\t\n    44\t### Formula Construction Rules\n    45\t\n    46\t#### Assumptions Placement\n    47\t- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells\n    48\t- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas\n    49\t- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05\n    50\t\n    51\t#### Formula Error Prevention\n    52\t- Verify all cell references are correct\n    53\t- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges\n    54\t- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods\n    55\t- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)\n    56\t- Verify no unintended circular references\n    57\t\n    58\t#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes\n    59\t- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: \"Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]\"\n    60\t- Examples:\n    61\t  - \"Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    62\t  - \"Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    63\t  - \"Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity\"\n    64\t  - \"Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen\"\n    65\t\n    66\t# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis\n    67\t\n    68\t## Overview\n    69\t\n    70\tA user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.\n    71\t\n    72\t## Important Requirements\n    73\t\n    74\t**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)\n    75\t\n    76\t## Reading and analyzing data\n    77\t\n    78\t### Data analysis with pandas\n    79\tFor data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:\n    80\t\n    81\t```python\n    82\timport pandas as pd\n    83\t\n    84\t# Read Excel\n    85\tdf = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet\n    86\tall_sheets = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name=None)  # All sheets as dict\n    87\t\n    88\t# Analyze\n    89\tdf.head()      # Preview data\n    90\tdf.info()      # Column info\n    91\tdf.describe()  # Statistics\n    92\t\n    93\t# Write Excel\n    94\tdf.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)\n    95\t```\n    96\t\n    97\t## Excel File Workflows\n    98\t\n    99\t## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values\n   100\t\n   101\t**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.\n   102\t\n   103\t### \u274c WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values\n   104\t```python\n   105\t# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result\n   106\ttotal = df['Sales'].sum()\n   107\tsheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000\n   108\t\n   109\t# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python\n   110\tgrowth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']\n   111\tsheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15\n   112\t\n   113\t# Bad: Python calculation for average\n   114\tavg = sum(values) / len(values)\n   115\tsheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5\n   116\t```\n   117\t\n   118\t### \u2705 CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas\n   119\t```python\n   120\t# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum\n   121\tsheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'\n   122\t\n   123\t# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula\n   124\tsheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'\n   125\t\n   126\t# Good: Average using Excel function\n   127\tsheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'\n   128\t```\n   129\t\n   130\tThis applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.\n   131\t\n   132\t## Common Workflow\n   133\t1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting\n   134\t2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file\n   135\t3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting\n   136\t4. **Save**: Write to file\n   137\t5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script\n   138\t   ```bash\n   139\t   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx\n   140\t   ```\n   141\t6. **Verify and fix any errors**: \n   142\t   - The script returns JSON with error details\n   143\t   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations\n   144\t   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again\n   145\t   - Common errors to fix:\n   146\t     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references\n   147\t     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero\n   148\t     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula\n   149\t     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name\n   150\t\n   151\t### Creating new Excel files\n   152\t\n   153\t```python\n   154\t# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting\n   155\tfrom openpyxl import Workbook\n   156\tfrom openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment\n   157\t\n   158\twb = Workbook()\n   159\tsheet = wb.active\n   160\t\n   161\t# Add data\n   162\tsheet['A1'] = 'Hello'\n   163\tsheet['B1'] = 'World'\n   164\tsheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])\n   165\t\n   166\t# Add formula\n   167\tsheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'\n   168\t\n   169\t# Formatting\n   170\tsheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')\n   171\tsheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')\n   172\tsheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')\n   173\t\n   174\t# Column width\n   175\tsheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20\n   176\t\n   177\twb.save('output.xlsx')\n   178\t```\n   179\t\n   180\t### Editing existing Excel files\n   181\t\n   182\t```python\n   183\t# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting\n   184\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   185\t\n   186\t# Load existing file\n   187\twb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')\n   188\tsheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet\n   189\t\n   190\t# Working with multiple sheets\n   191\tfor sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:\n   192\t    sheet = wb[sheet_name]\n   193\t    print(f\"Sheet: {sheet_name}\")\n   194\t\n   195\t# Modify cells\n   196\tsheet['A1'] = 'New Value'\n   197\tsheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2\n   198\tsheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3\n   199\t\n   200\t# Add new sheet\n   201\tnew_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')\n   202\tnew_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'\n   203\t\n   204\twb.save('modified.xlsx')\n   205\t```\n   206\t\n   207\t## Recalculating formulas\n   208\t\n   209\tExcel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:\n   210\t\n   211\t```bash\n   212\tpython scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tExample:\n   216\t```bash\n   217\tpython scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30\n   218\t```\n   219\t\n   220\tThe script:\n   221\t- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run\n   222\t- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets\n   223\t- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)\n   224\t- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts\n   225\t- Works on both Linux and macOS\n   226\t\n   227\t## Formula Verification Checklist\n   228\t\n   229\tQuick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:\n   230\t\n   231\t### Essential Verification\n   232\t- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model\n   233\t- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)\n   234\t- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)\n   235\t\n   236\t### Common Pitfalls\n   237\t- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`\n   238\t- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ \n   239\t- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first\n   240\t- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)\n   241\t- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)\n   242\t- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets\n   243\t\n   244\t### Formula Testing Strategy\n   245\t- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly\n   246\t- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist\n   247\t- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values\n   248\t\n   249\t### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output\n   250\tThe script returns JSON with error details:\n   251\t```json\n   252\t{\n   253\t  \"status\": \"success\",           // or \"errors_found\"\n   254\t  \"total_errors\": 0,              // Total error count\n   255\t  \"total_formulas\": 42,           // Number of formulas in file\n   256\t  \"error_summary\": {              // Only present if errors found\n   257\t    \"#REF!\": {\n   258\t      \"count\": 2,\n   259\t      \"locations\": [\"Sheet1!B5\", \"Sheet1!C10\"]\n   260\t    }\n   261\t  }\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t## Best Practices\n   266\t\n   267\t### Library Selection\n   268\t- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export\n   269\t- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features\n   270\t\n   271\t### Working with openpyxl\n   272\t- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)\n   273\t- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`\n   274\t- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost\n   275\t- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing\n   276\t- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values\n   277\t\n   278\t### Working with pandas\n   279\t- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`\n   280\t- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`\n   281\t- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`\n   282\t\n   283\t## Code Style Guidelines\n   284\t**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:\n   285\t- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments\n   286\t- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations\n   287\t- Avoid unnecessary print statements\n   288\t\n   289\t**For Excel files themselves**:\n   290\t- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions\n   291\t- Document data sources for hardcoded values\n   292\t- Include notes for key calculations and model sections",

Short-acting (4 hrs), intermediate (6-8 hrs), long-acting (10-14 hrs). NON-STIMULANTS (second-line): atomoxetine (Strattera), guanfacine (Intuniv), clonidine (Kapvay), viloxazine (Qelbree). Process: start low, titrate slowly, monitor for efficacy and side effects. Common side effects: appetite suppression, sleep difficulties, mood changes. Medication shortage has been ongoing issue (FDA Adderall shortage 2022-2024).\",\n     \"Prescribing Provider\\n(Pediatrician/Psychiatrist)\", \"Pharmacy\\nInsurance\",\n     \"Prescription. Prior authorization if required. Growth chart tracking. Side effect monitoring checklist.\",\n     \"Titration: 4-8 weeks to find optimal dose. Follow-up: monthly initially, then every 3-6 months.\",\n     \"\u2605 MEDICATION CHALLENGES: Medication shortage \u2014 71.5% of adults on stimulants had difficulty filling prescriptions (2024 CDC data). Prior authorization delays for specific medications. Generic vs brand-name differences. Side effects cause parents to discontinue without physician guidance. Medication doesn't cover full school day (wearing off). Diversion/misuse concerns at school. Medication stigma. Growth suppression concerns require monitoring.\",\n     \"Medication tracking log (dose, time, efficacy, side effects). Insurance formulary checker \u2014 is preferred medication covered? Prior auth template for specific ADHD medications. Side effect vs therapeutic effect monitoring tool. Medication shortage alert system. Growth chart tracker. Medication administration plan for school.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"3.3\", \"Behavioral Parent Training & Family Strategies\",\n     \"Evidence-based parent training programs: CHADD Parent-to-Parent (free, peer-led), Triple P (Positive Parenting Program), Parent-Child Interaction Therapy (PCIT, ages 2-7), Incredible Years. Key strategies: (1) Positive reinforcement systems (specific praise, token economies). (2) Clear, consistent expectations with visual supports. (3) Environmental modifications (reduce distractions, organize spaces). (4) Routine/structure with flexibility. (5) Break tasks into smaller steps. (6) Movement breaks. (7) Emotional coaching for dysregulation. (8) Collaborative problem-solving (Ross Greene's 'The Explosive Child' approach). Russell Barkley's 'Taking Charge of ADHD' widely recommended.\",\n     \"Therapist / CHADD\\nParent Training\", \"Pediatrician\\nSchool\",\n     \"Referral to behavioral therapist or parent training program. Insurance authorization.\",\n     \"Parent training: typically 8-16 sessions. Ongoing at home.\",\n     \"Insurance doesn't cover parent training programs. CHADD chapters not available in all areas. Waitlists for behavioral therapists. Parent burnout from implementing strategies consistently. Co-parent/family disagreement on approach. Strategies that work for typical ADHD may not work for ADHD + co-occurring conditions.\",\n     \"CHADD chapter finder by ZIP. Parent training program locator. CHADD free online course registration link. Evidence-based strategy library organized by challenge type. Home environment modification checklist. Daily routine builder with ADHD-friendly structure. Recommended book list with reading guides.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 4: SCHOOL SUPPORTS\n    [\"4\\nSchool\\nSupports\\n(IEP/504)\", \"4.1\", \"Request School District Evaluation\",\n     \"Parent sends written request for special education evaluation. ADHD qualifies under IEP category 'Other Health Impairment' (OHI) \u2014 specifically, ADHD causes 'limited alertness with respect to the educational environment' or 'heightened alertness to environmental stimuli' that results in limited alertness to educational tasks. IMPORTANT: ADHD diagnosis alone does NOT guarantee IEP eligibility \u2014 must demonstrate that ADHD adversely affects educational performance. Educational performance includes academics AND non-academic domains (social, behavioral, emotional regulation, organizational). OSEP has clarified 'educational performance' is NOT limited to grades.\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"Parent\\nPediatrician (documentation)\",\n     \"Written assessment request letter. OSEP has clarified that grades alone don't determine eligibility \u2014 but many districts still use this gatekeeping. District claims ADHD is 'medical, not educational.' District refuses OHI eligibility because child doesn't have physical health impairment (wrong \u2014 ADHD is specifically listed under OHI). Assessment only tests academics, misses behavioral/social/organizational impact. School psychologist doesn't assess executive functioning.\",\n     \"\u2605 Assessment request letter generator for ADHD \u2014 cites OHI eligibility and OSEP guidance on 'educational performance.' Pre-filled parent input form highlighting ADHD-specific challenges.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"4.2\", \"School District Evaluation \u2014 ADHD-Focused\",\n     \"Assessment plan should include: psychoeducational testing (to identify co-occurring LD), behavioral/social-emotional assessment (BASC-3, Conners teacher rating), executive function evaluation (BRIEF-2), classroom observation during structured AND unstructured activities, parent/teacher interviews, review of existing ADHD diagnosis and medical records. REQUEST SPECIFICALLY: (1) Executive function assessment \u2014 critical for ADHD. (2) Behavioral observation during HIGH-DEMAND tasks. (3) Academic fluency testing (not just accuracy \u2014 ADHD impacts speed/efficiency). (4) Social-emotional assessment across settings.\",\n     \"Local School District\\n(School Psychologist)\", \"Parent\\nTeacher\\nPediatrician\",\n     \"Signed assessment plan. (5) Written/visual instructions (not just verbal). (6) Organizational system support (binder checks, assignment tracking). (7) Reduced homework load. (8) Testing in separate quiet setting. IEP GOALS should target: executive functioning, self-regulation, organizational skills, self-advocacy, social skills \u2014 NOT just 'paying attention' or 'staying in seat.'\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"Parent\\nPediatrician\\nTherapist\",\n     \"All assessment reports. 'Extra time' and 'preferential seating' offered as only accommodations (insufficient). Behavior plan focuses on compliance rather than teaching self-regulation. Goals about 'staying on task' without addressing WHY (executive function deficit). Medication expected to be sole intervention \u2014 school reduces support. After-medication 'rebound' period not addressed. Substitute teachers don't implement accommodations. NOTE: 504 Plans do NOT include specialized instruction or related services \u2014 if child needs those, IEP is more appropriate.\",\n     \"Local School District\\n(504 Coordinator)\", \"Parent\\nPediatrician\",\n     \"ADHD diagnosis documentation. 504 referral. (5) Consistent routines with advance warnings for transitions. (6) Organization systems (labeled bins, color-coded folders, landing pad for belongings). Key EF skills to build: (1) Working memory \u2014 external memory aids (written lists, phone reminders, visual cues). (2) Time management \u2014 timers, visual time tools, 'time estimation' practice. (3) Organization \u2014 systems that are simple enough to maintain (the '2-minute rule'). (4) Planning/prioritizing \u2014 break projects into steps, use planners/apps. (5) Task initiation \u2014 'just do the first step,' body doubling, accountability partners. (6) Emotional regulation \u2014 identify triggers, use coping strategies, self-advocacy for breaks. (7) Flexible thinking \u2014 practice plan B thinking. (8) Self-monitoring \u2014 learn to recognize when attention has drifted. School and home not coordinating EF approach. Child resistant to 'more strategies.' Parent doing EF work FOR child instead of coaching. Can be: (1) Primary anxiety + ADHD (both independent conditions). (2) Anxiety CAUSED by ADHD experiences (academic failure, social rejection, constant criticism). (3) ADHD symptoms that LOOK like anxiety (restlessness, difficulty concentrating, avoidance). Treatment: anxiety may need to be treated first or alongside ADHD. SSRIs for anxiety can be combined with stimulants for ADHD. CBT for anxiety is first-line behavioral treatment. School accommodations should address BOTH conditions.\",\n     \"Pediatrician / Psychiatrist /\\nPsychologist\", \"Therapist\\nSchool\",\n     \"Anxiety screening (GAD-7, SCARED). Child may have IEP for LD and accommodate for ADHD, or IEP covering both. Evaluation should test academic skills AT CURRENT GRADE LEVEL. Gifted + ADHD + LD (triple exceptional) \u2014 all three mask each other.\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL: 'ADHD medication helping but grades still struggling?' \u2014 automatic LD screening prompt. Includes: ODD (Oppositional Defiant Disorder) \u2014 approximately 40% of ADHD children. Conduct Disorder \u2014 approximately 15-20% of ADHD children (more common in adolescence). ADHD + ODD/CD significantly increases risk for: school discipline, substance use, justice system involvement. Key: distinguish ADHD impulsivity from intentional defiance. ADHD impulsivity = acting without thinking. ODD defiance = deliberate opposition. Treatment: behavioral parent training effective for both. Some medications help both ADHD and aggression (guanfacine, risperidone for severe cases).\",\n     \"Psychiatrist /\\nPsychologist\", \"School\\nBehavioral therapist\",\n     \"Behavioral assessment. Includes: intense emotional reactions disproportionate to situation, difficulty calming down once upset, low frustration tolerance, rejection sensitivity ('Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria' \u2014 not a formal diagnosis but widely discussed), rapid mood shifts, emotional impulsivity (saying hurtful things in the moment).
