(2) Medication is CENTRAL to treatment (70-80% response rate for stimulants) \u2014 unlike autism where medication treats co-occurring but not core symptoms. (3) School eligibility is under OHI (Other Health Impairment), not Autism category. (4) 504 Plans are MORE COMMON than IEPs for ADHD (many ADHD children get 504 only). (5) Executive function is the CORE deficit \u2014 all supports should target EF. (6) Co-occurring conditions are the RULE, not exception (78% have at least one). (7) Girls/inattentive type systematically under-identified. (8) Medication shortage is an ongoing practical barrier.\",\n    \"\",\n    \"KEY DIFFERENCES FROM PDA JOURNEY: (1) Traditional behavioral approaches (reward systems, structure) generally WORK for ADHD \u2014 they BACKFIRE for PDA. (2) ADHD medication has strong evidence base \u2014 PDA has no medication-specific treatment. (3) ADHD evaluation is straightforward with validated tools \u2014 PDA has no standardized assessment. (4) ADHD is universally recognized by U.S. clinicians \u2014 PDA is not recognized by most. (5) Both share executive function deficits but mechanisms differ (ADHD = developmental delay; PDA = anxiety-driven avoidance).\",\n    \"\",\n    \"ADHD RESOURCES: CHADD (chadd.org) \u2022 ADDitude Magazine (additudemag.com) \u2022 CDC ADHD Materials (cdc.gov/adhd) \u2022 AACAP ADHD Resource Center \u2022 Understood.org \u2022 CHADD Parent Training Programs \u2022 AAP Clinical Practice Guideline (2019) \u2022 Books: 'Taking Charge of ADHD' (Barkley), 'The Explosive Child' (Greene), 'What Your ADHD Child Wishes You Knew' (Saline), '8 Keys to Parenting Children with ADHD' (Goldrich)\",\n]\nfor s in summaries:\n    ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value=s)\n    if s:\n        ws.cell(row=r, column=1).font = Font(name=\"Arial\", size=10, bold=True, color=NAVY)\n        ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=1, end_row=r, end_column=10)\n    r += 1\n\nwidths = [16, 6, 30, 55, 20, 22, 35, 30, 55, 55]\nfor i, w in enumerate(widths, 1):\n    ws.column_dimensions[get_column_letter(i)].width = w\nws.freeze_panes = \"D4\"\n\n# ============================================================\n# ADD ADHD ENTITIES TO MASTER MATRIX\n# ============================================================\nws_m = wb[\"Master Entity Matrix\"]\nmr = ws_m.max_row + 1\n\nadhd_entities = [\n    [\"CHADD (Children and Adults with ADHD)\", \"National Nonprofit \u2014 ADHD\", \"National\",\n     \"501(c)(3) nonprofit improving lives through education, advocacy, and support. (2) Predominantly Hyperactive-Impulsive \\u2014 fidgeting, excessive talking, difficulty waiting, acting without thinking. (3) Combined Presentation \\u2014 meets criteria for both. Short-acting (4 hrs), intermediate (6-8 hrs), long-acting (10-14 hrs). NON-STIMULANTS (second-line): atomoxetine (Strattera), guanfacine (Intuniv), clonidine (Kapvay), viloxazine (Qelbree). Process: start low, titrate slowly, monitor for efficacy and side effects. Common side effects: appetite suppression, sleep difficulties, mood changes. Medication shortage has been ongoing issue (FDA Adderall shortage 2022-2024).\\\",\\n     \\\"Prescribing Provider\\\\n(Pediatrician/Psychiatrist)\\\", \\\"Pharmacy\\\\nInsurance\\\",\\n     \\\"Prescription. Medication doesn't cover full school day (wearing off). Diversion/misuse concerns at school. Medication stigma. Key strategies: (1) Positive reinforcement systems (specific praise, token economies). (2) Clear, consistent expectations with visual supports. (3) Environmental modifications (reduce distractions, organize spaces). (4) Routine/structure with flexibility. (5) Break tasks into smaller steps. (6) Movement breaks. ADHD qualifies under IEP category 'Other Health Impairment' (OHI) \\u2014 specifically, ADHD causes 'limited alertness with respect to the educational environment' or 'heightened alertness to environmental stimuli' that results in limited alertness to educational tasks. IMPORTANT: ADHD diagnosis alone does NOT guarantee IEP eligibility \\u2014 must demonstrate that ADHD adversely affects educational performance. Educational performance includes academics AND non-academic domains (social, behavioral, emotional regulation, organizational). OSEP has clarified 'educational performance' is NOT limited to grades.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician (documentation)\\\",\\n     \\\"Written assessment request letter. District refuses OHI eligibility because child doesn't have physical health impairment (wrong \\u2014 ADHD is specifically listed under OHI). Assessment only tests academics, misses behavioral/social/organizational impact. School psychologist doesn't assess executive functioning.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Assessment request letter generator for ADHD \\u2014 cites OHI eligibility and OSEP guidance on 'educational performance.' REQUEST SPECIFICALLY: (1) Executive function assessment \\u2014 critical for ADHD. (2) Behavioral observation during HIGH-DEMAND tasks. (3) Academic fluency testing (not just accuracy \\u2014 ADHD impacts speed/efficiency). (4) Social-emotional assessment across settings.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\\n(School Psychologist)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nTeacher\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"Signed assessment plan. Only academic testing done \\u2014 behavioral and organizational impact missed. Observation done during preferred activity (looks fine) not during high-demand task. Assessment doesn't connect ADHD symptoms to functional impairment.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD-specific assessment plan review tool \\u2014 flags missing components. Teacher questionnaire that captures ADHD-specific classroom challenges.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.3\\\", \\\"IEP Development \\u2014 ADHD-Specific Accommodations & Goals\\\",\\n     \\\"If eligible under OHI, IEP team develops plan. KEY ADHD ACCOMMODATIONS: (1) Preferential seating (reduced distractions). (2) Extended time on tests/assignments. (3) Breaks for movement/sensory regulation. (4) Chunked assignments with check-ins. (5) Written/visual instructions (not just verbal). (6) Organizational system support (binder checks, assignment tracking). (7) Reduced homework load. (8) Testing in separate quiet setting. (9) Daily report card/home-school communication. (10) Executive function coaching. (11) Medication administration plan. (12) Teacher training on ADHD. (13) Transition support between activities. IEP GOALS should target: executive functioning, self-regulation, organizational skills, self-advocacy, social skills \\u2014 NOT just 'paying attention' or 'staying in seat.'\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician\\\\nTherapist\\\",\\n     \\\"All assessment reports. ADHD accommodations list. Parent concerns letter. Medical documentation including medication schedule.\\\",\\n     \\\"30 days from completed assessment to IEP meeting.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IEP PITFALLS: Generic accommodations that don't address specific child's ADHD presentation. 'Extra time' and 'preferential seating' offered as only accommodations (insufficient). Behavior plan focuses on compliance rather than teaching self-regulation. Goals about 'staying on task' without addressing WHY (executive function deficit). Medication expected to be sole intervention \\u2014 school reduces support. After-medication 'rebound' period not addressed. Substitute teachers don't implement accommodations. IEP goals not updated to address executive function as child ages.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 ADHD IEP accommodation menu organized by presentation type (inattentive, hyperactive, combined). Sample IEP goals for executive function, self-regulation, organization, self-advocacy. 'Red flag' checker for insufficient IEP plans. Medication administration plan template. Daily report card templates. Teacher training request template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.4\\\", \\\"504 Plan Alternative (If IEP Denied or Preferred)\\\",\\n     \\\"Many children with ADHD receive 504 Plans rather than IEPs. 504 Plan may be appropriate when: ADHD impacts functioning but child doesn't need specialized instruction (just accommodations). Section 504 broader eligibility: disability that 'substantially limits one or more major life activities' (learning, concentrating, thinking, reading, communicating). 504 accommodations can include: extended time, preferential seating, movement breaks, reduced homework, organizational support, testing modifications, behavioral expectations adjustments. NOTE: 504 Plans do NOT include specialized instruction or related services \\u2014 if child needs those, IEP is more appropriate.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\\n(504 Coordinator)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD diagnosis documentation. 504 referral. Medical documentation. Teacher input on functional impact.\\\",\\n     \\\"No specific federal timeline \\u2014 'reasonable' time.\\\",\\n     \\\"504 Plan offered as lesser alternative when IEP is warranted. 504 Plan is generic (only extra time, preferential seating). No enforcement mechanism as strong as IEP. 504 Plan not updated annually unless parent requests. Staff inconsistently implement 504. Substitute teachers unaware of 504 accommodations.\\\",\\n     \\\"504 vs IEP decision guide for ADHD. 504 accommodation menu organized by ADHD presentation. 504 monitoring checklist. Annual 504 review reminder. Rights comparison: 504 vs IEP.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.5\\\", \\\"IEP/504 Dispute Resolution\\\",\\n     \\\"If district denies eligibility or provides inadequate supports: (1) Request another IEP/504 meeting. (2) Provide additional documentation. (3) Request Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense. (4) File state complaint with CDE (California). (5) Request mediation. (6) Due process complaint. Key ADHD arguments: cite OSEP guidance that 'educational performance' includes non-academic areas. Cite specific functional impairment data. Provide private evaluation documenting executive function deficits. Document failed RTI/classroom interventions.\\\",\\n     \\\"School District / CDE / OAH\\\", \\\"DRC\\\\nSpecial Ed Attorney\\\\nAdvocate\\\",\\n     \\\"Written disagreement. Prior Written Notice. Documentation of denied services. Private evaluation. OSEP letters/guidance.\\\",\\n     \\\"CDE complaint: 60 days. Due process: 45 days from filing.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parent doesn't know they can appeal. District claims 'good grades = no disability.' IEE denied or district offers inadequate IEE provider. Attorney fees prohibitive. Parent burned out from adversarial process.\\\",\\n     \\\"Dispute resolution decision tree for ADHD. Template disagreement letters citing OSEP guidance. IEE request template. DRC referral. COPAA attorney directory. OSEP letter database on ADHD eligibility.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 5: INSURANCE & THERAPY\\n    [\\\"5\\\\nInsurance &\\\\nTherapy\\\", \\\"5.1\\\", \\\"Insurance Authorization for ADHD Treatment\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD treatment coverage: Medication \\u2014 generally covered under prescription benefit (stimulants may require prior auth, step therapy). Behavioral therapy \\u2014 covered under mental health parity (MHPAEA). Psychological testing/evaluation \\u2014 coverage varies widely. Parent training \\u2014 often NOT covered. Key: Mental Health Parity Act requires equivalent coverage for mental health conditions including ADHD. Medi-Cal covers ADHD evaluation, medication, and behavioral therapy. Private insurance: check formulary for preferred medications, prior auth requirements.\\\",\\n     \\\"Private Insurance / Medi-Cal\\\", \\\"Pediatrician\\\\nTherapist\\\\nPharmacy\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD diagnosis. Prescription. Prior authorization forms. Treatment plan from provider.\\\",\\n     \\\"Prior auth: 5-15 business days. Formulary check: immediate.\\\",\\n     \\\"Preferred medication not on formulary \\u2014 step therapy required (try cheaper option first). Prior auth delays for stimulant medications. Behavioral therapy sessions limited. Neuropsychological testing not covered or limited. Medication shortage compounds insurance issues. Out-of-network provider costs for specialists. Telehealth restrictions for controlled substance prescriptions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance formulary checker for ADHD medications. Prior auth template for stimulant medications. Step therapy appeal template. Mental health parity rights guide. Medi-Cal ADHD benefit explainer. Out-of-network exception request for ADHD specialists.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"5.2\\\", \\\"Access Behavioral & Organizational Therapy\\\",\\n     \\\"Evidence-based therapies for ADHD: (1) Behavioral Parent Training (ages 4-12): most supported by evidence. (2) CBT for ADHD (ages 12+): addresses organizational skills, time management, emotional regulation. (3) Organizational Skills Training (OST): evidence-based for academic organization. (4) Social Skills Groups: address peer relationship difficulties common in ADHD. (5) Executive Function Coaching: not therapy per se, but growing field for building skills. (6) Occupational Therapy: for sensory, motor, and organizational needs. CHADD offers free/low-cost parent education programs including online courses.\\\",\\n     \\\"Behavioral Therapist\\\\nCHADD\\\", \\\"Insurance\\\\nSchool\\\\nOT\\\",\\n     \\\"Referral from provider. Insurance authorization. Treatment goals aligned with functional needs.\\\",\\n     \\\"Therapy: weekly for 8-20 sessions typically. Parent training: 8-16 sessions. Ongoing as needed.\\\",\\n     \\\"Long waitlists for ADHD-specialized therapists. Insurance limits sessions. Generic therapy not ADHD-specific. OT often denied for ADHD by insurance. Parent training programs not locally available. Executive function coaching not insurance-covered.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD therapist finder by ZIP and specialty. CHADD chapter and program locator. Insurance appeal template for OT. Online parent training options (CHADD, Triple P). Executive function coaching directory. Therapy type recommendation engine by age and challenge area.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 6: HOME & DAILY LIFE\\n    [\\\"6\\\\nHome &\\\\nDaily Life\\\\nManagement\\\", \\\"6.1\\\", \\\"Build ADHD-Friendly Home Environment\\\",\\n     \\\"Environmental modifications: (1) Dedicated homework station \\u2014 minimal distractions, organized supplies. (2) Visual schedules and routines (morning, after-school, bedtime). (3) Timer-based task systems (Pomodoro for older kids). (4) Movement opportunities built into day. (5) Consistent routines with advance warnings for transitions. (6) Organization systems (labeled bins, color-coded folders, landing pad for belongings). (7) Technology management (content filters, app timers, phone-free homework zones). (8) Sleep hygiene \\u2014 ADHD children often have sleep difficulties (medication timing, racing thoughts). (9) Nutrition support \\u2014 regular meals/snacks (especially important with stimulant appetite suppression). (10) 'Body doubling' for focus support.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents\\\", \\\"Therapist\\\\nOT\\\",\\n     \\\"None formal. Home organization supplies. Visual schedule materials.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 implement gradually, not all at once.\\\",\\n     \\\"Overwhelm from trying to change everything at once. System works initially then compliance fades. Technology battles. Sleep difficulties persist despite hygiene changes. Stimulant appetite suppression causes nutritional concerns. Sibling resentment about 'different rules.' Parent burnout from constant structure-building.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD home environment audit checklist. Room-by-room organization guide. Visual schedule templates (morning, homework, bedtime). Technology management plan by age. Sleep hygiene checklist for ADHD. Meal planning around medication schedule. 'Start with one thing' implementation guide.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"6.2\\\", \\\"Executive Function Skill Building\\\",\\n     \\\"Executive function deficits are the CORE challenge of ADHD. Key EF skills to build: (1) Working memory \\u2014 external memory aids (written lists, phone reminders, visual cues). (2) Time management \\u2014 timers, visual time tools, 'time estimation' practice. (3) Organization \\u2014 systems that are simple enough to maintain (the '2-minute rule'). (4) Planning/prioritizing \\u2014 break projects into steps, use planners/apps. (5) Task initiation \\u2014 'just do the first step,' body doubling, accountability partners. (6) Emotional regulation \\u2014 identify triggers, use coping strategies, self-advocacy for breaks. (7) Flexible thinking \\u2014 practice plan B thinking. (8) Self-monitoring \\u2014 learn to recognize when attention has drifted. Programs: Unstuck and on Target, Understood.org EF guides, CHADD EF resources.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents\\\\nTherapist\\\\nSchool\\\", \\\"OT\\\\nExecutive function coach\\\",\\n     \\\"None formal. EF assessment results from school evaluation (BRIEF-2) helpful.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 EF skills develop through mid-20s in ADHD (delayed maturation).\\\",\\n     \\\"EF skills taught in isolated context \\u2014 don't generalize to daily life. School and home not coordinating EF approach. Child resistant to 'more strategies.' Parent doing EF work FOR child instead of coaching. Developmental expectations don't account for ADHD EF delay (30% maturity gap).\\\",\\n     \\\"EF skill builder organized by age and specific deficit. Understood.org EF tool integration. Home-school EF coordination template. Age-appropriate independence progression guide (accounting for ADHD 30% maturity gap). EF app and tool recommendations by age.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 7: CO-OCCURRING CONDITIONS\\n    [\\\"7\\\\nCo-occurring\\\\nConditions &\\\\nComplex ADHD\\\", \\\"7.1\\\", \\\"Screen & Address Anxiety (Co-occurs ~40%)\\\",\\n     \\\"Anxiety co-occurs with ADHD in approximately 40% of children. Can be: (1) Primary anxiety + ADHD (both independent conditions). (2) Anxiety CAUSED by ADHD experiences (academic failure, social rejection, constant criticism). (3) ADHD symptoms that LOOK like anxiety (restlessness, difficulty concentrating, avoidance). Treatment: anxiety may need to be treated first or alongside ADHD. SSRIs for anxiety can be combined with stimulants for ADHD. CBT for anxiety is first-line behavioral treatment. School accommodations should address BOTH conditions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / Psychiatrist /\\\\nPsychologist\\\", \\\"Therapist\\\\nSchool\\\",\\n     \\\"Anxiety screening (GAD-7, SCARED). Differentiation assessment. Medication review if both conditions require pharmacological treatment.\\\",\\n     \\\"Screening at initial ADHD evaluation and ongoing. Treatment concurrent.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Anxiety masked by ADHD diagnosis \\u2014 provider treats only ADHD. Stimulant medication worsens anxiety in some children. Anxiety causes school avoidance that is attributed to ADHD avoidance. CBT for anxiety conflicts with ADHD behavioral strategies. Child on multiple medications \\u2014 complex interactions.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD + anxiety co-occurrence screening tool. Medication interaction checker for combined treatment. School accommodation merger (ADHD + anxiety accommodations). CBT + ADHD behavioral strategy integration guide.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.2\\\", \\\"Screen & Address Learning Disabilities (Co-occurs ~30-50%)\\\",\\n     \\\"Learning disabilities co-occur with ADHD in 30-50% of children. Most common: dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, processing disorders. CRITICAL: ADHD medication improves attention but does NOT remediate LD \\u2014 both must be addressed separately. Child may have IEP for LD and accommodate for ADHD, or IEP covering both. Evaluation should test academic skills AT CURRENT GRADE LEVEL. Watch for: child whose reading/math/writing doesn't improve with ADHD treatment alone, significant discrepancy between ability and achievement, specific academic areas of persistent difficulty.\\\",\\n     \\\"School Psychologist /\\\\nNeuropsychologist\\\", \\\"School\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"Comprehensive psychoeducational evaluation. Achievement testing. Cognitive testing. Cross-reference with ADHD evaluation.\\\",\\n     \\\"School: within IEP timeline. Private: 2-8 weeks.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 LD MISSED: ADHD medication helps attention \\u2014 everyone assumes academics will improve. They don't. Underlying LD never identified. Child labeled 'not trying hard enough' despite medication. LD evaluation not requested because 'we already know it's ADHD.' School attributes academic difficulties to ADHD alone. Gifted + ADHD + LD (triple exceptional) \\u2014 all three mask each other.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: 'ADHD medication helping but grades still struggling?' \\u2014 automatic LD screening prompt. ADHD + LD co-occurrence guide for parents. Academic progress tracker post-medication to identify persistent deficits. LD evaluation request template. SLD Journey Map cross-reference.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.3\\\", \\\"Screen & Address Behavioral/Conduct Problems (Co-occurs ~50%)\\\",\\n     \\\"Behavioral or conduct problems co-occur with ADHD in approximately 50% of children. Includes: ODD (Oppositional Defiant Disorder) \\u2014 approximately 40% of ADHD children. Conduct Disorder \\u2014 approximately 15-20% of ADHD children (more common in adolescence). ADHD + ODD/CD significantly increases risk for: school discipline, substance use, justice system involvement. Key: distinguish ADHD impulsivity from intentional defiance. ADHD impulsivity = acting without thinking. ODD defiance = deliberate opposition. Treatment: behavioral parent training effective for both. Some medications help both ADHD and aggression (guanfacine, risperidone for severe cases).\\\",\\n     \\\"Psychiatrist /\\\\nPsychologist\\\", \\\"School\\\\nBehavioral therapist\\\",\\n     \\\"Behavioral assessment. FBA if school-based. Conduct disorder screening if severe/persistent.\\\",\\n     \\\"Screening at ADHD evaluation and ongoing.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD impulsivity mislabeled as ODD/defiance. Child disciplined/suspended for ADHD-driven behaviors. Zero tolerance policies disproportionately affect ADHD children. School-to-prison pipeline risk for ADHD + conduct issues. Medication alone insufficient for combined ADHD + ODD.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD impulsivity vs ODD defiance differentiation guide. Discipline record tracker with manifestation determination guidance. Behavioral parent training program finder. School discipline rights guide for ADHD families. FBA request template specifically addressing ADHD + behavioral concerns.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.4\\\", \\\"Address Emotional Dysregulation\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional dysregulation is increasingly recognized as a CORE feature of ADHD, not just a co-occurring condition. Includes: intense emotional reactions disproportionate to situation, difficulty calming down once upset, low frustration tolerance, rejection sensitivity ('Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria' \\u2014 not a formal diagnosis but widely discussed), rapid mood shifts, emotional impulsivity (saying hurtful things in the moment). Treatment: emotional regulation skills training, CBT, medication (some children respond to guanfacine or atomoxetine for emotional regulation), dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) skills for adolescents.\\\",\\n     \\\"Therapist\\\\nPsychiatrist\\\", \\\"School\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional regulation assessment. Mood tracking. Evaluation for co-occurring mood disorders.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 skill building is long-term.\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional dysregulation misdiagnosed as bipolar disorder or mood disorder. Medication changes made for 'mood instability' when issue is ADHD emotional dysregulation. RSD causes social withdrawal and avoidance. Emotional outbursts at school lead to suspension/restraint. Parent doesn't recognize emotional component as part of ADHD.\\\",\\n     \\\"Emotional dysregulation education resource for parents (it's ADHD, not just 'bad behavior'). Mood tracking tool. Coping skills library organized by age. RSD education and management strategies. School crisis plan template for emotional dysregulation. DBT skills app recommendations for teens.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 8: LONG-TERM & TRANSITION\\n    [\\\"8\\\\nLong-Term\\\\nPlanning &\\\\nTransition\\\", \\\"8.1\\\", \\\"Ongoing Medication Management & Monitoring\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD medication requires ongoing management: (1) Growth monitoring every 3-6 months (height, weight, BMI). (2) Medication efficacy review \\u2014 is current dose/medication still working? (3) Dosage adjustments as child grows. (4) Annual medication 'holiday' consideration (controversial \\u2014 AAP does not recommend routine drug holidays). (5) Transition from pediatric to adult prescriber. (6) Medication diversion prevention for stimulants. (7) Manage ongoing shortage issues. (8) Re-evaluation of medication need as child matures. 30.1% of children with current ADHD receive neither medication nor behavioral treatment \\u2014 significant treatment gap.\\\",\\n     \\\"Prescribing Provider\\\", \\\"Pharmacy\\\\nInsurance\\\\nSchool nurse\\\",\\n     \\\"Growth charts. Medication monitoring schedule. Rating scales (repeat annually at minimum). Side effect checklist.\\\",\\n     \\\"Every 3-6 months for medication review. Annually for comprehensive re-assessment.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medication complacency \\u2014 'set it and forget it.' Growth suppression not monitored. Medication stops working (tolerance or dose needs adjustment) \\u2014 parent assumes ADHD is untreatable. Adolescent medication non-compliance. Provider shortage for stimulant prescribers. Pharmacy switches generic manufacturers \\u2014 different response.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medication monitoring dashboard (dose, growth, efficacy, side effects). Renewal reminder system. Rating scale repeat prompts. Growth trajectory tracker. Medication appointment preparation guide.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.2\\\", \\\"Annual IEP/504 Review \\u2014 ADHD-Specific Monitoring\\\",\\n     \\\"Annual IEP/504 review should assess: (1) Are accommodations still appropriate for current ADHD presentation? (2) Are IEP goals addressing executive function development? (3) Has medication change affected school needs? (4) Are co-occurring conditions (anxiety, LD) adequately addressed? (5) Is organizational support scaling appropriately with increasing academic demands? (6) Does child need more/fewer supports based on current functioning? Key: academic demands INCREASE each year while ADHD supports should EVOLVE, not just continue unchanged.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nPediatrician\\\\nTherapist\\\",\\n     \\\"Progress reports. Rating scales. Teacher input. Updated medical documentation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Annual IEP review. Triennial reassessment every 3 years. Parent can request review anytime.\\\",\\n     \\\"Same accommodations carried over without update. Executive function supports reduced as child ages (opposite needed \\u2014 EF demands increase). Medication changes at home not communicated to school. Success with supports used to justify removing them. Transition to middle school/high school with inadequate accommodation transfer.\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD IEP review checklist by grade band (elementary vs middle vs high). Accommodation evolution guide as academic demands increase. School transition preparation toolkit (elementary\\u2192middle, middle\\u2192high). Medication-school coordination template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.3\\\", \\\"Transition Planning (Age 14+)\\\",\\n     \\\"By age 14-16, IEP must include transition goals. For ADHD: (1) Self-advocacy \\u2014 understanding ADHD and communicating needs. (2) Self-management \\u2014 medication management, using organizational tools independently. (3) College preparation \\u2014 SAT/ACT accommodations (apply 6+ months early), college disability services registration, 504/IEP documentation transfer. (4) Career exploration \\u2014 careers matching ADHD strengths (entrepreneurship, creative fields, emergency services, sales). (5) Driving \\u2014 ADHD increases accident risk 2-4x; medication timing for driving important. (6) DOR services if vocational support needed. (7) Financial literacy \\u2014 ADHD impulsivity affects spending.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"DOR\\\\nCollege disability office\\\\nDMV (driving)\\\",\\n     \\\"Transition assessments. Post-secondary goals. SAT/ACT accommodation application. College disability services documentation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Begin by age 14 (recommended), required by 16.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 TRANSITION GAPS: Self-advocacy skills never taught \\u2014 child always had adults advocating. College disability services require STUDENT to self-identify and request accommodations (different from K-12). IEP accommodations don't automatically transfer to college. Medication management transitions from parent to teen \\u2014 gap in adherence. ADHD impulsivity + new freedom = higher risk period (substance use, risky driving, academic failure). Financial impulsivity without parental oversight.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 ADHD transition planning checklist by age (14, 16, 18). SAT/ACT accommodation application timeline and template. College disability services preparation guide. Self-advocacy skills curriculum for ADHD teens. Driving safety plan with medication timing. Independent medication management transition guide. Financial literacy resources adapted for ADHD.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.4\\\", \\\"Adult ADHD \\u2014 Ongoing Support\\\",\\n     \\\"ADHD persists into adulthood in 60-75% of cases. Adult needs: (1) Transition to adult prescriber \\u2014 many pediatric patients lose continuity. (2) Workplace accommodations under ADA. (3) Ongoing medication management. (4) Relationship skills \\u2014 ADHD affects partnerships. (5) Financial management strategies. (6) Executive function support systems. (7) Mental health monitoring \\u2014 ADHD adults at higher risk for depression, anxiety, substance use. (8) If co-occurring LD: workplace and continuing education accommodations. 15.5 million U.S. adults had ADHD diagnosis by late 2023 \\u2014 growing recognition of lifelong nature.\\\",\\n     \\\"Adult prescriber\\\\n(Psychiatrist/PCP)\\\", \\\"Employer (ADA accommodations)\\\\nTherapist\\\",\\n     \\\"Medical records transfer. (2) Medication is CENTRAL to treatment (70-80% response rate for stimulants) \\u2014 unlike autism where medication treats co-occurring but not core symptoms. (3) School eligibility is under OHI (Other Health Impairment), not Autism category. (4) 504 Plans are MORE COMMON than IEPs for ADHD (many ADHD children get 504 only). (5) Executive function is the CORE deficit \\u2014 all supports should target EF. (6) Co-occurring conditions are the RULE, not exception (78% have at least one). (7) Girls/inattentive type systematically under-identified. (8) Medication shortage is an ongoing practical barrier.\\\",\\n    \\\"\\\",\\n    \\\"KEY DIFFERENCES FROM PDA JOURNEY: (1) Traditional behavioral approaches (reward systems, structure) generally WORK for ADHD \\u2014 they BACKFIRE for PDA. (2) ADHD medication has strong evidence base \\u2014 PDA has no medication-specific treatment. (3) ADHD evaluation is straightforward with validated tools \\u2014 PDA has no standardized assessment. (4) ADHD is universally recognized by U.S. clinicians \\u2014 PDA is not recognized by most.     "text": "**V7 \u2014 12 sheets, 63 entities, 48 sources, with the complete ADHD Journey Map.**\n\n**New Sheet: ADHD Journey Map \u2014 8 Phases, 26 Steps**\n\nHere's how ADHD differs from the Autism and PDA journeys:\n\n**Phase 1 \u2014 Recognition & Concern (2 steps)**\n- Parent/teacher notices attention, behavior, or learning patterns\n- Well-child visit with validated rating scales (Vanderbilt, Conners) \u2014 AAP requires multi-informant data (parent + teacher)\n- Girls/inattentive type systematically under-identified\n\n**Phase 2 \u2014 Evaluation & Diagnosis (2 steps)**\n- Full DSM-5 evaluation: **ADHD is a recognized diagnosis** (unlike PDA) \u2014 codes 314.00/314.01\n- Three presentations: Inattentive, Hyperactive-Impulsive, Combined\n- **1 in 9 U.S. children** (7.1M, 11.4%) have ever been diagnosed\n- 78% have at least one co-occurring condition\n\n**Phase 3 \u2014 Treatment Planning (3 steps)**\n- **Gold standard = medication + behavioral therapy** (this is the biggest difference from autism/PDA)\n- Stimulants: 70-80% response rate (methylphenidate, amphetamine families)\n- Behavioral parent training: CHADD Parent-to-Parent, Triple P, PCIT\n- MTA study evidence base for multimodal approach\n\n**Phase 4 \u2014 School Supports (5 steps)**\n- IEP eligibility under **OHI (Other Health Impairment)** \u2014 ADHD specifically listed\n- **504 Plans more common than IEPs for ADHD** (different from autism where IEP is standard)\n- OSEP has clarified: educational performance \u2260 just grades\n- Key accommodations: executive function support, organizational systems, daily report cards, movement breaks\n\n**Phase 5 \u2014 Insurance & Therapy (2 steps)**\n- Medication coverage with ongoing shortage issues (71.5% had difficulty filling prescriptions)\n- Behavioral therapy + organizational skills training + social skills groups\n\n**Phase 6 \u2014 Home & Daily Life (2 steps)**\n- ADHD-friendly home environment modifications\n- Executive function skill building \u2014 the **core deficit** of ADHD\n\n**Phase 7 \u2014 Co-occurring Conditions (4 steps)** \u2014 UNIQUE to ADHD journey\n- Anxiety co-occurrence (~40%)\n- Learning disabilities (~30-50%) \u2014 medication helps attention but does NOT remediate LD\n- Behavioral/conduct problems (~50%) \u2014 ODD, conduct disorder\n- Emotional dysregulation \u2014 increasingly recognized as core ADHD feature, not just co-occurring\n\n**Phase 8 \u2014 Long-Term Planning & Transition (4 steps)**\n- Ongoing medication management and monitoring\n- Annual IEP/504 evolution (demands increase each year)\n- Transition planning with SAT/ACT accommodations, college disability services, driving safety\n- Adult ADHD: 60-75% persistence, 15.5M U.S. adults diagnosed\n\n**3 ADHD entities added to Master Matrix:** CHADD, ADDitude Magazine, AACAP\n\n**10 ADHD sources added** (CHADD, ADDitude, CDC data, Danielson 2024 prevalence study, NCHS Data Brief, AACAP, AAP Guidelines, OSEP guidance, Understood.org ADHD, ADD Resource Center)\n\n**Key cross-journey comparisons built into the summary rows** showing how ADHD navigation differs from Autism, PDA, and SLD \u2014 critical for Waypoint's decision engine to route families to the right journey map.",

Once again, SLPs must utilize state regulations and any applicable guidance to \u00b7 ensure compliance with state and federal regulations when considering whether or not students \u00b7 continue to receive services in schools under IDEA. Evaluations of Students for Speech and Language Services \u00b7 Although SLPs are trained clinically to determine if an impairment exists, school-based \u00b7 SLPs must work as part of an interdisciplinary team that uses a variety of assessment tools and \u00b7 strategies to gather developmental, functional, and academic information (U.S. Department of \u00b7 Education, 2006; CFR 300.304). The team\u2019s evaluation should consider a student\u2019s abilities \u00b7 across tasks and settings to examine a student\u2019s communicative functioning in an educational ...\n\nSome children may require specific services to be written into their IEP so they can benefit \u00b7 from their special education program. In order to make decisions about related services, IEP teams \u00b7 should review evaluation data and document their decisions. Related service providers with \u00b7 expertise should have input and may be a part of the IEP team or may contribute information \u00b7 in writing or by consulting with parents or staff. Some states, such as Arizona, Florida, North \u00b7 Carolina, and Virginia, offer guidance for related services to assist IEP teams with evaluation and ... whether the child has an identification of SLI or is receiving speech-language services as a related ... federal and state criteria, then their identification as a child with SLI ends. There is no separate \u00b7 federal criteria to end services, and all requirements for evaluation and documentation apply \u00b7\n\nRoseberry-McKibbin, C. (2012, July). Narratives are sensitive indicators of language impairment in students; children and \u00b7 adolescents with compromised language skills typically produce shorter, less complete, and \u00b7 less elaborate narratives than their same-age, typical peers. Therefore, assessment of students\u2019 \u00b7 narrative abilities is an essential part of a comprehensive speech-language assessment. \u201cThe diagnostic accuracy of distinguishing a language difference from language impairment \u00b7 is substantially increased when LSA is used in conjunction with standardized testing\u201d (Horton-Ikard, 2010, p. 17). Research shows that the diagnostic accuracy of narrative retells is high in addition \u00b7 to being a sensitive indicator of language impairment. This makes LSA a valuable tool in resolving \u00b7 the difference vs. disorder conundrum (Horton-Ikard, 2010; Pe\u00f1a, Gilliam, & Bedore, 2004).\n\navailable to assist members of the IEP team in making a determination of need for services. Use of state-specific criteria is required and consideration of guidance and best practice \u00b7 information is critical. Recall that state eligibility criteria may contain specific language to: (1) mandate use of specific evaluation practices and ensure adequate documentation, (2) document \u00b7 the presence or absence of specific features, or (3) provide exclusions intended to limit mis- or over- identification. Additionally, attention to research and evidence-based practice recommendations \u00b7 for diagnostic accuracy, analysis and interpretation of assessment, considerations for culture and \u00b7 socioeconomic status, and other factors is vital for the decision making process. ...         "text": "Additionally, under IDEA, students who do not meet state eligibility criteria for speech-language impairment may still receive speech and language services as a related service. In order to receive related services, a child must be eligible under at least one disability category and the child&#x27;s Individual Education Program (IEP) team must determine that speech and language services are required (\u00a7300.304). Under IDEA and state regulations, all educational decisions are made by the team. No single person may determine if a child is eligible for services or determine the amount or type of services. Speech-language impairment is defined in IDEA as \u201ca communication disorder, such as stuttering, impaired articulation, a language impairment, or a voice impairment, that adversely affects a child&#x27;s educational performance,\u201d (34 CFR \u00a7300.8 a 11). Many states, including Louisiana, Ohio, Hawaii, Tennessee, Arizona, South Carolina, and North Dakota, use the federal definitions and/or regulations a\n\nIt is critically important that speech-language pathologists understand the difference between educational identification of a speech-language impairment under the Individuals with Disabilities Edu...\n\nAdditionally, under IDEA, students who do not meet state eligibility criteria for speech-language impairment may still receive speech and language services as a related service. In order to receive related services, a child must be eligible under at least one disability category and the child's Individual Education Program (IEP) team must determine that speech and language services are required (\u00a7300.304). Under IDEA and state regulations, all educational decisions are made by the team. No single person may determine if a child is eligible for services or determine the amount or type of services. Speech-language impairment is defined in IDEA as \u201ca communication disorder, such as stuttering, impaired articulation, a language impairment, or a voice impairment, that adversely affects a child's educational performance,\u201d (34 CFR \u00a7300.8 a 11). Many states, including Louisiana, Ohio, Hawaii, Tennessee, Arizona, South Carolina, and North Dakota, use the federal definitions and/or regulations a\n\nSimilarly, students who receive speech-language services as a related service have no separate federal criteria for ending a related service. In the absence of any state or local criteria, IEP teams must review the data and determine if the services continue to be required or are no longer required. Once again, SLPs must utilize state regulations and any applicable guidance to ensure compliance with state and federal regulations when considering whether or not students continue to receive services in schools under IDEA. Although SLPs are trained clinically to determine if an impairment exists, school-based SLPs must work as part of an interdisciplinary team that uses a variety of assessment tools and strategies to gather developmental, functional, and academic information (U.S. Department of Education, 2006; CFR 300.304). The team's evaluation should consider a student's abilities across tasks and settings to examine a student's communicative functioning in an educational program.\n\nOne way to highlight the difference is to review the criteria for the specific disability category mandated by IDEA and their own state and local special education regulations. Speech-language pathologists and teams should, and in some states are required to, have data to substantiate the educational impact and need for specially designed instruction that is a direct result of the child's impairment. It is possible for a student to have a clinical diagnosis of SLI but not meet the federal and state criteria for an educational identification as a child with a speech-language impairment under IDEA or require services through an IEP. Although this article addresses the requirements and best practice for identification of students under IDEA, many SLPs are increasingly being asked to support students in other programs that support students who may be struggling such as MTSS and RtI.",

        "text": "The first step in the dismissal process should occur when the student is first determined to be eligible for services through an IEP. The SLP should make the goals of speech-language pathology services clear to parents and teachers. The goals are as follows: to determine if the student&#x27;s communication disorder is adversely affecting academic achievement and functional performance; to provide intervention for those communication disorders that are adversely affecting academic achievement and functional performance, specifying goals leading to specific criteria for dismissal; to dismiss the student from speech-language pathology services once the criteria for eligibility are no longer met. Excerpt from: &quot;Implementing IDEA 2004 Part I: Conducting Educationally Relevant Evaluations, Technical Assistance for Speech-Language Pathologists&quot; (out of print).\n\nThe first step in the dismissal process should occur when the student is first determined to be eligible for services through an IEP. The SLP should make the goals of speech-language pathology services clear to parents and teachers. The goals are as follows: to determine if the student's communication disorder is adversely affecting academic achievement and functional performance; to provide intervention for those communication disorders that are adversely affecting academic achievement and functional performance, specifying goals leading to specific criteria for dismissal; to dismiss the student from speech-language pathology services once the criteria for eligibility are no longer met. This letter interprets the term \"adversely affects educational performance\" as it relates to students with speech language disorders. Cognitive Referencing is the practice of comparing IQ scores and language scores as a factor for determining eligibility for speech-language intervention. It is based on the assumption that language functioning cannot surpass cognitive levels. However, according to research, some language abilities may in fact surpass cognitive levels. Therefore, ASHA does not support the use of cognitive referencing. This interactive map provides links to state speech-language eligibility and dismissal guidelines. It categorizes states into three groups: those with both eligibility and dismissal guidelines, those with eligibility guidelines only, and those without guidelines.",

        "text": "The evaluation reports completed by individual professionals should clearly identify the presence of an impairment. However, the presence of a speech-language impairment does not equal eligibility for speech-language services under IDEA.\n\nSam, age 6, is receiving services for a speech-sound disorder from a hospital-based speech-language pathologist. Upon the clinician\u2019s recommendation, Sam\u2019s mother calls the school to arrange for her son to receive speech-language services there. The eligibility team decides that Sam is not eligible for special education services with a disability of speech-language impairment. Aaron, age 5, has parents who believe he should receive speech-language services in school and that they should obtain a prescription from his pediatrician for twice-weekly speech-language services. The school informs the parents that it will accept the referral but is not obligated to follow the physician\u2019s prescription. Maria, age 6, arrived in the United States from Guatemala nine months ago. Her first-grade teacher has referred Maria for an evaluation for special education based on the difficulty she is having understanding and speaking English in the classroom.\n\nIDEA defines special education as \u201cspecially designed instruction, at no cost to the parents, to meet the unique needs of a child with a disability.\u201d The focus is on instruction, highlighting the importance of enabling the child with a disability to succeed academically. The team may determine that a child with only a speech-language impairment is eligible for special education services (a \u201cspeech-only\u201d student). The establishment of services for a child who is eligible for special education is a separate step. Another interdisciplinary team (often with comparable membership) develops the Individualized Education Program (IEP), which identifies the educational goals and the services provided to meet those goals. IDEA specifies that the IEP team assigns related services. Speech-language impairments are unique in that IDEA views speech-language services as both special education and as related services.\n\nReaders of the ED figures regarding incidence of speech-language impairment must recognize that these figures represent approximately 45% of the population of children found eligible for special education with a speech-language impairment. Formal and informal assessment of articulation, language, voice, and fluency (e.g., standardized tests, language samples). ... Review of classroom performance (e.g., work samples, journals, narratives, criterion-referenced tests). ... Review of student record (e.g., performance on state or district assessments of pre-reading and reading abilities, attendance, discipline, health records, universal screening results). ...         "text": "Students who have a speech or language impairment, but do not qualify for special education services under IDEA, may be eligible for a 504 plan, under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. For more information, see ASHA\u2019s Practice Portal page on Documentation in Schools and ASHA\u2019s resource on individualized education programs (IEPs), individualized family service plans (IFSPs), and Section 504 plans. See also The ASHA Leader article on the IDEA guidance about the eligibility of developmental language disorders under IDEA. Children with spoken language disorders can show different patterns of strengths and weaknesses across listening, speaking, reading, and writing, and these patterns may vary over time. In some cases, the language skills of children with spoken language disorders can seem similar to those of children without spoken language disorders.\n\nStudents who have a speech or language impairment, but do not qualify for special education services under IDEA, may be eligible for a 504 plan, under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. For more information, see ASHA\u2019s Practice Portal page on Documentation in Schools and ASHA\u2019s resource on individualized education programs (IEPs), individualized family service plans (IFSPs), and Section 504 plans. See also The ASHA Leader article on the IDEA guidance about the eligibility of developmental language disorders under IDEA. Children with spoken language disorders can show different patterns of strengths and weaknesses across listening, speaking, reading, and writing, and these patterns may vary over time. In some cases, the language skills of children with spoken language disorders can seem similar to those of children without spoken language disorders.\n\nRather than a strictly dialect versus disorder framework, a disorder within dialect framework keeps the nature and prevalence of childhood language disorders across dialects at the forefront when considering screening, assessment, and treatment planning as well as when providing education about the services that clinicians provide (Oetting et al., 2016). Multilingualism is not a cause of speech or language disorders. In multilingual children, language dominance may vary across domains of language, and competence may vary across named languages. For example, children may exhibit receptive language dominance in one language and expressive language dominance in another. Dominance may also shift over time relative to environmental linguistic demands. For multilingual children who have language impairment, typical processes of multilingualism, such as code-mixing, will be present in their speech (Guti\u00e9rrez-Clellen et al., 2009).\n\nThe prevalence of specific language impairment for kindergarten children has been widely cited as 7.4% overall in the United States (Tomblin et al., 1997). This study\u2019s use of the term \u201cspecific language impairment\u201d refers to children who exhibit a language disorder but score within normal limits on nonverbal intelligence tests (Tomblin et al., 1997). It should be noted that the use of intelligence test score discrepancies to determine eligibility for language services is not a practice supported by ASHA. See the Cognitive Referencing section below for further explanation and a link to ASHA\u2019s resource on this topic. Studies reported results based on gender; however, there were no indications whether the data collected were based on sex assigned at birth and/or gender identity. In 4- to 5-year-old children, the prevalence of spoken language disorder, which may or may not be associated with a diagnosed medical condition, was higher in boys (Beitchman et al., 1986; Weindrich et al., 2000)\n\nResearchers do not fully understand the etiology of DLD or specific language impairment. It is useful to consider the cause as an interaction of multiple, complex factors. DLD tends to run in families (Bishop et al., 1995; Tomblin, 1989), and multiple genetic factors contribute to the presence of DLD (see Mountford et al., 2022, for a review). For example, a genetic variation might lead to a difference in brain morphology or function that leads to a difference in cognitive processing (Reed, 2012). Research on DLD reveals causation at multiple levels: cognitive processing deficits (e.g., Ellis Weismer & Evans, 2002; Leonard et al., 2007; Miller et al., 2001) ... neurological differences, observed through brain imaging studies (e.g., Ellis Weismer et al., 2005; Galaburda, 1989; Hugdahl et al., 2004) Speech-language pathologists (SLPs) play a critical role in the screening, assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of preschool and school-age children with spoken language disorders.",

The school informs the parents that it will accept the referral but is not obligated to follow the physician\u2019s prescription. Maria, age 6, arrived in the United States from Guatemala nine months ago. Her first-grade teacher has referred Maria for an evaluation for special education based on the difficulty she is having understanding and speaking English in the classroom.\n\nIDEA defines special education as \u201cspecially designed instruction, at no cost to the parents, to meet the unique needs of a child with a disability.\u201d The focus is on instruction, highlighting the importance of enabling the child with a disability to succeed academically. The team may determine that a child with only a speech-language impairment is eligible for special education services (a \u201cspeech-only\u201d student). The establishment of services for a child who is eligible for special education is a separate step. Another interdisciplinary team (often with comparable membership) develops the Individualized Education Program (IEP), which identifies the educational goals and the services provided to meet those goals. IDEA specifies that the IEP team assigns related services. Speech-language impairments are unique in that IDEA views speech-language services as both special education and as related services.\n\nReaders of the ED figures regarding incidence of speech-language impairment must recognize that these figures represent approximately 45% of the population of children found eligible for special education with a speech-language impairment. When a speech-language impairment is suspected, evaluation typically includes: Information obtained from the parents (medical, educational history; concerns regarding their child\u2019s communication). Hearing screening. Formal and informal assessment of articulation, language, voice, and fluency (e.g., standardized tests, language samples). Review of classroom performance (e.g., work samples, journals, narratives, criterion-referenced tests). Review of student record (e.g., performance on state or district assessments of pre-reading and reading abilities, attendance, discipline, health records, universal screening results). An Individualized Family Service Plan (IFSP) is a written plan for providing early intervention services to an infant or toddler (age 0\u20133), with a disability and to the family. The early intervention/IFSP process includes identification and referral, intake and family assessment, child evaluation and assessment, IFSP development, and service delivery and transition. The IFSP must include: a statement of the child\u2019s present levels of functioning including physical, cognitive, communication, and social-emotional development \u00b7 a statement of family concerns, priorities, and resources related to the child\u2019s development \u00b7\n\nSection 504 requires that school districts provide a free appropriate public education (FAPE) to qualified students in their jurisdictions who have a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities. Section 504 is a federal law designed to protect the rights of individuals with disabilities in programs and activities that receive federal financial assistance from the U.S. Department of Education (ED). Section 504 plans are designed for students who have disabilities but may not qualify to receive special education services. These plans include accommodations or modifications to support student access to the educational curriculum. This page seeks to highlight the requirements of each program.         "text": "We considered systematic reviews, ... and one review. Results. Early intensive intervention in three- and four-year-old children has a positive effect on phonological expressive and receptive skills and acquisitions are maintained in the medium term....\n\nBackground. Language disorder is the most frequent developmental disorder in childhood and it has a significant negative impact on children\u2019s development. The goal of the present review was to systematically analyze the effectiveness of interventions in children with developmental language disorder (DLD) from an evidence-based perspective. Methods. We considered systematic reviews, meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials (RCTs), control group cohort studies on any type of intervention aimed at improving children\u2019s skills in the phono-articulatory, phonological, semantic-lexical, and morpho-syntactic fields in preschool and primary school children (up to eight years of age) that were diagnosed with DLD. We identified 27 full-length studies, 26 RCT and one review. Results. Early intensive intervention in three- and four-year-old children has a positive effect on phonological expressive and receptive skills and acquisitions are maintained in the medium term.\n\nThe purpose of Gillam et al.\u2019s study [87] was to determine whether a computer instructional program that was designed to improve auditory temporal processing skills (Fast ForWord-Language\u2014FFW-L [100]) was more effective than other types of intervention for improving language and auditory processing in children with DLD. Two-hundred and sixteen children were randomly assigned to one of the four arms of intervention (see Appendix B for details). The children in all four arms made significant improvement in auditory processing, receptive and expressive language measures from pre- to post-testing, as well as in the follow-up three and six months later. Nevertheless, the children who received FFW-L did not do better than children in other interventions of equal intensity and primary outcome. In Cohen et al.\u2019s study [88], seventy-seven children with receptive-expressive specific language impairment were randomly assigned to Fast ForWord intervention [100], an alternative computer-based inter\n\nAlthough the term DLD is now frequently used [34], the terms \u2018Primary Language Impairment\u2019 and \u2018Primary Language Disorder\u2019 have also been used to account for the a-specificity of this language disorder and its unknown origin [24,35,36] (for further details, see the Method section). Thus, following the more recent international consensus [34], we will refer to language problems throughout the present systematic review in terms of DLD, regardless of how authors of previous papers, as reported in this review, named it. The language difficulties of children with DLD often have severe consequences in pre-school and early primary school.\n\nIn fact, language intervention during development may not only have short-term outcomes on the language component treated, but also medium- and long-term influences on the global development path. The links between oral language acquisition and written language learning are well-known; also important are the negative consequences on the quality of social integration and the emotional development of children with language disorder (e.g., Ref. [44]). At the same time, research in this area is made complex by the need to identify modalities of intervention that, on the one hand, reflect the variability of the disorder in its components and in different age groups and, on the other hand, take the variables that intervene in determining stable and lasting changes in children with DLD into account. The international literature offers a wide range of rehabilitation interventions aimed at children with DLD.",

        "text": "With an estimated prevalence of 7.58% (Norbury et al., 2016; see also Tomblin et al., 1997), it is nearly 7 times more common than autism spectrum disorder (ASD; prevalence = 1.1%; Brugha et al., 2012) and 46 times more common than permanent ...\n\nPurposeFor over two decades, we have known that children with developmental language disorder (DLD) are underserved. We have also known that DLD does not attract the research attention that it meri...\n\nDevelopmental language disorder (DLD) is a neurodevelopmental condition that emerges in early childhood and frequently persists into adulthood. People with DLD have significant difficulty learning, understanding, and using spoken language. Under U.S. Public Law 101-476 (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act [IDEA], 2004; first issued in 1990 and reissued in 2004), children may be eligible for school-based services, typically under the category \u201cspeech-language impairment,\u201d if their DLD affects educational performance and requires specially designed support. DLD is one of the most common neurodevelopmental disorders. With an estimated prevalence of 7.58% (Norbury et al., 2016; see also Tomblin et al., 1997), it is nearly 7 times more common than autism spectrum disorder (ASD; prevalence = 1.1%; Brugha et al., 2012) and 46 times more common than permanent childhood hearing impairment (prevalence = 0.165%; Fortnum et al., 2001).\n\nAlthough we were alerted to the situation more than 20 years ago, we continue to underserve children with DLD. Worse still, some are more likely to go without service than others. Morgan et al. (2017) analyzed data from two nationally representative cohorts of U.S. kindergartners, one collected in 1998\u20131999 (N = 16,800) and another collected in 2010\u20132011 (N = 12,080), to determine whether receipt of special education services for children deemed eligible under the category of speech-language impairment varied with family and child characteristics, academic achievement, and behavioral health. Numerous, long-standing inequities were evident. Morgan et al. (2017) found that boys were significantly more likely to receive services than girls in 1999 and 2011. This disproportionality also characterizes language services in U.K. schools (Lindsay & Strand, 2016). To some extent, the larger representation of boys could be fair given that community samples do reveal a higher prevalence of DLD am\n\nIn 2010, Bishop authored a paper titled \u201cWhich neurodevelopmental disorders get researched and why?\u201d She searched Web of Science, an extensive repository of published research, for titles that included the names of 35 neurodevelopmental disorders. A primary question concerned the attention given to DLD (at that time, usually called specific language impairment) relative to the other disorders. To make a fair comparison, she considered two variables: prevalence, given the logic that more prevalent disorders should be studied more often than those less prevalent, and severity, given the logic that more severely disabling disorders should be studied more often than those less severe. Fortunately, the two are inversely related; severely disabling conditions are rare. As predicted, severity was a positive predictor of publication rate (see Figure 1), and the consideration of prevalence estimates further strengthened that prediction.",

        "text": "In the school setting, children with spoken language disorders who meet the eligibility criteria under IDEA receive special education services through an individualized education program under the primary or secondary disability category \u201cspeech or language impairment\u201d (IDEA, 2004). Younger children may qualify under the category \u201cdevelopmental delay,\u201d whereas older children often qualify under \u201cspecific learning disability.\u201d Eligibility criteria include a disability that adversely affects educational performance, which is not limited to academic performance. See ASHA\u2019s resource on eligibility and dismissal in schools. Students who have a speech or language impairment, but do not qualify for special education services under IDEA, may be eligible for a 504 plan, under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. For more information, see ASHA\u2019s Practice Portal page on Documentation in Schools and ASHA\u2019s resource on individualized education programs (IEPs), individualized family servic\n\nThe following studies of developmental language disorder (DLD) estimate the prevalence of spoken language disorder that cannot be attributed to a medical diagnosis or condition. For 4- to 6-year-olds in the United Kingdom and China, the prevalence of DLD was 7.58%\u20138.5% (Norbury et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2023). Another study found that the prevalence of DLD was 6.4% at 10 years of age for children in Australia (Calder et al., 2022). Assuming a 7.5% prevalence rate, the estimated number of cases of DLD in the United States in 2019 was 5,461,200 (McGregor, 2020). The prevalence of specific language impairment for kindergarten children has been widely cited as 7.4% overall in the United States (Tomblin et al., 1997). This study\u2019s use of the term \u201cspecific language impairment\u201d refers to children who exhibit a language disorder but score within normal limits on nonverbal intelligence tests (Tomblin et al., 1997).\n\nIn the school setting, children with spoken language disorders who meet the eligibility criteria under IDEA receive special education services through an individualized education program under the primary or secondary disability category \u201cspeech or language impairment\u201d (IDEA, 2004). Younger children may qualify under the category \u201cdevelopmental delay,\u201d whereas older children often qualify under \u201cspecific learning disability.\u201d Eligibility criteria include a disability that adversely affects educational performance, which is not limited to academic performance. See ASHA\u2019s resource on eligibility and dismissal in schools. Students who have a speech or language impairment, but do not qualify for special education services under IDEA, may be eligible for a 504 plan, under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. For more information, see ASHA\u2019s Practice Portal page on Documentation in Schools and ASHA\u2019s resource on individualized education programs (IEPs), individualized family servic\n\nDevelopmental language disorder (DLD) is used when the spoken language disorder is a primary disability without a known medical cause and persisting at school age and beyond (Bishop et al., 2017). DLD is also used when the language disorder co-occurs with other diagnoses such as attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder or developmental coordination disorder, but the causal relationship is not as obvious (Bishop et al., 2017). Specific language impairment also appears in the literature. Some researchers may still use \u201cspecific language impairment\u201d with distinctions from DLD (McGregor et al., 2020; Rice, 2020). Language disorder associated with [condition] is used to describe a spoken language disorder that is secondary to another condition or diagnosis, such autism, Down syndrome, intellectual disability, traumatic brain injury, or sensory impairment.\n\nZero-tolerance policies may enforce punishment without consideration of how language impairments and additional diagnoses impact a student\u2019s behavior (Stanford, 2020). For youth involved in the juvenile justice system up to 21 years of age, 60%\u201364% were found to have a language disorder, with 50% being considered to have a moderate disorder and 10% a severe language disorder (Chow et al., 2022; Zupan et al., 2022). See The ASHA Leader article, Casualties of Misunderstanding: Communication Disorders and Juvenile Injustice. Spoken language disorders have a variety of causes. In some cases, the cause is clearly environmental such as traumatic brain injury or fetal alcohol syndrome. Sometimes, the cause is due to a single affected gene, such as Down syndrome or fragile X syndrome. Most cases are due to multiple affected genes, such as autism, intellectual disability, and developmental language disorder (DLD).",

Among those with any communication disorder, non-Hispanic black and Hispanic children were less likely than non-Hispanic white children to receive intervention services for their disorders. Girls were less likely than boys and older children were less likely than younger children to receive intervention services.         "text": "The prevalence of DLD in this study was lower than previous reports in school-aged children (Norbury et al., 2016; Tomblin et al., 1997), at 3.70% among cases+controls. Identification of DLD cases was based on retrospective self-reported language ...\n\nThe prevalence of DLD in this study was lower than previous reports in school-aged children (Norbury et al., 2016; Tomblin et al., 1997), at 3.70% among cases+controls. Identification of DLD cases was based on retrospective self-reported language difficulties and having received language intervention during childhood, not on prior or current assessment of language impairment symptoms. Thus, we expect that recall bias is likely to contribute to the low prevalence, especially given that not all children with DLD receive intervention (Tomblin et al., 1997). Also, we would miss cases who self-reported language difficulties but did not receive language intervention or vice versa, but it is unlikely that these would be misclassified as DLD controls due to the DLD control inclusion criteria. The prevalence of DLD was much lower (0.04%) when considering ICD diagnoses from the hospital registers.\n\nThe reason for that was that we did not expect participants to have reliable recollection of such childhood services. If some individuals did remember receiving consultation-based intervention, they probably responded \u2018yes\u2019 to question 3d about other form of language intervention (Supplementary Material). As indicated in Fig. 2, there was no clear change over time for this form of intervention. The expected increase over time of inclusive education for individuals with DLD was not confirmed by the present study (de L\u00f3pez and Knudsen, 2019). DLD status was highly associated with both reading and learning difficulties. The prevalence of reading impairment in DLD cases was \u223c19%. This represents the lower range of previously reported prevalence in population-based DLD samples of school-aged children (Catts et al., 2005; Nippold and Tomblin, 2014).\n\nDevelopmental language disorder (DLD) is characterized by enduring low language abilities with a significant functional impact, in the absence of biomedical conditions in which language impairment is part of a complex of impairments. There is a lack of awareness of DLD even among healthcare professionals. Here we estimated the prevalence of DLD and its links to reading and learning difficulties and physical and mental health in the Danish Blood Donor Study (N = 46,547), where DLD-related information is based on questionnaires (self-report). We compared the questionnaire-derived DLD status with the relevant language-related diagnoses from hospital registers. We also investigated the genetic architecture of DLD in a subset of the cohort (N = 18,380). DLD was significantly associated with reading and learning difficulties and poorer mental and physical health.\n\nDLD prevalence was 3.36%\u20133.70% based on questionnaires, compared with 0.04% in hospital registers. Our genetic analyses identified one genome-wide significant locus, but not a significant heritability estimate. Our study shows that DLD has health-related implications that may last into adulthood, and that DLD may be undiagnosed in general healthcare. Furthermore, DLD is likely more genetically heterogeneous than narrower developmental language phenotypes. Our results emphasize the need to raise awareness of DLD and consider criteria for molecular studies of DLD to reduce case heterogeneity. ... Developmental language disorder (DLD) is defined as enduring substantially low language abilities which significantly affect daily functioning, in the absence of biomedical conditions (e.g. sensory-neural hearing loss, intellectual impairment or autism spectrum disorder) where language impairment is only part of a complex of impairments (Bishop et al., 2017).",

By the first grade, roughly 5% of children have noticeable speech disorders, including stuttering, speech sound disorders, and dysarthria; the majority of these speech disorders have no known cause.6,7 \u00b7 More than 3 million Americans (about 1%) stutter. Stuttering can affect individuals of all ages but occurs most frequently in young children between the ages of 2 and 6. Boys are two to three times more likely than girls to stutter. Although most children who stutter outgrow the condition while young, as many as 1 in 4 will continue to stutter for the rest of their lives, a condition known as persistent developmental stuttering.8,9 \u00b7 Developmental language disorder (also called specific language impairment) has a prevalence of 7% or approximately 1 in 14 children.10,11 \u00b7\n\nResearch suggests that the first 6 months of life are the most crucial to a child\u2019s development of language skills. For a person to become fully competent in any language, exposure must begin as early as possible, preferably before school age.12,13 \u00b7 Anyone can acquire aphasia (a loss of the ability to use or understand language), but most people who have aphasia are in their middle to late years. Men and women are equally affected. Nearly 180,000 Americans acquire the disorder each year.14 About 2 million people in the United States currently have aphasia.14 \u00b7 Black, LI; Vahratian, A; Hoffman, HJ (2015): Communication disorders and use of intervention services among children aged 3\u201317 years: United States, 2012.         "title": "Developmental Language Disorder | NIDCD",
        "url": "https://www.nidcd.nih.gov/health/developmental-language-disorder",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

        "text": "It is one of the most common developmental disorders, affecting approximately 1 in 14 children in kindergarten. The impact of DLD persists into adulthood. DLD is a neurodevelopmental disorder.\n\nDevelopmental language disorder (DLD) is a communication disorder that interferes with learning, understanding, and using language. These language difficulties are not explained by other conditions, such as hearing loss or autism, or by extenuating circumstances, such as lack of exposure to language. DLD can affect a child\u2019s speaking, listening, reading, and writing. DLD has also been called specific language impairment, language delay, or developmental dysphasia. It is one of the most common developmental disorders, affecting approximately 1 in 14 children in kindergarten. The impact of DLD persists into adulthood. DLD is a neurodevelopmental disorder. Neurodevelopmental disorders are caused by complex interactions between genes and the environment that change brain development. The exact causes of the brain differences that lead to DLD are unknown.\n\nNeurodevelopmental disorders tend to run in families. Children with DLD are more likely than those without DLD to have parents and siblings who have also had difficulties and delays in language development. In fact, 50 to 70 percent of children with DLD have at least one family member with the disorder. In addition, other potentially related neurodevelopmental disorders, such as dyslexia or autism, are more common in the family members of a child with DLD. Learning more than one language at a time does not cause DLD. The disorder can, however, affect both multilingual children and children who speak only one language. For multilingual children, DLD will impact all languages spoken by a child. Importantly, learning multiple languages is not harmful for a child with DLD. A multilingual child with DLD will not struggle more than a child with DLD who speaks only one language.\n\nA child with DLD often has a history of being a late talker (reaching spoken language milestones later than peers). Although some late talkers eventually catch up with peers, children with DLD have persistent language difficulties. ... Be late to put words together into sentences. Struggle to learn new words and make conversation. Have difficulty following directions, not because they are stubborn, but because they do not fully understand the words spoken to them. Make frequent grammatical errors when speaking. Symptoms common in older children and adults with DLD include: Limited use of complex sentences. Difficulty finding the right words. Difficulty understanding figurative language. Reading problems. Disorganized storytelling and writing. Frequent grammatical and spelling errors. Language difficulties may be misinterpreted as a behavioral issue.\n\nIf a doctor, teacher, or parent suspects that a child has DLD, a speech-language pathologist (a professional trained to assess and treat people with speech or language problems) can evaluate the child\u2019s language skills. The type of evaluation depends on the child's age and the concerns that led to the evaluation. In general, an evaluation includes: Direct observation of the child. Interviews and questionnaires completed by parents and/or teachers. Assessments of the child\u2019s learning ability. Standardized tests of current language performance. These tools allow the speech-language pathologist to compare the child's language skills to those of same-age peers, identify specific difficulties, and plan for potential treatment targets. DLD is not the same thing as a learning disability. Instead, DLD is a risk factor for learning disabilities since problems with basic language skills affect classroom performance.",

          "url": "https://www.nidcd.nih.gov/health/developmental-language-disorder",
