        "text": "Approximately 9.9 % of children present with difficulties in language development (DLD), 7.6 % without serious additional impairments and 2.3 % associated with language-relevant comorbidities, e.g., hearing loss. Notably, in a consensus statement by experts in German-speaking countries, in ...\n\nApproximately 9.9 % of children present with difficulties in language development (DLD), 7.6 % without serious additional impairments and 2.3 % associated with language-relevant comorbidities, e.g., hearing loss. Notably, in a consensus statement by experts in German-speaking countries, in the guideline presented here, and further in this article, all of these disorders are referred to as \u201cdevelopmental language disorders\u201d (DLD), whereas the international consortium CATALISE only refers to those without comorbidities as DLD. DLDs are among the most commonly treated childhood disorders and, if persistent, often reduce educational and socio-economic outcome. Children in their third year of life with developmental language delay (late talkers, LT) are at risk of a later DLD. This German interdisciplinary clinical practice guideline reflects current knowledge regarding evidence-based interventions for developmental language delay and disorders.\n\nThe guideline recommends parent training (Hedges g = 0.38 to 0.82) for LTs with expressive language delay, language therapy (Cohen\u2019s d = \u20130.20 to 0.90) for LTs with additional receptive language delay or further DLD risk factors, phonological or integrated phonological treatment methods (Cohen\u2019s d = 0.89 to 1.04) for phonological speech sound disorders (SSDs), a motor approach for isolated phonetic SSDs (non-DLD), and for lexical-semantic and morpho-syntactic impairments combinations of implicit and explicit intervention approaches (including input enrichment, modeling techniques, elicitation methods, creation of production opportunities, meta-linguistic-approaches, visualizations; Cohen\u2019s d = 0.89\u20131.04). Recommendations were also made for DLD associated with pragmatic-communicative impairment, bi-/multilingualism, hearing loss, intellectual disability, autism-spectrum disorders, selective mutism, language-relevant syndromes or multiple disabilities, and for intensive inpatient languag\n\nApproximately 9.9% of children present with difficulties in language development (1), making these among the most common and most frequently treated childhood disorders. Around 9% of all girls and 14.3% of boys insured by Germany\u2018s largest health insurance company currently receive speech-language therapy, mostly between the ages of 5 and 9 (2). In 7.4\u20137.6% of such cases, no serious co-occurring impairments are to be expected (1, 3). ICD-10 (F80.-) names such disorders \u201cspecific developmental disorders of speech and language\u201d (e1) but ICD-11 (6A01.0) refers to them simply as \u201cdevelopmental speech or language disorders\u201d and (6A01.2) \u201cdevelopmental language disorders\u201d (DLD) (e2). In a further 2.3% of children, problems with language development are associated with comorbidities, such as hearing loss, autism spectrum disorders (ASD), neurological disorders, or intellectual disability (1, 4, 5).\n\nIn the German guideline presented here (1) and in a consensus statement by experts in German-speaking countries (4), all of the above-named types of non-acquired childhood language disorders are referred to as \u201edevelopmental language disorders\u201c (DLD), whereas the international consortium CATALISE only refers to those without comorbidities as DLD (4). DLDs affect one or more linguistic domains expressively, i.e., concerning language production, and/or receptively, i.e., concerning language comprehension: phonological (speech sound production and use), lexical-semantic (vocabulary and word meaning), morpho-syntactic (grammar; structure of sentences and words), and/or communication (pragmatics) (6) (Box 1). The main components of grammar are syntax and morphology. Syntax determines how words are arranged into phrases and sentences, while morphology refers to the internal structure of words. The grammatical functions of words are indicated by noun and verb inflection, i.e., number and case",

        "title": "Childhood Speech and Language Disorders in the General U.S. Population - Speech and Language Disorders in Children - NCBI Bookshelf",

        "metadata": {

        "text": "Speech and language disorders in children include a variety of conditions that disrupt children&#x27;s ability to communicate. Severe speech and language disorders are particularly serious, preventing or impeding children&#x27;s participation in family and community, school achievement, and eventual employment. This chapter begins by providing an overview of speech and language development and disorders. It then addresses the following topics within the committee&#x27;s charge: (1) current standards of care for assessing and diagnosing speech and language disorders; (2) causes of and risk factors for these disorders; (3) their prevalence and its relationship to age, development, and gender; and (4) common comorbidities (i.e., other co-occurring conditions).\n\nEvidence indicates that stuttering affects only slightly more boys than girls during the preschool period, although higher ratios of affected males to females have been observed at later ages. Finally, approximately 60 percent of cases of developmental stuttering co-occur with other speech and language disorders (Kent and Vorperian, 2013). As with speech disorders, estimates of the prevalence of language disorders vary across studies by age, the presence of other neurodevelopmental disorders, and the diagnostic criteria employed. Language disorders with no known cause, sometimes referred to as \u201cspecific\u201d (or \u201cprimary\u201d) language impairments (e.g., Reilly et al., 2014), are highly prevalent, affecting 6-15 percent of children when identified through formal norm-referenced testing in population-based samples (Law et al., 2000). This is consistent with the cutoff values of 1.0-1.5 standard deviations below the mean employed in several investigations (e.g., Tomblin et al., 1997b).\n\nEstimates of the Prevalence of Speech and Language Disorders from Studies of U.S. Children. An examination of comorbidities (i.e., other co-occurring conditions) of speech and language disorders is complicated by the central role of language and communication in the development and behavior of children and adolescents. Speech and language disorders are a definitional component of certain conditions, most prominently autism spectrum disorder (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Other neurodevelopmental disorders, including cognitive impairment, are universally associated with varying degrees of delays and deficits in language and communication skills (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). In addition to their co-occurrence with a wide range of neurodevelopmental disorders, speech and language delays in toddlers and preschool-age children are associated with a significantly increased risk for long-term developmental challenges, such as language-based learning disorders (Beitchman\n\nFinally, speech and language delays and deficits may lead to impairments in other aspects of a child's functional skills (e.g., social interaction, behavior, academic achievement) even when not associated with other diagnosable disorders (Beitchman et al., 1996c, 2001, 2014; Brownlie et al., 2004; Voci et al., 2006; Young et al., 2002). This section, therefore, examines the association of speech and language disorders from the following perspectives: (1) speech and language disorders that are comorbid with other diagnosable disorders, and (2) speech and language disorders in early childhood that confer a quantifiable risk for the later development of comorbid conditions. Together, these two perspectives create a comprehensive picture of the association of speech and language disorders with other neurodevelopmental disorders. Autism spectrum disorder is a highly prevalent neurodevelopmental disorder, affecting an estimated 1 in 68 8-year-old children in the United States (CDC, 2014).\n\nChildren with comorbid conditions can be expected to be more severely impaired and to experience greater functional limitations (due to the interactive and cumulative effects of multiple conditions) than children who do not have comorbid conditions. Furthermore, young children with language impairments are at high risk for later manifestation of learning and psychiatric disorders. It is therefore important both to carefully examine the speech and language skills of children with other developmental disorders and to identify other neurodevelopmental disorders among children presenting with speech and language impairment. Among populations of children with conditions as diverse as autism spectrum disorder, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, traumatic brain injury, and genetic disorders, speech and language disorders may be the most easily identified impairments because of the central role of language and communication in the functional capacity of children and adolescents. 2-1. Bilingual development guide (bilingualism does NOT cause SLI). Hearing screening prompt at every concern. Late talker risk calculator. Link to ASHA parent resources.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"1.2\", \"Pediatrician Screening & Referral\",\n     \"AAP recommends developmental screening at 9, 18, and 30 months (ASQ-3 or PEDS). Speech/language-specific concerns should trigger: (1) Hearing test \u2014 MUST rule out hearing loss as cause of speech/language delay. (2) Referral to SLP (Speech-Language Pathologist) for evaluation. (3) For children under 3: referral to Early Start (California Part C early intervention). (4) For children 3+: referral to school district for evaluation. Pediatrician should NOT wait \u2014 early intervention is evidence-based and more effective than 'watch and wait.'\",\n     \"Pediatrician / PCP\", \"Audiologist (hearing test)\\nEarly Start / School District\",\n     \"Developmental screening results. Hearing test results (REQUIRED \u2014 hearing loss must be ruled out). Referral to SLP or Early Start/school district.\",\n     \"Hearing test: schedule ASAP. Early Start referral: services begin within 45 days. School district: 60 days from consent.\",\n     \"Pediatrician uses 'wait and see' instead of referring. Hearing test not done. Speech concerns addressed but language comprehension not assessed. Referral only to private SLP (family doesn't know about free school/Early Start services). Insurance doesn't cover evaluation or limits sessions.\",\n     \"Hearing test reminder at every speech/language concern. Early Start (Part C) auto-referral guide for under 3. School district referral guide for 3+. Private vs public SLP pathway comparison. Insurance speech therapy coverage checker.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 2\n    [\"2\\nEarly\\nIntervention\\n(Birth-3)\", \"2.1\", \"Early Start Referral & IFSP (Ages 0-3)\",\n     \"California's Early Start program (IDEA Part C) provides early intervention for infants/toddlers with developmental delays. Self-referral accepted \u2014 no doctor referral required. Regional Center coordinates evaluation and services. IFSP (Individualized Family Service Plan) developed \u2014 family-centered, addresses child AND family needs. Speech-language services may include: direct SLP therapy, parent coaching (teaching parents strategies for daily routines), and assistive technology. Hanen Centre 'It Takes Two to Talk' program widely used \u2014 teaches parents to be their child's primary language facilitator. Services delivered in 'natural environments' (home, daycare).\",\n     \"Regional Center\\n(Early Start)\", \"SLP\\nAudiologist\\nPediatrician\",\n     \"Referral to Early Start (self, doctor, or anyone). Developmental evaluation. IFSP development with family.\",\n     \"45 days from referral to IFSP and start of services. Services continue until age 3.\",\n     \"\u2605 EARLY START GAPS: Regional Center evaluates but may use narrow eligibility criteria \u2014 child must show 33% delay in one area or 25% in two areas. 'Mild' delay may not qualify despite parent concern. Service frequency may be insufficient (1x/month instead of weekly). Transition from Early Start to school district at age 3 is a critical vulnerability \u2014 services can stop abruptly. SLP shortage means waitlists even within Early Start.\",\n     \"\u2605 Early Start eligibility calculator (33%/25% delay thresholds). IFSP goal generator for speech/language. Parent coaching strategy library (Hanen-aligned). Transition planning prompt at 2.5 years (start planning 6 months before age 3). Service frequency advocacy guide.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"2.2\", \"Transition from Early Start to School District (Age 3)\",\n     \"At age 3, children transition from Early Start (Part C) to school district (Part B). This is one of the MOST CRITICAL transitions \u2014 services can change dramatically. School district must evaluate and develop IEP by child's 3rd birthday. Parent should: (1) Start transition planning at 2.5 years. (2) Attend transition meeting with RC and school district. (3) Request comprehensive speech-language evaluation. (4) Understand that service delivery model changes: from home-based/natural environment to school-based/pull-out or push-in. IFSP goals \u2260 IEP goals \u2014 IEP must address 'educational performance.'\",\n     \"Regional Center \u2192\\nSchool District\", \"SLP\\nParent\",\n     \"Transition meeting invitation. School district evaluation consent. IFSP records transfer. Written parental consent for evaluation.\",\n     \"Transition conference: at least 90 days before 3rd birthday. IEP in place by 3rd birthday.\",\n     \"\u2605 TRANSITION CLIFF: Services stop at age 3 if school district doesn't complete evaluation on time. School district provides less therapy than Early Start (30 min/week vs weekly home visits). Service model change (home \u2192 school) disrupts child's progress. Child doesn't qualify for IEP because educational impact not yet documented (they haven't been in school yet). 'Speech-only' IEP limits access to broader special education supports.\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL: Transition timeline tracker (countdown to 3rd birthday). Comparison tool: Early Start services vs. school IEP services. Transition meeting preparation checklist. Request for comprehensive evaluation letter. 'What changes at age 3' parent guide.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 3\n    [\"3\\nEvaluation &\\nDiagnosis\\n(Age 3+)\", \"3.1\", \"School District Speech-Language Evaluation\",\n     \"Parent requests evaluation (written request). Under IDEA, SLI is defined as 'a communication disorder, such as stuttering, impaired articulation, a language impairment, or a voice impairment, that adversely affects a child's educational performance' (34 CFR \u00a7300.8(c)(11)). Evaluation by school SLP should include: (1) Parent interview and history. (2) Hearing screening (REQUIRED). (3) Oral mechanism exam. (4) Standardized speech/language tests (articulation, receptive language, expressive language). (5) Language sample analysis. (6) Classroom observation. (7) Teacher input on educational impact. (8) Review of academic performance. SLI covers FOUR domains: articulation/speech sounds, language (receptive + expressive), fluency (stuttering), and voice.\",\n     \"Local School District\\n(School SLP)\", \"Parent\\nTeacher\\nAudiologist\",\n     \"Written evaluation request. Signed assessment plan (15-day timeline to present plan). Prior Early Start records. Hearing screening results. Medical history.\",\n     \"15 days: district presents assessment plan. 60 days from consent: evaluation completed.\",\n     \"\u2605 EVALUATION RISKS: Hearing not screened \u2014 hearing loss causes speech/language delays that are treatable but misidentified as SLI. Standardized tests biased against bilingual/bidialectal children \u2014 language DIFFERENCE misidentified as DISORDER. Cognitive referencing used (comparing IQ to language scores) \u2014 ASHA does NOT support this practice. Only expressive language tested \u2014 receptive language deficits missed. Language sample not collected \u2014 standardized tests alone miss real-world communication difficulties. Educational impact narrowly defined as academic grades only.\",\n     \"\u2605 Evaluation request letter generator for SLI. Hearing screening verification prompt. Bilingual evaluation rights guide (must evaluate in ALL languages). Cognitive referencing objection template (cite ASHA position). Comprehensive evaluation checklist covering all 4 SLI domains. Educational impact documentation beyond grades.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"3.2\", \"Private SLP Evaluation (Optional/Supplemental)\",\n     \"Private SLP evaluation may be pursued: (1) If school evaluation is delayed or incomplete. (2) For second opinion. (3) For more comprehensive assessment than school provides. (4) If parent suspects school will not qualify child. Private SLPs can diagnose speech/language disorders clinically \u2014 but clinical diagnosis \u2260 school eligibility. IMPORTANT: a child can have a clinical speech/language diagnosis but NOT qualify for school services if educational impact isn't demonstrated. Conversely, school can identify SLI without clinical diagnosis. Insurance may cover private SLP evaluation and therapy (check plan). Medi-Cal covers speech therapy.\",\n     \"Private SLP\", \"Insurance / Medi-Cal\\nPediatrician (referral)\",\n     \"Physician referral (some insurance requires). Insurance pre-authorization. Prior records.\",\n     \"Private evaluation: 1-4 weeks. Insurance auth: 5-15 business days.\",\n     \"Private evaluation contradicts school evaluation \u2014 confusing for parents. Insurance limits evaluation to brief screening, not comprehensive assessment. Private SLP diagnoses disorder but school says 'no educational impact.' Medi-Cal reimbursement rates low \u2014 fewer SLPs accept it. SLP shortage \u2014 waitlists for private evaluation, especially in rural areas.\",\n     \"Private vs school evaluation comparison guide. Insurance SLP coverage checker. Medi-Cal SLP provider finder. How to use private evaluation to support school IEP request.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"3.3\", \"Understand Diagnosis: Speech Sound Disorder vs DLD vs Stuttering vs Voice\",\n     \"SLI encompasses four distinct types, each with different prognosis and treatment: (1) SPEECH SOUND DISORDER (articulation/phonological) \u2014 difficulty producing sounds correctly. Most common type. ~8-9% of young children. Many resolve by school age with therapy. (2) DEVELOPMENTAL LANGUAGE DISORDER (DLD) \u2014 persistent difficulty understanding and/or using spoken language. 1 in 14 children (~7%). Does NOT resolve on its own. Impacts reading, writing, social skills, academic performance long-term. Previously called 'Specific Language Impairment.' (3) STUTTERING (fluency disorder) \u2014 affects ~1% of children. 75% recover naturally, 25% persist. (4) VOICE DISORDER \u2014 hoarseness, breathiness, abnormal pitch. Less common in children. Rarest SLI type.\",\n     \"SLP (School or Private)\", \"Pediatrician\\nENT (for voice disorders)\",\n     \"Completed evaluation with specific diagnosis type. Severity rating. Functional impact assessment.\",\n     \"At evaluation completion.\",\n     \"\u2605 DIAGNOSIS CONFUSION: Parents told child has 'speech delay' without distinguishing type \u2014 treatment differs dramatically for articulation vs DLD. DLD underdiagnosed \u2014 it's 7x more common than autism but gets far less research attention and public awareness. Stuttering assessed too early (normal disfluency in 2-5 year olds) or too late (early intervention most effective). Voice disorder not evaluated \u2014 hoarseness normalized. DLD misattributed to 'laziness,' ADHD, or behavior problems.\",\n     \"\u2605 SLI type differentiator tool \u2014 helps parents understand which type their child has. DLD awareness content ('the most common disorder you've never heard of'). Prognosis guide by type (articulation = good prognosis; DLD = persistent, needs ongoing support). Stuttering natural recovery vs persistence risk factors. Link to NIDCD DLD resources.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 4\n    [\"4\\nSchool\\nServices\\n(IEP)\", \"4.1\", \"IEP Development \u2014 SLI-Specific Services & Goals\",\n     \"SLI is UNIQUE under IDEA: speech-language services can be BOTH special education AND a related service. 'Speech-only' IEP: child's sole disability is SLI, and speech-language is the primary service. IEP SERVICES may include: (1) Direct SLP therapy (individual or small group). (2) Classroom-based/push-in services. (3) Consultation model (SLP advises teacher). (4) AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices/training if needed. IEP GOALS should be: specific, measurable, tied to educational impact (not just 'improve articulation' but 'produce /r/ sound in 80% of opportunities in classroom reading'). Common goal areas: articulation accuracy, sentence length/complexity, vocabulary, comprehension of directions, narrative skills, social communication, fluency management.\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"SLP\\nParent\\nTeacher\",\n     \"All evaluation reports. SLI diagnosis with type and severity. Educational impact documentation. Parent concerns letter.\",\n     \"30 days from completed assessment to IEP meeting.\",\n     \"\u2605 IEP PITFALLS: Therapy frequency insufficient (20-30 min 1x/week for severe disorder). Group therapy used when individual is needed. 'Articulation only' goals when child also has language disorder. Goals not connected to curriculum/classroom performance. Consultation-only model when direct service is needed. School SLP has excessive caseload (national average 48 students, many have 60+) \u2014 reduces service quality. 'Speech-only' IEP limits access to other supports child may need.\",\n     \"\u2605 SLI IEP goal bank organized by disorder type and age. Service frequency recommendation guide by severity. Caseload impact awareness (is your SLP overloaded?). Push-in vs pull-out vs consultation decision guide. AAC evaluation request template. Progress monitoring tools for parents.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"4.2\", \"Advocate for Appropriate Service Delivery Model\",\n     \"Service delivery models for SLI: (1) PULL-OUT: child removed from classroom for individual/small group therapy. Most traditional. (2) PUSH-IN/CLASSROOM-BASED: SLP works with child within classroom context. Better for generalization. (3) CONSULTATION: SLP trains teacher, who implements strategies. Least direct. (4) COMBINED: mix of models based on goals. Best practice is often a combination. For DLD specifically: classroom-based services help language skills transfer to academic contexts. For articulation: pull-out may be efficient for drill-based practice. Research shows collaboration between SLP and teacher produces better outcomes than isolated pull-out therapy.\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"SLP\\nTeacher\\nParent\",\n     \"IEP service page specifying model, frequency, duration, and location.\",\n     \"Established at IEP meeting. Reviewed annually.\",\n     \"School defaults to pull-out only (easier to schedule). Consultation model used when direct service is warranted (saves district money). Frequency reduced due to SLP caseload, not child's needs. Group size too large for effective therapy. Service minutes not actually delivered as written.\",\n     \"Service delivery model comparison tool. Frequency/intensity recommendation by diagnosis and severity. Service delivery tracking log for parents. Template IEP language specifying model and frequency.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"4.3\", \"504 Plan Alternative (If IEP Not Warranted)\",\n     \"Some children with speech/language concerns may not qualify for IEP but need accommodations. 504 Plan appropriate when: mild articulation errors that don't significantly impact academics, stuttering that primarily affects social participation, voice disorder that needs accommodations but not specialized instruction. 504 accommodations may include: extra time for verbal responses, alternative to oral presentations, preferential seating, reduced public speaking pressure for stuttering, classroom amplification system for voice conservation, note-taking support for children with language processing difficulties.\",\n     \"Local School District\\n(504 Coordinator)\", \"Parent\\nSLP\\nPhysician\",\n     \"Documentation of speech/language condition. Insurance coverage: most plans cover speech therapy as 'habilitative' service (developing skills not previously had) or 'rehabilitative' (restoring lost skills). Medi-Cal: covers speech-language services with physician referral. Common therapy approaches by type: ARTICULATION \u2014 drill-based practice, minimal pairs, phonological awareness. DLD \u2014 language stimulation, narrative intervention, vocabulary instruction, Hanen strategies. Medi-Cal SLP provider search.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"5.2\", \"Parent-Implemented Language Strategies (Home Program)\",\n     \"Parents are the MOST IMPORTANT intervention agent \u2014 more time with child than any therapist. Evidence-based parent strategies: (1) Follow child's lead in play \u2014 comment on what they're interested in. (2) Expand and extend child's utterances ('Doggy!' \u2192 'Big brown doggy!'). (3) Model correct production \u2014 don't correct, recast ('I seed a cat' \u2192 'You SAW a cat!'). (4) Read together daily \u2014 pause, point, ask open questions. (5) Reduce questions, increase comments and descriptions. (6) Create communication opportunities (put desired items in sight but out of reach). (7) Narrate daily routines. Programs: Hanen 'It Takes Two to Talk' (language), Hanen 'More Than Words' (autism/limited communication), Mommy Speech Therapy (free videos).\",\n     \"Parents\", \"SLP (coaching)\\nHanen Centre\",\n     \"None formal. SLP home program recommendations. Hanen parent training if available.\",\n     \"Ongoing \u2014 daily embedded in routines.\",\n     \"Parents correct child instead of modeling \u2014 increases anxiety and reduces communication attempts. Too much questioning ('What's this? What color?') becomes drill, not conversation. Parents compare to siblings or peers \u2014 increases stress. Home strategies not aligned with therapy goals. Parent burnout from 'always being the therapist.' Cultural differences in parent-child communication styles not respected.\",\n     \"\u2605 Daily language strategy library (by age and skill). 'Instead of correcting, try this' recast examples. Book recommendation list by language level. Video modeling for parent strategies (Mommy Speech Therapy link). Hanen program finder by location. Home-school-clinic strategy alignment tool.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"5.3\", \"AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) \u2014 If Needed\",\n     \"For children with severe expressive language impairments, AAC provides alternative ways to communicate. AAC ranges from LOW-TECH (picture boards, PECS \u2014 Picture Exchange Communication System) to HIGH-TECH (speech-generating devices, tablet apps like Proloquo2Go, TouchChat). KEY MYTH TO DISPEL: AAC does NOT prevent speech development \u2014 research consistently shows AAC SUPPORTS spoken language. AAC evaluation should be conducted by SLP with AAC expertise. Funding: school district responsible for AAC as assistive technology under IEP. Insurance/Medi-Cal may cover devices. Regional Center may fund AAC for children with autism/ID.\",\n     \"SLP (AAC specialist)\", \"School District\\nInsurance\\nRegional Center\\nATAP (CA Assistive Technology)\",\n     \"AAC evaluation by SLP. Assistive technology assessment through school. Insurance pre-authorization for device. Device trial period documentation.\",\n     \"AAC evaluation: within IEP timeline. Device acquisition: varies (insurance 4-8 weeks, school district varies).\",\n     \"\u2605 AAC BARRIERS: School claims child must 'try to talk first' before getting AAC \u2014 WRONG, no prerequisite skills required. Device funding denied by insurance. School provides low-tech when high-tech is needed. Staff not trained to implement AAC across settings. AAC device stays at school \u2014 child can't communicate at home. Only one communication app evaluated instead of trial of multiple options.\",\n     \"\u2605 AAC myth-buster for parents ('AAC doesn't prevent speech'). AAC evaluation request template. Training resources for AAC implementation.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 6\n    [\"6\\nCo-occurring\\nConditions &\\nAcademic\\nImpact\", \"6.1\", \"Screen for Reading/Literacy Impact (DLD \u2192 SLD Risk)\",\n     \"DLD is a MAJOR risk factor for reading and learning disabilities. The oral language \u2192 written language connection: children who struggle to understand and use spoken language are at significantly higher risk for dyslexia, reading comprehension difficulties, and written expression problems. By school age, children with DLD often qualify under BOTH 'Speech-Language Impairment' AND 'Specific Learning Disability' categories. Important: as children age, SLI identification may shift to SLD \u2014 child 'exits' speech services but underlying language disorder continues to affect reading/writing. The language disorder doesn't go away \u2014 it shows up differently in academic demands.\",\n     \"School SLP / School Psychologist\", \"Reading specialist\\nSpecial educator\",\n     \"Literacy assessment. Reading fluency and comprehension testing. Written language samples. Cross-reference with speech-language evaluation.\",\n     \"Screen at kindergarten entry and ongoing. Formal LD evaluation if concerns persist.\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL RISK: Child exits speech therapy because articulation improved, but underlying language disorder (DLD) continues to impact reading and writing \u2014 now UNFUNDED. School doesn't connect prior SLI to current reading difficulties. Child re-evaluated years later for LD when language was the issue all along. 'Speech-only' IEP didn't address literacy connections. DLD goes 'underground' as child ages \u2014 shows up as poor reading comprehension, weak writing, difficulty with abstract language.\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL: DLD \u2192 literacy risk alert at every annual IEP review. Reading screening prompt for all children with language disorders. 'Your child's speech improved \u2014 but monitor these literacy skills' transition guide. SLI-to-SLD eligibility shift documentation. SLD Journey Map cross-reference.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"6.2\", \"Screen for Social-Emotional Impact\",\n     \"Children with SLI frequently experience social-emotional difficulties: (1) Social isolation \u2014 difficulty making friends due to communication barriers. (2) Behavioral issues \u2014 frustration from inability to express needs leads to acting out. (3) Bullying \u2014 children with speech differences are targets. (4) Anxiety \u2014 fear of speaking, avoidance of social situations. (5) Low self-esteem \u2014 awareness of communication difficulties. (6) Juvenile justice risk \u2014 60-64% of youth in juvenile justice system have language disorders (often undiagnosed). CRITICAL: language difficulties are often misinterpreted as behavior problems, defiance, or low intelligence.\",\n     \"School counselor / Psychologist\", \"SLP\\nParent\\nTherapist\",\n     \"Social-emotional assessment. Behavioral observations. Parent/teacher input on social functioning.\",\n     \"Screen at evaluation and ongoing.\",\n     \"Behavior problems treated without identifying underlying communication cause. Child punished for 'not listening' when they don't understand. Social skills groups use language above child's level. Bullying not addressed. Anxiety about speaking not recognized. Zero-tolerance discipline applied without communication disorder consideration.\",\n     \"Social-emotional screening prompt for all SLI children. Behavior-communication link assessment tool. Anti-bullying resources for speech/language differences. Social skills intervention guide adapted for children with language limitations. Discipline rights guide (manifestation determination for SLI).\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"6.3\", \"Address Co-occurring Conditions (Autism, ADHD, Hearing Loss)\",\n     \"SLI frequently co-occurs with other conditions: (1) AUTISM \u2014 speech-language delay is often first sign; many children initially identified with SLI are later diagnosed with autism. If child has social communication deficits beyond language disorder, screen for autism. (2) ADHD \u2014 attention/executive function impacts language processing and therapy participation; 30-50% co-occurrence. (3) HEARING LOSS \u2014 even mild/fluctuating hearing loss (chronic ear infections) significantly impacts speech-language development. Hearing must be re-tested if progress is slow. (4) INTELLECTUAL DISABILITY \u2014 language disorder associated with ID has different trajectory than DLD. (5) APRAXIA OF SPEECH \u2014 motor planning disorder for speech, requires specialized treatment (not standard articulation therapy).\",\n     \"Pediatrician / Developmental Ped\\nAudiologist\", \"SLP\\nSchool\\nRegional Center\",\n     \"Comprehensive developmental evaluation. Audiological evaluation. Autism screening if social concerns. ADHD screening if attention concerns.\",\n     \"Ongoing \u2014 reassess as child develops and new concerns emerge.\",\n     \"Autism identified late because SLI was the initial diagnosis. Hearing re-check not done when progress plateaus. Apraxia misdiagnosed as typical articulation disorder (treatment approach differs significantly). ADHD medication helps attention but doesn't resolve language disorder. Multiple providers not coordinating care.\",\n     \"Co-occurring condition screening prompts based on progress patterns. Autism screening trigger for SLI children with social communication concerns. Hearing re-test prompt when therapy progress stalls. Apraxia vs articulation differentiation guide. Multi-provider coordination template.\"],\n\n    # PHASE 7\n    [\"7\\nLong-Term\\nPlanning &\\nTransition\", \"7.1\", \"Ongoing IEP Review \u2014 SLI-Specific Monitoring\",\n     \"Annual IEP review should assess: (1) Is therapy frequency/model still appropriate? (2) Are goals advancing (not repeating same goals yearly)? (3) Has diagnosis type changed or clarified? (4) Is literacy impact being monitored? (5) Should eligibility category shift from SLI to SLD as academic impacts emerge? (6) Is dismissal from speech services appropriate? ASHA dismissal guidelines: (1) Goals met \u2014 communication no longer adversely affects educational performance. (2) Plateau \u2014 child has reached maximum benefit from current approach. (3) Progress maintained without direct services. IMPORTANT: dismissal from speech DOESN'T mean disorder is gone \u2014 child may need ongoing classroom supports.\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"SLP\\nParent\\nTeacher\",\n     \"Progress reports. Updated assessment data. Parent input on communication at home. Teacher input on classroom performance.\",\n     \"Annual IEP review. Child dismissed prematurely because articulation improved but language disorder persists. Triennial re-evaluation not comprehensive. SLP leaves and replacement doesn't understand child's history. Therapy minutes reduced due to caseload, not child's needs. Parent not informed about dismissal criteria.\",\n     \"SLI IEP review checklist. (2) SAT/ACT accommodations for language processing (extended time, reader for instructions). (3) College disability services \u2014 language disorder documentation for accommodations. (4) Career exploration \u2014 careers that match communication strengths. (5) Workplace communication strategies. (6) DOR referral for vocational support if needed. DLD impacts PERSIST into adulthood: difficulty with complex language, abstract reasoning, reading comprehension, written expression, social communication in professional settings.\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"DOR\\nCollege disability office\",\n     \"Transition assessments. DOR referral for DLD.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"7.3\", \"Stuttering-Specific Long-Term Management\",\n     \"For children/teens who stutter: stuttering management is LONG-TERM, not a 'cure.' Approaches evolve with age: (1) Preschool: Lidcombe Program (parent-delivered, most evidence). (2) School-age: fluency shaping + stuttering modification + avoidance reduction. Self-advocacy and disclosure skills. (3) Teen/adult: continued therapy as needed, support groups (NSA \u2014 National Stuttering Association), self-acceptance and desensitization. Key: goal is NOT fluent speech \u2014 it's effective communication with reduced impact on participation. IEP should address: classroom accommodations (extra time for verbal responses, alternative to oral reports), social-emotional support, self-advocacy.\",\n     \"SLP (stuttering specialist)\", \"NSA (National Stuttering Association)\\nSchool\\nParent\",\n     \"Stuttering evaluation. IEP accommodations. NSA resources.\",\n     \"Ongoing \u2014 long-term management, not cure.\",\n     \"SLP not specialized in stuttering \u2014 uses outdated approaches. School pressures child to speak in front of class. Peers/teachers interrupt or finish sentences. Child told to 'slow down' or 'take a breath' (increases anxiety, doesn't help). Stuttering dismissed as 'they'll grow out of it.' Covert stuttering (hiding it) not recognized.\",\n     \"NSA referral for peer support. Stuttering-specific SLP finder. Classroom accommodation list for stuttering. 'What NOT to say to someone who stutters' resource for teachers/peers. Self-advocacy skills curriculum. Support group connections.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"7.4\", \"CalABLE Account & Financial Planning\",\n     \"For children with persistent communication disabilities: CalABLE account available at any age (no minimum age). Allows saving up to $19K/year without affecting SSI/Medi-Cal eligibility ($100K SSI threshold). Tax-advantaged savings for disability-related expenses including therapy, assistive technology, AAC devices. SSI may be available if speech/language impairment is severe enough to significantly limit functioning. Most children with SLI alone do NOT qualify for SSI, but SLI + co-occurring conditions (autism, intellectual disability) may qualify.\",\n     \"CalABLE / SSA\", \"Regional Center (if eligible)\\nFinancial planner\",\n     \"CalABLE enrollment. Most common type. ~8-9% of young children. Many resolve by school age with therapy. (2) DEVELOPMENTAL LANGUAGE DISORDER (DLD) \\u2014 persistent difficulty understanding and/or using spoken language. 1 in 14 children (~7%). Does NOT resolve on its own. Impacts reading, writing, social skills, academic performance long-term. Previously called 'Specific Language Impairment.' (3) STUTTERING (fluency disorder) \\u2014 affects ~1% of children. 75% recover naturally, 25% persist. (4) VOICE DISORDER \\u2014 hoarseness, breathiness, abnormal pitch. Less common in children. Rarest SLI type.\\\",\\n     \\\"SLP (School or Private)\\\", \\\"Pediatrician\\\\nENT (for voice disorders)\\\",\\n     \\\"Completed evaluation with specific diagnosis type. Severity rating. Functional impact assessment.\\\",\\n     \\\"At evaluation completion.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 DIAGNOSIS CONFUSION: Parents told child has 'speech delay' without distinguishing type \\u2014 treatment differs dramatically for articulation vs DLD. DLD underdiagnosed \\u2014 it's 7x more common than autism but gets far less research attention and public awareness. Stuttering assessed too early (normal disfluency in 2-5 year olds) or too late (early intervention most effective). Voice disorder not evaluated \\u2014 hoarseness normalized. DLD misattributed to 'laziness,' ADHD, or behavior problems.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 SLI type differentiator tool \\u2014 helps parents understand which type their child has. DLD awareness content ('the most common disorder you've never heard of'). Prognosis guide by type (articulation = good prognosis; DLD = persistent, needs ongoing support). Stuttering natural recovery vs persistence risk factors. Link to NIDCD DLD resources.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 4\\n    [\\\"4\\\\nSchool\\\\nServices\\\\n(IEP)\\\", \\\"4.1\\\", \\\"IEP Development \\u2014 SLI-Specific Services & Goals\\\",\\n     \\\"SLI is UNIQUE under IDEA: speech-language services can be BOTH special education AND a related service. 'Speech-only' IEP: child's sole disability is SLI, and speech-language is the primary service. IEP SERVICES may include: (1) Direct SLP therapy (individual or small group). (2) Classroom-based/push-in services. (3) Consultation model (SLP advises teacher). (4) AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices/training if needed. IEP GOALS should be: specific, measurable, tied to educational impact (not just 'improve articulation' but 'produce /r/ sound in 80% of opportunities in classroom reading'). Common goal areas: articulation accuracy, sentence length/complexity, vocabulary, comprehension of directions, narrative skills, social communication, fluency management.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"SLP\\\\nParent\\\\nTeacher\\\",\\n     \\\"All evaluation reports. SLI diagnosis with type and severity. Educational impact documentation. Parent concerns letter.\\\",\\n     \\\"30 days from completed assessment to IEP meeting.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IEP PITFALLS: Therapy frequency insufficient (20-30 min 1x/week for severe disorder). Group therapy used when individual is needed. 'Articulation only' goals when child also has language disorder. Goals not connected to curriculum/classroom performance. Consultation-only model when direct service is needed. School SLP has excessive caseload (national average 48 students, many have 60+) \\u2014 reduces service quality. 'Speech-only' IEP limits access to other supports child may need.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 SLI IEP goal bank organized by disorder type and age. Service frequency recommendation guide by severity. Caseload impact awareness (is your SLP overloaded?). Push-in vs pull-out vs consultation decision guide. AAC evaluation request template. Progress monitoring tools for parents.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.2\\\", \\\"Advocate for Appropriate Service Delivery Model\\\",\\n     \\\"Service delivery models for SLI: (1) PULL-OUT: child removed from classroom for individual/small group therapy. Most traditional. (2) PUSH-IN/CLASSROOM-BASED: SLP works with child within classroom context. Better for generalization. (3) CONSULTATION: SLP trains teacher, who implements strategies. Least direct. (4) COMBINED: mix of models based on goals. Best practice is often a combination. For DLD specifically: classroom-based services help language skills transfer to academic contexts. For articulation: pull-out may be efficient for drill-based practice. Research shows collaboration between SLP and teacher produces better outcomes than isolated pull-out therapy.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"SLP\\\\nTeacher\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"IEP service page specifying model, frequency, duration, and location.\\\",\\n     \\\"Established at IEP meeting. Reviewed annually.\\\",\\n     \\\"School defaults to pull-out only (easier to schedule). Consultation model used when direct service is warranted (saves district money). Frequency reduced due to SLP caseload, not child's needs. Group size too large for effective therapy. Service minutes not actually delivered as written.\\\",\\n     \\\"Service delivery model comparison tool. Frequency/intensity recommendation by diagnosis and severity. Service delivery tracking log for parents. Template IEP language specifying model and frequency.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.3\\\", \\\"504 Plan Alternative (If IEP Not Warranted)\\\",\\n     \\\"Some children with speech/language concerns may not qualify for IEP but need accommodations. 504 Plan appropriate when: mild articulation errors that don't significantly impact academics, stuttering that primarily affects social participation, voice disorder that needs accommodations but not specialized instruction. 504 accommodations may include: extra time for verbal responses, alternative to oral presentations, preferential seating, reduced public speaking pressure for stuttering, classroom amplification system for voice conservation, note-taking support for children with language processing difficulties.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\\n(504 Coordinator)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nSLP\\\\nPhysician\\\",\\n     \\\"Documentation of speech/language condition. Functional impact evidence. Medical documentation if applicable.\\\",\\n     \\\"No specific federal timeline.\\\",\\n     \\\"504 not offered when IEP denied. Stuttering accommodations not included. Teacher doesn't implement 504 consistently. 504 not reviewed regularly.\\\",\\n     \\\"SLI 504 accommodation menu. Stuttering-specific 504 accommodations. Annual 504 review reminder. Rights guide for 504 enforcement.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 5\\n    [\\\"5\\\\nTherapy &\\\\nHome\\\\nSupport\\\", \\\"5.1\\\", \\\"Private Speech Therapy (Insurance/Medi-Cal)\\\",\\n     \\\"Private speech therapy may supplement or replace school services. Insurance coverage: most plans cover speech therapy as 'habilitative' service (developing skills not previously had) or 'rehabilitative' (restoring lost skills). Medi-Cal: covers speech-language services with physician referral. Common therapy approaches by type: ARTICULATION \\u2014 drill-based practice, minimal pairs, phonological awareness. DLD \\u2014 language stimulation, narrative intervention, vocabulary instruction, Hanen strategies. STUTTERING \\u2014 Lidcombe Program (ages 3-6), fluency shaping, stuttering modification, avoidance reduction therapy. Frequency: typically 1-2x/week, 30-60 min sessions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Private SLP\\\", \\\"Insurance / Medi-Cal\\\\nPediatrician (referral)\\\",\\n     \\\"Physician referral. Insurance pre-authorization. Treatment plan from SLP.\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance auth: 5-15 business days. Therapy: ongoing, reviewed every 6-12 months.\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance caps sessions per year (often 20-30 visits). Prior authorization required for continued therapy. SLP shortage \\u2014 waitlists common, especially for specialized areas (stuttering, AAC, bilingual SLPs). Medi-Cal reimbursement rates low. Telehealth may be effective but not offered. Private and school SLPs don't coordinate goals.\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance SLP session limit checker. Prior auth renewal reminder. Specialized SLP finder (stuttering, AAC, bilingual). School-private SLP coordination template. Telehealth speech therapy guide. Medi-Cal SLP provider search.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"5.2\\\", \\\"Parent-Implemented Language Strategies (Home Program)\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents are the MOST IMPORTANT intervention agent \\u2014 more time with child than any therapist. Evidence-based parent strategies: (1) Follow child's lead in play \\u2014 comment on what they're interested in. (2) Expand and extend child's utterances ('Doggy!' \\u2192 'Big brown doggy!'). (3) Model correct production \\u2014 don't correct, recast ('I seed a cat' \\u2192 'You SAW a cat!'). (4) Read together daily \\u2014 pause, point, ask open questions. (5) Reduce questions, increase comments and descriptions. (6) Create communication opportunities (put desired items in sight but out of reach). (7) Narrate daily routines. Programs: Hanen 'It Takes Two to Talk' (language), Hanen 'More Than Words' (autism/limited communication), Mommy Speech Therapy (free videos).\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents\\\", \\\"SLP (coaching)\\\\nHanen Centre\\\",\\n     \\\"None formal. SLP home program recommendations. Hanen parent training if available.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 daily embedded in routines.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents correct child instead of modeling \\u2014 increases anxiety and reduces communication attempts. Too much questioning ('What's this? What color?') becomes drill, not conversation. Parents compare to siblings or peers \\u2014 increases stress. Home strategies not aligned with therapy goals. Parent burnout from 'always being the therapist.' Cultural differences in parent-child communication styles not respected.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Daily language strategy library (by age and skill). 'Instead of correcting, try this' recast examples. Book recommendation list by language level. Video modeling for parent strategies (Mommy Speech Therapy link). Hanen program finder by location. Home-school-clinic strategy alignment tool.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"5.3\\\", \\\"AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) \\u2014 If Needed\\\",\\n     \\\"For children with severe expressive language impairments, AAC provides alternative ways to communicate. AAC ranges from LOW-TECH (picture boards, PECS \\u2014 Picture Exchange Communication System) to HIGH-TECH (speech-generating devices, tablet apps like Proloquo2Go, TouchChat). KEY MYTH TO DISPEL: AAC does NOT prevent speech development \\u2014 research consistently shows AAC SUPPORTS spoken language. AAC evaluation should be conducted by SLP with AAC expertise. Funding: school district responsible for AAC as assistive technology under IEP. Insurance/Medi-Cal may cover devices. Regional Center may fund AAC for children with autism/ID.\\\",\\n     \\\"SLP (AAC specialist)\\\", \\\"School District\\\\nInsurance\\\\nRegional Center\\\\nATAP (CA Assistive Technology)\\\",\\n     \\\"AAC evaluation by SLP. Device acquisition: varies (insurance 4-8 weeks, school district varies).\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 AAC BARRIERS: School claims child must 'try to talk first' before getting AAC \\u2014 WRONG, no prerequisite skills required. Device funding denied by insurance. School provides low-tech when high-tech is needed. Staff not trained to implement AAC across settings. AAC device stays at school \\u2014 child can't communicate at home. Only one communication app evaluated instead of trial of multiple options.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 AAC myth-buster for parents ('AAC doesn't prevent speech'). AAC evaluation request template. Device funding decision tree (school vs insurance vs RC). AAC app comparison guide. ATAP (CA Assistive Technology Access Project) referral. Training resources for AAC implementation.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 6\\n    [\\\"6\\\\nCo-occurring\\\\nConditions &\\\\nAcademic\\\\nImpact\\\", \\\"6.1\\\", \\\"Screen for Reading/Literacy Impact (DLD \\u2192 SLD Risk)\\\",\\n     \\\"DLD is a MAJOR risk factor for reading and learning disabilities. The oral language \\u2192 written language connection: children who struggle to understand and use spoken language are at significantly higher risk for dyslexia, reading comprehension difficulties, and written expression problems. By school age, children with DLD often qualify under BOTH 'Speech-Language Impairment' AND 'Specific Learning Disability' categories. Important: as children age, SLI identification may shift to SLD \\u2014 child 'exits' speech services but underlying language disorder continues to affect reading/writing. The language disorder doesn't go away \\u2014 it shows up differently in academic demands.\\\",\\n     \\\"School SLP / School Psychologist\\\", \\\"Reading specialist\\\\nSpecial educator\\\",\\n     \\\"Literacy assessment. Reading fluency and comprehension testing. Written language samples. Cross-reference with speech-language evaluation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Screen at kindergarten entry and ongoing. Formal LD evaluation if concerns persist.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL RISK: Child exits speech therapy because articulation improved, but underlying language disorder (DLD) continues to impact reading and writing \\u2014 now UNFUNDED. School doesn't connect prior SLI to current reading difficulties. Child re-evaluated years later for LD when language was the issue all along. 'Speech-only' IEP didn't address literacy connections. DLD goes 'underground' as child ages \\u2014 shows up as poor reading comprehension, weak writing, difficulty with abstract language.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: DLD \\u2192 literacy risk alert at every annual IEP review. Reading screening prompt for all children with language disorders. 'Your child's speech improved \\u2014 but monitor these literacy skills' transition guide. SLI-to-SLD eligibility shift documentation. SLD Journey Map cross-reference.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"6.2\\\", \\\"Screen for Social-Emotional Impact\\\",\\n     \\\"Children with SLI frequently experience social-emotional difficulties: (1) Social isolation \\u2014 difficulty making friends due to communication barriers. (2) Behavioral issues \\u2014 frustration from inability to express needs leads to acting out. (3) Bullying \\u2014 children with speech differences are targets. (4) Anxiety \\u2014 fear of speaking, avoidance of social situations. (5) Low self-esteem \\u2014 awareness of communication difficulties. (6) Juvenile justice risk \\u2014 60-64% of youth in juvenile justice system have language disorders (often undiagnosed). CRITICAL: language difficulties are often misinterpreted as behavior problems, defiance, or low intelligence.\\\",\\n     \\\"School counselor / Psychologist\\\", \\\"SLP\\\\nParent\\\\nTherapist\\\",\\n     \\\"Social-emotional assessment. Behavioral observations. Child punished for 'not listening' when they don't understand. Social skills groups use language above child's level. Bullying not addressed. Anxiety about speaking not recognized. Zero-tolerance discipline applied without communication disorder consideration.\\\",\\n     \\\"Social-emotional screening prompt for all SLI children. Behavior-communication link assessment tool. Anti-bullying resources for speech/language differences. Social skills intervention guide adapted for children with language limitations. Discipline rights guide (manifestation determination for SLI).\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"6.3\\\", \\\"Address Co-occurring Conditions (Autism, ADHD, Hearing Loss)\\\",\\n     \\\"SLI frequently co-occurs with other conditions: (1) AUTISM \\u2014 speech-language delay is often first sign; many children initially identified with SLI are later diagnosed with autism. If child has social communication deficits beyond language disorder, screen for autism. (2) ADHD \\u2014 attention/executive function impacts language processing and therapy participation; 30-50% co-occurrence. (3) HEARING LOSS \\u2014 even mild/fluctuating hearing loss (chronic ear infections) significantly impacts speech-language development. Hearing must be re-tested if progress is slow. (4) INTELLECTUAL DISABILITY \\u2014 language disorder associated with ID has different trajectory than DLD. (5) APRAXIA OF SPEECH \\u2014 motor planning disorder for speech, requires specialized treatment (not standard articulation therapy).\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / Developmental Ped\\\\nAudiologist\\\", \\\"SLP\\\\nSchool\\\\nRegional Center\\\",\\n     \\\"Comprehensive developmental evaluation. ADHD screening if attention concerns.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 reassess as child develops and new concerns emerge.\\\",\\n     \\\"Autism identified late because SLI was the initial diagnosis. Hearing re-check not done when progress plateaus. Apraxia misdiagnosed as typical articulation disorder (treatment approach differs significantly). ADHD medication helps attention but doesn't resolve language disorder. Multiple providers not coordinating care.\\\",\\n     \\\"Co-occurring condition screening prompts based on progress patterns. Multi-provider coordination template.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 7\\n    [\\\"7\\\\nLong-Term\\\\nPlanning &\\\\nTransition\\\", \\\"7.1\\\", \\\"Ongoing IEP Review \\u2014 SLI-Specific Monitoring\\\",\\n     \\\"Annual IEP review should assess: (1) Is therapy frequency/model still appropriate? (2) Are goals advancing (not repeating same goals yearly)? (3) Has diagnosis type changed or clarified? (4) Is literacy impact being monitored? (5) Should eligibility category shift from SLI to SLD as academic impacts emerge? (6) Is dismissal from speech services appropriate? (3) Progress maintained without direct services. Approaches evolve with age: (1) Preschool: Lidcombe Program (parent-delivered, most evidence). (2) School-age: fluency shaping + stuttering modification + avoidance reduction. Self-advocacy and disclosure skills. (3) Teen/adult: continued therapy as needed, support groups (NSA \\u2014 National Stuttering Association), self-acceptance and desensitization. Key: goal is NOT fluent speech \\u2014 it's effective communication with reduced impact on participation. IEP should address: classroom accommodations (extra time for verbal responses, alternative to oral reports), social-emotional support, self-advocacy.\\\",\\n     \\\"SLP (stuttering specialist)\\\", \\\"NSA (National Stuttering Association)\\\\nSchool\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"Stuttering evaluation. mandatory hearing test \u2014 hearing loss MUST be ruled out\n- Bilingual development guide (bilingualism does NOT cause SLI)\n\n**Phase 2 \u2014 Early Intervention Birth-3 (2 steps)**\n- Early Start/Regional Center IFSP (45-day timeline, natural environment services)\n- **Age 3 transition to school district \u2014 the #1 vulnerability point** for SLI families (services can stop abruptly, service model shifts from home to school)\n\n**Phase 3 \u2014 Evaluation & Diagnosis Age 3+ (3 steps)**\n- IDEA SLI definition: \"communication disorder that adversely affects educational performance\" (34 CFR \u00a7300.8(c)(11))\n- Covers 4 domains: articulation, language (DLD), fluency (stuttering), voice\n- **DLD prevalence: 7% (1 in 14 children)** \u2014 7x more common than autism but far less known\n- Bilingual evaluation rights (ASHA position: must evaluate in ALL languages, cognitive referencing NOT supported)\n\n**Phase 4 \u2014 School Services/IEP (3 steps)**\n- SLI is UNIQUE under IDEA \u2014 speech-language services are both special education AND related service\n- Service delivery models: pull-out vs push-in vs consultation vs combined\n- 504 alternative for mild cases (especially stuttering accommodations)\n\n**Phase 5 \u2014 Therapy & Home Support (3 steps)**\n- Private SLP therapy (insurance/Medi-Cal coverage)\n- Parent-implemented strategies \u2014 Hanen \"It Takes Two to Talk\" approach (parents are the most important intervention agent)\n- **AAC for severe cases \u2014 myth-buster: AAC does NOT prevent speech development** (research consistently shows AAC supports spoken language)\n\n**Phase 6 \u2014 Co-occurring Conditions & Academic Impact (3 steps)**\n- **DLD \u2192 literacy connection is critical** \u2014 many SLI children later qualify as SLD (reading/writing difficulties)\n- Social-emotional impact: 60-64% of youth in juvenile justice have undiagnosed language disorders\n- Co-occurring conditions: autism (SLI often first sign), ADHD, hearing loss, apraxia of speech\n\n**Phase 7 \u2014 Long-Term Planning & Transition (4 steps)**\n- Ongoing IEP monitoring with dismissal readiness assessment\n- DLD-specific transition planning (DLD persists into adulthood)\n- Stuttering-specific long-term management (NSA support groups, self-advocacy, classroom accommodations)\n- CalABLE account & financial planning\n\n**3 SLI entities         "text": "For example, in California, individuals with ID are generally eligible for Regional Center services at any age. In other states, they may be eligible under the Department of Developmental Services.\n\nIntellectual disability is considered a developmental disability, so state disability agencies may provide support. For example, in California, individuals with ID are generally eligible for Regional Center services at any age. In other states, they may be eligible under the Department of Developmental Services. Children under age three will qualify for early intervention services, such as Early Start in California. Where the disability impacts two or more aspects of daily living, children in California older than age three may also be eligible for Medi-Cal and possibly for IHSS. Typically, the process of assessing a child for an IEP under ID will include an assessment of their IQ and an assessment of their adaptive skills. Parents need to be familiar with how standardized tests work, in terms of placing their child\u2019s performance on the test on a bell curve to establish if they have \u201csignificantly subaverage general intellectual functioning.\u201d Dr.\n\nIf you find that your child was not appropriately assessed for any of the reasons above, or you are in disagreement with the district\u2019s assessment for any reason (so long as it is within the two-year statute of limitations), you have the right to request an independent educational evaluation (IEE), which is a comprehensive assessment conducted by a qualified clinician who is not associated with the school district. As a parent, you will be asked to sign a consent for your child\u2019s initial IEP assessment and subsequent triennial assessments. This is a good time to have a conversation with the school psychologist and express your concerns about how your child will perform on an IQ test, given their communication and behavioral challenges. You may want to put these concerns in writing. Some parents prefer that their children not be subjected to IQ tests, for a variety of reasons.\n\nIn simpler terms, intellectual disability (ID) is diagnosed when a person has significant limitations in both intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior. Intellectual functioning or intelligence refers to general cognitive capacity, such as learning, reasoning, and problem-solving. This is usually measured by an IQ test, where a below-average score can indicate a significant limitation in intellectual functioning. In general, scores of between 80 and 120 on an IQ test are considered average in the United States. Scores of 70 or 75 are sometimes given as the cutoff for determining possible intellectual disability. Adaptive behavior refers to self-care skills across several domains, including conceptual, social, and practical skills that are learned and performed by people in their everyday lives: The conceptual domain includes skills such as language, reading, writing, math, reasoning, knowledge, memory, and self-direction.\n\nDr. Simun explains that the second part of identifying ID is assessing intellectual functioning. Hear in this clip how psychologists use IQ tests to identify intellectual disability in children by looking at scores in standardized tests of cognitive ability: The third step is to look at the child's adaptive skills and behaviors. Adaptive skills are often assessed by interviewing parents, caregivers, and teachers but may also be determined by a standardized test like the Adaptive Behavior Assessment System (ABAS): Intellectual disability affects more than two million individuals in the U.S. alone \u2014 approximately 1\u20133% of the population. Males are more likely than females to be diagnosed with intellectual disability. ID is commonly associated with conditions such as Down syndrome, Fragile X, autism, and cerebral palsy; however, that does not mean every child diagnosed with these conditions will necessarily have an intellectual disability.",

Note: A diagnosis of intellectual disability is possible for individuals with full scale IQ scores between 71 and 75 if they have significant deficits in the areas of adaptive behavior listed above. The opinion of an independent expert may be required to establish this. See http://www.ddhealthinfo.org for more information. Cerebral Palsy is an umbrella term that describes a set of conditions that affect the control a person has over movement. It usually starts at birth or during the first few years of life. Symptoms may change over time, but generally the condition itself is not progressive. It is caused by damage to the parts of the brain that control movement and posture or the failure of those parts to develop appropriately.\n\nHowever, there is a measurement error of approximately 5 points in assessing IQ, so experts may diagnose intellectual disability in a person with an IQ between 70 and 75, if he or she exhibits significant deficits in adaptive behavior. In practice, it is exceedingly rare for an ALJ to find intellectual disability eligibility when the claimant has an IQ of 70-75. OAH decisions tend to employ a sharp IQ cutoff of 69 for intellectual disability, reserving scores of 70-75 for 5th category eligibility. From an advocacy perspective, a claimant with an IQ of 70-75 should argue thatmeasurement error includes her IQ score within the range of intellectual disability eligibility. However, this approach has been largely unsuccessful so far. (7) Multiple disabilities means concomitant impairments, such as intellectual disability-blindness or intellectual disability-orthopedic impairment, the combination of which causes such severe educational needs that they cannot be accommodated in special education programs solely for one of the impairments. \"Multiple disabilities\" does not include deaf-blindness. (8) Orthopedic impairment means a severe orthopedic impairment that adversely affects a child's educational performance. The term includes impairments caused by a congenital anomaly, impairments caused by disease (e.g., poliomyelitis, bone tuberculosis), and impairments from other causes (e.g., cerebral palsy, amputations, and fractures or burns that cause contractures).\n\n(a) A child shall qualify as an individual with exceptional needs, pursuant to Education Code section 56026, if the results of the assessment as required by Education Code section 56320 demonstrate that the degree of the child's impairment as described in subdivisions (b)(1) through (b)(13) requires special education in one or more of the program options authorized by Education Code section 56361. The decision as to whether or not the assessment results demonstrate that the degree of the child's impairment requires special education shall be made by the IEP team, including personnel in accordance with Education Code section 56341(b). The IEP team shall take into account all the relevant material which is available on the child. No single score or product of scores shall be used as the sole criterion for the decision of the IEP team as to the child's eligibility for special education.\n\n3. If the standardized tests do not reveal a severe discrepancy as defined in subdivisions 1. or 2. above, the IEP team may find that a severe discrepancy does exist, provided that the team documents in a written report that the severe discrepancy between ability and achievement exists as a result of a disorder in one or more of the basic psychological processes. The report shall include a statement of the area, the degree, and the basis and method used in determining the discrepancy. The report shall contain information considered by the team which shall include, but not be limited to: (i) Data obtained from standardized assessment instruments; ... Riles case, the California State Department of Education (CDE), in 2022 updated its earlier directive to state that school districts must not use any assessment that is a \u201cstandardized intelligence test\u201d for purposes of identifying or placing Black students in a category or placement that is \u201csubstantially equivalent\u201d to what was formerly known as EMR.[2] The California Association of School Psychologists has interpreted CDE\u2019s directive to mean that IQ tests may not be used to place Black students in the category of Intellectual Disability (\u201cID\u201d), but school psychologists may still exercise their professional judgment as to whether to use IQ tests for other suspected areas of disability, so long as they follow all other legal requirements.[3]\u201d See Chapter 2, Information on Evaluations/Assessments. 34 C.F.R. Lexile: A reading measure that provides information about an individual&#x27;s reading ability or the difficulty ...\n\nIntellectual or Cognitive Tests: A test designed to measure intellectual ability and/or potential. Examples of commonly used tests are the Weschler Individual Achievement Test (WISC IV), the Stanford Binet 5, and the Universal Nonverbal Intelligence Test (UNIT). Baseline: Describes a student\u2019s current performance of a skill or strategy in measurable terms before intervention or treatment. The baseline serves as a starting point for an IEP. Examples may include words per minute or level of prompts necessary to sustain a behavior. Diagnostic Test: A test used to identify or diagnose a child\u2019s problem areas. Intelligence Quotient (IQ): A score from a test designed to measure intelligence. Tables are used to compare children\u2019s performance to the performance of same age peers. Lexile: A reading measure that provides information about an individual's reading ability or the difficulty of a text.\n\nIf the evaluation recommends the student is eligible for special education, they should also make recommendations about the student\u2019s IEP. For example, they may make recommendations on appropriate goals, services, supports, accommodations, and modifications. You might consider asking the evaluator their opinion on your child\u2019s goals, services, supports, accommodations, and modifications during the IEP meeting. Achievement Test: A test of academic subjects such as Reading, Math, and Writing. Examples include the Woodcock-Johnson II Test of Achievement, the Kaufman Test of Educational Achievement, Second Edition (KTEA II), and the Wide Range Achievement Test 4 (WRAT-4). Adaptive Behavior Rating Scale: Examines a child\u2019s ability to perform certain tasks, such as eating, dressing, completing schoolwork, etc., and is completed by both the parent and the child\u2019s teacher.\n\nFor younger children, examiners will often use parent questionnaires to assess the student\u2019s social/emotional and behavioral functioning. They may also ask teachers to complete a questionnaire about your student\u2019s learning and behavior. Examiners may also have your student complete a questionnaire on their feelings as they grow older. Your student may also be given a test to measure how they cope with, and view, social relationships. The evaluation should include a recommendation on your child\u2019s eligibility for special education. For an initial evaluation, the examiner should make a recommendation about whether your child qualifies for special education based on the assessments they administered. For a triennial evaluation, the examiner should make a recommendation about whether your child continues to qualify for special education. There are 13 different categories of eligibility for special education.\n\nT-Score: Used by behavior scales to report results, a T-score has a mean or average of 50 and a standard deviation of 10. Explore More: Understanding Intellectual Disability in Children \u00b7 Intellectual Disability isn't just about IQ; it also involves significant limitations in adaptive behavior.\n\nAn IQ assessment is a standardized evaluation administered by a qualified psychologist to measure cognitive abilities, including reasoning, problem-solving, and learning capacity. In the context of a potential Intellectual Disability (ID), this assessment is a critical piece of a larger diagnostic puzzle. It is always conducted alongside a thorough evaluation of adaptive behavior\u2014the practical, everyday skills a child uses to function. At Cadabam's, backed by our 30+ years of legacy in mental health and neurodevelopment, we understand that an assessment is far more than a score. It is the first, vital step toward identifying your child's unique strengths and challenges, allowing us to co-create a tailored support plan that unlocks their full potential. Choosing where to get an IQ assessment for intellectual disability for your child is a significant decision.\n\nYou'll speak with one of our developmental experts to share your concerns in a confidential setting. We will conduct an initial developmental assessment for intellectual disability to understand your child's history and current challenges. This helps us determine if a comprehensive evaluation is the right next step. The core cognitive testing is conducted one-on-one by a licensed clinical psychologist. We use gold-standard, internationally recognized assessment tools that are appropriate for your child's age (such as the WISC-V or SB-5). The focus is on making the experience positive and stress-free for your child. This step is just as important as the IQ test itself.         "text": "Under this approach, a student must be found to have a severe discrepancy between their intellectual ability and their achievement in oral expression, listening comprehension, written expression, basic reading skills, reading comprehension, mathematical calculation, or mathematical reasoning.[3] Federal special education law allows school districts to use another assessment method known as \u201cresponse to scientific, research-based intervention model\u201d or \u201cResponse to Intervention (RTI) model\u201d to determine if a student qualifies for special education with a learning disability.[4] Federal law prohibits states from requiring school districts to use only the discrepancy model and requires states to also allow their school districts to use the RTI model.[5] Because of the federal law, California law permits schools to use either the discrepancy model or RTI model in determining whether a student has a specific learning disability.[6] Before consenting to a special education evaluation for you\n\n(3.9) Are IQ scores the only basis for eligibility for special education based on intellectual disability? (3.10) What are the eligibility criteria for emotional disturbance? (3.11) My child has been diagnosed with a conduct/behavior disorder, such as oppositional defiant disorder. Can they qualify for special education? (3.12) Can a child with Attention Deficit Disorder (ADD) or Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) be eligible for special education services? (3.13) What are the eligibility criteria and evaluation process for a Specific Learning Disability (SLD)? (3.14) How does the \u201cdiscrepancy model\u201d of determining whether a student has a specific learning disability work? (3.15) What is the \u201cResponse to Intervention (RTI) model\u201d and what part does it play in determining if my child has a specific learning disability? (3.16) What additional eligibility criteria are used by the IEP team to determine if a child has a Specific Learning Disability?\n\nSLD does not include learning problems primarily due to visual, hearing, or motor disabilities, or intellectual disabilities, or emotional disturbance, or environmental, cultural, or economic disadvantage.[1] However, a specific learning disability does include a disability within the function of vision which results in visual perceptual or visual motor dysfunction.[2] The specific learning disability category of special education eligibility is the largest category of special education students and the most complicated in terms of the factors which must be considered and the evaluation processes school districts may use in making this determination. In California, school districts often use what is known as the \u201cdiscrepancy model\u201d to determine whether a student has a specific learning disability.\n\nUnder this approach, a student must be found to have a severe discrepancy between their intellectual ability and their achievement in oral expression, listening comprehension, written expression, basic reading skills, reading comprehension, mathematical calculation, or mathematical reasoning.[3] Federal special education law allows school districts to use another assessment method known as \u201cresponse to scientific, research-based intervention model\u201d or \u201cResponse to Intervention (RTI) model\u201d to determine if a student qualifies for special education with a learning disability.[4] Federal law prohibits states from requiring school districts to use only the discrepancy model and requires states to also allow their school districts to use the RTI model.[5] Because of the federal law, California law permits schools to use either the discrepancy model or RTI model in determining whether a student has a specific learning disability.[6] Before consenting to a special education evaluation for you\n\n(3.23) I think the IEP team\u2019s eligibility determination is incorrect because it did not account for my multilingual child\u2019s language needs. What can I do? (3.24) If my family moves to a new school district, does my child need to be found eligible again for special education by the new school district? (3.25) If my child does not meet special education eligibility, can my child get Section 504 services to address educational problems? (3.26) If a student is eligible for services under Section 504 only, can they receive special education services? (3.27) My child is progressing from grade to grade. Can they still be eligible for special education? (3.28) Can the school district limit the services that my child receives based on their disability? (3.29) My child is eligible for special education under one of the eligibility categories, but they have other problems which affect their learning.",

          "type": "webpage_metadata",
