        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Intellectual disabilities manifest themselves before the age of 18 and generally are lifelong conditions. Usually, an IQ below 70 falls within the range of Intellectual Disability.\n\nIt involves the ability to reason, plan, solve problems, think abstractly, comprehend complex ideas, learn quickly and learn from experience. Intellectual functioning is represented by Intelligent Quotient (IQ) scores obtained from standardized tests given by trained professionals. Intellectual disability is generally thought to be present if an individual has an IQ test score of approximately 70 or below. ... Adaptive behavior is the collection of conceptual, social and practical skills that have been learned by people in order to function in their everyday lives. Significant limitations in adaptive behavior impact a person\u2019s daily life and affect his or her ability to respond to a particular situation or to the environment. Standardized testing aims to measure the following skills: Conceptual skills: receptive and expressive language, reading and writing, money concepts, self-direction.\n\nCharacterized by below-average intellectual function, intellectual disabilities impact a person\u2019s day-to-day functioning and last throughout a person\u2019s lifetime. Children with intellectual disabilities may fail to reach developmental milestones in their communications, behavior, play, movement, or learning which will prompt a parent or physician to ask for a comprehensive evaluation. ... Generally the more severe the intellectual disability the earlier it is noticed. Intellectual disabilities manifest themselves before the age of 18 and generally are lifelong conditions. Usually, an IQ below 70 falls within the range of Intellectual Disability. It is not precisely known how many people have intellectual and developmental disabilities but most dependable sources tend to agree on a range of 1.5% to 2.5% of the total general population. Based on the most recent US Census, the US population was reported to be approximately 311 million people which translates to roughly 4.6 million to 7.7 m\n\nHowever with appropriate supports over a sustained period, the life-functioning of the person with Intellectual Disability generally improves. Appropriate supports includes an array of services, supportive individuals, and receptive settings that match an individual\u2019s needs. Every person is different, so each person with Intellectual Disabilities is evaluated and reevaluated so that their needs are being met. For most people supports are needed over an extended period of time, and for many persons, supports will be lifelong. For some, however, certain supports may be intermittent or short-term. Most people with an Intellectual Disability will improve their functioning with early intervention, appropriate education and supports as an adult allowing them to live a satisfying, meaningful, productive, independent, and integrated life in the community.",

IQ \u226470 generally indicates ID (with 5-point measurement error allowing 70-75 range). (2) Adaptive behavior assessment \u2014 Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales (VABS-3) or ABAS-3, measuring conceptual (language, reading, math), social (interpersonal, responsibility), and practical (self-care, daily living) skills. Riles ruling \u2014 in California, standardized IQ tests CANNOT be used to identify or place Black students in ID category. School districts must use alternative assessments for Black students. Culturally/linguistically biased testing may overidentify minority children or underidentify those from non-dominant cultures. IQ score near cutoff (70-75) creates eligibility disputes. Adaptive behavior not assessed comprehensively \u2014 only parent report collected, not teacher/clinician observation. Child performs better on structured testing than in daily life (or vice versa).\",\n     \"\u2605 CRITICAL: Larry P. compliance checker \u2014 alerts if Black student being evaluated with IQ test for ID placement. MODERATE (IQ ~35-49, ~10%): academic skills to ~2nd grade, semi-independent living with moderate support, supported employment. SEVERE (IQ ~20-34, ~3-4%): limited communication, significant daily support needed, may learn sight words and basic self-care. PROFOUND (IQ <20, ~1-2%): requires 24-hour support, very limited communication, may use AAC. California Code of Regulations (5 CCR \u00a73030). School evaluation includes: IQ testing, adaptive behavior assessment, academic achievement testing, speech/language evaluation, motor assessment, social-emotional assessment, observation. CALIFORNIA-SPECIFIC: Larry P. v. Riles (1979/1986) \u2014 IQ tests CANNOT be used to place Black students in ID category or substantially equivalent placements. LRE = Least Restrictive Environment. Child must be educated with non-disabled peers to maximum extent appropriate. (2) SERVICES: specialized academic instruction (modified curriculum), speech-language therapy, OT, PT, behavioral support, adaptive PE, assistive technology/AAC, 1:1 aide if needed. (3) GOALS: functional academics, communication, self-care/adaptive skills, social skills, community skills, pre-vocational skills. (4) MODIFICATIONS (not just accommodations): modified curriculum, alternative assessments (California Alternate Assessment for state testing). ID with RC eligibility = automatic Medi-Cal eligibility in many cases. (2) SSI: Supplemental Security Income for children with disabilities. ID is one of the highest-approval-rate conditions for SSI. Function report critical \u2014 describe daily challenges in detail. (3) IHSS: In-Home Supportive Services \u2014 personal care, domestic services, protective supervision. Many children with moderate-severe ID qualify for protective supervision. (4) CalABLE: No minimum age. $19K/year tax-advantaged savings. SSI $100K threshold. Common co-occurring medical conditions: seizure disorders/epilepsy (~30% of ID), hearing and vision impairments, cardiac conditions (especially Down syndrome), feeding/swallowing difficulties, sleep disorders, dental issues, obesity. Therapeutic services: SLP (communication/AAC/feeding), OT (fine motor, sensory, self-care), PT (gross motor, mobility), behavioral support (ABA may be appropriate depending on co-occurring autism), psychology/psychiatry (mental health \u2014 ID + mental health co-occurs 3-4x more than general population).\",\n     \"Pediatrician / Medical Home\\nSpecialists\", \"RC\\nSchool\\nInsurance/Medi-Cal\",\n     \"Medical records. School therapy notes.\",\n     \"Ongoing \u2014 medical care is lifelong for many ID-associated conditions.\",\n     \"Medical needs overshadow developmental needs (or vice versa). No medical home coordination. Specialists don't communicate with each other or with RC/school. Insurance denies therapies. Mental health needs unaddressed \u2014 behavior attributed to ID rather than co-occurring depression/anxiety. Polypharmacy without adequate monitoring.\",\n     \"Medical-developmental coordination dashboard. Specialist appointment tracker. Insurance therapy coverage checker. Options: (1) FULL CONSERVATORSHIP: most restrictive \u2014 another person makes all decisions. Court process, attorney required, costly, removes nearly all rights. Should be LAST resort. (2) LIMITED CONSERVATORSHIP: most common for ID \u2014 conservator has authority in specific areas (residence, medical, education, social) but individual retains other rights. Preferred over full conservatorship. (3) SUPPORTED DECISION-MAKING (SDM): emerging alternative \u2014 individual retains all legal rights but designates trusted supporters to help with decisions. Growing movement. (4) POWER OF ATTORNEY: if individual can understand and consent. Start planning 12-18 months before 18th birthday.\",\n     \"Attorney / Court\\nRegional Center\", \"Parent\\nDRC\",\n     \"Legal consultation (12-18 months before 18). ID is one of the 5 qualifying Lanterman Act conditions (ID, cerebral palsy, epilepsy, autism, 5th category). Self-referral accepted at any age \\u2014 no doctor referral required. For children under 3: Regional Center provides Early Start (Part C) services. For children 3+: Regional Center provides ongoing service coordination AND school district provides IEP. KEY: ID qualifies for BOTH Regional Center services AND school district special education \\u2014 DUAL SYSTEM. This is different from SLD/ADHD which are school-district only.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\", \\\"Pediatrician (optional referral)\\\",\\n     \\\"Self-referral (phone call, walk-in). No documentation required to refer. Bring any existing medical/developmental records.\\\",\\n     \\\"RC must complete intake within 15 working days of referral.\\\",\\n     \\\"Family doesn't know about Regional Center. Family referred only to school district \\u2014 misses RC services. Language barriers prevent self-referral. RC intake delayed. Family told child is 'too young' or 'not delayed enough' for referral.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Regional Center auto-referral guide by ZIP. Lanterman Act eligibility explainer. Self-referral script in multiple languages. 'You don't need a doctor referral' prominent messaging. RC vs school district: what each provides comparison tool.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 2\\n    [\\\"2\\\\nEvaluation &\\\\nDiagnosis\\\", \\\"2.1\\\", \\\"Regional Center Comprehensive Evaluation\\\",\\n     \\\"RC conducts multidisciplinary evaluation: (1) Cognitive/IQ testing \\u2014 standardized tests (WISC-V, Stanford-Binet 5, Bayley for infants). IQ \\u226470 generally indicates ID (with 5-point measurement error allowing 70-75 range). (2) Adaptive behavior assessment \\u2014 Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales (VABS-3) or ABAS-3, measuring conceptual (language, reading, math), social (interpersonal, responsibility), and practical (self-care, daily living) skills. (3) Developmental history. (4) Medical evaluation. DSM-5 criteria: (A) Deficits in intellectual functions, (B) Deficits in adaptive functioning, (C) Onset during developmental period. DSM-5 severity levels based on ADAPTIVE functioning (not IQ): Mild, Moderate, Severe, Profound.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\", \\\"Psychologist\\\\nPediatrician\\\\nSLP\\\",\\n     \\\"Signed consent. Medical records. Developmental history. Parent interview.\\\",\\n     \\\"120 days from referral to eligibility determination.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 EVALUATION ISSUES: Larry P. v. Riles ruling \\u2014 in California, standardized IQ tests CANNOT be used to identify or place Black students in ID category. School districts must use alternative assessments for Black students. Culturally/linguistically biased testing may overidentify minority children or underidentify those from non-dominant cultures. IQ score near cutoff (70-75) creates eligibility disputes. Adaptive behavior not assessed comprehensively \\u2014 only parent report collected, not teacher/clinician observation. Child performs better on structured testing than in daily life (or vice versa).\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: Larry P. compliance checker \\u2014 alerts if Black student being evaluated with IQ test for ID placement. Culturally responsive evaluation advocacy guide. IQ-adaptive behavior concordance tool. Alternative assessment request template. Evaluation timeline tracker (120-day clock).\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"2.2\\\", \\\"Understand ID Severity Levels & What They Mean\\\",\\n     \\\"DSM-5 severity is based on ADAPTIVE FUNCTIONING (not IQ scores alone): MILD (IQ ~50-70, ~85% of ID population): can develop academic skills to ~6th grade level, live independently with support, competitive employment possible with accommodations. MODERATE (IQ ~35-49, ~10%): academic skills to ~2nd grade, semi-independent living with moderate support, supported employment. SEVERE (IQ ~20-34, ~3-4%): limited communication, significant daily support needed, may learn sight words and basic self-care. PROFOUND (IQ <20, ~1-2%): requires 24-hour support, very limited communication, may use AAC. KEY: ID is LIFELONG but with appropriate supports, functioning improves throughout life.\\\",\\n     \\\"Diagnosing Provider /\\\\nRegional Center\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nMedical team\\\",\\n     \\\"Completed evaluation with severity level. DSM-5 diagnostic code. Associated condition documentation.\\\",\\n     \\\"At evaluation completion.\\\",\\n     \\\"Severity level misunderstood as 'ceiling' for growth. Parents given prognosis without hope. Medical model focuses on deficits rather than strengths and support needs. Severity level drives expectations rather than individualized assessment. Older terminology (mental retardation, EMR/TMR) still used by some professionals \\u2014 stigmatizing.\\\",\\n     \\\"ID severity level explainer with strengths-based framing. 'What support needs does this level indicate?' (not 'what can't my child do?'). Terminology guide (current person-first language). Strengths inventory tool. Peer stories from families at each level. Link to The Arc resources.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"2.3\\\", \\\"Process Diagnosis \\u2014 Emotional Support & Next Steps\\\",\\n     \\\"ID diagnosis often triggers profound grief, fear, and uncertainty for families. Key support: (1) Connect with peer families \\u2014 Family Resource Centers (FRCNCA), UC Davis MIND Institute Family Navigator, Parents Helping Parents (PHP). (2) Understand that diagnosis opens doors to services and supports. (3) Begin learning about dual system (RC + school). (4) Join parent support groups (online and in-person). (5) Learn about person-first language and strengths-based approach. (6) Connect with The Arc (national) and local Arc chapters. Programs: UC Davis MIND Institute \\u2014 peer support, monthly virtual groups, parent coaching, Padres Para Padres (Spanish). FRCNCA \\u2014 statewide network of family resource centers.\\\",\\n     \\\"Family Resource Centers\\\\nUC Davis MIND Institute\\\\nParents Helping Parents\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\nThe Arc\\\",\\n     \\\"None \\u2014 self-referral to support programs. RC intake may facilitate connections.\\\",\\n     \\\"Immediately upon diagnosis \\u2014 don't delay connection to support.\\\",\\n     \\\"Family isolated \\u2014 no peer connection. Cultural stigma prevents seeking support. Non-English-speaking families lack accessible resources. Medical professionals deliver diagnosis without connecting to support. Parents given only medical/clinical framing without strengths/community perspective.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Automatic peer support referral at diagnosis: FRC by county, MIND Institute, PHP. Culturally/linguistically matched family connector. 'What to do in the first 30 days after diagnosis' action plan. Emotional support resources for parents and siblings. The Arc chapter finder by ZIP.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 3\\n    [\\\"3\\\\nEarly\\\\nIntervention\\\\n(Birth-3)\\\", \\\"3.1\\\", \\\"Early Start IFSP \\u2014 ID-Specific Services\\\",\\n     \\\"For children under 3 with ID: Regional Center Early Start provides family-centered intervention. IFSP services typically include: speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, physical therapy (especially for motor delays), developmental specialist, parent training/coaching, behavioral support if needed, assistive technology/AAC. Services in natural environments (home, daycare). For ID with known etiology (Down syndrome, genetic conditions): medical management and monitoring also critical. Frequency should match severity \\u2014 more severe ID needs more intensive early intervention. Hanen 'More Than Words' program often used for children with limited communication.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\\n(Early Start)\\\", \\\"SLP\\\\nOT\\\\nPT\\\\nDev Specialist\\\",\\n     \\\"IFSP developed with family. Signed consent. All evaluations.\\\",\\n     \\\"45 days from referral to IFSP. Services begin immediately after IFSP.\\\",\\n     \\\"Service intensity insufficient for child's needs. Therapy focused on deficit remediation rather than functional participation. Parent coaching not provided \\u2014 parent becomes passive observer of therapy. Transition to school at age 3 not planned early enough. Medical management not coordinated with developmental services.\\\",\\n     \\\"IFSP goal generator for ID by severity. Service intensity recommendation guide. Parent coaching integration prompts. Medical-developmental coordination template. Age 2.5 transition planning trigger.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"3.2\\\", \\\"Transition from Early Start to School District + Ongoing RC (Age 3)\\\",\\n     \\\"At age 3, DUAL SYSTEM begins: child transitions to school district IEP AND continues Regional Center services. This is DIFFERENT from autism (where some children exit RC if mild) \\u2014 ID almost always continues with RC. School district evaluation and IEP must be in place by 3rd birthday. RC continues IPP (Individual Program Plan) with different but complementary services. Key: school provides EDUCATIONAL services (classroom instruction, school-based therapies). RC provides COMMUNITY/HOME services (respite, after-school programs, parent training, behavioral support, community inclusion). Parent must coordinate between two systems.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center \\u2192\\\\nSchool District + RC\\\", \\\"SLP\\\\nOT\\\\nPT\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"Transition meeting (RC + school district). School evaluation consent. IFSP records. RC IPP continuation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Transition conference: 90+ days before 3rd birthday. IEP in place by 3rd birthday.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 DUAL SYSTEM CONFUSION: Parents don't understand RC continues after school starts \\u2014 they think school 'replaces' RC. Services duplicated or gaps where each system thinks the other covers it. RC service coordinator and school case manager don't communicate. IPP goals and IEP goals not aligned. School provides less therapy than Early Start \\u2014 parents feel 'cliff.'\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: Dual system explainer \\u2014 'school provides education, RC provides community/home supports.' RC-IEP coordination template. Service gap analysis tool (what school provides vs what RC provides). Transition timeline countdown. IPP-IEP goal alignment tool.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 4\\n    [\\\"4\\\\nSchool\\\\nDistrict IEP\\\\n(Age 3+)\\\", \\\"4.1\\\", \\\"School District Evaluation for ID Eligibility\\\",\\n     \\\"Under IDEA, ID is defined as 'significantly subaverage general intellectual functioning, existing concurrently with deficits in adaptive behavior and manifested during the developmental period that adversely affects a child's educational performance' (34 CFR \\u00a7300.8(c)(6)). California Code of Regulations (5 CCR \\u00a73030). School evaluation includes: IQ testing, adaptive behavior assessment, academic achievement testing, speech/language evaluation, motor assessment, social-emotional assessment, observation. CALIFORNIA-SPECIFIC: Larry P. v. Riles (1979/1986) \\u2014 IQ tests CANNOT be used to place Black students in ID category or substantially equivalent placements. Alternative assessments required.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\\n(School Psychologist)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nRegional Center\\\",\\n     \\\"Written evaluation request (or transition from Early Start). Signed assessment plan. All RC records. Medical documentation.\\\",\\n     \\\"15 days to present assessment plan. 60 days from consent to completed evaluation.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Larry P. COMPLIANCE: School district uses IQ test for Black student \\u2014 ILLEGAL in California for ID placement. Alternative assessments for Black students may be less well-standardized. Adaptive behavior assessment incomplete. Child tested in non-native language. Evaluation focuses on deficits only \\u2014 no strengths documented. Mild ID may not be identified if child compensates well in structured testing.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Larry P. compliance alert (automatic for Black students). Alternative assessment guide for ID when IQ tests prohibited. Comprehensive evaluation checklist for ID. Culturally responsive assessment advocacy guide. Strengths documentation request template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.2\\\", \\\"IEP Development \\u2014 ID-Specific Placement & Services\\\",\\n     \\\"IEP for ID must address: (1) PLACEMENT on the continuum \\u2014 from general education with supports (LRE principle) through resource specialist, special day class (SDC), to separate school. LRE = Least Restrictive Environment. Child must be educated with non-disabled peers to maximum extent appropriate. (2) SERVICES: specialized academic instruction (modified curriculum), speech-language therapy, OT, PT, behavioral support, adaptive PE, assistive technology/AAC, 1:1 aide if needed. (3) GOALS: functional academics, communication, self-care/adaptive skills, social skills, community skills, pre-vocational skills. (4) MODIFICATIONS (not just accommodations): modified curriculum, alternative assessments (California Alternate Assessment for state testing). Extended School Year (ESY) \\u2014 many students with ID qualify because of regression risk.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\nParent\\\\nAdvocate\\\",\\n     \\\"All evaluations. Parent concerns letter. RC IPP. Medical documentation. Placement preference letter.\\\",\\n     \\\"30 days from completed evaluation to IEP meeting.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 PLACEMENT BATTLES: Child automatically placed in most restrictive setting (separate school) without considering LRE. 'Modified curriculum' used to justify segregated placement. General education teachers untrained for inclusive practices. District claims inclusion is 'too expensive.' Parents not informed of full placement continuum. IDEA's strong preference for inclusion not honored. Paraprofessional support used as substitute for qualified teacher instruction. ESY denied despite regression evidence.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 LRE/inclusion advocacy toolkit. Placement continuum explainer with rights. Inclusion success stories and research. ESY eligibility checklist. Modified curriculum vs accommodation comparison. Paraprofessional training requirements. IEP goal bank for ID by severity level and age.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.3\\\", \\\"IEP Dispute Resolution \\u2014 ID-Specific Issues\\\",\\n     \\\"Common ID-specific disputes: (1) Overly restrictive placement. (2) Failure to provide inclusion supports. (3) Inadequate behavioral support. (4) ESY denied. (5) Transition services inadequate. (6) Larry P. violations. Remedies: IEP meeting request \\u2192 CDE state complaint (60 days) \\u2192 mediation \\u2192 due process (45 days). Disability Rights California (DRC) provides free advocacy for ID families. The Arc provides advocacy resources and local chapter support. Key: ID families more frequently face placement disputes than families of children with ADHD or SLD.\\\",\\n     \\\"School District / CDE / OAH\\\", \\\"DRC\\\\nThe Arc\\\\nSpecial Ed Attorney\\\",\\n     \\\"Documentation of disagreement. Prior Written Notice. Placement data. Inclusion research. DRC intake form.\\\",\\n     \\\"CDE complaint: 60 days. Due process: 45 days.\\\",\\n     \\\"Family doesn't know about DRC free services. Larry P. violations not reported.\\\",\\n     \\\"Dispute resolution decision tree for ID. DRC referral generator. The Arc advocacy resource link. Inclusion research brief for hearing officers.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 5\\n    [\\\"5\\\\nRegional Center\\\\nIPP & Services\\\\n(Lifelong)\\\", \\\"5.1\\\", \\\"IPP Development \\u2014 ID-Specific Services\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center IPP (Individual Program Plan) is the LIFELONG service plan for individuals with ID. IPP services may include: respite care (relief for families), behavioral support services, day programs/after-school programs, social/recreational activities, community integration support, independent living skills training, supported employment, residential services (as adult), transportation, assistive technology, parent training, crisis intervention. IPP is reviewed at least annually. Services are individualized \\u2014 no 'menu' of standard services. POS (Purchase of Service) codes fund specific services. Family must actively participate in IPP development. CA DDS budget: $18.7B (FY 2025-26, largest in history).\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\\n(Service Coordinator)\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nService providers\\\",\\n     \\\"IPP meeting. All evaluations. Parent input on priorities. Service provider reports.\\\",\\n     \\\"IPP within 60 days of eligibility. Annual review required.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 RC SERVICE GAPS: Service coordinator caseload too high (100+ clients) \\u2014 limited advocacy capacity. POS spending disparities by race/ethnicity well-documented. Services denied as 'not necessary' without adequate justification. Family not informed of available services. Generic IPP not individualized to child's needs. Wait lists for behavioral support, respite, SLS. RC says 'school is responsible' for things RC should provide (and vice versa).\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IPP preparation guide with service menu. POS spending rights awareness. RC service request template. Racial equity in RC services resources (DRC reports). Service coordinator contact tracker. IPP goal generator by age and severity. Wait list tracking tool.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"5.2\\\", \\\"Apply for Medi-Cal, SSI, IHSS, CalABLE\\\",\\n     \\\"BENEFITS PACKAGE for ID: (1) MEDI-CAL: HCBS waiver, 45-day determination, covers medical + therapy. ID with RC eligibility = automatic Medi-Cal eligibility in many cases. (2) SSI: Supplemental Security Income for children with disabilities. ID is one of the highest-approval-rate conditions for SSI. Function report critical \\u2014 describe daily challenges in detail. (3) IHSS: In-Home Supportive Services \\u2014 personal care, domestic services, protective supervision. Many children with moderate-severe ID qualify for protective supervision. (4) CalABLE: No minimum age. $19K/year tax-advantaged savings. SSI $100K threshold. For ID, CalABLE is essential for long-term financial planning since ID is lifelong.\\\",\\n     \\\"SSA (SSI)\\\\nDSS (IHSS)\\\\nMedi-Cal\\\\nCalABLE\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"SSI application. IHSS application. Medi-Cal enrollment. CalABLE enrollment. Medical/RC documentation.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medi-Cal: 45 days. SSI: 3-6 months. IHSS: 30-60 days. CalABLE: immediate.\\\",\\n     \\\"SSI denied despite qualifying ID diagnosis \\u2014 appeal is critical (high overturn rate). IHSS protective supervision denied \\u2014 requires strong documentation. Medi-Cal HCBS waiver not offered. CalABLE not opened early. Benefits coordination complex \\u2014 SSI and IHSS amounts interact. Self-direction options for IHSS not explained.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Benefits application coordinator \\u2014 tracks all 4 applications simultaneously. SSI function report generator for ID. IHSS protective supervision documentation template. CalABLE enrollment prompt at EVERY age. Benefits interaction calculator. Appeal templates for denials.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 6\\n    [\\\"6\\\\nTherapy,\\\\nHealth &\\\\nDaily Life\\\", \\\"6.1\\\", \\\"Coordinate Medical & Therapeutic Care\\\",\\n     \\\"Many children with ID have complex medical needs alongside developmental needs. Common co-occurring medical conditions: seizure disorders/epilepsy (~30% of ID), hearing and vision impairments, cardiac conditions (especially Down syndrome), feeding/swallowing difficulties, sleep disorders, dental issues, obesity. Therapeutic services: SLP (communication/AAC/feeding), OT (fine motor, sensory, self-care), PT (gross motor, mobility), behavioral support (ABA may be appropriate depending on co-occurring autism), psychology/psychiatry (mental health \\u2014 ID + mental health co-occurs 3-4x more than general population).\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / Medical Home\\\\nSpecialists\\\", \\\"RC\\\\nSchool\\\\nInsurance/Medi-Cal\\\",\\n     \\\"Medical records. Specialist referrals. Insurance authorizations. RC service plan. School therapy notes.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 medical care is lifelong for many ID-associated conditions.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medical needs overshadow developmental needs (or vice versa). No medical home coordination. Specialists don't communicate with each other or with RC/school. Insurance denies therapies. Mental health needs unaddressed \\u2014 behavior attributed to ID rather than co-occurring depression/anxiety. Polypharmacy without adequate monitoring.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medical-developmental coordination dashboard. Specialist appointment tracker. Insurance therapy coverage checker. Mental health screening prompt for ID. Medication tracker. Medical-school-RC communication template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"6.2\\\", \\\"Build Adaptive Skills & Community Inclusion\\\",\\n     \\\"Core focus for ID families: building functional independence through adaptive skill development. Daily living skills: self-care (hygiene, dressing, toileting), meal preparation, household tasks, money management, time management, safety skills. Community skills: transportation, shopping, restaurant, recreation, social interaction. Self-advocacy: understanding own needs, communicating preferences, making choices. KEY PRINCIPLE: Presume competence \\u2014 assume the individual CAN learn, given appropriate support and instruction. Community inclusion: Special Olympics, adaptive recreation, inclusive community programs, faith community inclusion. Self-Determination movement: individuals with ID have the right to direct their own lives.\\\",\\n     \\\"Parents\\\\nRC providers\\\\nSchool\\\\nCommunity programs\\\", \\\"The Arc\\\\nSpecial Olympics\\\",\\n     \\\"Adaptive skills assessment (from IEP/IPP). Opportunities for community inclusion limited. Social isolation, especially in adolescence. Community programs not accessible or welcoming. Skills taught in isolation (school) don't transfer to community. Peer relationships limited to disability-specific settings.\\\",\\n     \\\"Adaptive skill-building checklist by age and severity. Social skills and friendship-building resources.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 7\\n    [\\\"7\\\\nTransition\\\\nPlanning\\\\n(Age 14+)\\\", \\\"7.1\\\", \\\"Begin Transition Planning \\u2014 ITP Required\\\",\\n     \\\"Transition planning for ID is MORE COMPLEX than any other disability category because it involves both school district AND Regional Center adult services. By age 14-16: ITP (Individual Transition Plan) within IEP. Key areas: (1) Post-secondary education/training (community college disability services, adult education, certificate programs). (2) Employment (supported employment, customized employment, competitive integrated employment \\u2014 CIE). (3) Independent living (supported living, independent living, residential care). (4) Community participation. (5) Self-advocacy and self-determination. Connect to DOR (Dept of Rehabilitation) by age 16. Connect to RC adult services planning by age 16-17.\\\",\\n     \\\"School District\\\\nRegional Center\\\", \\\"DOR\\\\nSSA\\\\nIHSS\\\\nCalABLE\\\",\\n     \\\"Transition assessments. ITP goals. DOR referral. RC adult services planning. Post-secondary exploration.\\\",\\n     \\\"ITP required by age 16 (begin earlier). DOR connection by 16. RC adult planning by 17.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 TRANSITION CLIFF: School services end at 22 or diploma \\u2014 adult services don't seamlessly replace them. RC adult services have waitlists. DOR vocational services limited. Supported employment underutilized (sheltered workshops still common despite evidence for CIE). Conservatorship pursued when less restrictive alternatives (SDM) would suffice. SSI transition from child to adult criteria at 18 \\u2014 many lose benefits. Housing crisis for adults with ID.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL: Transition planning dashboard (school + RC + DOR + SSA simultaneously). CIE vs sheltered workshop comparison. RC adult services planning checklist.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.2\\\", \\\"Conservatorship / Alternatives at Age 18\\\",\\n     \\\"At age 18, child becomes legal adult regardless of cognitive ability. Options: (1) FULL CONSERVATORSHIP: most restrictive \\u2014 another person makes all decisions. Court process, attorney required, costly, removes nearly all rights. Should be LAST resort. (2) LIMITED CONSERVATORSHIP: most common for ID \\u2014 conservator has authority in specific areas (residence, medical, education, social) but individual retains other rights. Preferred over full conservatorship. (3) SUPPORTED DECISION-MAKING (SDM): emerging alternative \\u2014 individual retains all legal rights but designates trusted supporters to help with decisions. Growing movement. (4) POWER OF ATTORNEY: if individual can understand and consent. Start planning 12-18 months before 18th birthday.\\\",\\n     \\\"Attorney / Court\\\\nRegional Center\\\", \\\"Parent\\\\nDRC\\\",\\n     \\\"Legal consultation (12-18 months before 18). Medical/psychological documentation. Court filing if pursuing conservatorship. SDM agreement if using SDM.\\\",\\n     \\\"Begin planning at 16-17. File 6-12 months before 18th birthday if conservatorship needed.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Conservatorship pursued by default without considering SDM or limited conservatorship. Full conservatorship used when limited would suffice. Court process overwhelming and expensive ($3,000-5,000+). Individual's preferences not considered. RC doesn't inform families about SDM alternative. Conservatorship restricts voting rights in some cases.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Conservatorship vs SDM decision guide. Limited conservatorship checklist. SDM agreement template. Court filing timeline. DRC resources on conservatorship alternatives. Cost and process comparison. Rights retained under each option.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"7.3\\\", \\\"Adult Services Planning (RC IPP, Employment, Housing)\\\",\\n     \\\"After school exit (22 or diploma): Regional Center becomes primary service system. Adult IPP should address: (1) DAY SERVICES: day program, supported employment, community-based activities. (2) EMPLOYMENT: CIE (Competitive Integrated Employment) is the GOLD STANDARD \\u2014 supported employment with job coach, customized employment, self-employment. DOR provides vocational rehabilitation. (3) RESIDENTIAL: family home with supports, supported living services (SLS), independent living, group homes, specialized residential. (4) RECREATION/SOCIAL: adaptive recreation, social groups, community inclusion programs. (5) HEALTH: continued medical management, mental health support. RC budget for adult services is largest portion of $18.7B DDS budget.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\\nDOR\\\", \\\"SSA\\\\nIHSS\\\\nMedi-Cal\\\\nHousing authority\\\",\\n     \\\"Adult IPP. DOR IPE (Individual Plan for Employment). SSI adult application. IHSS re-assessment. Housing applications.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 annual IPP review. DOR services until employed. Housing: multi-year waitlists.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 HOUSING CRISIS: Affordable accessible housing waitlists are YEARS long. Sheltered workshops still exist despite federal push for CIE. Day programs are 'custodial' rather than skill-building. RC service coordinator overwhelmed \\u2014 insufficient advocacy. Family aging out (parent caregivers aging). Self-determination program underutilized. Racial disparities in adult service access persistent.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Adult services navigation dashboard. CIE employment opportunity finder. Housing resource guide by county. Self-determination program enrollment. Aging caregiver planning tools. Racial equity monitoring for RC services. Day program quality comparison tool.\\\"],\\n\\n    # PHASE 8\\n    [\\\"8\\\\nLifelong\\\\nSupport &\\\\nAdvocacy\\\", \\\"8.1\\\", \\\"Ongoing Benefits Management & Monitoring\\\",\\n     \\\"Lifelong benefits coordination: SSI CDR (Continuing Disability Review) every 1-7 years. IHSS annual reassessment. Medi-Cal annual renewal. CalABLE contributions and spending tracking. SNT (Special Needs Trust) if assets exceed SSI/Medi-Cal limits. SSI and IHSS amounts adjust with living situation changes. Report changes to SSA within 10 days (overpayment risk). Benefits coordination for working adults with ID \\u2014 PASS (Plan to Achieve Self-Support), IRWE (Impairment-Related Work Expenses), 1619(b) for Medi-Cal retention.\\\",\\n     \\\"SSA / IHSS / Medi-Cal / CalABLE\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\nFinancial planner\\\\nSNT trustee\\\",\\n     \\\"Benefit renewal documentation. Ongoing monitoring.\\\",\\n     \\\"Benefits overpayment due to unreported changes. CDR results in loss of benefits (requires appeal). IHSS hours reduced at reassessment. CalABLE/SNT coordination errors. Working adults lose benefits unexpectedly. Complex benefits rules overwhelm families.\\\",\\n     \\\"Benefits renewal calendar and tracker. CDR preparation guide. Change reporting reminder system.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"8.2\\\", \\\"Long-Term Advocacy & Community Engagement\\\",\\n     \\\"Lifelong advocacy needs for individuals with ID: (1) The Arc: largest national IDD advocacy organization \\u2014 local chapters, policy advocacy, community inclusion programs, Achieve with Us employment network. (2) Self-advocacy: People First movement, self-advocacy groups. (3) Voting rights: most individuals with ID CAN vote (conservatorship may affect this \\u2014 know your rights). (4) Community inclusion: friendships, recreation, faith community, civic participation. (5) Health advocacy: preventive care, mental health access, healthy aging (life expectancy for ID has increased dramatically but healthcare disparities persist). (6) Policy advocacy: Lanterman Act protection, DDS budget, HCBS settings rule, Employment First policy.\\\",\\n     \\\"The Arc\\\\nSelf-advocacy organizations\\\\nRegional Center\\\", \\\"DRC\\\\nCommunity organizations\\\",\\n     \\\"The Arc membership. Self-advocacy group connection. Voter registration if applicable.\\\",\\n     \\\"Ongoing \\u2014 lifelong.\\\",\\n     \\\"Isolation increases as person ages. Self-advocacy opportunities not offered. Voting rights restricted without basis. Health care providers lack training on ID. Aging with ID presents unique medical challenges.     "text": "**V9 delivered \u2014 14 sheets, 72 entities, 71 sources.**\n\n---\n\n### \u2705 New Column: \"Waypoint Integration Opportunity\" (Column 15 in Master Entity Matrix)\n\nAdded to all 72 entities \u2014 mirrors the actionable integration notes from National Resources Directory, giving each entity a specific Waypoint automation hook (e.g., \"Auto-connect to local Arc chapter by ZIP,\" \"Larry P. compliance alert for Black students\").\n\n---\n\n### New Sheet: ID Journey Map \u2014 8 Phases, 25 Steps\n\nThis is the most complex journey map yet because ID families navigate a **DUAL SYSTEM** (school district IEP + Regional Center IPP) that continues across the entire lifespan.\n\n**Phase 1 \u2014 Early Recognition (2 steps)**\n- Developmental delays across multiple domains (motor, speech, cognitive, self-care)\n- Regional Center self-referral \u2014 no doctor referral needed, ID is one of 5 Lanterman Act qualifying conditions\n\n**Phase 2 \u2014 Evaluation & Diagnosis (3 steps)**\n- DSM-5 criteria: IQ \u226470 + adaptive behavior deficits + onset during development\n- Severity levels based on ADAPTIVE functioning (not IQ): Mild (85%), Moderate (10%), Severe (3-4%), Profound (1-2%)\n- **Larry P. v. Riles: IQ tests CANNOT be used to identify or place Black students in ID category in California** \u2014 alternative assessments required\n- Emotional support and peer connection at diagnosis (FRCNCA, MIND Institute Family Navigator, PHP)\n\n**Phase 3 \u2014 Early Intervention Birth-3 (2 steps)**\n- Early Start IFSP with multi-therapy approach\n- **Age 3 DUAL SYSTEM begins:** child transitions to school IEP AND continues RC services \u2014 this is unique to ID/DD\n\n**Phase 4 \u2014 School District IEP (3 steps)**\n- ID eligibility under IDEA (34 CFR \u00a7300.8(c)(6))\n- LRE/inclusion advocacy \u2014 strong IDEA preference for education with non-disabled peers\n- Placement continuum battles (segregated placement as default vs. supported inclusion)\n- ESY (Extended School Year) \u2014 many ID students qualify due to regression risk\n- Modified curriculum and California Alternate Assessment for state testing\n\n**Phase 5 \u2014 Regional Center IPP & Lifelong Services (2 steps)**\n- IPP as the LIFELONG service plan \u2014 respite, behavioral support, day programs, supported employment, residential\n- **Full benefits package:** Medi-Cal, SSI (high approval rate for ID), IHSS (protective supervision), CalABLE\n- POS spending disparities by race/ethnicity documented and significant\n\n**Phase 6 \u2014 Therapy, Health & Daily Life (2 steps)**\n- Complex medical co-occurring conditions (epilepsy ~30%, hearing/vision, cardiac, feeding)\n- Mental health co-occurs 3-4x higher than general population \u2014 often attributed to ID rather than diagnosed separately\n- Adaptive skill building and community inclusion (Special Olympics, self-determination)\n\n**Phase 7 \u2014 Transition Planning Age 14+ (3 steps)**\n- **Most complex transition of any disability** \u2014 school district + RC + DOR + SSA simultaneously\n- **Conservatorship vs. Supported Decision-Making (SDM)** at age 18 \u2014 critical rights decision\n- CIE (Competitive Integrated Employment) as gold standard replacing sheltered workshops\n- Adult residential options in context of California's housing crisis\n- DDS budget: $18.7B (FY 2025-26, largest in history)\n\n**Phase 8 \u2014 Lifelong Support & Advocacy (2 steps)**\n- Benefits monitoring (SSI CDR, IHSS, Medi-Cal renewals, CalABLE/SNT coordination)\n- The Arc advocacy, self-advocacy (People First), voting rights, healthy aging\n\n**6 ID entities added to Master Matrix:** The Arc, FRCNCA, UC Davis MIND Institute, Parents Helping Parents, UCP, CA Dept of Education Family Involvement\n\n**13 ID sources added** (The Arc, FRCNCA, MIND Institute, PHP, DRC Eligibility Packet, Undivided ID 101, Alta RC, SERR/DRC, CA DDS, UCP, US DOE IDEA, CDE Family Involvement)\n\n**Key strategic insight for Waypoint:** ID is the only journey that requires **lifelong dual-system navigation** \u2014 school IEP (ages 3-22) plus Regional Center IPP (birth through entire adult life). Every other journey map (Autism, ADHD, SLI, SLD, PDA) primarily resolves through the school system, but ID families must coordinate two bureaucracies simultaneously for decades. Waypoint's highest-value automation for ID families is a **dual-system coordination dashboard** that tracks IEP goals, IPP goals, and ensures no service gaps between the two systems.",

This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.\"\n     4\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     5\t---\n     6\t\n     7\t# Requirements for Outputs\n     8\t\n     9\t## All Excel files\n    10\t\n    11\t### Professional Font\n    12\t- Use a consistent, professional font (e.g., Arial, Times New Roman) for all deliverables unless otherwise instructed by the user\n    13\t\n    14\t### Zero Formula Errors\n    15\t- Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)\n    16\t\n    17\t### Preserve Existing Templates (when updating templates)\n    18\t- Study and EXACTLY match existing format, style, and conventions when modifying files\n    19\t- Never impose standardized formatting on files with established patterns\n    20\t- Existing template conventions ALWAYS override these guidelines\n    21\t\n    22\t## Financial models\n    23\t\n    24\t### Color Coding Standards\n    25\tUnless otherwise stated by the user or existing template\n    26\t\n    27\t#### Industry-Standard Color Conventions\n    28\t- **Blue text (RGB: 0,0,255)**: Hardcoded inputs, and numbers users will change for scenarios\n    29\t- **Black text (RGB: 0,0,0)**: ALL formulas and calculations\n    30\t- **Green text (RGB: 0,128,0)**: Links pulling from other worksheets within same workbook\n    31\t- **Red text (RGB: 255,0,0)**: External links to other files\n    32\t- **Yellow background (RGB: 255,255,0)**: Key assumptions needing attention or cells that need to be updated\n    33\t\n    34\t### Number Formatting Standards\n    35\t\n    36\t#### Required Format Rules\n    37\t- **Years**: Format as text strings (e.g., \"2024\" not \"2,024\")\n    38\t- **Currency**: Use $#,##0 format; ALWAYS specify units in headers (\"Revenue ($mm)\")\n    39\t- **Zeros**: Use number formatting to make all zeros \"-\", including percentages (e.g., \"$#,##0;($#,##0);-\")\n    40\t- **Percentages**: Default to 0.0% format (one decimal)\n    41\t- **Multiples**: Format as 0.0x for valuation multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E)\n    42\t- **Negative numbers**: Use parentheses (123) not minus -123\n    43\t\n    44\t### Formula Construction Rules\n    45\t\n    46\t#### Assumptions Placement\n    47\t- Place ALL assumptions (growth rates, margins, multiples, etc.) in separate assumption cells\n    48\t- Use cell references instead of hardcoded values in formulas\n    49\t- Example: Use =B5*(1+$B$6) instead of =B5*1.05\n    50\t\n    51\t#### Formula Error Prevention\n    52\t- Verify all cell references are correct\n    53\t- Check for off-by-one errors in ranges\n    54\t- Ensure consistent formulas across all projection periods\n    55\t- Test with edge cases (zero values, negative numbers)\n    56\t- Verify no unintended circular references\n    57\t\n    58\t#### Documentation Requirements for Hardcodes\n    59\t- Comment or in cells beside (if end of table). Format: \"Source: [System/Document], [Date], [Specific Reference], [URL if applicable]\"\n    60\t- Examples:\n    61\t  - \"Source: Company 10-K, FY2024, Page 45, Revenue Note, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    62\t  - \"Source: Company 10-Q, Q2 2025, Exhibit 99.1, [SEC EDGAR URL]\"\n    63\t  - \"Source: Bloomberg Terminal, 8/15/2025, AAPL US Equity\"\n    64\t  - \"Source: FactSet, 8/20/2025, Consensus Estimates Screen\"\n    65\t\n    66\t# XLSX creation, editing, and analysis\n    67\t\n    68\t## Overview\n    69\t\n    70\tA user may ask you to create, edit, or analyze the contents of an .xlsx file. You have different tools and workflows available for different tasks.\n    71\t\n    72\t## Important Requirements\n    73\t\n    74\t**LibreOffice Required for Formula Recalculation**: You can assume LibreOffice is installed for recalculating formula values using the `scripts/recalc.py` script. The script automatically configures LibreOffice on first run, including in sandboxed environments where Unix sockets are restricted (handled by `scripts/office/soffice.py`)\n    75\t\n    76\t## Reading and analyzing data\n    77\t\n    78\t### Data analysis with pandas\n    79\tFor data analysis, visualization, and basic operations, use **pandas** which provides powerful data manipulation capabilities:\n    80\t\n    81\t```python\n    82\timport pandas as pd\n    83\t\n    84\t# Read Excel\n    85\tdf = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # Default: first sheet\n    86\tall_sheets = Statistics\n    92\t\n    93\t# Write Excel\n    94\tdf.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)\n    95\t```\n    96\t\n    97\t## Excel File Workflows\n    98\t\n    99\t## CRITICAL: Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values\n   100\t\n   101\t**Always use Excel formulas instead of calculating values in Python and hardcoding them.** This ensures the spreadsheet remains dynamic and updateable.\n   102\t\n   103\t### \u274c WRONG - Hardcoding Calculated Values\n   104\t```python\n   105\t# Bad: Calculating in Python and hardcoding result\n   106\ttotal = df['Sales'].sum()\n   107\tsheet['B10'] = total  # Hardcodes 5000\n   108\t\n   109\t# Bad: Computing growth rate in Python\n   110\tgrowth = (df.iloc[-1]['Revenue'] - df.iloc[0]['Revenue']) / df.iloc[0]['Revenue']\n   111\tsheet['C5'] = growth  # Hardcodes 0.15\n   112\t\n   113\t# Bad: Python calculation for average\n   114\tavg = sum(values) / len(values)\n   115\tsheet['D20'] = avg  # Hardcodes 42.5\n   116\t```\n   117\t\n   118\t### \u2705 CORRECT - Using Excel Formulas\n   119\t```python\n   120\t# Good: Let Excel calculate the sum\n   121\tsheet['B10'] = '=SUM(B2:B9)'\n   122\t\n   123\t# Good: Growth rate as Excel formula\n   124\tsheet['C5'] = '=(C4-C2)/C2'\n   125\t\n   126\t# Good: Average using Excel function\n   127\tsheet['D20'] = '=AVERAGE(D2:D19)'\n   128\t```\n   129\t\n   130\tThis applies to ALL calculations - totals, percentages, ratios, differences, etc. The spreadsheet should be able to recalculate when source data changes.\n   131\t\n   132\t## Common Workflow\n   133\t1. **Choose tool**: pandas for data, openpyxl for formulas/formatting\n   134\t2. **Create/Load**: Create new workbook or load existing file\n   135\t3. **Modify**: Add/edit data, formulas, and formatting\n   136\t4. **Save**: Write to file\n   137\t5. **Recalculate formulas (MANDATORY IF USING FORMULAS)**: Use the scripts/recalc.py script\n   138\t   ```bash\n   139\t   python scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx\n   140\t   ```\n   141\t6. **Verify and fix any errors**: \n   142\t   - The script returns JSON with error details\n   143\t   - If `status` is `errors_found`, check `error_summary` for specific error types and locations\n   144\t   - Fix the identified errors and recalculate again\n   145\t   - Common errors to fix:\n   146\t     - `#REF!`: Invalid cell references\n   147\t     - `#DIV/0!`: Division by zero\n   148\t     - `#VALUE!`: Wrong data type in formula\n   149\t     - `#NAME?`: Unrecognized formula name\n   150\t\n   151\t### Creating new Excel files\n   152\t\n   153\t```python\n   154\t# Using openpyxl for formulas and formatting\n   155\tfrom openpyxl import Workbook\n   156\tfrom openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment\n   157\t\n   158\twb = Workbook()\n   159\tsheet = wb.active\n   160\t\n   161\t# Add data\n   162\tsheet['A1'] = 'Hello'\n   163\tsheet['B1'] = 'World'\n   164\tsheet.append(['Row', 'of', 'data'])\n   165\t\n   166\t# Add formula\n   167\tsheet['B2'] = '=SUM(A1:A10)'\n   168\t\n   169\t# Formatting\n   170\tsheet['A1'].font = Font(bold=True, color='FF0000')\n   171\tsheet['A1'].fill = PatternFill('solid', start_color='FFFF00')\n   172\tsheet['A1'].alignment = Alignment(horizontal='center')\n   173\t\n   174\t# Column width\n   175\tsheet.column_dimensions['A'].width = 20\n   176\t\n   177\twb.save('output.xlsx')\n   178\t```\n   179\t\n   180\t### Editing existing Excel files\n   181\t\n   182\t```python\n   183\t# Using openpyxl to preserve formulas and formatting\n   184\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   185\t\n   186\t# Load existing file\n   187\twb = load_workbook('existing.xlsx')\n   188\tsheet = wb.active  # or wb['SheetName'] for specific sheet\n   189\t\n   190\t# Working with multiple sheets\n   191\tfor sheet_name in wb.sheetnames:\n   192\t    sheet = wb[sheet_name]\n   193\t    print(f\"Sheet: {sheet_name}\")\n   194\t\n   195\t# Modify cells\n   196\tsheet['A1'] = 'New Value'\n   197\tsheet.insert_rows(2)  # Insert row at position 2\n   198\tsheet.delete_cols(3)  # Delete column 3\n   199\t\n   200\t# Add new sheet\n   201\tnew_sheet = wb.create_sheet('NewSheet')\n   202\tnew_sheet['A1'] = 'Data'\n   203\t\n   204\twb.save('modified.xlsx')\n   205\t```\n   206\t\n   207\t## Recalculating formulas\n   208\t\n   209\tExcel files created or modified by openpyxl contain formulas as strings but not calculated values. Use the provided `scripts/recalc.py` script to recalculate formulas:\n   210\t\n   211\t```bash\n   212\tpython scripts/recalc.py <excel_file> [timeout_seconds]\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tExample:\n   216\t```bash\n   217\tpython scripts/recalc.py output.xlsx 30\n   218\t```\n   219\t\n   220\tThe script:\n   221\t- Automatically sets up LibreOffice macro on first run\n   222\t- Recalculates all formulas in all sheets\n   223\t- Scans ALL cells for Excel errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.)\n   224\t- Returns JSON with detailed error locations and counts\n   225\t- Works on both Linux and macOS\n   226\t\n   227\t## Formula Verification Checklist\n   228\t\n   229\tQuick checks to ensure formulas work correctly:\n   230\t\n   231\t### Essential Verification\n   232\t- [ ] **Test 2-3 sample references**: Verify they pull correct values before building full model\n   233\t- [ ] **Column mapping**: Confirm Excel columns match (e.g., column 64 = BL, not BK)\n   234\t- [ ] **Row offset**: Remember Excel rows are 1-indexed (DataFrame row 5 = Excel row 6)\n   235\t\n   236\t### Common Pitfalls\n   237\t- [ ] **NaN handling**: Check for null values with `pd.notna()`\n   238\t- [ ] **Far-right columns**: FY data often in columns 50+ \n   239\t- [ ] **Multiple matches**: Search all occurrences, not just first\n   240\t- [ ] **Division by zero**: Check denominators before using `/` in formulas (#DIV/0!)\n   241\t- [ ] **Wrong references**: Verify all cell references point to intended cells (#REF!)\n   242\t- [ ] **Cross-sheet references**: Use correct format (Sheet1!A1) for linking sheets\n   243\t\n   244\t### Formula Testing Strategy\n   245\t- [ ] **Start small**: Test formulas on 2-3 cells before applying broadly\n   246\t- [ ] **Verify dependencies**: Check all cells referenced in formulas exist\n   247\t- [ ] **Test edge cases**: Include zero, negative, and very large values\n   248\t\n   249\t### Interpreting scripts/recalc.py Output\n   250\tThe script returns JSON with error details:\n   251\t```json\n   252\t{\n   253\t  \"status\": \"success\",           // or \"errors_found\"\n   254\t  \"total_errors\": 0,              // Total error count\n   255\t  \"total_formulas\": 42,           // Number of formulas in file\n   256\t  \"error_summary\": {              // Only present if errors found\n   257\t    \"#REF!\": {\n   258\t      \"count\": 2,\n   259\t      \"locations\": [\"Sheet1!B5\", \"Sheet1!C10\"]\n   260\t    }\n   261\t  }\n   262\t}\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\t## Best Practices\n   266\t\n   267\t### Library Selection\n   268\t- **pandas**: Best for data analysis, bulk operations, and simple data export\n   269\t- **openpyxl**: Best for complex formatting, formulas, and Excel-specific features\n   270\t\n   271\t### Working with openpyxl\n   272\t- Cell indices are 1-based (row=1, column=1 refers to cell A1)\n   273\t- Use `data_only=True` to read calculated values: `load_workbook('file.xlsx', data_only=True)`\n   274\t- **Warning**: If opened with `data_only=True` and saved, formulas are replaced with values and permanently lost\n   275\t- For large files: Use `read_only=True` for reading or `write_only=True` for writing\n   276\t- Formulas are preserved but not evaluated - use scripts/recalc.py to update values\n   277\t\n   278\t### Working with pandas\n   279\t- Specify data types to avoid inference issues: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', dtype={'id': str})`\n   280\t- For large files, read specific columns: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', usecols=['A', 'C', 'E'])`\n   281\t- Handle dates properly: `pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', parse_dates=['date_column'])`\n   282\t\n   283\t## Code Style Guidelines\n   284\t**IMPORTANT**: When generating Python code for Excel operations:\n   285\t- Write minimal, concise Python code without unnecessary comments\n   286\t- Avoid verbose variable names and redundant operations\n   287\t- Avoid unnecessary print statements\n   288\t\n   289\t**For Excel files themselves**:\n   290\t- Add comments to cells with complex formulas or important assumptions\n   291\t- Document data sources for hardcoded values\n   292\t- Include notes for key calculations and model sections",

(2) Cultural stigma prevents families from seeking evaluation \u2014 Latino/Black children diagnosed 2+ years later than White children. (3) Family members (grandparents, spouse) dismiss concerns \u2014 'My brother didn't talk until 3 and he's fine.' (4) Girls with autism missed \u2014 they mask/camouflage social difficulties better. (5) Child meets SOME milestones so parent assumes all is fine \u2014 autism is about quality of social interaction, not just presence/absence of skills.\",\n     # PARENT TRAINING\n     \"FREE PARENT TRAINING:\\n\u2022 CHADD Parent-to-Parent: chadd.org (covers neurodevelopmental concerns broadly)\\n\u2022 Autism Speaks 100 Day Kit: autismspeaks.org/tool-kit (free download at diagnosis)\\n\u2022 First Signs: firstsigns.org (video library of early signs)\\n\u2022 CDC 'Learn the Signs, Act Early': cdc.gov/actearly\\n\u2022 CA PTI Network: Your local Parent Training & Information center \u2014 find at parentcenterhub.org\\n\u2022 TASK (Team of Advocates for Special Kids): taskca.org\\n\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org (Bay Area)\",\n     # WAYPOINT\n     \"\u2605 Milestone tracker with autism-specific red flags by age. Push notifications at key screening ages (9, 12, 18, 24 mo). Video library of early signs. 'Should I be concerned?' decision tool. Auto-identify correct RC by ZIP. Culturally-sensitive prompts in 7+ languages. Pre-visit pediatrician concern list generator.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"1.2\", \"Well-Child Visit Screening (M-CHAT-R/F)\",\n     \"REQUEST M-CHAT-R/F SCREENING: (1) At the 18-month and 24-month well-child visits, ASK your pediatrician: 'Will you be administering the M-CHAT autism screening today?' (2) If they don't offer it, request it explicitly \u2014 it's a simple 20-question parent questionnaire that takes 5 minutes. (3) Complete the M-CHAT honestly \u2014 do not 'help' your child's score by assuming what they CAN do vs what they ACTUALLY DO. (4) If M-CHAT is positive (score 3+), insist on the Follow-Up Interview (M-CHAT-R/F) \u2014 this reduces false positives. (5) If screening is positive, immediately request referral to Regional Center AND a developmental pediatrician. (6) Even if M-CHAT is negative but you still have concerns, pursue evaluation anyway \u2014 M-CHAT misses ~20% of children who are later diagnosed.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Skip the 18- and 24-month well-child visits \u2014 these are the recommended screening windows. (2) Accept 'we don't do that screening' from your pediatrician \u2014 M-CHAT is AAP recommended and free. (3) Assume a negative M-CHAT means no autism \u2014 false negatives occur, especially for girls and milder presentations. (4) Let the pediatrician screen without completing the M-CHAT questionnaire (just asking 'does your child point?' in passing is NOT a valid screen). (5) Wait for a positive screen to act \u2014 if you have concerns regardless of screening result, self-refer to RC immediately.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Before your child's 18-month visit, call the office and ask: 'Will the M-CHAT-R/F be administered at this visit? If not, I'd like to request it.'\\nSTEP 2: Complete the M-CHAT-R/F questionnaire honestly in the waiting room. Score is 0-20 \u2014 scores of 3-7 = medium risk (follow-up needed), 8+ = high risk (refer immediately).\\nSTEP 3: If score is 3+, the pediatrician should administer the Follow-Up Interview. If they don't, request it or score it yourself at mchatscreen.com.\\nSTEP 4: If screening is positive, say to your pediatrician: 'Based on this screening, I'd like two things today: (a) a referral to a developmental pediatrician, and (b) the phone number for our Regional Center so I can self-refer for evaluation.'\\nSTEP 5: Do NOT leave the appointment without a written plan for next steps. If the doctor says 'let's rescreen in 6 months,' say: 'I'd prefer to begin the evaluation process now and cancel if concerns resolve.'\",\n     \"PEDIATRICIAN: Your child's doctor administers the M-CHAT at well-child visits.\\nM-CHAT ONLINE: mchatscreen.com \u2014 free, you can complete and score it yourself at any time.\\nIF PEDIATRICIAN WON'T SCREEN: Complete M-CHAT yourself at mchatscreen.com and bring results to appointment. Say: 'I completed the M-CHAT and my child scored [X]. I'd like to discuss this and get referrals.'\\nDEVELOPMENTAL PEDIATRICIAN REFERRAL: Ask pediatrician for referral to developmental pediatrician at nearest children's hospital (e.g., UCSF, Stanford, Children's Oakland).\\nREGIONAL CENTER: Self-refer directly \u2014 no doctor needed.\",\n     \"\u2022 M-CHAT-R/F completed questionnaire (keep a copy)\\n\u2022 M-CHAT-R/F Follow-Up Interview results (if applicable)\\n\u2022 Well-child visit summary/after-visit note\\n\u2022 Referral to developmental pediatrician (written)\\n\u2022 Your developmental concerns journal and videos\\n\u2022 Any prior developmental screening results\",\n     \"18-month well-child visit: First M-CHAT screening.\\n24-month well-child visit: Second M-CHAT screening.\\nAAP also recommends general developmental screening at 9, 18, and 30 months.\\nIf positive screen: referral should happen SAME DAY. Do not wait for next appointment.\",\n     \"Pediatrician / PCP\", \"None at this stage\",\n     \"\u2605 SCREENING FAILURES: (1) M-CHAT not administered \u2014 many pediatricians skip it despite AAP recommendation. (2) Screening done casually (verbal questions only, not formal questionnaire). (3) False negatives \u2014 M-CHAT sensitivity is ~83%, meaning ~17% of autistic children screen negative. (4) Positive screen but no follow-up \u2014 pediatrician says 'let's wait.' (5) Latino and Black children 40% less likely to be screened and referred. (6) Screening at 18 months too early for some children \u2014 repeat at 24 months critical.\",\n     \"M-CHAT ONLINE: mchatscreen.com (free, validated, score instantly)\\nAAP SCREENING GUIDELINES: aap.org/autism\\nCDC 'Learn the Signs, Act Early': cdc.gov/actearly \u2014 free materials in multiple languages\\nAutism Navigator: autismnavigator.com \u2014 video-based screening tool for providers\",\n     \"\u2605 M-CHAT auto-scoring tool with interpretation. Pre-visit screening reminder at 18 and 24 months. Script for requesting screening if not offered. Auto-generate referral requests if positive. Track screening results over time.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"1.3\", \"Referral to Regional Center (Self-Refer \u2014 No Doctor Needed)\",\n     \"SELF-REFER TO YOUR REGIONAL CENTER TODAY: (1) Call your local RC intake line \u2014 you do NOT need a doctor referral, teacher letter, or any documentation. A parent phone call is sufficient. (2) Say: 'I am a parent and I'd like to refer my child for a developmental evaluation. My child is [age] and I have concerns about [speech/social interaction/behavior].' (3) Ask for the name and direct number of the intake coordinator assigned to your case. (4) Confirm: 'What is my intake appointment date? I understand you have 15 working days to complete intake.' (5) Request a Spanish/other language interpreter if needed \u2014 RC is required to provide this. (6) You can ALSO pursue a school district evaluation (for age 3+) simultaneously \u2014 these are separate processes and you should do both.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Wait for your pediatrician to make the referral \u2014 self-refer TODAY. (2) Assume you need a diagnosis first \u2014 RC evaluates and diagnoses. (3) Be intimidated by the intake process \u2014 you have a RIGHT to services under the Lanterman Act. (4) Accept 'we'll put you on a waitlist' without getting a specific intake date within 15 working days. (5) Skip the RC referral because you're already pursuing school evaluation \u2014 RC provides different services (ABA, respite, parent training) that school does not.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Find your RC at dds.ca.gov/rc/listing. Escalation template if deadlines missed.\"],\n\n    # ===== PHASE 2: EVALUATION & DIAGNOSIS =====\n    [\"2\\nEvaluation &\\nDiagnosis\", \"2.1\", \"RC Intake Assessment\",\n     \"PREPARE FOR INTAKE MEETING: (1) Organize your documentation in a folder: medical records, concerns journal, videos, milestone tracking. (2) Write a 1-page 'Parent Concerns Letter' summarizing your specific concerns \u2014 this becomes part of the official record. (3) At intake, be specific and detailed: not 'he doesn't talk much' but 'at 22 months he has 3 words (mama, dada, ball), does not point to request items, does not follow simple commands like bring me your shoes, and does not look when I call his name.' (5) Request that evaluation be conducted in your home language if applicable.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Understate your concerns \u2014 this is not the time to be optimistic. Describe your child's DAILY challenges, not their best moments. (2) Fail to mention regression (loss of skills) if it occurred \u2014 this is an urgent indicator. (3) Sign consent forms without reading them. (4) Accept a screening-level evaluation when your child needs comprehensive assessment. (5) Forget to ask about the evaluation timeline \u2014 120-day clock starts at referral.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Before intake, write your Parent Concerns Letter. (2) Autism-specific tools should include: ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule \u2014 gold standard), ADI-R or DISCO (parent interview), cognitive testing (Bayley-4 for young children, WISC-V for older), adaptive behavior (Vineland-3 or ABAS-3). (3) You have the RIGHT to be present during evaluations. (4) Ask evaluators to document strengths as well as challenges. (5) If your child is bilingual, request bilingual evaluator or interpreter \u2014 evaluation in one language only may underestimate abilities. (6) Share your videos and concerns journal with evaluators.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Accept a diagnosis based on a single brief office visit \u2014 comprehensive evaluation typically requires 4-8 hours across multiple sessions. (2) Let evaluators see your child only at their best \u2014 if mornings are hard, schedule morning sessions. (3) Fail to mention all your concerns to every evaluator \u2014 each specialist needs the full picture. (4) Accept an evaluation conducted entirely in English if your child's primary language is different. (5) Skip sharing your videos \u2014 evaluators can't observe everything in a clinic setting.\",\n     \"STEP 1: When RC schedules evaluations, ask: 'Which professionals will evaluate my child? What specific tests will be administered? How many sessions?'\\nSTEP 2: Before each evaluation session, provide evaluators with: your Parent Concerns Letter, videos, milestone tracking notes, any medical records.\\nSTEP 3: During evaluations, take notes on what you observe. If anything seems inaccurate, write a response letter to be included in the file.\",\n     \"RC-CONTRACTED EVALUATORS: RC arranges these \u2014 you don't choose the providers, but you CAN request specific qualifications (e.g., bilingual, autism specialist).\\nIF YOU DISAGREE WITH RC EVALUATION: You can request an Independent Evaluation \u2014 contact OCRA at (800) 390-7032.\\nPRIVATE EVALUATION (OPTIONAL): If you want a second opinion or faster diagnosis, ask your pediatrician for referral to developmental pediatrician. (2) Review reports carefully \u2014 look for: DSM-5 autism diagnosis (299.00), severity level (Level 1/2/3), adaptive behavior scores (Vineland-3 composite), cognitive scores, speech-language findings. (5) If child is found eligible: immediately ask about service coordinator assignment, IPP scheduling, and interim services. (6) If child is found NOT eligible: do NOT accept this without understanding why and knowing your appeal rights.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Go to the eligibility meeting without having read the reports first. (2) Accept denial without requesting a written explanation (Prior Written Notice equivalent). (3) Assume denial is final \u2014 you have appeal rights including fair hearing. (4) Sign anything you don't understand \u2014 ask for time to review. (5) Agree to 'provisional eligibility' without understanding what it means for services.\",\n     \"STEP 1: 2 weeks before eligibility meeting, call your RC contact: 'I'd like copies of all evaluation reports mailed/emailed to me at least 5 business days before the eligibility meeting.'\\nSTEP 2: Review all reports. Highlight anything you disagree with. Note your questions.\\nSTEP 3: At the meeting, take notes (or bring someone to take notes). Pre-meeting report review checklist. If denied: auto-generates appeal letter with legal citations. Flags provisional eligibility limitations. POS spending equity alerts. Service Coordinator caseload tracker.\"],\n\n    # ===== PHASE 3: SERVICE PLANNING =====\n    [\"3\\nService\\nPlanning\", \"3.1\", \"IPP Development (Initial Service Plan)\",\n     \"MAXIMIZE YOUR IPP: (1) IPP (Individual Program Plan) is your child's service plan at RC \u2014 equivalent to an IEP at school but for community/home services. (2) Before the IPP meeting, make a written list of EVERYTHING your child needs: ABA therapy (hours per week), speech therapy, occupational therapy, respite care, parent training, social skills, adaptive behavior support, crisis services. (3) Research what services RC CAN provide (visit dds.ca.gov or ask other parents). (4) At the meeting, present your list and advocate for each item. (5) IPP goals should be SPECIFIC, MEASURABLE, and TIME-BOUND. (6) Know that RC POS (Purchase of Service) spending should be equitable \u2014 if you're a family of color, you may need to advocate harder due to documented spending disparities.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Accept whatever the SC offers without advocating for what your child needs. (2) Agree to vague goals ('improve communication') \u2014 demand measurable goals ('Child will use 50 functional words in 3 months'). (3) Let RC say 'school will provide that' for services RC should fund (e.g., ABA, respite, parent training). (4) Sign the IPP if you disagree \u2014 you can request changes and have 30 days to sign. (5) Assume the SC will proactively offer all available services \u2014 many families don't receive services they're entitled to because they don't know to ask.\",\n     \"STEP 1: 2 weeks before IPP, prepare a written 'Parent Service Request' listing: requested services, hours/frequency, justification for each. Example: 'Requesting 20 hours/week ABA therapy \u2014 evaluation documents significant behavioral and adaptive skill deficits requiring intensive intervention.'\\nSTEP 2: At IPP meeting, present your list. For each item, ask: 'Will this service be included in the IPP? If not, please document the reason for denial in writing.'\\nSTEP 3: Review proposed IPP goals. For each goal, ask: 'How will we measure progress? What is the specific target? By when?'\\nSTEP 4: Before signing, ask: 'Can I take this home to review? I have 30 days to sign.' Review with an advocate or other parents.\\nSTEP 5: Once IPP is signed, ask: 'How quickly will services begin? Who are the approved providers? Can I choose my ABA company?'\",\n     \"REGIONAL CENTER SERVICE COORDINATOR: Your assigned SC manages your IPP \u2014 get their direct number and email.\\nABA PROVIDERS: RC provides a vendor list. Ask for multiple options and interview providers before choosing.\\nRESPITE AGENCIES: RC provides vendor list for respite workers.\\nIF SC IS UNRESPONSIVE: Contact RC supervisor. If still unresponsive, contact OCRA: (800) 390-7032.\\nPOS SPENDING DATA: dds.ca.gov/rc/purchase-of-service-data \u2014 check how your RC spends relative to demographics.\",\n     \"IPP DOCUMENTS:\\n\u2022 Written IPP (your copy \u2014 keep in file)\\n\u2022 Service authorization letters for each service\\n\u2022 Provider vendor list\\n\u2022 POS codes for authorized services\\n\u2022 Parent rights notification\\n\\nPARENT SHOULD PREPARE:\\n\u2022 Written Service Request letter\\n\u2022 List of requested services with justifications\\n\u2022 Questions about each proposed goal\\n\u2022 Research on available services\",\n     \"IPP meeting within 60 DAYS of eligibility determination. IPP reviewed at least ANNUALLY. Parent can request IPP meeting at ANY time if needs change. Services should begin within 30 days of signed IPP (though ABA waitlists may cause delays).\",\n     \"Regional Center\\n(Service Coordinator)\", \"Therapy providers\\nParent\",\n     \"\u2605 IPP SERVICE GAPS: (1) SC doesn't inform parent of all available services \u2014 parent gets only what SC offers, not what child needs. (2) POS spending disparities well-documented \u2014 White families receive ~$14K more in services per year than Latino families at many RCs. (3) ABA hours approved at lower level than recommended. (4) Respite denied or given minimal hours. (5) Parent training not offered. (6) IPP goals too vague to measure progress. (7) SC caseload 60-80 clients \u2014 limited time for advocacy.\",\n     \"IPP PREPARATION:\\n\u2022 Undivided IPP guide: undivided.io\\n\u2022 DRC IPP rights guide: disabilityrightsca.org\\n\u2022 SERR Manual: serr.disabilityrightsca.org\\n\u2022 POS spending equity data: dds.ca.gov\\nPARENT TRAINING ON ADVOCACY:\\n\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org\\n\u2022 TASK: taskca.org\\n\u2022 Autism Society of America: autism-society.org\\n\u2022 Local ABA company parent training programs\",\n     \"\u2605 Service recommendation engine by diagnosis + age. POS spending equity benchmark. IPP goal generator with measurable targets. Pre-fills parent concerns letter. Service authorization tracker. Provider comparison tool.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"3.2\", \"Apply for Medi-Cal, SSI, IHSS, CalABLE (Full Benefits Package)\",\n     \"APPLY FOR ALL FOUR BENEFITS SIMULTANEOUSLY: (1) MEDI-CAL: RC eligibility often opens HCBS-DD waiver pathway \u2014 only child's income counts (institutional deeming). Apply at coveredca.com or county social services office. (2) SSI: Apply at ssa.gov or local SSA office. For autism, the Function Report is CRITICAL \u2014 describe WORST day, not average day. High initial denial rate \u2014 plan to appeal. (3) IHSS: Apply at county IHSS office. Requires Medi-Cal. County worker does in-home assessment. Parent CAN be paid provider. Request protective supervision if applicable. (4) CalABLE: Open account at calable.ca.gov \u2014 no minimum age, $19K/year, doesn't affect SSI under $100K.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Understate your child's challenges on SSI Function Report \u2014 describe the WORST day, not the best. (2) Skip IHSS because you don't know about it \u2014 many autism families qualify. (3) Forget CalABLE \u2014 open it immediately regardless of child's age. (4) Assume SSI denial is final \u2014 appeal rates are high and many win on appeal. (5) Let one application delay another \u2014 apply for all four simultaneously.\",\n     \"MEDI-CAL: Apply online at coveredca.com or call (800) 300-1506. For Alameda County: (510) 263-2420.\\nSSI: Apply at ssa.gov/benefits/disability or call (800) 772-1213. Bring: birth certificate, medical records, evaluation reports, school records.\\nIHSS: Apply at Alameda County Social Services: (510) 577-1900. Bring: Medi-Cal card, medical certification form (SOC 873), physician statement.\\nCalABLE: Open at calable.ca.gov (online, takes 15 minutes). Need: SSN, proof of disability.\\nSTEP-BY-STEP: Start all 4 applications within the same week. Track each separately.\",\n     \"MEDI-CAL:\\n\u2022 Covered California: coveredca.com or (800) 300-1506\\n\u2022 Alameda County: (510) 263-2420\\nSSI:\\n\u2022 SSA: ssa.gov or (800) 772-1213\\n\u2022 Local SSA office (Oakland): 150 Frank H Ogawa Plaza, Oakland 94612\\nIHSS:\\n\u2022 Alameda County IHSS: (510) 577-1900\\n\u2022 IHSS Helpline: ihssunion.org for provider resources\\nCalABLE:\\n\u2022 calable.ca.gov (online enrollment)\\nHELP WITH APPLICATIONS:\\n\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\n\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org\",\n     \"MEDI-CAL: Application, income documentation, residency proof\\nSSI: Application, medical evidence, Function Report (SSA-3375), financial disclosure\\nIHSS: Application (SOC 295), Medical Certification (SOC 873), physician statement, Medi-Cal enrollment proof\\nCalABLE: Online application, SSN, disability documentation\\n\\nKEEP COPIES OF EVERYTHING. Create a 'Benefits' folder with subfolders for each program.\",\n     \"Medi-Cal: 45 days for determination. SSI: 3-6 months (plan for appeal). IHSS: 30-60 days from application to assessment. START ALL FOUR THIS WEEK.\",\n     \"SSA (SSI)\\nDHCS (Medi-Cal)\\nCounty IHSS\\nCalABLE\", \"Regional Center\\nPediatrician\",\n     \"\u2605 SSI denied initially (common \u2014 ~60% denial rate).
