Renewal deadline tracker.\"],\n\n    # ===== PHASE 4: SCHOOL DISTRICT ENGAGEMENT =====\n    [\"4\\nSchool District\\nIEP\", \"4.1\", \"Request School District Evaluation (Written Request)\",\n     \"SEND WRITTEN EVALUATION REQUEST: (1) Write a letter to your school district's Special Education Department requesting a comprehensive evaluation. This triggers legal timelines. (2) Be SPECIFIC about areas of concern: social communication, restricted/repetitive behaviors, sensory processing, adaptive behavior, academic readiness, speech-language, fine/gross motor. (3) Hand-deliver the letter AND send by email \u2014 keep copies of both with date stamps. (4) District must respond with an assessment plan within 15 CALENDAR DAYS. (5) You do NOT need the district's permission, teacher's agreement, or to wait for RTI. (6) Share RC evaluation reports with the school \u2014 they're required to consider them.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Make a verbal request only \u2014 it MUST be in writing to trigger legal timelines. (2) Let district say 'wait until next year' or 'let's try the classroom first.' (3) Accept an assessment plan that doesn't cover ALL areas of suspected disability. (4) Sign the assessment plan without reviewing it \u2014 you can request additions. (5) Assume school IEP replaces RC services \u2014 these are SEPARATE systems.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Write your evaluation request letter. Include: child's name, DOB, school, your specific concerns, request for 'comprehensive psychoeducational evaluation in all areas of suspected disability including but not limited to: cognitive, academic, speech-language, occupational therapy, social-emotional, adaptive behavior, and autism-specific assessment.'\\nSTEP 2: Hand-deliver to school office AND email to Special Education Director. Get a date-stamped receipt.\\nSTEP 3: Mark calendar: 15 days from delivery = deadline for assessment plan. If no plan received, send follow-up: 'I sent my evaluation request on [date]. Under California law, you have 15 calendar days to provide an assessment plan. Please respond immediately.'\\nSTEP 4: Review assessment plan. If it doesn't include all areas, write back: 'I request the following areas be added to the assessment plan: [list areas].'\\nSTEP 5: Sign the plan only after all requested areas are included. 60-day assessment clock starts when you sign.\",\n     \"SCHOOL DISTRICT SPECIAL EDUCATION DEPARTMENT: Find contact at your district's website under 'Special Education' or 'Student Services.'\\nOAKLAND UNIFIED (OUSD): Special Education, 1000 Broadway Suite 680, Oakland, CA 94607. Phone: (510) 879-8110. Email: specialeducation@ousd.org\\nSELPA (Special Education Local Plan Area): Your district's SELPA handles dispute resolution.\\nFREE ADVOCACY:\\n\u2022 Disability Rights California: (800) 776-5746\\n\u2022 DRC SERR Manual: serr.disabilityrightsca.org\\n\u2022 Community Alliance for Special Education (CASE): caseadvocacy.org (Bay Area)\\n\u2022 DREDF: dredf.org\",\n     \"PARENT CREATES:\\n\u2022 Written evaluation request letter (dated, keep copy)\\n\u2022 Date-stamped delivery confirmation\\n\u2022 Email copy with read receipt\\n\\nDISTRICT PROVIDES:\\n\u2022 Assessment plan (within 15 days)\\n\u2022 Parent rights notification (procedural safeguards)\\n\u2022 Prior Written Notice if refusing evaluation\\n\\nSHARE WITH DISTRICT:\\n\u2022 RC evaluation reports\\n\u2022 Private evaluation reports\\n\u2022 Medical records\\n\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter\",\n     \"15 CALENDAR DAYS: District must provide assessment plan after receiving written request.\\n60 CALENDAR DAYS: From signed consent to completed evaluation (excluding school breaks >5 days).\\n30 CALENDAR DAYS: From completed evaluation to IEP meeting.\\nNO DEADLINE TO REQUEST: Parent can request evaluation at any time during the school year.\",\n     \"Local School District\", \"Regional Center\\n(transition if under 3)\",\n     \"\u2605 SCHOOL BARRIERS: (1) District delays response beyond 15 days. (4) Parent makes verbal request only \u2014 no paper trail. (5) District doesn't share assessment plan or parent rights. (6) District doesn't accept RC evaluations. (7) Assessment not conducted in child's primary language.\",\n     \"EVALUATION REQUEST HELP:\\n\u2022 CASE (Community Alliance for Special Education): caseadvocacy.org \u2014 free advocacy, Bay Area\\n\u2022 DREDF: dredf.org \u2014 free advocacy\\n\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746 \u2014 free legal help\\n\u2022 TASK: taskca.org \u2014 free parent training\\n\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org \u2014 Bay Area IEP training\\n\u2022 Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com \u2014 comprehensive SpEd law resource\\n\u2022 Understood: understood.org \u2014 parent-friendly IEP guides\",\n     \"\u2605 Assessment request letter generator with autism-specific areas and legal citations. 15-day and 60-day deadline trackers. Cross-reference with RC evaluation to identify gaps.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"4.2\", \"School District Evaluation & IEP Development\",\n     \"PREPARE FOR AND PARTICIPATE IN EVALUATION AND IEP: (1) School psychologist conducts evaluation (cognitive, academic, social-emotional, adaptive). SLP evaluates communication. OT evaluates sensory/motor. (2) Provide school with RC reports, private evaluations, and your documentation. (3) After evaluation is complete, REQUEST REPORTS 5 days before IEP meeting. Review carefully. (4) At IEP meeting, advocate for: autism eligibility category, speech therapy, OT, behavioral support (BIP if needed), 1:1 aide if appropriate, specialized academic instruction, ESY (extended school year), and APPROPRIATE placement (LRE). (5) Bring a support person \u2014 advocate, spouse, friend who takes notes. (6) If you disagree with the IEP offer, you do NOT have to sign \u2014 request a continuation meeting.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Go to the IEP meeting without reading reports first. (2) Go alone \u2014 bring support. (3) Accept an IEP that doesn't match your child's evaluation results. (4) Accept vague goals. (5) Accept a placement without visiting the classroom first. (6) Sign the IEP if you disagree \u2014 you can request time to review. (7) Forget to coordinate IEP services with RC IPP services.\",\n     \"STEP 1: After evaluation, call school: 'When is the IEP meeting scheduled? I'd like copies of all reports at least 5 business days before.'\\nSTEP 2: Read reports. Highlight areas of concern and disagreement.\\nSTEP 3: Write an IEP preparation letter listing: your priorities, requested services, placement preference, specific goals you want addressed.\\nSTEP 4: At IEP meeting: introduce your support person, present your priorities, review each goal, ask about placement options.\\nSTEP 5: For each proposed goal, ask: 'How will this be measured? How often? What happens if my child doesn't make progress?'\\nSTEP 6: If satisfied, you may sign. If not: 'I'd like to take this home to review. When can we schedule a follow-up meeting?'\\nSTEP 7: After IEP is signed, confirm: 'When do services begin? Who are the providers? How will I receive progress reports?'\",\n     \"SCHOOL DISTRICT IEP TEAM: Teacher, school psychologist, special ed coordinator, SLP, OT, and parent.\\nADVOCACY SUPPORT:\\n\u2022 CASE: caseadvocacy.org \u2014 free IEP meeting advocate (Bay Area)\\n\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\n\u2022 TASK: taskca.org \u2014 IEP training and coaching\\n\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org\\n\u2022 Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com\\nIF DISPUTE:\\n\u2022 CDE Complaint: cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/cmplntproc.asp\\n\u2022 Mediation/Due Process: OAH at dgs.ca.gov/OAH\\n\u2022 SELPA for your district\",\n     \"IEP DOCUMENTS (district provides):\\n\u2022 IEP document (full written plan)\\n\u2022 Assessment reports (psych, SLP, OT, etc.)\\n\u2022 Prior Written Notice of any decisions\\n\u2022 Parent rights (Procedural Safeguards)\\n\u2022 Behavioral Intervention Plan (BIP) if applicable\\n\u2022 ESY eligibility determination\\n\u2022 Placement offer letter\\n\\nPARENT SHOULD BRING:\\n\u2022 RC IPP (for coordination)\\n\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter\\n\u2022 Requested services list\\n\u2022 Prior evaluations\\n\u2022 Work samples/videos\",\n     \"30 CALENDAR DAYS from completed evaluation to IEP meeting. (2) Goals not measurable or ambitious enough. (3) Placement too restrictive (segregated) OR not enough support (dumped in gen ed without services). (4) No coordination between IEP and RC IPP. (5) Parent overwhelmed in meeting. (6) ESY denied despite regression evidence. Meeting notes template.\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"4.3\", \"IEP Dispute Resolution (If Needed)\",\n     \"IF YOU DISAGREE WITH THE IEP: (1) First try informal resolution \u2014 request another IEP meeting. Put disagreements in writing. (2) If informal fails, you have 4 formal options: (a) CDE State Complaint: file at cde.ca.gov \u2014 CDE investigates within 60 days. Free, no attorney needed. (b) Mediation: voluntary, conducted by OAH mediator. Free. Often resolves disputes. (c) Due Process Hearing: formal legal proceeding through OAH. 45 days to hearing. Attorney strongly recommended. (d) ADR (Alternative Dispute Resolution): your district may offer this. (3) You may request IEE (Independent Educational Evaluation) at district expense if you disagree with their evaluation. (4) Consider contacting DRC for free legal assistance \u2014 especially for low-income families.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Give up because you're intimidated by the process. (2) File due process without trying other options first (it's adversarial and stressful). (3) Miss the 2-year statute of limitations for filing. (4) Accept retaliation \u2014 it's illegal for the district to retaliate against you for advocating. (5) Sign an IEP you disagree with \u2014 you lose leverage.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Put your disagreement in writing: 'I disagree with [specific aspect] of the IEP proposed on [date]. I request [specific remedy].'\\nSTEP 2: Request another IEP meeting to try to resolve informally.\\nSTEP 3: If no resolution, choose your path:\\n  \u2022 CDE Complaint: File at cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/cmplntproc.asp \u2014 free online form.\\n  \u2022 Mediation: Request through OAH at dgs.ca.gov/OAH \u2014 free, often effective.\\n  \u2022 Due Process: File at OAH \u2014 strongly recommend consulting DRC first: (800) 776-5746.\\nSTEP 4: Throughout, DOCUMENT EVERYTHING \u2014 save emails, keep notes of conversations with dates.\",\n     \"CDE COMPLIANCE COMPLAINT: cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/cmplntproc.asp (online filing)\\nOAH (OFFICE OF ADMINISTRATIVE HEARINGS): dgs.ca.gov/OAH \u2014 for mediation and due process. Phone: (916) 263-0880.\\nDISABILITY RIGHTS CALIFORNIA: (800) 776-5746 \u2014 free legal help, priority for low-income families.\\nCASE: caseadvocacy.org \u2014 free advocacy support (Bay Area)\\nDREDF: dredf.org \u2014 free legal advocacy\\nSELPA: Contact your district's SELPA for local dispute resolution.\\nSPECIAL ED ATTORNEYS (if needed): CASE, DRC, or Education Law referral through your local bar association.\",\n     \"DISPUTE DOCUMENTS:\\n\u2022 Written disagreement letter (dated)\\n\u2022 Prior Written Notice request\\n\u2022 CDE complaint form (if filing)\\n\u2022 OAH mediation/due process request\\n\u2022 IEE request letter (if applicable)\\n\u2022 Complete documentation binder (all IEP records, evaluations, correspondence)\\n\\nTIMELINES:\\n\u2022 CDE complaint: 60 days to investigate\\n\u2022 Due process: hearing within 45 days of filing\\n\u2022 Statute of limitations: 2 years from known violation\\n\u2022 IEE: District must respond (pay or file due process)\",\n     \"CDE complaint: 60 days to investigate. Mediation: scheduled within weeks. Due process: hearing within 45 days of filing. 2-YEAR statute of limitations on complaints. IEE: district must respond within reasonable time.\",\n     \"School District /\\nCDE / OAH\", \"DRC\\nSpecial Ed Attorney\\nParent advocate\",\n     \"\u2605 Parent doesn't know appeal rights. District pressures parent to sign. Attorney fees $300-600/hr prohibitive. Emotional toll of adversarial process. 2-year statute of limitations passes. District retaliates (illegal but happens).\",\n     \"DISPUTE RESOLUTION TRAINING:\\n\u2022 Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com/info/disres.index.htm\\n\u2022 DRC: disabilityrightsca.org\\n\u2022 CASE advocacy training: caseadvocacy.org\\n\u2022 CDE dispute resolution info: cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/\\n\u2022 CADRE (Consortium for Dispute Resolution): directionservice.org/cadre\",\n     \"\u2605 Dispute resolution decision tree. Letter templates for each stage. DRC referral with income screening. Attorney cost-recovery guidance (if family prevails, district pays attorney fees). Documentation organizer with timeline. IEE request template.\"],\n\n    # ===== PHASE 5: INSURANCE & THERAPY =====\n    [\"5\\nInsurance &\\nTherapy\", \"5.1\", \"Insurance Authorization for ABA Therapy\",\n     \"GET ABA THERAPY AUTHORIZED: (1) California law (SB 946) mandates health insurance coverage for behavioral health treatment (ABA) for autism \u2014 this includes Medi-Cal managed care plans. (2) Get physician referral/prescription for ABA from your pediatrician: 'My child has an autism diagnosis and I'm requesting a referral for ABA therapy.' (3) Call your insurance company: 'I need to start the prior authorization process for ABA therapy for my child who has an autism diagnosis.' (4) Choose an ABA provider \u2014 ask RC for their vendor list, also check insurance provider directory. Interview 2-3 providers before selecting. (5) ABA provider submits treatment plan to insurance. Typical request: 10-40 hours/week depending on severity. (6) If authorized, confirm: hours approved, duration, renewal process. (7) If denied, appeal immediately \u2014 CA parity law is strong.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Accept denial without appealing \u2014 CA law mandates ABA coverage for autism. (2) Wait for RC to provide ABA \u2014 insurance should be billed first (RC is payer of last resort). (3) Choose the first ABA provider without interviewing others. (4) Assume 'ABA' means one thing \u2014 quality varies enormously. Ask about their approach, qualifications, supervision ratios. (5) Let ABA hours be less than what the evaluation recommends without documented justification.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Get ABA referral from pediatrician (1 phone call \u2014 'I need an ABA referral for my autistic child').\\nSTEP 2: Call insurance (number on card): 'I need to begin prior authorization for ABA therapy. What is the process? What documentation is needed?'\\nSTEP 3: Search for ABA providers: (a) Insurance provider directory, (b) RC vendor list, (c) bacb.com (BACB registry of certified analysts).\\nSTEP 4: Interview 2-3 ABA providers. Ask: 'What is your supervision model? What is the BCBA-to-client ratio? Do you use naturalistic teaching? How do you involve parents? What does a typical session look like?'\\nSTEP 5: Selected provider submits treatment plan to insurance. Insurance has 5-15 business days for prior authorization.\\nSTEP 6: If APPROVED: confirm hours, start date, authorization period. If DENIED: appeal immediately \u2014 contact DRC or Health Consumer Alliance for free help.\",\n     \"INSURANCE COMPANY: Call number on back of insurance card. Ask for 'behavioral health prior authorization.'\\nMEDI-CAL MANAGED CARE: Each plan has its own ABA authorization process. Alameda Alliance: (510) 747-4500.\\nABA PROVIDER SEARCH:\\n\u2022 BACB registry: bacb.com/find-a-certificant\\n\u2022 Insurance provider directory (online)\\n\u2022 RC vendor list (ask Service Coordinator)\\nIF DENIED:\\n\u2022 DMHC (HMO plans): (888) 466-2219 \u2014 Independent Medical Review\\n\u2022 CDI (PPO plans): (800) 927-4357\\n\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org \u2014 free help\\n\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\",\n     \"INSURANCE DOCUMENTS:\\n\u2022 Physician referral for ABA\\n\u2022 Autism diagnosis report\\n\u2022 ABA treatment plan (from provider)\\n\u2022 Prior authorization request\\n\u2022 Authorization approval letter\\n\u2022 If denied: denial letter, appeal letter, medical necessity evidence\\n\\nKEEP TRACKING:\\n\u2022 Authorization start/end dates\\n\u2022 Hours approved vs hours used\\n\u2022 Renewal deadlines\\n\u2022 Progress reports from provider\",\n     \"Prior auth: 5-15 business days. If denied, internal appeal: 30 days. DMHC Independent Medical Review: 45 days. Urgent review: 72 hours. Authorization renewal: typically every 6-12 months.\",\n     \"Private Insurance /\\nMedi-Cal\", \"Pediatrician\\nABA provider\",\n     \"\u2605 Authorization denied despite CA parity law. Insufficient hours approved. Provider not in-network. Medi-Cal managed care barriers. ABA waitlists 3-6 months. Quality varies enormously across providers. Authorization gaps between renewals.\",\n     \"INSURANCE HELP:\\n\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org \u2014 free insurance advocacy\\n\u2022 DMHC Help Center: (888) 466-2219\\n\u2022 CDI Consumer Hotline: (800) 927-4357\\n\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\nABA QUALITY GUIDE:\\n\u2022 Autism Speaks ABA guide: autismspeaks.org\\n\u2022 BACB: bacb.com\\n\u2022 Association for Behavior Analysis International: abainternational.org\",\n     \"\u2605 ABA pre-authorization template. Denial appeal letter with CA parity law citations. In-network provider finder by ZIP. Authorization renewal tracker. Provider quality comparison. DMHC vs CDI identifier (which regulator for your plan).\"],\n\n    [\"\", \"5.2\", \"Begin and Coordinate All Therapy Services\",\n     \"COORDINATE YOUR CHILD'S FULL SERVICE PACKAGE: (1) Your child may receive services from 3-4 DIFFERENT payers simultaneously: insurance-billed ABA, RC-vendored services (respite, parent training), school district IEP services (speech, OT, SDI), and potentially private pay therapies. (2) Create a MASTER SCHEDULE that accounts for all services without conflicts. (3) Ensure all providers communicate \u2014 school SLP, private ABA team, RC service coordinator should all know what others are doing. (4) You are the HUB of coordination \u2014 no one else will do this for you. (5) Track authorization dates, progress reports, and renewal deadlines for each payer.\",\n     \"DO NOT: (1) Let services overlap without coordination (e.g., school speech goals and private speech goals should align). (2) Miss authorization renewal deadlines \u2014 services stop if authorization expires. (3) Assume one provider knows what another is doing \u2014 they usually don't communicate unless you facilitate. (4) Over-schedule your child \u2014 leave time for play, family, and rest. (5) Forget to track which payer covers which service.\",\n     \"STEP 1: Create a spreadsheet or use a planner with: Service | Provider | Payer | Schedule | Auth dates | Renewal deadline | Progress report due dates.\\nSTEP 2: Share your child's full service schedule with every provider. Ask each provider: 'Are you aware of my child's other services? How will you coordinate?'\\nSTEP 3: Set calendar reminders for ALL renewal deadlines (at 60, 30, and 15 days before expiration).\\nSTEP 4: Request progress reports from every provider quarterly. Compare progress across settings.\\nSTEP 5: Hold a quarterly 'coordination call' or email update with all providers.\",\n     \"YOUR SERVICE COORDINATOR HUB:\\n\u2022 ABA Provider: BCBA supervisor (get direct number)\\n\u2022 RC Service Coordinator: direct number and email\\n\u2022 School IEP Case Manager: direct number and email\\n\u2022 Private therapists (if applicable): direct contacts\\nCOORDINATION TOOLS:\\n\u2022 Google Calendar shared with providers\\n\u2022 Binder or digital folder organized by provider\",\n     \"SERVICE TRACKING DOCUMENTS:\\n\u2022 Master service schedule (all providers/payers)\\n\u2022 Authorization letters (all payers)\\n\u2022 Progress reports (quarterly from each provider)\\n\u2022 Renewal deadline calendar\\n\u2022 Insurance EOBs (Explanations of Benefits)\\n\u2022 RC service authorization letters\\n\u2022 IEP service page\\n\\nCREATE A 'SERVICE BINDER' with sections for: Insurance, RC, School, Private, Medical\",\n     \"ABA waitlists: 2-6 months. Save up to $19K/year. Balances under $100K don't affect SSI. Tax-advantaged growth. (2) Special Needs Trust (SNT): If your child will receive inheritances or large gifts, SNT protects SSI/Medi-Cal eligibility. Consult an SNT attorney ($2K-$5K to establish). (3) AT AGE 18: (a) SSI re-evaluation (child \u2192 adult criteria \u2014 PREPARE), (b) Conservatorship vs Supported Decision-Making decision, (c) Transition to adult RC services. (4) Start planning for age 18 by age 16. (4) Default to full conservatorship without considering SDM (Supported Decision-Making). (5) Forget the Letter of Intent \u2014 it's not legally binding but invaluable.\",\n     \"STEP 1: CalABLE \u2014 go to calable.ca.gov TODAY. Click 'Open an Account.' Takes 15 minutes. PHASE 1: CONCERN & SCREENING =====\\n    [\\\"1\\\\nConcern &\\\\nScreening\\\", \\\"1.1\\\", \\\"Parent Notices Developmental Concerns\\\",\\n     # SHOULD DO\\n     \\\"START DOCUMENTING IMMEDIATELY: (1) Keep a dated journal of specific behaviors \\u2014 'Jan 15: Teddy did not respond to name called 5 times at park.' (2) Video record concerning behaviors on your phone \\u2014 these are INVALUABLE for evaluators. (3) Track milestones against CDC/AAP checklist: by 12 mo = responds to name, points, waves; by 18 mo = at least 6 words, points to show you things, copies others; by 24 mo = 2-word phrases, follows 2-step instructions, notices when others are hurt. (4) Note any REGRESSION \\u2014 loss of previously acquired words or skills is an urgent red flag requiring immediate action. (5) Talk to your pediatrician at the NEXT scheduled visit \\u2014 do not wait for a special appointment. (6) Trust your instincts \\u2014 parents are right about developmental concerns more often than not.\\\",\\n     # AVOID\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) 'Wait and see' \\u2014 this is the #1 cause of delayed diagnosis. Every month of delay is lost intervention during the critical brain plasticity window (ages 0-3). (2) Compare to siblings or other children \\u2014 autism presents differently in every child. (3) Let anyone dismiss your concerns with 'boys talk later' or 'Einstein didn't talk until 4.' (4) Rely solely on verbal milestones \\u2014 social engagement, play skills, and sensory responses are equally important. (5) Assume bilingualism is causing delays \\u2014 it does NOT cause autism-like symptoms. (6) Wait for school to notice \\u2014 you know your child best.\\\",\\n     # EXACTLY HOW\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Download CDC Milestone Tracker app (free, cdc.gov/milestones) and complete age-appropriate checklist TODAY.\\\\nSTEP 2: Start a 'concerns notebook' \\u2014 write date + specific observation + context. Example: 'Feb 3: At playgroup, Teddy lined up all cars instead of rolling them. Did not look at me when I said his name 3x.'\\\\nSTEP 3: Record 2-3 short videos (30-60 sec each) showing concerning behaviors in natural settings.\\\\nSTEP 4: At next pediatrician visit, say: 'I have specific developmental concerns I'd like to discuss. I've been tracking milestones and have notes and videos to share.'\\\\nSTEP 5: If pediatrician says 'wait and see,' say: 'I understand, but I'd like a referral to the Regional Center and a developmental screening today. Early intervention has the strongest evidence base, and I don't want to lose time.'\\\",\\n     # WHO TO CONTACT\\n     \\\"PEDIATRICIAN: Your child's primary care doctor \\u2014 bring your notes and videos to the next visit.\\\\nREGIONAL CENTER (RC): Find yours at dds.ca.gov/rc (21 RCs in California). Intake line available M-F 8am-5pm.\\\\nCDC MILESTONE TRACKER: cdc.gov/milestones (free app, iOS/Android)\\\\nFIRST SIGNS: firstsigns.org \\u2014 video library of autism red flags\\\",\\n     # DOCUMENTS\\n     \\\"No documents required at this stage. BEGIN CREATING:\\\\n\\u2022 Developmental concerns journal (dated entries)\\\\n\\u2022 Video recordings of behaviors (save to dedicated phone folder)\\\\n\\u2022 Printed CDC milestone checklist with notes\\\\n\\u2022 List of specific questions for pediatrician\\\\n\\u2022 Baby book/medical records with developmental history\\\",\\n     # TIMELINE\\n     \\\"Act NOW \\u2014 do not wait. Every month of delay during ages 0-3 is critical lost intervention time. The AAP recommends universal autism screening at 18 and 24 months, but if you have concerns at ANY age, act immediately. Call your Regional Center TODAY if you have concerns.\\\",\\n     # PRIMARY AGENCY\\n     \\\"None\\\\n(Parent-initiated)\\\", \\\"Pediatrician\\\",\\n     # WHAT CAN GO WRONG\\n     \\\"\\u2605 CRITICAL DELAYS: (1) Pediatrician says 'wait and see' \\u2014 delays intervention by 6-12 months on average. (2) Cultural stigma prevents families from seeking evaluation \\u2014 Latino/Black children diagnosed 2+ years later than White children. (3) Family members (grandparents, spouse) dismiss concerns \\u2014 'My brother didn't talk until 3 and he's fine.' (4) Girls with autism missed \\u2014 they mask/camouflage social difficulties better. (5) Child meets SOME milestones so parent assumes all is fine \\u2014 autism is about quality of social interaction, not just presence/absence of skills.\\\",\\n     # PARENT TRAINING\\n     \\\"FREE PARENT TRAINING:\\\\n\\u2022 CHADD Parent-to-Parent: chadd.org (covers neurodevelopmental concerns broadly)\\\\n\\u2022 Autism Speaks 100 Day Kit: autismspeaks.org/tool-kit (free download at diagnosis)\\\\n\\u2022 First Signs: firstsigns.org (video library of early signs)\\\\n\\u2022 CDC 'Learn the Signs, Act Early': cdc.gov/actearly\\\\n\\u2022 CA PTI Network: Your local Parent Training & Information center \\u2014 find at parentcenterhub.org\\\\n\\u2022 TASK (Team of Advocates for Special Kids): taskca.org\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org (Bay Area)\\\",\\n     # WAYPOINT\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Milestone tracker with autism-specific red flags by age. Push notifications at key screening ages (9, 12, 18, 24 mo). Video library of early signs. 'Should I be concerned?' decision tool. Auto-identify correct RC by ZIP. Culturally-sensitive prompts in 7+ languages. Pre-visit pediatrician concern list generator.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"1.2\\\", \\\"Well-Child Visit Screening (M-CHAT-R/F)\\\",\\n     \\\"REQUEST M-CHAT-R/F SCREENING: (1) At the 18-month and 24-month well-child visits, ASK your pediatrician: 'Will you be administering the M-CHAT autism screening today?' (2) If they don't offer it, request it explicitly \\u2014 it's a simple 20-question parent questionnaire that takes 5 minutes. (3) Complete the M-CHAT honestly \\u2014 do not 'help' your child's score by assuming what they CAN do vs what they ACTUALLY DO. (4) If M-CHAT is positive (score 3+), insist on the Follow-Up Interview (M-CHAT-R/F) \\u2014 this reduces false positives. (5) If screening is positive, immediately request referral to Regional Center AND a developmental pediatrician. (6) Even if M-CHAT is negative but you still have concerns, pursue evaluation anyway \\u2014 M-CHAT misses ~20% of children who are later diagnosed.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Skip the 18- and 24-month well-child visits \\u2014 these are the recommended screening windows. (2) Accept 'we don't do that screening' from your pediatrician \\u2014 M-CHAT is AAP recommended and free. (3) Assume a negative M-CHAT means no autism \\u2014 false negatives occur, especially for girls and milder presentations. (4) Let the pediatrician screen without completing the M-CHAT questionnaire (just asking 'does your child point?' in passing is NOT a valid screen). (5) Wait for a positive screen to act \\u2014 if you have concerns regardless of screening result, self-refer to RC immediately.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Before your child's 18-month visit, call the office and ask: 'Will the M-CHAT-R/F be administered at this visit? If not, I'd like to request it.'\\\\nSTEP 2: Complete the M-CHAT-R/F questionnaire honestly in the waiting room. Score is 0-20 \\u2014 scores of 3-7 = medium risk (follow-up needed), 8+ = high risk (refer immediately).\\\\nSTEP 3: If score is 3+, the pediatrician should administer the Follow-Up Interview. If they don't, request it or score it yourself at mchatscreen.com.\\\\nSTEP 4: If screening is positive, say to your pediatrician: 'Based on this screening, I'd like two things today: (a) a referral to a developmental pediatrician, and (b) the phone number for our Regional Center so I can self-refer for evaluation.'\\\\nSTEP 5: Do NOT leave the appointment without a written plan for next steps. If the doctor says 'let's rescreen in 6 months,' say: 'I'd prefer to begin the evaluation process now and cancel if concerns resolve.'\\\",\\n     \\\"PEDIATRICIAN: Your child's doctor administers the M-CHAT at well-child visits.\\\\nM-CHAT ONLINE: mchatscreen.com \\u2014 free, you can complete and score it yourself at any time.\\\\nIF PEDIATRICIAN WON'T SCREEN: Complete M-CHAT yourself at mchatscreen.com and bring results to appointment. Say: 'I completed the M-CHAT and my child scored [X]. I'd like to discuss this and get referrals.'\\\\nDEVELOPMENTAL PEDIATRICIAN REFERRAL: Ask pediatrician for referral to developmental pediatrician at nearest children's hospital (e.g., UCSF, Stanford, Children's Oakland).\\\\nREGIONAL CENTER: Self-refer directly \\u2014 no doctor needed.\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2022 M-CHAT-R/F completed questionnaire (keep a copy)\\\\n\\u2022 M-CHAT-R/F Follow-Up Interview results (if applicable)\\\\n\\u2022 Well-child visit summary/after-visit note\\\\n\\u2022 Referral to developmental pediatrician (written)\\\\n\\u2022 Your developmental concerns journal and videos\\\\n\\u2022 Any prior developmental screening results\\\",\\n     \\\"18-month well-child visit: First M-CHAT screening.\\\\n24-month well-child visit: Second M-CHAT screening.\\\\nAAP also recommends general developmental screening at 9, 18, and 30 months.\\\\nIf positive screen: referral should happen SAME DAY. Do not wait for next appointment.\\\",\\n     \\\"Pediatrician / PCP\\\", \\\"None at this stage\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 SCREENING FAILURES: (1) M-CHAT not administered \\u2014 many pediatricians skip it despite AAP recommendation. (2) Screening done casually (verbal questions only, not formal questionnaire). (3) False negatives \\u2014 M-CHAT sensitivity is ~83%, meaning ~17% of autistic children screen negative. (4) Positive screen but no follow-up \\u2014 pediatrician says 'let's wait.' (5) Latino and Black children 40% less likely to be screened and referred. (6) Screening at 18 months too early for some children \\u2014 repeat at 24 months critical.\\\",\\n     \\\"M-CHAT ONLINE: mchatscreen.com (free, validated, score instantly)\\\\nAAP SCREENING GUIDELINES: aap.org/autism\\\\nCDC 'Learn the Signs, Act Early': cdc.gov/actearly \\u2014 free materials in multiple languages\\\\nAutism Navigator: autismnavigator.com \\u2014 video-based screening tool for providers\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 M-CHAT auto-scoring tool with interpretation. Pre-visit screening reminder at 18 and 24 months. Script for requesting screening if not offered. Auto-generate referral requests if positive. Track screening results over time.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"1.3\\\", \\\"Referral to Regional Center (Self-Refer \\u2014 No Doctor Needed)\\\",\\n     \\\"SELF-REFER TO YOUR REGIONAL CENTER TODAY: (1) Call your local RC intake line \\u2014 you do NOT need a doctor referral, teacher letter, or any documentation. A parent phone call is sufficient. (2) Say: 'I am a parent and I'd like to refer my child for a developmental evaluation. My child is [age] and I have concerns about [speech/social interaction/behavior].' (3) Ask for the name and direct number of the intake coordinator assigned to your case. (4) Confirm: 'What is my intake appointment date? I understand you have 15 working days to complete intake.' (5) Request a Spanish/other language interpreter if needed \\u2014 RC is required to provide this. (6) You can ALSO pursue a school district evaluation (for age 3+) simultaneously \\u2014 these are separate processes and you should do both.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Wait for your pediatrician to make the referral \\u2014 self-refer TODAY. (2) Assume you need a diagnosis first \\u2014 RC evaluates and diagnoses. (3) Be intimidated by the intake process \\u2014 you have a RIGHT to services under the Lanterman Act. (4) Accept 'we'll put you on a waitlist' without getting a specific intake date within 15 working days. (5) Skip the RC referral because you're already pursuing school evaluation \\u2014 RC provides different services (ABA, respite, parent training) that school does not.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Find your RC at dds.ca.gov/rc/listing. In Oakland/East Bay: EBRC (510) 618-6100.\\\\nSTEP 2: Call the intake line during business hours (M-F 8am-5pm). If you get voicemail, leave: your name, child's name, DOB, phone number, brief concern, and request for intake appointment.\\\\nSTEP 3: If no callback within 3 business days, call again. Document every call attempt (date, time, who you spoke with).\\\\nSTEP 4: At intake, bring: child's birth certificate, proof of CA residency (utility bill), medical records if available (but NOT required), your concerns journal and videos.\\\\nSTEP 5: After intake, ask: 'What is the next step? When will the evaluation be scheduled? Who is my service coordinator?' Get name and direct phone number.\\\\nSTEP 6: Mark your calendar: 120 days from referral = deadline for RC to complete evaluation and make eligibility determination.\\\",\\n     \\\"EAST BAY REGIONAL CENTER (EBRC): 500 Davis St, San Leandro, CA 94577. Phone: (510) 618-6100. Website: eastbayrc.org\\\\nALL 21 CA REGIONAL CENTERS: dds.ca.gov/rc/listing\\\\nRC INTAKE HELP: If your RC is unresponsive, contact: Office of Clients' Rights Advocacy (OCRA) at (800) 390-7032, or Disability Rights California at (800) 776-5746.\\\\nLANTERMAN ACT INFORMATION: Disability Rights California publishes free guides at disabilityrightsca.org\\\\nUNDIVIDED NAVIGATOR: undivided.io \\u2014 free family navigator service that helps with RC referrals\\\",\\n     \\\"TO BRING TO INTAKE:\\\\n\\u2022 Child's birth certificate (copy)\\\\n\\u2022 Proof of CA residency (utility bill, lease)\\\\n\\u2022 Medi-Cal card or insurance card\\\\n\\u2022 Any existing medical/developmental records\\\\n\\u2022 Your concerns journal and videos\\\\n\\u2022 Immunization records (optional)\\\\n\\\\nDOCUMENTS RC WILL PROVIDE:\\\\n\\u2022 Referral acknowledgment\\\\n\\u2022 Intake assessment form\\\\n\\u2022 Rights booklet (Lanterman Act rights)\\\\n\\u2022 Consent for evaluation forms\\\",\\n     \\\"Lanterman Act requires: intake within 15 WORKING DAYS of referral. Complete evaluation and eligibility determination within 120 CALENDAR DAYS. If RC misses these deadlines, document and escalate to DDS or OCRA.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\", \\\"Pediatrician (optional referral)\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 RC ACCESS BARRIERS: (1) Phone lines busy/understaffed \\u2014 may take multiple calls. (2) Language barriers if intake staff don't speak family's language. (3) Rural families may be far from RC office. (4) RC may try to defer to school district \\u2014 insist on RC evaluation for Lanterman eligibility. (5) Families with undocumented status may fear contact with government agency \\u2014 RC does NOT share immigration information. (6) Intake coordinator may minimize concerns or discourage evaluation.\\\",\\n     \\\"LANTERMAN ACT RIGHTS GUIDE: disabilityrightsca.org/publications \\u2014 free, in multiple languages\\\\nOCRA (Office of Clients' Rights Advocacy): (800) 390-7032 \\u2014 free advocacy if RC is unresponsive\\\\nUNDIVIDED: undivided.io \\u2014 free navigator service to help with RC process\\\\nFRCNCA: frcnca.org \\u2014 Family Resource Center in your area for peer support\\\\nSERR MANUAL: serr.disabilityrightsca.org \\u2014 complete special education and RC rights reference\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Auto-identify correct RC by ZIP code with direct phone number. Pre-fill intake information. 15-day intake deadline tracker. 120-day evaluation deadline tracker. Script generator for intake call. Document checklist. Escalation template if deadlines missed.\\\"],\\n\\n    # ===== PHASE 2: EVALUATION & DIAGNOSIS =====\\n    [\\\"2\\\\nEvaluation &\\\\nDiagnosis\\\", \\\"2.1\\\", \\\"RC Intake Assessment\\\",\\n     \\\"PREPARE FOR INTAKE MEETING: (1) Organize your documentation in a folder: medical records, concerns journal, videos, milestone tracking. (2) Write a 1-page 'Parent Concerns Letter' summarizing your specific concerns \\u2014 this becomes part of the official record. (3) At intake, be specific and detailed: not 'he doesn't talk much' but 'at 22 months he has 3 words (mama, dada, ball), does not point to request items, does not follow simple commands like bring me your shoes, and does not look when I call his name.' (4) Ask the intake coordinator: 'Will my child be evaluated by a psychologist, speech-language pathologist, and occupational therapist? I want a comprehensive multidisciplinary evaluation.' (5) Request that evaluation be conducted in your home language if applicable.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Understate your concerns \\u2014 this is not the time to be optimistic. Describe your child's DAILY challenges, not their best moments. (2) Fail to mention regression (loss of skills) if it occurred \\u2014 this is an urgent indicator. (3) Sign consent forms without reading them. (4) Accept a screening-level evaluation when your child needs comprehensive assessment. (5) Forget to ask about the evaluation timeline \\u2014 120-day clock starts at referral.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Before intake, write your Parent Concerns Letter. Include: child's age, specific concerns by domain (communication, social, behavior, sensory, motor), when you first noticed, what others have observed (daycare, family), any regression.\\\\nSTEP 2: Call RC 1 week before intake to confirm: date, time, location, what to bring, whether interpreter will be present.\\\\nSTEP 3: At intake meeting, give the coordinator your written concerns and ask them to include it in the file. Ask: 'Can I get a copy of the intake report when it's completed?'\\\\nSTEP 4: Before leaving, confirm: 'When will evaluations be scheduled? How many evaluation sessions will there be? Will I receive a copy of all evaluation reports?'\\\\nSTEP 5: Write down the name and direct contact of your intake coordinator. Follow up in writing (email) confirming what was discussed.\\\",\\n     \\\"REGIONAL CENTER INTAKE COORDINATOR: You'll be assigned one at intake \\u2014 get their name, direct phone, and email.\\\\nIF RC IS UNRESPONSIVE: Office of Clients' Rights Advocacy (OCRA): (800) 390-7032\\\\nDISABILITY RIGHTS CALIFORNIA: (800) 776-5746 \\u2014 free legal advocacy\\\\nFAMILY RESOURCE NAVIGATOR: undivided.io \\u2014 can help prepare for intake\\\",\\n     \\\"BRING TO INTAKE:\\\\n\\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter (written, dated, keep copy)\\\\n\\u2022 Medical records and birth records\\\\n\\u2022 Developmental history notes and videos\\\\n\\u2022 Prior evaluations (if any)\\\\n\\u2022 Proof of CA residency\\\\n\\u2022 Birth certificate\\\\n\\u2022 Medi-Cal/insurance card\\\\n\\\\nRC PROVIDES AT INTAKE:\\\\n\\u2022 Intake assessment report\\\\n\\u2022 Consent for evaluation\\\\n\\u2022 Lanterman Act rights booklet\\\\n\\u2022 Service Coordinator assignment (may come later)\\\",\\n     \\\"15 WORKING DAYS from referral to intake appointment (Lanterman Act mandate). If RC schedules intake beyond 15 days, call and say: 'Under the Lanterman Act, intake must occur within 15 working days of referral. My referral date was [date]. Please schedule within this timeline.'\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\", \\\"Pediatrician (records)\\\",\\n     \\\"RC delays intake beyond 15 days. Intake is superficial \\u2014 doesn't capture full picture of concerns. Records requests slow process. Parent doesn't know what to bring. Coordinator minimizes concerns. Bilingual family not offered interpreter.\\\",\\n     \\\"UNDIVIDED: undivided.io \\u2014 free family navigator, intake preparation help\\\\nFRCNCA: frcnca.org \\u2014 peer support, may have parent mentor who's been through RC process\\\\nDRC PUBLICATIONS: disabilityrightsca.org/publications \\u2014 RC eligibility guides in English/Spanish\\\\nMATRIX PARENT NETWORK: matrixparents.org \\u2014 Bay Area parent training on navigating RC\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Document checklist by RC. 15-day deadline tracker with auto-escalation template. Parent Concerns Letter generator with autism-specific prompts. Pre-organizes records. Intake confirmation email template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"2.2\\\", \\\"Multidisciplinary Evaluation (RC-Arranged)\\\",\\n     \\\"ENSURE COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION: (1) RC should arrange evaluation by a TEAM: psychologist (cognitive/autism diagnostic), speech-language pathologist (communication), occupational therapist (sensory/motor/adaptive). (2) Autism-specific tools should include: ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule \\u2014 gold standard), ADI-R or DISCO (parent interview), cognitive testing (Bayley-4 for young children, WISC-V for older), adaptive behavior (Vineland-3 or ABAS-3). (3) You have the RIGHT to be present during evaluations. (4) Ask evaluators to document strengths as well as challenges. (5) If your child is bilingual, request bilingual evaluator or interpreter \\u2014 evaluation in one language only may underestimate abilities. (6) Share your videos and concerns journal with evaluators.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Accept a diagnosis based on a single brief office visit \\u2014 comprehensive evaluation typically requires 4-8 hours across multiple sessions. (2) Let evaluators see your child only at their best \\u2014 if mornings are hard, schedule morning sessions. (3) Fail to mention all your concerns to every evaluator \\u2014 each specialist needs the full picture. (4) Accept an evaluation conducted entirely in English if your child's primary language is different. (5) Skip sharing your videos \\u2014 evaluators can't observe everything in a clinic setting.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: When RC schedules evaluations, ask: 'Which professionals will evaluate my child? What specific tests will be administered? How many sessions?'\\\\nSTEP 2: Before each evaluation session, provide evaluators with: your Parent Concerns Letter, videos, milestone tracking notes, any medical records.\\\\nSTEP 3: During evaluations, take notes on what you observe. Note anything that seems unusual about the testing environment or your child's behavior that day.\\\\nSTEP 4: After evaluations are complete, request copies of ALL reports. You have a legal right to these. Say: 'I'd like copies of all evaluation reports as soon as they're available, before the eligibility meeting.'\\\\nSTEP 5: Review reports carefully. If anything seems inaccurate, write a response letter to be included in the file.\\\",\\n     \\\"RC-CONTRACTED EVALUATORS: RC arranges these \\u2014 you don't choose the providers, but you CAN request specific qualifications (e.g., bilingual, autism specialist).\\\\nIF YOU DISAGREE WITH RC EVALUATION: You can request an Independent Evaluation \\u2014 contact OCRA at (800) 390-7032.\\\\nPRIVATE EVALUATION (OPTIONAL): If you want a second opinion or faster diagnosis, ask your pediatrician for referral to developmental pediatrician. UC Davis MIND Institute: (916) 703-0280. UCSF Autism Clinic: (415) 353-2101. Stanford Autism Center.\\\",\\n     \\\"EVALUATION REPORTS (request copies of ALL):\\\\n\\u2022 Psychological/Cognitive evaluation report\\\\n\\u2022 Speech-Language evaluation report\\\\n\\u2022 Occupational Therapy evaluation report\\\\n\\u2022 Autism diagnostic report (ADOS-2 results)\\\\n\\u2022 Adaptive behavior assessment (Vineland-3)\\\\n\\u2022 Developmental history summary\\\\n\\\\nPARENT SHOULD PROVIDE:\\\\n\\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter\\\\n\\u2022 Video recordings\\\\n\\u2022 Medical records\\\\n\\u2022 Prior evaluations\\\\n\\u2022 Daycare/preschool reports\\\",\\n     \\\"120 CALENDAR DAYS from initial referral to RC eligibility determination. Evaluations should be completed well before this deadline to allow time for the eligibility meeting. If evaluations are delayed, call your Service Coordinator and say: 'I'm tracking the 120-day timeline. When will evaluations be completed?'\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\", \\\"RC-contracted evaluators\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 EVALUATION RISKS: (1) Evaluator not autism-qualified \\u2014 may miss diagnosis or apply incorrect criteria. (2) ADOS-2 may miss girls/high-masking children \\u2014 they score better on social items due to learned social strategies. (3) Bilingual child evaluated only in English \\u2014 scores artificially depressed. (4) Evaluation conducted in unfamiliar clinical setting \\u2014 child may perform differently than at home. (5) Evaluator relies on standardized tests only \\u2014 misses real-world functional challenges. (6) Parent interview not thorough enough \\u2014 evaluator doesn't understand home context.\\\",\\n     \\\"UNDERSTANDING YOUR CHILD'S EVALUATION:\\\\n\\u2022 Autism Speaks 100 Day Kit: autismspeaks.org/tool-kit\\\\n\\u2022 Undivided evaluation guide: undivided.io\\\\n\\u2022 ASHA parent resources on evaluation: asha.org/public\\\\n\\u2022 DRC guide to RC evaluations: disabilityrightsca.org\\\\nPARENT TRAINING ON ADVOCACY:\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org\\\\n\\u2022 TASK: taskca.org \\u2014 free workshops on evaluation and IEP\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 120-day evaluation clock tracker. Evaluation completeness checker (were all domains assessed?). Evaluator credentials verification. ADOS-2 requirement flag. Bilingual evaluation rights reminder. Report comparison tool (RC vs private eval). Parent response letter template if report is inaccurate.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"2.3\\\", \\\"Private Diagnostic Evaluation (Optional but Valuable)\\\",\\n     \\\"PURSUE PRIVATE EVALUATION IF: (1) RC evaluation is delayed or incomplete. (2) You want a more detailed assessment (neuropsychological). (3) You want a second opinion. (4) You need a faster diagnosis to begin insurance-authorized ABA. HOW: Ask pediatrician for referral to developmental pediatrician or neuropsychologist. Insurance may cover \\u2014 check your plan's behavioral health or neurodevelopmental evaluation benefits. Medi-Cal covers developmental evaluations. Wait times: 3-12 months for developmental pediatrician, 2-8 weeks for private neuropsychologist.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Assume private eval replaces RC eval \\u2014 you need BOTH for different purposes (private = clinical diagnosis for insurance; RC = Lanterman eligibility for RC services). (2) Pay $3K-$6K out of pocket without checking insurance first. (3) Assume a long waitlist means no options \\u2014 call multiple providers and ask to be put on cancellation lists. (4) Use an evaluator who is not autism-qualified (look for ADOS-2 certified).\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Call your insurance company: 'Does my plan cover a neurodevelopmental or autism evaluation? What is my copay/coinsurance? Do I need a referral? Which providers are in-network?'\\\\nSTEP 2: Ask pediatrician for referral letter to developmental pediatrician or neuropsychologist.\\\\nSTEP 3: Call 3-5 providers to compare wait times. Ask to be on cancellation lists.\\\\nSTEP 4: When you get the appointment, bring ALL RC evaluation reports plus your documentation.\\\\nSTEP 5: After private eval, share results with RC to support eligibility determination AND with school district for IEP.\\\",\\n     \\\"DEVELOPMENTAL PEDIATRICIANS (Bay Area):\\\\n\\u2022 UCSF Autism Clinic: (415) 353-2101\\\\n\\u2022 Stanford Autism Center: (650) 736-1235\\\\n\\u2022 Children's Hospital Oakland: (510) 428-3885\\\\n\\u2022 UC Davis MIND Institute: (916) 703-0280\\\\nNEUROPSYCHOLOGISTS: Ask pediatrician for referral. Insurance pre-auth: 5-15 business days. CANCEL LIST: Always ask to be on cancellation list \\u2014 significantly reduces wait.\\\",\\n     \\\"Private Provider / Insurance\\\", \\\"Medi-Cal\\\\nPediatrician (referral)\\\",\\n     \\\"Insurance denies authorization. Medi-Cal covers but reimbursement rates low (fewer providers accept).\\\",\\n     \\\"INSURANCE NAVIGATION:\\\\n\\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org \\u2014 free insurance help\\\\n\\u2022 Medi-Cal Ombudsman: (888) 452-8609\\\\n\\u2022 DMHC Help Center: (888) 466-2219 (HMO plans)\\\\n\\u2022 CDI Consumer Hotline: (800) 927-4357 (PPO plans)\\\\nEVALUATION PREPARATION:\\\\n\\u2022 Autism Speaks First Concern to Action: autismspeaks.org\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Insurance coverage checker for autism evaluation.
