Results integration with RC evaluation.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"2.4\\\", \\\"Eligibility Determination (RC Decision)\\\",\\n     \\\"PREPARE FOR ELIGIBILITY MEETING: (1) Request copies of ALL evaluation reports at least 5 days before the meeting. (2) Review reports carefully \\u2014 look for: DSM-5 autism diagnosis (299.00), severity level (Level 1/2/3), adaptive behavior scores (Vineland-3 composite), cognitive scores, speech-language findings. (3) Write a summary of any disagreements or corrections to the reports. (4) You may bring a support person (advocate, family member, friend) to the meeting. (5) If child is found eligible: immediately ask about service coordinator assignment, IPP scheduling, and interim services. (6) If child is found NOT eligible: do NOT accept this without understanding why and knowing your appeal rights.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Go to the eligibility meeting without having read the reports first. (2) Accept denial without requesting a written explanation (Prior Written Notice equivalent). (3) Assume denial is final \\u2014 you have appeal rights including fair hearing. (4) Sign anything you don't understand \\u2014 ask for time to review. (5) Agree to 'provisional eligibility' without understanding what it means for services.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: 2 weeks before eligibility meeting, call your RC contact: 'I'd like copies of all evaluation reports mailed/emailed to me at least 5 business days before the eligibility meeting.'\\\\nSTEP 2: Review all reports. Highlight anything you disagree with. Note your questions.\\\\nSTEP 3: At the meeting, take notes (or bring someone to take notes). Ask: 'Based on these evaluations, is my child eligible for Regional Center services under the autism category?'\\\\nSTEP 4: IF ELIGIBLE: Ask: 'Who is my Service Coordinator? When will we schedule the IPP meeting? Can we begin receiving services immediately?' (RC must develop IPP within 60 days.)\\\\nSTEP 5: IF DENIED: Say: 'I disagree with this determination. Please provide me with a written notice of denial with the specific reasons, and information about my fair hearing rights.' You have 30 days to request a fair hearing.\\\",\\n     \\\"REGIONAL CENTER: Your assigned intake coordinator or service coordinator.\\\\nIF DENIED \\u2014 APPEAL HELP:\\\\n\\u2022 OCRA (Office of Clients' Rights Advocacy): (800) 390-7032 \\u2014 FREE legal help with RC appeals\\\\n\\u2022 Disability Rights California: (800) 776-5746\\\\n\\u2022 Fair Hearing request: File with DDS Office of Administrative Hearings\\\\nFOR ADVOCACY SUPPORT:\\\\n\\u2022 Undivided: undivided.io (free navigator)\\\\n\\u2022 Your local Family Resource Center: frcnca.org\\\",\\n     \\\"RC PROVIDES AT ELIGIBILITY MEETING:\\\\n\\u2022 Eligibility determination letter\\\\n\\u2022 All evaluation reports (you should already have these)\\\\n\\u2022 Rights notification\\\\n\\u2022 If eligible: Service Coordinator assignment\\\\n\\u2022 If denied: Written denial with reasons and fair hearing rights\\\\n\\\\nPARENT SHOULD HAVE:\\\\n\\u2022 Copies of all evaluation reports (reviewed)\\\\n\\u2022 Your questions/disagreements written down\\\\n\\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter (updated)\\\\n\\u2022 Support person if desired\\\",\\n     \\\"120 CALENDAR DAYS from initial referral to eligibility determination (Lanterman Act mandate). If RC misses this deadline: call OCRA at (800) 390-7032. RC must provide written determination. If eligible, IPP meeting within 60 days of eligibility.\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\", \\\"DDS (oversight)\\\\nOCRA (if appeal needed)\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 ELIGIBILITY RISKS: (1) Denied eligibility despite clinical diagnosis \\u2014 RC uses narrower criteria than DSM-5 in some cases. (2) 'Provisional eligibility' for ages 3-4 \\u2014 may be used to delay full services. (3) Service Coordinator assigned with 60-80 caseload \\u2014 limited attention to your child. (4) POS spending disparities: White families receive ~2x more in services than Black/Latino families. (5) Family doesn't understand the difference between RC eligibility and school IEP eligibility \\u2014 BOTH are needed.\\\",\\n     \\\"RC RIGHTS AND APPEALS:\\\\n\\u2022 OCRA: (800) 390-7032 \\u2014 free attorney assistance\\\\n\\u2022 DRC Fair Hearing Guide: disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 SERR Manual Chapter on RC: serr.disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 Lanterman Act full text: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov\\\\nPEER SUPPORT:\\\\n\\u2022 Parents of autistic children who've been through RC: connect through FRCNCA, Undivided, or local autism parent groups\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 120-day eligibility deadline tracker. Pre-meeting report review checklist. If denied: auto-generates appeal letter with legal citations. Flags provisional eligibility limitations. POS spending equity alerts. Service Coordinator caseload tracker.\\\"],\\n\\n    # ===== PHASE 3: SERVICE PLANNING =====\\n    [\\\"3\\\\nService\\\\nPlanning\\\", \\\"3.1\\\", \\\"IPP Development (Initial Service Plan)\\\",\\n     \\\"MAXIMIZE YOUR IPP: (1) IPP (Individual Program Plan) is your child's service plan at RC \\u2014 equivalent to an IEP at school but for community/home services. (2) Before the IPP meeting, make a written list of EVERYTHING your child needs: ABA therapy (hours per week), speech therapy, occupational therapy, respite care, parent training, social skills, adaptive behavior support, crisis services. (3) Research what services RC CAN provide (visit dds.ca.gov or ask other parents). (4) At the meeting, present your list and advocate for each item. (5) IPP goals should be SPECIFIC, MEASURABLE, and TIME-BOUND. (6) Know that RC POS (Purchase of Service) spending should be equitable \\u2014 if you're a family of color, you may need to advocate harder due to documented spending disparities.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Accept whatever the SC offers without advocating for what your child needs. (2) Agree to vague goals ('improve communication') \\u2014 demand measurable goals ('Child will use 50 functional words in 3 months'). (3) Let RC say 'school will provide that' for services RC should fund (e.g., ABA, respite, parent training). (4) Sign the IPP if you disagree \\u2014 you can request changes and have 30 days to sign. (5) Assume the SC will proactively offer all available services \\u2014 many families don't receive services they're entitled to because they don't know to ask.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: 2 weeks before IPP, prepare a written 'Parent Service Request' listing: requested services, hours/frequency, justification for each. Example: 'Requesting 20 hours/week ABA therapy \\u2014 evaluation documents significant behavioral and adaptive skill deficits requiring intensive intervention.'\\\\nSTEP 2: At IPP meeting, present your list. For each item, ask: 'Will this service be included in the IPP? If not, please document the reason for denial in writing.'\\\\nSTEP 3: Review proposed IPP goals. For each goal, ask: 'How will we measure progress? What is the specific target? By when?'\\\\nSTEP 4: Before signing, ask: 'Can I take this home to review? I have 30 days to sign.' Review with an advocate or other parents.\\\\nSTEP 5: Once IPP is signed, ask: 'How quickly will services begin? Who are the approved providers? Can I choose my ABA company?'\\\",\\n     \\\"REGIONAL CENTER SERVICE COORDINATOR: Your assigned SC manages your IPP \\u2014 get their direct number and email.\\\\nABA PROVIDERS: RC provides a vendor list. Ask for multiple options and interview providers before choosing.\\\\nRESPITE AGENCIES: RC provides vendor list for respite workers.\\\\nIF SC IS UNRESPONSIVE: Contact RC supervisor. If still unresponsive, contact OCRA: (800) 390-7032.\\\\nPOS SPENDING DATA: dds.ca.gov/rc/purchase-of-service-data \\u2014 check how your RC spends relative to demographics.\\\",\\n     \\\"IPP DOCUMENTS:\\\\n\\u2022 Written IPP (your copy \\u2014 keep in file)\\\\n\\u2022 Service authorization letters for each service\\\\n\\u2022 Provider vendor list\\\\n\\u2022 POS codes for authorized services\\\\n\\u2022 Parent rights notification\\\\n\\\\nPARENT SHOULD PREPARE:\\\\n\\u2022 Written Service Request letter\\\\n\\u2022 List of requested services with justifications\\\\n\\u2022 Questions about each proposed goal\\\\n\\u2022 Research on available services\\\",\\n     \\\"IPP meeting within 60 DAYS of eligibility determination. IPP reviewed at least ANNUALLY. Parent can request IPP meeting at ANY time if needs change. Services should begin within 30 days of signed IPP (though ABA waitlists may cause delays).\\\",\\n     \\\"Regional Center\\\\n(Service Coordinator)\\\", \\\"Therapy providers\\\\nParent\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IPP SERVICE GAPS: (1) SC doesn't inform parent of all available services \\u2014 parent gets only what SC offers, not what child needs. (2) POS spending disparities well-documented \\u2014 White families receive ~$14K more in services per year than Latino families at many RCs. (3) ABA hours approved at lower level than recommended. (4) Respite denied or given minimal hours. (5) Parent training not offered. (6) IPP goals too vague to measure progress. (7) SC caseload 60-80 clients \\u2014 limited time for advocacy.\\\",\\n     \\\"IPP PREPARATION:\\\\n\\u2022 Undivided IPP guide: undivided.io\\\\n\\u2022 DRC IPP rights guide: disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 SERR Manual: serr.disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 POS spending equity data: dds.ca.gov\\\\nPARENT TRAINING ON ADVOCACY:\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org\\\\n\\u2022 TASK: taskca.org\\\\n\\u2022 Autism Society of America: autism-society.org\\\\n\\u2022 Local ABA company parent training programs\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Service recommendation engine by diagnosis + age. POS spending equity benchmark. IPP goal generator with measurable targets. Pre-fills parent concerns letter. Service authorization tracker. Provider comparison tool.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"3.2\\\", \\\"Apply for Medi-Cal, SSI, IHSS, CalABLE (Full Benefits Package)\\\",\\n     \\\"APPLY FOR ALL FOUR BENEFITS SIMULTANEOUSLY: (1) MEDI-CAL: RC eligibility often opens HCBS-DD waiver pathway \\u2014 only child's income counts (institutional deeming). Apply at coveredca.com or county social services office. (2) SSI: Apply at ssa.gov or local SSA office. For autism, the Function Report is CRITICAL \\u2014 describe WORST day, not average day. High initial denial rate \\u2014 plan to appeal. (3) IHSS: Apply at county IHSS office. Requires Medi-Cal. County worker does in-home assessment. Parent CAN be paid provider. Request protective supervision if applicable. (4) CalABLE: Open account at calable.ca.gov \\u2014 no minimum age, $19K/year, doesn't affect SSI under $100K.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Understate your child's challenges on SSI Function Report \\u2014 describe the WORST day, not the best. (2) Skip IHSS because you don't know about it \\u2014 many autism families qualify. (3) Forget CalABLE \\u2014 open it immediately regardless of child's age. (4) Assume SSI denial is final \\u2014 appeal rates are high and many win on appeal. (5) Let one application delay another \\u2014 apply for all four simultaneously.\\\",\\n     \\\"MEDI-CAL: Apply online at coveredca.com or call (800) 300-1506. For Alameda County: (510) 263-2420.\\\\nSSI: Apply at ssa.gov/benefits/disability or call (800) 772-1213. Bring: birth certificate, medical records, evaluation reports, school records.\\\\nIHSS: Apply at Alameda County Social Services: (510) 577-1900. Bring: Medi-Cal card, medical certification form (SOC 873), physician statement.\\\\nCalABLE: Open at calable.ca.gov (online, takes 15 minutes). Need: SSN, proof of disability.\\\\nSTEP-BY-STEP: Start all 4 applications within the same week. Track each separately.\\\",\\n     \\\"MEDI-CAL:\\\\n\\u2022 Covered California: coveredca.com or (800) 300-1506\\\\n\\u2022 Alameda County: (510) 263-2420\\\\nSSI:\\\\n\\u2022 SSA: ssa.gov or (800) 772-1213\\\\n\\u2022 Local SSA office (Oakland): 150 Frank H Ogawa Plaza, Oakland 94612\\\\nIHSS:\\\\n\\u2022 Alameda County IHSS: (510) 577-1900\\\\n\\u2022 IHSS Helpline: ihssunion.org for provider resources\\\\nCalABLE:\\\\n\\u2022 calable.ca.gov (online enrollment)\\\\nHELP WITH APPLICATIONS:\\\\n\\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\\\n\\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org\\\",\\n     \\\"MEDI-CAL: Application, income documentation, residency proof\\\\nSSI: Application, medical evidence, Function Report (SSA-3375), financial disclosure\\\\nIHSS: Application (SOC 295), Medical Certification (SOC 873), physician statement, Medi-Cal enrollment proof\\\\nCalABLE: Online application, SSN, disability documentation\\\\n\\\\nKEEP COPIES OF EVERYTHING. Create a 'Benefits' folder with subfolders for each program.\\\",\\n     \\\"Medi-Cal: 45 days for determination. SSI: 3-6 months (plan for appeal). IHSS: 30-60 days from application to assessment. CalABLE: Immediate enrollment online. START ALL FOUR THIS WEEK.\\\",\\n     \\\"SSA (SSI)\\\\nDHCS (Medi-Cal)\\\\nCounty IHSS\\\\nCalABLE\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\nPediatrician\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 SSI denied initially (common \\u2014 ~60% denial rate). Function Report is too positive. IHSS protective supervision denied. Medi-Cal redetermination causes gaps. CalABLE not opened early. Benefits interactions complex (SSI + IHSS + Medi-Cal). Parent doesn't know they can be paid IHSS provider.\\\",\\n     \\\"BENEFITS HELP:\\\\n\\u2022 DRC SSI Guide: disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 SSI denied? DRC Appeal Help: (800) 776-5746\\\\n\\u2022 IHSS Advocacy: Personal Assistance Services Council\\\\n\\u2022 CalABLE Info: calable.ca.gov/faqs\\\\n\\u2022 Benefits calculator: db101.org (Disability Benefits 101)\\\\nPARENT TRAINING:\\\\n\\u2022 Undivided benefits webinars: undivided.io\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix Parent Network benefits training\\\\n\\u2022 TASK benefits workshops: taskca.org\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Benefits application coordinator \\u2014 tracks all 4 applications simultaneously. SSI Function Report drafting assistant. IHSS hour estimator. CalABLE setup wizard. Cross-benefit impact calculator. Appeal letter generator for denials. Renewal deadline tracker.\\\"],\\n\\n    # ===== PHASE 4: SCHOOL DISTRICT ENGAGEMENT =====\\n    [\\\"4\\\\nSchool District\\\\nIEP\\\", \\\"4.1\\\", \\\"Request School District Evaluation (Written Request)\\\",\\n     \\\"SEND WRITTEN EVALUATION REQUEST: (1) Write a letter to your school district's Special Education Department requesting a comprehensive evaluation. This triggers legal timelines. (2) Be SPECIFIC about areas of concern: social communication, restricted/repetitive behaviors, sensory processing, adaptive behavior, academic readiness, speech-language, fine/gross motor. (3) Hand-deliver the letter AND send by email \\u2014 keep copies of both with date stamps. (4) District must respond with an assessment plan within 15 CALENDAR DAYS. (5) You do NOT need the district's permission, teacher's agreement, or to wait for RTI. (6) Share RC evaluation reports with the school \\u2014 they're required to consider them.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Make a verbal request only \\u2014 it MUST be in writing to trigger legal timelines. (2) Let district say 'wait until next year' or 'let's try the classroom first.' (3) Accept an assessment plan that doesn't cover ALL areas of suspected disability. (4) Sign the assessment plan without reviewing it \\u2014 you can request additions. (5) Assume school IEP replaces RC services \\u2014 these are SEPARATE systems.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Write your evaluation request letter. Include: child's name, DOB, school, your specific concerns, request for 'comprehensive psychoeducational evaluation in all areas of suspected disability including but not limited to: cognitive, academic, speech-language, occupational therapy, social-emotional, adaptive behavior, and autism-specific assessment.'\\\\nSTEP 2: Hand-deliver to school office AND email to Special Education Director. Get a date-stamped receipt.\\\\nSTEP 3: Mark calendar: 15 days from delivery = deadline for assessment plan. If no plan received, send follow-up: 'I sent my evaluation request on [date]. Under California law, you have 15 calendar days to provide an assessment plan. Please respond immediately.'\\\\nSTEP 4: Review assessment plan. If it doesn't include all areas, write back: 'I request the following areas be added to the assessment plan: [list areas].'\\\\nSTEP 5: Sign the plan only after all requested areas are included. 60-day assessment clock starts when you sign.\\\",\\n     \\\"SCHOOL DISTRICT SPECIAL EDUCATION DEPARTMENT: Find contact at your district's website under 'Special Education' or 'Student Services.'\\\\nOAKLAND UNIFIED (OUSD): Special Education, 1000 Broadway Suite 680, Oakland, CA 94607. Phone: (510) 879-8110. Email: specialeducation@ousd.org\\\\nSELPA (Special Education Local Plan Area): Your district's SELPA handles dispute resolution.\\\\nFREE ADVOCACY:\\\\n\\u2022 Disability Rights California: (800) 776-5746\\\\n\\u2022 DRC SERR Manual: serr.disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 Community Alliance for Special Education (CASE): caseadvocacy.org (Bay Area)\\\\n\\u2022 DREDF: dredf.org\\\",\\n     \\\"PARENT CREATES:\\\\n\\u2022 Written evaluation request letter (dated, keep copy)\\\\n\\u2022 Date-stamped delivery confirmation\\\\n\\u2022 Email copy with read receipt\\\\n\\\\nDISTRICT PROVIDES:\\\\n\\u2022 Assessment plan (within 15 days)\\\\n\\u2022 Parent rights notification (procedural safeguards)\\\\n\\u2022 Prior Written Notice if refusing evaluation\\\\n\\\\nSHARE WITH DISTRICT:\\\\n\\u2022 RC evaluation reports\\\\n\\u2022 Private evaluation reports\\\\n\\u2022 Medical records\\\\n\\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter\\\",\\n     \\\"15 CALENDAR DAYS: District must provide assessment plan after receiving written request.\\\\n60 CALENDAR DAYS: From signed consent to completed evaluation (excluding school breaks >5 days).\\\\n30 CALENDAR DAYS: From completed evaluation to IEP meeting.\\\\nNO DEADLINE TO REQUEST: Parent can request evaluation at any time during the school year.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\n(transition if under 3)\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 SCHOOL BARRIERS: (1) District delays response beyond 15 days. (2) Assessment plan too narrow \\u2014 misses OT, adaptive behavior, or autism-specific testing. (3) District says 'we need RTI data first' \\u2014 ILLEGAL to delay parent-requested evaluation for RTI. (4) Parent makes verbal request only \\u2014 no paper trail. (5) District doesn't share assessment plan or parent rights. (6) District doesn't accept RC evaluations. (7) Assessment not conducted in child's primary language.\\\",\\n     \\\"EVALUATION REQUEST HELP:\\\\n\\u2022 CASE (Community Alliance for Special Education): caseadvocacy.org \\u2014 free advocacy, Bay Area\\\\n\\u2022 DREDF: dredf.org \\u2014 free advocacy\\\\n\\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746 \\u2014 free legal help\\\\n\\u2022 TASK: taskca.org \\u2014 free parent training\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org \\u2014 Bay Area IEP training\\\\n\\u2022 Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com \\u2014 comprehensive SpEd law resource\\\\n\\u2022 Understood: understood.org \\u2014 parent-friendly IEP guides\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Assessment request letter generator with autism-specific areas and legal citations. 15-day and 60-day deadline trackers. Assessment plan completeness checker for autism. Parent rights notification tracker. Cross-reference with RC evaluation to identify gaps.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.2\\\", \\\"School District Evaluation & IEP Development\\\",\\n     \\\"PREPARE FOR AND PARTICIPATE IN EVALUATION AND IEP: (1) School psychologist conducts evaluation (cognitive, academic, social-emotional, adaptive). SLP evaluates communication. OT evaluates sensory/motor. (2) Provide school with RC reports, private evaluations, and your documentation. (3) After evaluation is complete, REQUEST REPORTS 5 days before IEP meeting. Review carefully. (4) At IEP meeting, advocate for: autism eligibility category, speech therapy, OT, behavioral support (BIP if needed), 1:1 aide if appropriate, specialized academic instruction, ESY (extended school year), and APPROPRIATE placement (LRE). (5) Bring a support person \\u2014 advocate, spouse, friend who takes notes. (6) If you disagree with the IEP offer, you do NOT have to sign \\u2014 request a continuation meeting.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Go to the IEP meeting without reading reports first. (2) Go alone \\u2014 bring support. (3) Accept an IEP that doesn't match your child's evaluation results. (4) Accept vague goals. (5) Accept a placement without visiting the classroom first. (6) Sign the IEP if you disagree \\u2014 you can request time to review. (7) Forget to coordinate IEP services with RC IPP services.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: After evaluation, call school: 'When is the IEP meeting scheduled? I'd like copies of all reports at least 5 business days before.'\\\\nSTEP 2: Read reports. Highlight areas of concern and disagreement.\\\\nSTEP 3: Write an IEP preparation letter listing: your priorities, requested services, placement preference, specific goals you want addressed.\\\\nSTEP 4: At IEP meeting: introduce your support person, present your priorities, review each goal, ask about placement options.\\\\nSTEP 5: For each proposed goal, ask: 'How will this be measured? How often? What happens if my child doesn't make progress?'\\\\nSTEP 6: If satisfied, you may sign. If not: 'I'd like to take this home to review. When can we schedule a follow-up meeting?'\\\\nSTEP 7: After IEP is signed, confirm: 'When do services begin? Who are the providers? How will I receive progress reports?'\\\",\\n     \\\"SCHOOL DISTRICT IEP TEAM: Teacher, school psychologist, special ed coordinator, SLP, OT, and parent.\\\\nADVOCACY SUPPORT:\\\\n\\u2022 CASE: caseadvocacy.org \\u2014 free IEP meeting advocate (Bay Area)\\\\n\\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\\\n\\u2022 TASK: taskca.org \\u2014 IEP training and coaching\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix Parent Network: matrixparents.org\\\\n\\u2022 Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com\\\\nIF DISPUTE:\\\\n\\u2022 CDE Complaint: cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/cmplntproc.asp\\\\n\\u2022 Mediation/Due Process: OAH at dgs.ca.gov/OAH\\\\n\\u2022 SELPA for your district\\\",\\n     \\\"IEP DOCUMENTS (district provides):\\\\n\\u2022 IEP document (full written plan)\\\\n\\u2022 Assessment reports (psych, SLP, OT, etc.)\\\\n\\u2022 Prior Written Notice of any decisions\\\\n\\u2022 Parent rights (Procedural Safeguards)\\\\n\\u2022 Behavioral Intervention Plan (BIP) if applicable\\\\n\\u2022 ESY eligibility determination\\\\n\\u2022 Placement offer letter\\\\n\\\\nPARENT SHOULD BRING:\\\\n\\u2022 RC IPP (for coordination)\\\\n\\u2022 Parent Concerns Letter\\\\n\\u2022 Requested services list\\\\n\\u2022 Prior evaluations\\\\n\\u2022 Work samples/videos\\\",\\n     \\\"30 CALENDAR DAYS from completed evaluation to IEP meeting. IEP reviewed ANNUALLY. Triennial re-evaluation every 3 years. Parent can request IEP meeting at ANY time.\\\",\\n     \\\"Local School District\\\", \\\"Regional Center\\\\nParent advocate\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IEP RISKS: (1) School offers minimal services. (2) Goals not measurable or ambitious enough. (3) Placement too restrictive (segregated) OR not enough support (dumped in gen ed without services). (4) No coordination between IEP and RC IPP. (5) Parent overwhelmed in meeting. (6) ESY denied despite regression evidence. (7) BIP based on compliance rather than understanding function of behavior.\\\",\\n     \\\"IEP PREPARATION:\\\\n\\u2022 CASE IEP training: caseadvocacy.org\\\\n\\u2022 Wrightslaw IEP guide: wrightslaw.com/info/iep.index.htm\\\\n\\u2022 Understood IEP guide: understood.org\\\\n\\u2022 TASK IEP workshops: taskca.org\\\\n\\u2022 DRC IEP rights: disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 Matrix IEP training: matrixparents.org\\\\nBOOKS:\\\\n\\u2022 'From Emotions to Advocacy' by Pam Wright\\\\n\\u2022 'The Complete IEP Guide' by Lawrence Siegel\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 IEP prep packet generator. Goal bank by area of need. Service comparison (RC vs school). Placement option comparison. ESY eligibility checklist. BIP review tool. IEP-IPP coordination dashboard. Meeting notes template.\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"4.3\\\", \\\"IEP Dispute Resolution (If Needed)\\\",\\n     \\\"IF YOU DISAGREE WITH THE IEP: (1) First try informal resolution \\u2014 request another IEP meeting. Put disagreements in writing. (2) If informal fails, you have 4 formal options: (a) CDE State Complaint: file at cde.ca.gov \\u2014 CDE investigates within 60 days. Free, no attorney needed. (b) Mediation: voluntary, conducted by OAH mediator. Free. Often resolves disputes. (c) Due Process Hearing: formal legal proceeding through OAH. 45 days to hearing. Attorney strongly recommended. (d) ADR (Alternative Dispute Resolution): your district may offer this. (3) You may request IEE (Independent Educational Evaluation) at district expense if you disagree with their evaluation. (4) Consider contacting DRC for free legal assistance \\u2014 especially for low-income families.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Give up because you're intimidated by the process. (2) File due process without trying other options first (it's adversarial and stressful). (3) Miss the 2-year statute of limitations for filing. (4) Accept retaliation \\u2014 it's illegal for the district to retaliate against you for advocating. (5) Sign an IEP you disagree with \\u2014 you lose leverage.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Put your disagreement in writing: 'I disagree with [specific aspect] of the IEP proposed on [date]. I request [specific remedy].'\\\\nSTEP 2: Request another IEP meeting to try to resolve informally.\\\\nSTEP 3: If no resolution, choose your path:\\\\n  \\u2022 CDE Complaint: File at cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/cmplntproc.asp \\u2014 free online form.\\\\n  \\u2022 Mediation: Request through OAH at dgs.ca.gov/OAH \\u2014 free, often effective.\\\\n  \\u2022 Due Process: File at OAH \\u2014 strongly recommend consulting DRC first: (800) 776-5746.\\\\nSTEP 4: Throughout, DOCUMENT EVERYTHING \\u2014 save emails, keep notes of conversations with dates.\\\",\\n     \\\"CDE COMPLIANCE COMPLAINT: cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/cmplntproc.asp (online filing)\\\\nOAH (OFFICE OF ADMINISTRATIVE HEARINGS): dgs.ca.gov/OAH \\u2014 for mediation and due process. Phone: (916) 263-0880.\\\\nDISABILITY RIGHTS CALIFORNIA: (800) 776-5746 \\u2014 free legal help, priority for low-income families.\\\\nCASE: caseadvocacy.org \\u2014 free advocacy support (Bay Area)\\\\nDREDF: dredf.org \\u2014 free legal advocacy\\\\nSELPA: Contact your district's SELPA for local dispute resolution.\\\\nSPECIAL ED ATTORNEYS (if needed): CASE, DRC, or Education Law referral through your local bar association.\\\",\\n     \\\"DISPUTE DOCUMENTS:\\\\n\\u2022 Written disagreement letter (dated)\\\\n\\u2022 Prior Written Notice request\\\\n\\u2022 CDE complaint form (if filing)\\\\n\\u2022 OAH mediation/due process request\\\\n\\u2022 IEE request letter (if applicable)\\\\n\\u2022 Complete documentation binder (all IEP records, evaluations, correspondence)\\\\n\\\\nTIMELINES:\\\\n\\u2022 CDE complaint: 60 days to investigate\\\\n\\u2022 Due process: hearing within 45 days of filing\\\\n\\u2022 Statute of limitations: 2 years from known violation\\\\n\\u2022 IEE: District must respond (pay or file due process)\\\",\\n     \\\"CDE complaint: 60 days to investigate. Mediation: scheduled within weeks. Due process: hearing within 45 days of filing. 2-YEAR statute of limitations on complaints. IEE: district must respond within reasonable time.\\\",\\n     \\\"School District /\\\\nCDE / OAH\\\", \\\"DRC\\\\nSpecial Ed Attorney\\\\nParent advocate\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Parent doesn't know appeal rights. District pressures parent to sign. Attorney fees $300-600/hr prohibitive. Emotional toll of adversarial process. 2-year statute of limitations passes. District retaliates (illegal but happens).\\\",\\n     \\\"DISPUTE RESOLUTION TRAINING:\\\\n\\u2022 Wrightslaw: wrightslaw.com/info/disres.index.htm\\\\n\\u2022 DRC: disabilityrightsca.org\\\\n\\u2022 CASE advocacy training: caseadvocacy.org\\\\n\\u2022 CDE dispute resolution info: cde.ca.gov/sp/se/qa/\\\\n\\u2022 CADRE (Consortium for Dispute Resolution): directionservice.org/cadre\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Dispute resolution decision tree. Letter templates for each stage. DRC referral with income screening. Attorney cost-recovery guidance (if family prevails, district pays attorney fees). Documentation organizer with timeline. IEE request template.\\\"],\\n\\n    # ===== PHASE 5: INSURANCE & THERAPY =====\\n    [\\\"5\\\\nInsurance &\\\\nTherapy\\\", \\\"5.1\\\", \\\"Insurance Authorization for ABA Therapy\\\",\\n     \\\"GET ABA THERAPY AUTHORIZED: (1) California law (SB 946) mandates health insurance coverage for behavioral health treatment (ABA) for autism \\u2014 this includes Medi-Cal managed care plans. (2) Get physician referral/prescription for ABA from your pediatrician: 'My child has an autism diagnosis and I'm requesting a referral for ABA therapy.' (3) Call your insurance company: 'I need to start the prior authorization process for ABA therapy for my child who has an autism diagnosis.' (4) Choose an ABA provider \\u2014 ask RC for their vendor list, also check insurance provider directory. Interview 2-3 providers before selecting. (5) ABA provider submits treatment plan to insurance. Typical request: 10-40 hours/week depending on severity. (6) If authorized, confirm: hours approved, duration, renewal process. (7) If denied, appeal immediately \\u2014 CA parity law is strong.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Accept denial without appealing \\u2014 CA law mandates ABA coverage for autism. (2) Wait for RC to provide ABA \\u2014 insurance should be billed first (RC is payer of last resort). (3) Choose the first ABA provider without interviewing others. (4) Assume 'ABA' means one thing \\u2014 quality varies enormously. Ask about their approach, qualifications, supervision ratios. (5) Let ABA hours be less than what the evaluation recommends without documented justification.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Get ABA referral from pediatrician (1 phone call \\u2014 'I need an ABA referral for my autistic child').\\\\nSTEP 2: Call insurance (number on card): 'I need to begin prior authorization for ABA therapy. What is the process? What documentation is needed?'\\\\nSTEP 3: Search for ABA providers: (a) Insurance provider directory, (b) RC vendor list, (c) bacb.com (BACB registry of certified analysts).\\\\nSTEP 4: Interview 2-3 ABA providers. Ask: 'What is your supervision model? What is the BCBA-to-client ratio? Do you use naturalistic teaching? How do you involve parents? What does a typical session look like?'\\\\nSTEP 5: Selected provider submits treatment plan to insurance. Insurance has 5-15 business days for prior authorization.\\\\nSTEP 6: If APPROVED: confirm hours, start date, authorization period. If DENIED: appeal immediately \\u2014 contact DRC or Health Consumer Alliance for free help.\\\",\\n     \\\"INSURANCE COMPANY: Call number on back of insurance card. Ask for 'behavioral health prior authorization.'\\\\nMEDI-CAL MANAGED CARE: Each plan has its own ABA authorization process. Alameda Alliance: (510) 747-4500.\\\\nABA PROVIDER SEARCH:\\\\n\\u2022 BACB registry: bacb.com/find-a-certificant\\\\n\\u2022 Insurance provider directory (online)\\\\n\\u2022 RC vendor list (ask Service Coordinator)\\\\nIF DENIED:\\\\n\\u2022 DMHC (HMO plans): (888) 466-2219 \\u2014 Independent Medical Review\\\\n\\u2022 CDI (PPO plans): (800) 927-4357\\\\n\\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org \\u2014 free help\\\\n\\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\\",\\n     \\\"INSURANCE DOCUMENTS:\\\\n\\u2022 Physician referral for ABA\\\\n\\u2022 Autism diagnosis report\\\\n\\u2022 ABA treatment plan (from provider)\\\\n\\u2022 Prior authorization request\\\\n\\u2022 Authorization approval letter\\\\n\\u2022 If denied: denial letter, appeal letter, medical necessity evidence\\\\n\\\\nKEEP TRACKING:\\\\n\\u2022 Authorization start/end dates\\\\n\\u2022 Hours approved vs hours used\\\\n\\u2022 Renewal deadlines\\\\n\\u2022 Progress reports from provider\\\",\\n     \\\"Prior auth: 5-15 business days. If denied, internal appeal: 30 days. DMHC Independent Medical Review: 45 days. Urgent review: 72 hours. Authorization renewal: typically every 6-12 months.\\\",\\n     \\\"Private Insurance /\\\\nMedi-Cal\\\", \\\"Pediatrician\\\\nABA provider\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 Authorization denied despite CA parity law. Insufficient hours approved. Provider not in-network. Medi-Cal managed care barriers. ABA waitlists 3-6 months. Quality varies enormously across providers. Authorization gaps between renewals.\\\",\\n     \\\"INSURANCE HELP:\\\\n\\u2022 Health Consumer Alliance: healthconsumer.org \\u2014 free insurance advocacy\\\\n\\u2022 DMHC Help Center: (888) 466-2219\\\\n\\u2022 CDI Consumer Hotline: (800) 927-4357\\\\n\\u2022 DRC: (800) 776-5746\\\\nABA QUALITY GUIDE:\\\\n\\u2022 Autism Speaks ABA guide: autismspeaks.org\\\\n\\u2022 BACB: bacb.com\\\\n\\u2022 Association for Behavior Analysis International: abainternational.org\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 ABA pre-authorization template. Denial appeal letter with CA parity law citations. In-network provider finder by ZIP. Authorization renewal tracker. Provider quality comparison. DMHC vs CDI identifier (which regulator for your plan).\\\"],\\n\\n    [\\\"\\\", \\\"5.2\\\", \\\"Begin and Coordinate All Therapy Services\\\",\\n     \\\"COORDINATE YOUR CHILD'S FULL SERVICE PACKAGE: (1) Your child may receive services from 3-4 DIFFERENT payers simultaneously: insurance-billed ABA, RC-vendored services (respite, parent training), school district IEP services (speech, OT, SDI), and potentially private pay therapies. (2) Create a MASTER SCHEDULE that accounts for all services without conflicts. (3) Ensure all providers communicate \\u2014 school SLP, private ABA team, RC service coordinator should all know what others are doing. (4) You are the HUB of coordination \\u2014 no one else will do this for you. (5) Track authorization dates, progress reports, and renewal deadlines for each payer.\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Let services overlap without coordination (e.g., school speech goals and private speech goals should align). (2) Miss authorization renewal deadlines \\u2014 services stop if authorization expires. (3) Assume one provider knows what another is doing \\u2014 they usually don't communicate unless you facilitate. (4) Over-schedule your child \\u2014 leave time for play, family, and rest. (5) Forget to track which payer covers which service.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: Create a spreadsheet or use a planner with: Service | Provider | Payer | Schedule | Auth dates | Renewal deadline | Progress report due dates.\\\\nSTEP 2: Share your child's full service schedule with every provider. Ask each provider: 'Are you aware of my child's other services? How will you coordinate?'\\\\nSTEP 3: Set calendar reminders for ALL renewal deadlines (at 60, 30, and 15 days before expiration).\\\\nSTEP 4: Request progress reports from every provider quarterly. Compare progress across settings.\\\\nSTEP 5: Hold a quarterly 'coordination call' or email update with all providers.\\\",\\n     \\\"YOUR SERVICE COORDINATOR HUB:\\\\n\\u2022 ABA Provider: BCBA supervisor (get direct number)\\\\n\\u2022 RC Service Coordinator: direct number and email\\\\n\\u2022 School IEP Case Manager: direct number and email\\\\n\\u2022 Private therapists (if applicable): direct contacts\\\\nCOORDINATION TOOLS:\\\\n\\u2022 Google Calendar shared with providers\\\\n\\u2022 Binder or digital folder organized by provider\\\",\\n     \\\"SERVICE TRACKING DOCUMENTS:\\\\n\\u2022 Master service schedule (all providers/payers)\\\\n\\u2022 Authorization letters (all payers)\\\\n\\u2022 Progress reports (quarterly from each provider)\\\\n\\u2022 Renewal deadline calendar\\\\n\\u2022 Insurance EOBs (Explanations of Benefits)\\\\n\\u2022 RC service authorization letters\\\\n\\u2022 IEP service page\\\\n\\\\nCREATE A 'SERVICE BINDER' with sections for: Insurance, RC, School, Private, Medical\\\",\\n     \\\"ABA waitlists: 2-6 months. Other services typically begin within 30 days of authorization. Insurance re-auth: every 3-12 months. IEP services: begin upon consent. RC services: begin upon IPP signing.\\\",\\n     \\\"Multiple providers\\\\nand payers\\\", \\\"Insurance\\\\nRC\\\\nMedi-Cal\\\\nSchool District\\\",\\n     \\\"Provider waitlists. Scheduling conflicts. Authorization gaps. Billing disputes. Providers don't communicate. Parent burnout from coordination burden. One change cascades across agencies.\\\",\\n     \\\"MANAGING MULTIPLE SERVICES:\\\\n\\u2022 Undivided: undivided.io \\u2014 service coordination tools\\\\n\\u2022 Autism Speaks Resource Guide: autismspeaks.org/resource-guide\\\\n\\u2022 Your ABA company's parent coordinator\\\\nSELF-CARE FOR PARENTS:\\\\n\\u2022 FRCNCA support groups: frcnca.org\\\\n\\u2022 Autism parent support: autismspeaks.org/family-services\\\",\\n     \\\"\\u2605 MASTER SERVICE DASHBOARD: unified calendar across all providers/payers. Authorization renewal reminders 60/30/15 days out. Cross-agency impact alerts. Provider communication facilitator. Conflict-free schedule builder. Progress comparison across settings.\\\"],\\n\\n    # ===== PHASE 6: FINANCIAL PROTECTION & ONGOING =====\\n    [\\\"6\\\\nFinancial\\\\nProtection &\\\\nLifelong\\\\nManagement\\\", \\\"6.1\\\", \\\"CalABLE, SNT & Age-18 Planning\\\",\\n     \\\"LONG-TERM FINANCIAL AND LEGAL PLANNING: (1) CalABLE: Open account at calable.ca.gov immediately \\u2014 no minimum age. Save up to $19K/year. Balances under $100K don't affect SSI. Tax-advantaged growth. (2) Special Needs Trust (SNT): If your child will receive inheritances or large gifts, SNT protects SSI/Medi-Cal eligibility. Consult an SNT attorney ($2K-$5K to establish). (3) AT AGE 18: (a) SSI re-evaluation (child \\u2192 adult criteria \\u2014 PREPARE), (b) Conservatorship vs Supported Decision-Making decision, (c) Transition to adult RC services. (4) Start planning for age 18 by age 16. (5) Consider: Letter of Intent (non-legal document describing your child's daily life, preferences, needs \\u2014 for future caregivers).\\\",\\n     \\\"DO NOT: (1) Skip CalABLE \\u2014 every year of delay is lost tax-advantaged saving. (2) Put money in your child's name without SNT \\u2014 it can disqualify them from SSI/Medi-Cal. (3) Wait until 17 to plan for age 18 \\u2014 conservatorship takes 6-12 months to process. (4) Default to full conservatorship without considering SDM (Supported Decision-Making). (5) Forget the Letter of Intent \\u2014 it's not legally binding but invaluable.\\\",\\n     \\\"STEP 1: CalABLE \\u2014 go to calable.ca.gov TODAY. Click 'Open an Account.' Takes 15 minutes. Fund with even $25/month.\\\\nSTEP 2: By age 14, consult an SNT attorney. Ask your RC Service Coordinator or DRC for referrals to disability-focused estate planning attorneys.\\\\nSTEP 3: By age 16, begin age-18 planning: conservatorship vs SDM research. Read DRC's guide at disabilityrightsca.org.\\\\nSTEP 4: By age 17, if pursuing conservatorship, retain attorney and file petition.
